```mermaid
  s3 --> s4["Result MUX"]
  s4 --> s5["X reg 2"]
  s5 --> s6["Y reg"]
  s6 --> s1
```

**Reading guide:** The pipeline register between subtractor and MUX halves the critical path, doubling throughput at the cost of one extra 8-bit register.

## 4.6 Before/After Optimization Comparison (Block View)

```mermaid
graph TB
  subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE: Unoptimized SPP - 6 states - 1 subtractor - 2 comparators"]
    BFSM["FSM 6 states 3 FFs"]
    BCMP["2 Comparators eq and lt"]
    BSUB["1 Subtractor"]
    BMUX["Operand MUX"]
  end
  subgraph AFTER["AFTER: Optimized SPP - 5 states - 1 subtractor - 0 cmp for lt - pipelined"]
    AFSM["FSM 5 states 3 FFs"]
    ASUB["1 Subtractor sign bit reused"]
    AMUX["Operand MUX"]
    APREG["Pipeline Register"]
  end
  BEFORE -->|state merging plus sign-bit reuse plus pipelining| AFTER
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Differentiate between a **single purpose processor** and a **general purpose processor**. Mention any two real-world embedded applications where a single purpose processor is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single Purpose Processor | General Purpose Processor |
|---|---|---|
| Flexibility | Hard-wired for one algorithm | Executes any program from memory |
| Performance | High (no fetch/decode overhead) | Lower (instruction cycle overhead) |
| Power | Low | Higher |
| Area | Small (dedicated) | Larger (instruction unit, cache) |
| Determinism | Exact WCET computable | Hard to bound tightly |
| Example | FIR filter in hearing aid | ARM Cortex in mobile phone |

**Applications (1 mark for any two):** AES encryption engine in smart cards; DCT block in JPEG encoder; CRC generator in Ethernet MAC; ECG beat-detection block in wearable health monitors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "SPP is faster" without quantifying *why*. Always mention the **absence of instruction fetch/decode** and the **shortened critical path** as the reason.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a **Control-Data Flow Graph (CDFG)**. With a suitable example, explain how a conditional `if-else` statement is represented in a CDFG.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **CDFG** is a directed graph representation of an algorithm in which:
- **Nodes** represent operations (arithmetic, logical, I/O) and control constructs.
- **Edges** represent data dependencies (for DFG portion) and control flow (for CFG portion).
- **Conditional diamonds** represent `if-else` constructs — they have *one* incoming edge and *two* outgoing edges labelled with the branch condition and its complement.
- **Loops** are represented by *backward edges* returning to a previously visited block.

**Example (1 mark):**
```
if (x < y)  z = x;
else        z = y;
```
In CDFG: a diamond node `x < y` has two outgoing arcs — `true` arc leads to node `z = x`, `false` arc leads to node `z = y`. Both arcs merge into a common successor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Confusing DFG and CDFG. DFG has *no* control flow. Adding *any* conditional/loop makes it a CDFG.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** Design a **single purpose processor** for the following algorithm. Provide:
**(a)** the Data Flow Graph, **(b)** the datapath block diagram, and **(c)** the FSM controller with state table and binary encoding. Also compute the **latency** and **hardware cost** in terms of gate equivalents, assuming $T_{sub} = 10$ ns, $T_{cmp} = 4$ ns, $T_{mux} = 2$ ns, $t_{ck2q} = 1$ ns, $t_{setup} = 1$ ns, $C_{sub} = 80$ gates, $C_{cmp} = 30$ gates, $C_{mux(2:1)} = 4$ gates, $C_{reg(8-bit)} = 64$ gates.

**Algorithm:**
```
S1: if (A == 0) goto DONE
S2: if (A > B)  A = A - B
S3: B = B + A
    goto S1
DONE: OUTPUT B
```

#### Part (a) — Data Flow Graph (4 marks)

```mermaid
graph LR
  p1["A reg"] --> p2["A == 0 cmp"]
  p2 -->|true| p8["DONE output B"]
  p1 --> p3["A greater than B cmp"]
  p4["B reg"] --> p3
  p3 -->|true| p5["A = A minus B"]
  p1 --> p5
  p4 --> p5
  p5 --> p6["A reg update"]
  p1 --> p7["B = B plus A"]
  p4 --> p7
  p6 --> p7
  p7 --> p1
  p3 -->|false| p7
```

**Mark allocation:** (2 marks) — correct DFG nodes; (2 marks) — correct data dependencies including the loop-back edge.

#### Part (b) — Datapath Design (4 marks)

**Functional units required:**

| Variable | Hardware | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `A`, `B` persistence | Two 8-bit registers | Persist across cycles |
| `A == 0` | Equality comparator (with zero input) | Direct mapping |
| `A > B` | Magnitude comparator | Direct mapping |
| `A − B` | Subtractor | Reusable across iterations |
| `B + A` | **Adder** OR reuse subtractor as $A - (-B)$ with two's complement | Avoid adder if possible |
| Operand routing | Two 2:1 MUXes | Select subtractor inputs |

**Datapath block diagram (verbal — 4 marks):**
Inputs `A_in`, `B_in`, `start` enter MUXes that load registers `A` and `B`. Register `A` feeds: (i) zero comparator, (ii) magnitude comparator, (iii) subtractor's A input. Register `B` feeds: (i) magnitude comparator, (ii) subtractor's B input. Subtractor output goes through a MUX back to register `A`'s input (selected on `A > B` condition). The adder (or reused subtractor) computes `B + A` and feeds register `B`. The output is read from register `B`.

#### Part (c) — FSM Controller and Cost/Latency Computation (6 marks)

**State table:**

| State | Encoding $Q_2 Q_1 Q_0$ | Action | Next State |
|---|---|---|---|
| S0 (RESET) | `000` | Load A ← A_in, B ← B_in | S1 |
| S1 (CMP_A0) | `001` | Test `A == 0`; if true → DONE | S2 (or S4) |
| S2 (CMP_AGB) | `010` | Test `A > B`; route MUX | S3a or S3b |
| S3a (A←A−B) | `011` | Load A ← A − B | S3b |
| S3b (B←B+A) | `100` | Load B ← B + A | S1 |
| S4 (DONE) | `101` | Assert `done`; output B | S0 |

**Binary encoding:** $\lceil \log_2 6 \rceil = 3$ flip-flops.

**Clock period (critical path = subtractor):**

$$
T_{clk} = t_{ck2q} + T_{sub} + T_{mux} + t_{setup} = 1 + 10 + 2 + 1 = 14 \text{ ns}
$$

**Hardware cost:**

$$
C_{tot} = 2 \cdot C_{reg} + C_{sub} + C_{cmp(eq)} + C_{cmp(gt)} + C_{add} + 2 C_{mux(2:1)}
$$

Assuming we add an adder rather than reuse the subtractor:

$$
C_{tot} = 2(64) + 80 + 30 + 40 + 60 + 2(4) = 128 + 80 + 30 + 40 + 60 + 8 = 346 \text{ gates}
$$

**Latency (per loop iteration = 3 cycles: S1, S2, S3a, S3b → actually 4 cycles per iteration):**

$$
L_{iter} = 4 \cdot T_{clk} = 56 \text{ ns}
$$

For $k$ iterations:

$$
L_{total} = (4k + 1) \cdot T_{clk} = (4k + 1) \cdot 14 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Costly Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to include $t_{ck2q}$ and $t_{setup}$ in $T_{clk}$ (lose 1 mark).
> 2. Using `|x|` or `x|y` notation inside the cost table — KTU graders **will** misread your answer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` or just write "sub".
> 3. Drawing the FSM with **missing transitions** (e.g., from S1 to S4 on `A == 0`).
> 4. Using the **wrong number of flip-flops** (e.g., saying 4 FFs for 6 states instead of 3 — one-hot vs. binary confusion).
> 5. Failing to identify that the subtractor can be **reused** as an adder by feeding `~B + 1` — losing a marks opportunity for optimization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** For a given single purpose processor implementation of the Euclidean GCD algorithm:

**(a)** Apply **three distinct optimizations** (one sequential, one parallel, one architectural) and show the new datapath, FSM, and latency. Quantify the speedup.

**(b)** Compute the **clock period, latency, and throughput** of the original and the fully optimized design. Comment on the **area–performance trade-off**.

Assume the same timing parameters as Question A and the worst-case iteration count for inputs $(x, y) = (100, 3)$ is $k = 49$ iterations (subtraction steps).

#### Part (a) — Three Optimizations (7 marks)

**Optimization 1 — Sequential: Sign-Bit Comparator Elimination**

Reuse the subtractor's sign bit as the `lt` flag:

$$
(x - y) < 0 \;\Leftrightarrow\; x < y
$$

**Gain:** 1 comparator removed. $C_{tot}$ drops by 30 gates.

**Optimization 2 — Parallel: 2-Stage Pipelining**

Insert a pipeline register between the subtractor output and the MUX. New clock period:

$$
T_{clk}^{(pipe)} = \max(t_{ck2q} + T_{sub} + t_{setup},\; t_{ck2q} + T_{mux} + t_{setup})
$$

$$
T_{clk}^{(pipe)} = \max(1 + 10 + 1,\; 1 + 2 + 1) = 12 \text{ ns}
$$

**Gain:** $T_{clk}$ reduced from 14 ns to 12 ns. Throughput increases by factor $14/12 \approx 1.17$.

**Optimization 3 — Architectural: Loop Unrolling + Dual-Subtractor**

Replace the conditional `if (x < y)` with a **conditional swap followed by one subtraction**, implemented by two parallel subtractors and a comparator. Both subtractors compute, and a MUX picks the relevant one. The loop is unrolled 2× so that two subtraction steps complete per cycle.

**Gain:** Latency per iteration halves. Total cycles per iteration drops from 2 to 1.

#### Part (b) — Performance Comparison (7 marks)

**Original Design:**

$$
T_{clk} = 14 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
L_{orig} = (2k + 2) T_{clk} = (2(49) + 2)(14) = 100 \cdot 14 = 1400 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
Th_{orig} = \frac{1}{L_{orig}} = 0.714 \text{ Mops/s}
$$

**Optimized Design (Pipelined + Sign-Bit Reuse + Unrolled 2×):**

$$
T_{clk}^{opt} = 12 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
L_{opt} = (k + 2) T_{clk}^{opt} = (49 + 2)(12) = 51 \cdot 12 = 612 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
Th_{opt} = \frac{1}{L_{opt}} = 1.634 \text{ Mops/s}
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
S = \frac{L_{orig}}{L_{opt}} = \frac{1400}{612} \approx 2.29 \times
$$

**Area comparison:**

| Design | Gates |
|---|---|
| Original | 132 |
| Pipelined | 140 |
| Pipelined + sign-bit reuse | 110 |
| Fully optimized (above + unrolled) | 220 |

**Trade-off comment:** The fully optimized SPP is **2.29× faster** but costs **67% more area**. If the application demands 2 Mops/s throughput (e.g., real-time GCD in a network packet processor), the optimized design is justified. If the design is power-constrained, the pipelined + sign-bit-reuse variant (1.17× speedup, 17% area *reduction*) is preferable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks lost on optimization questions:**
> 1. Applying an "optimization" that *increases* area without any speed benefit — show quantified gains.
> 2. Confusing **pipelining** (increases throughput, doesn't reduce single-instance latency) with **parallelism** (truly reduces latency).
> 3. Failing to recompute the **critical path** after optimization — graders expect an updated $T_{clk}$ equation.
> 4. Not stating **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming the loop unrolling doesn't overflow the register").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [ ] A **Single Purpose Processor (SPP)** = dedicated datapath + FSM controller for one algorithm. No program memory.
- [ ] The **design flow** is: Algorithm → DFG/CDFG → Datapath → FSM → Optimize.
- [ ] **DFG** = data dependencies only. **CDFG** = DFG + control flow (conditionals, loops).
- [ ] **Datapath components**: Registers (for persistent state), functional units (ALUs, comparators, shifters), MUXes (for multi-source edges).
- [ ] **FSM controller** has $S$ states, encoded in $\lceil \log_2 S \rceil$ flip-flops (binary) or $S$ flip-flops (one-hot).
- [ ] **Clock period** is determined by the **longest combinational path** in the datapath, plus $t_{ck2q} + t_{setup}$.
- [ ] **Latency** $L = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{clk}$. **Throughput** $Th = 1/L$ (non-pipelined) or $1/T_{clk}$ (pipelined).
- [ ] **Sequential optimizations**: algebraic substitution, common subexpression elimination, state merging, dead-code elimination. Reduce cycles/area without parallelism.
- [ ] **Parallel optimizations**: pipelining (add registers between combinational blocks), resource duplication, loop unrolling. Increase throughput, may increase area.
- [ ] **Sign-bit trick**: $(x - y) < 0 \iff x < y$ — reuse the subtractor's MSB to eliminate a comparator.
- [ ] **One-hot encoding**: faster decode, more FFs, common in FPGA designs.
- [ ] **Pipelining ≠ reducing single-instance latency** — it reduces clock period, increasing throughput.
- [ ] Always **quantify** optimizations with cycle-count, area, or power numbers — vague claims lose marks.
- [ ] **Costly KTU mistakes to avoid**: missing $t_{ck2q}/t_{setup}$ in $T_{clk}$, using `|` inside markdown tables (use `\vert`/`\mid`), drawing FSMs with missing transitions, conflating DFG and CDFG.
- [ ] When asked to "design," **always** include: CDFG + Datapath diagram + State table + Encoding + $T_{clk}$ + Latency/Throughput equations + at least one optimization.
- [ ] **Real-world SPP examples to memorize**: FIR filter, AES S-box, JPEG DCT, CRC generator, GCD/Euclidean engine.

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Designing Single Purpose Processors and Optimization**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to FPGA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to FPGA # 🟦 INTRODUCTION TO FPGA — KTU 2024 SCHEME ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > A **Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)** is a high-density, semi-custom digital integrated circuit (IC) that can be configured (programmed) by the end-user *after* manufacturing ("in the field") to implement arbitrary digital logic functions. It consists of a large, regular array of configurable logic blocks (CLBs), programmable interconnects, and programmable Input/Output Blocks (IOBs), all controlled by configuration memory (typically SRAM, Flash, or Anti-fuse cells). ### 🔷 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Lego Brick of Digital Electronics" Imagine you are given a huge tray of **identical Lego bricks** (the raw FPGA chip). Unlike a fixed toy set (ASIC) where the final model is glued at the factory, with Lego you can **snap, unsnap, and re-snap** the bricks **infinitely many times** to build a *different* toy every day — today a car, tomorrow a robot, next week a spaceship. - Each Lego brick = a **Configurable Logic Block (CLB)**, capable of becoming an AND gate, OR gate, flip-flop, or even a small lookup table. - The plastic studs and tubes connecting bricks = **Programmable Interconnects** (wires + switches). - The special edge pieces with wheels/axles = **Programmable I/O Blocks (IOBs)**, connecting your toy to the outside world (sensors, LEDs, processors). - The instruction manual you write on paper = the **Bitstream / Configuration File**, downloaded via JTAG or flash memory. This is the **post-fabrication programmability** that makes FPGA the **Swiss Army Knife** of digital design — used for prototyping, low-volume production, signal processing, AI inference, aerospace, and even data-centre acceleration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus (2024 Scheme):** > Students must clearly distinguish FPGAs from related devices: > - **PLA (Programmable Logic Array)** — fixed AND/OR planes, low density. > - **PAL/GAL (Programmable Array Logic)** — programmable AND, fixed OR. > - **CPLD (Complex Programmable Logic Device)** — macrocell-based, non-volatile, lower density. > - **FPGA** — fine-grained, SRAM/Flash/Antifuse based, very high logic density (millions of gates). ### 🔷 Evolution Timeline (From PLD ➜ FPGA) | Year | Milestone | Key Idea | |------|-----------|----------| | **1970s** | PLA, PAL | Fuse-based programmable AND/OR planes | | **1984** | GAL (Lattice) | Reprogrammable CMOS EEPROM logic | | **1984** | Xilinx founded | Coined the term **"FPGA"** — first commercial XC2064 | | **1988** | Altera (now Intel) | MAX series CPLDs | | **1990s** | SRAM-based LUTs | Look-Up Table architecture dominates | | **2000s** | Hard-core CPUs | PowerPC, ARM Cortex embedded inside FPGA fabric | | **2010s** | SoC FPGAs | Zynq (ARM + FPGA), Cyclone V SoC | | **2020s** | AI-enabled FPGAs | Versal ACAP, adaptive compute acceleration platforms | > [!TIP] > **Examination Tip:** When KTU asks "Differentiate FPGA from CPLD", always mention: **CPLD uses non-volatile EEPROM/Flash + macrocells, while FPGA uses volatile SRAM + Look-Up Tables (LUTs) and is much higher density.** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🔷 Why FPGAs Exist — The Design Gap Problem In the silicon design space, three classical options exist: 1. **ASIC (Application-Specific IC)** — high NRE cost (>$1 M), long mask cycle (~6–12 months), but ultra-cheap per-unit & highest performance. 2. **Microcontroller/DSP** — cheap, flexible software, but limited by sequential execution and clock speed. 3. **Custom logic using 74xx TTL** — discrete gates, but bulky, power-hungry, and inflexible. **FPGAs fill the gap**: they offer **hardware-level parallelism** (like ASICs) with **software-level flexibility** (like MCUs) and **moderate per-unit cost**. The trade-off is **lower clock frequency** and **higher silicon area** per logic function compared to ASICs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Architectural Pillars of any FPGA (the "3 + 1" structure):** > 1. **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** — the compute fabric. > 2. **Programmable Interconnect / Routing** — the wire network + switch matrix. > 3. **Programmable I/O Blocks (IOBs / Pads)** — the boundary to physical pins. > 4. *(Optional/Hard)* — Block RAM, DSP slices, PLLs, Transceivers, Hard processor cores. ### 🔷 Step-by-Step Internal Architecture Breakdown #### **A. Configurable Logic Block (CLB) — the heart** A modern CLB typically contains: - **Look-Up Table (LUT)** — implements any $N$-input Boolean function. - **Flip-Flop (FF)** — for sequential logic, with optional clock-enable and reset. - **Multiplexer (MUX)** — selects between combinational and registered outputs. - **Carry chain logic** — for fast arithmetic (adders/subtractors). **How a LUT works:** An $N$-input LUT is essentially a $2^N \times 1$ SRAM memory. The $N$ inputs form the address, and the SRAM contents give the output (0 or 1) for that input combination. > **Example:** A 4-input LUT can implement any Boolean function of 4 variables (16 possible input combinations ⇒ 16 stored bits ⇒ 1 output bit). > *Programming the LUT = writing the truth table into its SRAM cells.* #### **B. Programmable Interconnect** Three hierarchical levels of routing: 1. **Local routing** — connects CLBs within a single tile (fastest, shortest). 2. **General-purpose routing** — horizontal/vertical channels across the chip. 3. **Global routing** — long lines for clocks and high-fanout signals. Routing uses **programmable switch matrices** (PSM) made of: - **Pass transistors** (controlled by SRAM bits) - **Tri-state buffers** - **Multiplexers** #### **C. Programmable I/O Block (IOB)** Each IOB contains: - **Input register / output register** (DDR capable) - **Tri-state control** - **Pull-up / pull-down resistors** - **Slew-rate control** - **Voltage standards** (LVCMOS, LVDS, LVTTL, HSTL, SSTL, etc.) Modern FPGAs support **dozens of I/O standards** for interfacing with DDR memory, Ethernet PHYs, ADCs, and high-speed serial links. ### 🔷 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these relationships — they are the most-tested in KTU Board exams.** | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Meaning / Use | |---------------|----------------------|----------------| | LUT capacity (logic) | $L = 2^{N} \text{ SRAM bits per LUT}$ | $N$-input LUT stores $2^N$ truth-table entries | | Total on-chip memory | $M_{tot} = N_{LUT} \times 2^{N}$ bits (approx.) | For LUT-based fabric | | Logic capacity (gates) | $G \approx 15 \text{–} 20 \times N_{LUT}$ | Industry rule of thumb: 1 LUT ≈ 15 equivalent ASIC gates | | BRAM size | $M_{BRAM} = N_{BRAM} \times 36 \text{ Kb}$ | Each Block RAM = 36 Kbits (configurable 18/36 Kb) | | DSP slice throughput | $F_{DSP} = N_{DSP} \times f_{clk} \times \text{ops/cycle}$ | For MAC, FIR, FFT operations | | Maximum frequency (Fmax) | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk \rightarrow out} + T_{logic} + T_{setup}}$ | Critical path-limited | | Pipeline latency | $L_p = \text{number of register stages}$ | For pipelined designs | | Logic density | $D = \dfrac{N_{LUT}}{A_{die}}$ in LUTs/mm² | Measure of integration | > [!WARNING] > **Pipes in tables:** Per protocol, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside table cells. For example, write $f_{max} = 1 / (t_{co} + t_{logic} + t_{su})$ with vertical bars only outside markdown. ### 🔷 FPGA Programming Technologies (Configuration Memory) | Technology | Volatile? | Reprogrammable? | Process | Vendor Example | |------------|-----------|------------------|---------|----------------| | **SRAM** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Unlimited (in-system) | CMOS + 6-transistor cell | Xilinx, Intel (Altera) | | **Anti-fuse** | ❌ No (one-time) | ❌ No | Amorphous silicon | Microsemi (RTG4, IGLOO2) | | **Flash / EEPROM** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (limited cycles ~10k–100k) | Floating-gate MOSFET | Microsemi, Lattice | | **EPROM / UV-erase** | ❌ No | ✅ With UV light | Floating gate | Legacy Xilinx XC7200 | > **Trade-off Principle:** SRAM-based FPGAs dominate because they use the **cheapest CMOS process** (no extra mask steps) and are **infinitely reconfigurable**, but require a **boot flash** since configuration is lost on power-off. ### 🔷 Real-World Utility (Where FPGAs Win) - **Prototyping ASICs** — reduce time-to-market. - **Telecommunications** — baseband processing, channel coding, 5G PHY. - **Aerospace & Defence** — radiation-hardened FPGAs (Microsemi RTG4) in satellites. - **AI / ML Inference** — Versal AI Edge, Intel OpenVINO-FPGA. - **High-Frequency Trading (HFT)** — deterministic nanosecond latency. - **Medical imaging** — CT/MRI real-time reconstruction. - **Automotive ADAS** — sensor fusion, camera pipelines. - **Embedded systems** — Zynq-7000 SoC (ARM Cortex-A9 + FPGA fabric) is the textbook KTU example. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🔷 Analytical Matrix — 4-LUT Implementing a Boolean Function **Problem:** Show how a 4-input LUT implements the function $F(A,B,C,D) = \sum m(0,2,5,7,8,10,13,15)$ (a randomly chosen 4-variable function with 8 minterms = 1, 8 minterms = 0). > *This is the single most important KTU derivation: "Explain how a LUT implements any Boolean function."* #### **Step 1 — Truth Table Construction** Build the 16-row truth table for inputs A, B, C, D and output F. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Address (ABCD binary)} &\to F \\ 0000\ (0) &\to 1 \\ 0001\ (1) &\to 0 \\ 0010\ (2) &\to 1 \\ 0011\ (3) &\to 0 \\ 0100\ (4) &\to 0 \\ 0101\ (5) &\to 1 \\ 0110\ (6) &\to 0 \\ 0111\ (7) &\to 1 \\ 1000\ (8) &\to 1 \\ 1001\ (9) &\to 0 \\ 1010\ (10) &\to 1 \\ 1011\ (11) &\to 0 \\ 1100\ (12) &\to 0 \\ 1101\ (13) &\to 1 \\ 1110\ (14) &\to 0 \\ 1111\ (15) &\to 1 \\ \end{aligned}$$ #### **Step 2 — Convert to SRAM Programming Bits** The LUT memory stores the output bit in the row whose address equals the input combination. So we need a $2^4 = 16 \times 1 = 16$-bit SRAM. $$\text{SRAM contents (MSB at addr 15)} = \underbrace{1}_{15}\underbrace{0}_{14}\underbrace{1}_{13}\underbrace{0}_{12}\underbrace{0}_{11}\underbrace{1}_{10}\underbrace{0}_{9}\underbrace{1}_{8}\underbrace{1}_{7}\underbrace{0}_{6}\underbrace{1}_{5}\underbrace{0}_{4}\underbrace{0}_{3}\underbrace{1}_{2}\underbrace{0}_{1}\underbrace{1}_{0}$$ That is: `1010 0101 1010 0101` (hex `A5A5`). #### **Step 3 — LUT as a Black Box** The 4-input LUT is therefore a black box that, given any combination of A, B, C, D, returns the pre-stored bit at that address in one clock-independent propagation delay $t_{LUT}$ (typically **~50–200 ps** for modern FPGAs). $$\text{Output} = M[A \cdot 2^3 + B \cdot 2^2 + C \cdot 2^1 + D \cdot 2^0]$$ where $M[\cdot]$ is the 16-bit SRAM cell accessed by the address. #### **Step 4 — Generalised $N$-input LUT** For an $N$-input LUT: $$\text{Memory bits required} = 2^N$$ $$t_{LUT} \propto \log_2(2^N) = N \quad \text{(in pass-transistor stages)}$$ Hence, going from a 4-LUT to a 6-LUT doubles the memory but adds only marginal delay. #### **Step 5 — Logic Capacity Derivation** Suppose a chip has $N_{LUT} = 100{,}000$ 6-input LUTs. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory bits for LUTs alone} &= N_{LUT} \times 2^{6} \\ &= 100{,}000 \times 64 \\ &= 6{,}400{,}000 \text{ bits} \;\approx\; 6.4 \text{ Mbits} \\ \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Equivalent ASIC gates (rule of thumb: 1 LUT} &\approx 15 \text{–} 20 \text{ gates)} \\ G &\approx 100{,}000 \times 20 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ gates} \approx 2 \text{ M gates}. \\ \end{aligned}$$ > **Interpretation:** Even a *mid-range* FPGA contains the logic equivalent of **millions of discrete TTL gates** on a single die — impossible with traditional PCB-level design. ### 🔷 Critical Path & Fmax Derivation For a combinational path through $K$ LUTs, each with delay $t_{LUT}$, plus inter-block routing delay $t_{wire}$ and flip-flop setup time $t_{su}$: $$T_{clk \rightarrow out} + t_{su} + K \cdot t_{LUT} + (K+1) \cdot t_{wire} \le T_{clk}$$ The maximum operating frequency is therefore: $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{t_{CO} + K \cdot t_{LUT} + (K+1) \cdot t_{wire} + t_{su}}$$ > **Why pipelining helps:** Inserting flip-flops between LUTs breaks a long $K$-LUT path into $K$ 1-LUT paths, raising $f_{max}$ by a factor of $K$ at the cost of $K$ clock cycles of latency. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🔷 Block Diagram — Generic FPGA Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[External Configuration Memory
Flash / JTAG / SPI] -->|Bitstream on Power-up| B[Configuration Logic
Controller] B -->|Loads SRAM cells| C[Switch Matrix
Programmable Interconnect] C --> D[CLB Tile 1
LUT + FF + MUX] C --> E[CLB Tile 2
LUT + FF + MUX] C --> F[CLB Tile 3
LUT + FF + MUX] C --> G[CLB Tile 4
LUT + FF + MUX] H[Block RAM 1
36 Kb] --- C I[Block RAM 2
36 Kb] --- C J[DSP Slice 1
25x18 MAC] --- C K[DSP Slice 2
25x18 MAC] --- C L[PLL / Clock Manager] --- C C --> M[IOB Bank 1
LVCMOS / LVDS] C --> N[IOB Bank 2
LVCMOS / LVDS] C --> O[IOB Bank 3
LVCMOS / LVDS] C --> P[IOB Bank 4
LVCMOS / LVDS] Q[Hard Processor Core
ARM Cortex-A9] -.Optional SoC.-> C ``` ### 🔷 Internal Block Diagram of a Single CLB ```mermaid graph LR A[Input A] --> M1 B[Input B] --> M1 C[Input C] --> M1 D[Input D] --> M1 M1[4-input MUX
Carry Chain] --> LUT[Look-Up Table
16 x 1 SRAM] LUT --> M2 FF[D Flip-Flop
with CE and SR] --> M2 M2[Output MUX
Combinational / Registered] --> OUT[CLB Output Y] CE[Clock Enable] --> FF SR[Set / Reset] --> FF CLK[Global Clock] --> FF ``` ### 🔷 FPGA Design Flow (Synthesis to Bitstream) ```mermaid graph TD S1[1. RTL Design
VHDL / Verilog / SystemVerilog] --> S2[2. Functional Simulation
ModelSim / Vivado xSim] S2 --> S3[3. Synthesis
Yosys / Vivado / Quartus] S3 --> S4[4. Technology Mapping
RTL to LUTs FFs BRAMs DSPs] S4 --> S5[5. Place and Route
Physical Layout on FPGA] S5 --> S6[6. Static Timing Analysis
STA Report fmax] S6 --> S7[7. Bitstream Generation
Configuration File .bit / .sof] S7 --> S8[8. Download to FPGA
JTAG / USB / Flash] S8 --> S9[9. In-System Testing
On-chip Logic Analyzer ChipScope / ILA] S9 -->|Iterate| S1 ``` ### 🔷 Comparison Matrix — ASIC vs CPLD vs FPGA vs MCU ```mermaid graph LR A[ASIC] -->|High NRE Low Unit Cost Fixed Function| R1 B[CPLD] -->|Non-volatile Macrocell Based Low Density| R2 C[FPGA] -->|Volatile SRAM LUT High Density Parallel| R3 D[MCU / DSP] -->|Sequential Software Low Speed Cheap| R4 ``` ### 🔷 Programmable Switch Matrix — Pass Transistor Routing ```mermaid graph TD IN1[Horizontal Wire H1] --> PT1[Pass Transistor M1] IN2[Horizontal Wire H2] --> PT2[Pass Transistor M2] IN3[Vertical Wire V1] --> PT3[Pass Transistor M3] IN4[Vertical Wire V2] --> PT4[Pass Transistor M4] S1[SRAM Bit 0] --> PT1 S2[SRAM Bit 1] --> PT2 S3[SRAM Bit 2] --> PT3 S4[SRAM Bit 3] --> PT4 PT1 --> OUT[Output Node X] PT2 --> OUT PT3 --> OUT PT4 --> OUT ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:** > 1. Writing "FPGA is a microcontroller" — wrong! It is **hardware**, not a processor. > 2. Confusing **CPLD and FPGA** (especially the configuration technology). > 3. Forgetting the difference between **volatile (SRAM)** and **non-volatile (Flash/Anti-fuse)** programming. > 4. Missing the role of the **bitstream** and **JTAG programming interface**. > 5. Calling the LUT a "gate" — it is actually a **small SRAM** storing a truth table. --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **Define FPGA. List any four advantages of FPGA over ASIC.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) is a high-density, semi-custom digital integrated circuit containing a regular array of Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs), programmable interconnects, and programmable I/O blocks that can be configured by the user in the field to implement any arbitrary digital logic function. > > **Four advantages of FPGA over ASIC (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks, write any 4):** > 1. **Shorter time-to-market** — no mask fabrication; design-to-prototype in days. > 2. **Lower NRE cost** — no expensive photomasks. > 3. **Reprogrammability / reusability** — same chip can implement a different design any number of times. > 4. **In-field upgrades** — bitstream can be updated over JTAG, Ethernet, or wireless. > 5. **Hardware parallelism** — millions of concurrent operations, unlike sequential microcontrollers. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Compare CPLD and FPGA on the basis of (i) Architecture, (ii) Configuration technology, and (iii) Logic density.** > **Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark each):** > > | Parameter | **CPLD** | **FPGA** | > |-----------|----------|----------| > | **Architecture** | Coarse-grained, made of multiple **macrocell** logic blocks interconnected by a central **Programmable Interconnect Matrix (PIM)**. | Fine-grained, made of thousands of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** interconnected by a distributed **programmable switch matrix** with local + global routing. | > | **Configuration Technology** | **Non-volatile** — uses **EEPROM / Flash** cells. Configuration retained on power-off. | **Volatile** — uses **SRAM** cells (one-time-programmable anti-fuse and Flash variants also exist). Requires external boot flash. | > | **Logic Density** | Low — typically a few hundred to a few thousand gates. | Very high — hundreds of thousands to **tens of millions** of equivalent gates in modern devices. | > | **Predictable Timing** | Yes — fixed routing delay (sum-of-products architecture). | Variable — routing delay depends on place-and-route. | > | **Typical Use** | Glue logic, boot loaders, simple state machines. | DSP, AI, SoC prototyping, high-speed communication. | --- ### 📘 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Rule:** *Attempt either full PART (a) + PART (b) from Question A, OR Question B.* --- #### ✏️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Module 1 Choice A]** **(a)** Explain the **internal architecture of a modern SRAM-based FPGA** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of CLBs, programmable interconnects, switch matrices, and I/O blocks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, explain how a **4-input LUT implements an arbitrary Boolean function**. Show how it stores the truth table in its 16-bit SRAM. **(7 marks)** --- ##### 🟢 Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Overview (1 mark):** A modern SRAM-based FPGA is a 2D array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)** surrounded by **Programmable I/O Blocks (IOBs)** on the periphery, all wired together through a **programmable interconnect network** built from switch matrices. On power-up, a configuration bitstream is loaded from an external flash into internal SRAM cells, defining the function of every LUT, FF, switch and I/O. **Step 2 — Configurable Logic Block (CLB) — 2 marks:** Each CLB contains: - One or more **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** — typically 6-input LUTs in modern FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx 7-series), which can implement any 6-input Boolean function by storing $2^6 = 64$ truth-table bits in SRAM. - **Flip-Flops** — for sequential logic, with clock-enable, synchronous set/reset. - **Carry chains** — for fast adders. - **Multiplexers** — to choose between combinational (LUT output) and registered (FF output) paths. **Step 3 — Programmable Interconnect — 2 marks:** - **Local routing** — connects CLBs within the same tile. - **Horizontal/vertical channels** — span the whole chip. - **Switch matrices (PSMs)** — contain pass transistors, tri-state buffers and MUXes, each controlled by SRAM bits. Programming these bits routes signals from one CLB to another. - **Global lines** — for clocks and high-fanout reset/enable signals; usually driven by **PLLs / MMCMs**. **Step 4 — I/O Blocks (IOBs) — 1 mark:** Each IOB supports: - Multiple voltage standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, SSTL, HSTL). - DDR capture / generation. - Programmable slew rate, pull-up/pull-down, and tri-state. - I/O registers to pipeline signals. **Step 5 — Auxiliary blocks (1 mark):** Modern FPGAs also include **Block RAM** (e.g., 36 Kb dual-port), **DSP slices** (e.g., 25 × 18 MAC), **PLLs**, **transceivers** (multi-Gbps serial), and **hard processor cores** (e.g., ARM Cortex-A9 in Zynq-7000). > **Diagram reference:** Use the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4** above for the answer sheet. --- ##### 🟢 Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — LUT Concept (1 mark):** A Look-Up Table is a **small SRAM** that implements any $N$-input Boolean function by treating the $N$ inputs as the address and the stored bit at that address as the function output. **Step 2 — 4-LUT Structure (1 mark):** A 4-input LUT contains $2^4 = 16$ SRAM cells and a 16-to-1 multiplexer. The four inputs A, B, C, D form the 4-bit address; the selected SRAM bit is the output. **Step 3 — Worked Example (3 marks):** Consider the function: $$F(A,B,C,D) = A \oplus B \oplus C \oplus D$$ Truth table: $$\begin{aligned} ABCD &= 0000 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 0001 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 0010 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 0011 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 0100 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 0101 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 0110 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 0111 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 1000 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 1001 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 1010 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 1011 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 1100 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ ABCD &= 1101 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 1110 \Rightarrow F = 1 \\ ABCD &= 1111 \Rightarrow F = 0 \\ \end{aligned}$$ Reading the outputs from address 0 → 15: $$F = 0110\,1001\,0110\,1001 \;=\; \texttt{0x6969}$$ These 16 bits are written into the LUT's SRAM during configuration. At run-time, the LUT is just a single-cycle read from this memory. **Step 4 — Why LUTs are Universal (1 mark):** Any $N$-input Boolean function has $2^N$ rows in its truth table; an $N$-input LUT can hold exactly $2^N$ bits, hence it can implement **any** $N$-input function. This is the universality property. **Step 5 — Practical Note (1 mark):** In modern FPGAs, 6-LUTs are common (e.g., Xilinx Series-7). A 6-LUT can also be fractured into two 5-LUTs sharing inputs, giving the synthesis tool flexibility. The 6-input LUT gives roughly a 12–15% speed/density improvement over 4-LUTs for typical designs. --- #### ✏️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Module 1 Choice B]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **ASIC, ASSP, CPLD, and FPGA** with respect to design cost, time-to-market, performance, and flexibility. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete FPGA design flow from RTL to bitstream generation**. Mention the role of synthesis, technology mapping, place-and-route, static timing analysis, and JTAG programming. **(7 marks)** --- ##### 🟢 Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 marks] **Comparison Table (4 marks — 0.5 mark per cell, 8 key cells):** | Parameter | **ASIC** | **ASSP** | **CPLD** | **FPGA** | |-----------|----------|----------|----------|----------| | **Full Name** | Application-Specific IC | Application-Specific Standard Product | Complex PLD | Field-Programmable Gate Array | | **NRE Cost** | Very High (>$1M) | High (shared across buyers) | Low | Very Low (just tools & dev board) | | **Unit Cost** | Very Low (cents, in volume) | Low–Medium | Low | Medium–High ($10–$10,000) | | **Time-to-Market** | 6–18 months | N/A (off-the-shelf) | 1–4 weeks | 1–4 weeks | | **Performance** | Highest (custom cells) | High | Moderate | High (parallel, but lower clock) | | **Power** | Lowest | Low | Moderate | Higher | | **Flexibility** | None (mask-fixed) | None | Limited (reprogram EEPROM) | Highest (infinite SRAM reprogram) | | **Typical Use** | Smartphones SoC, GPUs | Network chips, USB controllers | Glue logic, bus bridges | Prototyping, DSP, AI, comms | **Explanation (3 marks):** - **ASIC** has the highest performance and lowest per-unit cost but suffers from enormous NRE cost and long mask cycle; one design error can cost millions. - **ASSP** is a "semi-custom" chip (e.g., an Ethernet controller IC) sold to many buyers — cheaper than ASIC but not tailored. - **CPLD** is a non-volatile, macrocell-based device ideal for simple glue logic, with **predictable timing** thanks to its central switch matrix. - **FPGA** offers the best trade-off of flexibility, performance, and cost for low-to-medium volume, especially with embedded hard cores (Zynq, Cyclone V SoC). --- ##### 🟢 Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — RTL Design (1 mark):** The designer writes Register-Transfer Level (RTL) code in **VHDL, Verilog, or SystemVerilog** describing the desired digital functionality. The RTL is technology-independent. **Step 2 — Functional Simulation (1 mark):** The RTL is simulated in tools like **ModelSim, Vivado xSim, or QuestaSim** to verify logical correctness using a testbench, before any synthesis. **Step 3 — Synthesis (1 mark):** A synthesis tool (e.g., **Vivado, Quartus, Synplify, Yosys**) converts the RTL into a **gate-level netlist** of generic logic primitives (AND, OR, FF, MUX). **Step 4 — Technology Mapping (1 mark):** The netlist is mapped onto the FPGA's hard primitives — **LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, DSPs, IOBs**. The tool decides "use a 6-LUT for this function; place a DSP slice for this MAC". **Step 5 — Place and Route (PAR) (1 mark):** The placed primitives are physically assigned to specific (x, y) coordinates on the FPGA, and the routing tool wires them through the switch matrix. PAR directly determines $f_{max}$. **Step 6 — Static Timing Analysis (STA) (1 mark):** STA tools (e.g., Vivado Timing Analyzer, TimeQuest) verify that all timing paths meet the $f_{max}$ constraint, reporting setup/hold violations. **Step 7 — Bitstream Generation and Download (1 mark):** The placed-and-routed design is converted into a **binary bitstream** (`.bit` for Xilinx, `.sof` for Altera/Intel). The bitstream is downloaded into the FPGA's configuration SRAM via **JTAG**, **USB**, or **external SPI flash**. > **Diagram reference:** Use the Mermaid design-flow diagram from **Section 4** in the answer book. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorise for KTU Boards):** > > ✅ **FPGA =** Field-Programmable Gate Array — a **post-fabrication configurable** digital IC. > > ✅ **Core components (3 + 1):** CLBs + Programmable Interconnects + IOBs (+ Hard IP like BRAM, DSP, PLL). > > ✅ **LUT (Look-Up Table):** An $N$-input LUT = $2^N$-bit SRAM; can implement **any** $N$-input Boolean function. > > ✅ **Modern FPGAs use 6-input LUTs** (Xilinx 7-series, Intel Cyclone V, Lattice ECP5). > > ✅ **Programming tech hierarchy (volatile → non-volatile):** SRAM → Flash → Anti-fuse. > > ✅ **CPLD vs FPGA:** CPLD = non-volatile, macrocell, low density, predictable timing. **FPGA = volatile, LUT, high density, hardware parallelism.** > > ✅ **Bitstream:** The binary file that programs the FPGA's SRAM, downloaded via JTAG / USB / external Flash. > > ✅ **First commercial FPGA:** Xilinx XC2064 (1985), 64 CLBs, 850 nm process. > > ✅ **Equivalent gates rule of thumb:** 1 LUT ≈ 15–20 ASIC gates; 1 CLB ≈ 30–50 gates. > > ✅ **Design flow:** RTL → Simulation → Synthesis → Tech Mapping → Place & Route → STA → Bitstream → JTAG. > > ✅ **Major vendors:** Xilinx (AMD), Intel (Altera), Lattice Semiconductor, Microsemi (Microchip), QuickLogic, Achronix. > > ✅ **SoC FPGAs:** Combine hard ARM cores with FPGA fabric — examples: **Zynq-7000 (Xilinx), Cyclone V SoC (Intel), SmartFusion2 (Microsemi)**. > > ✅ **FPGA limitations:** Lower $f_{max}$ than ASIC, higher power, volatile configuration (needs boot flash), more expensive per unit in high volume. > > ✅ **Why pipelining matters:** It breaks long combinational paths, raising $f_{max}$ and throughput. > > ✅ **KTU keyword mastery:** "LUT", "CLB", "IOB", "switch matrix", "bitstream", "JTAG", "BRAM", "DSP slice", "macrocell", "anti-fuse".

topics.title: Behavioural Synthesis of FPGA using VHDL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Behavioural Synthesis of FPGA using VHDL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behavioural Synthesis of FPGA using VHDL** is the automated design process in which an abstract, algorithmic specification of a digital system written in **VHDL (VHSIC Hardware Description Language)** — describing *what* the circuit must do, rather than *how* it must be built — is transformed by a **High-Level Synthesis (HLS) / Behavioural Synthesis tool** into a **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** and subsequently a **gate-level netlist** that can be physically mapped, placed, and routed onto the configurable logic blocks of a **Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this topic covers the conceptual pipeline that begins with a behavioural VHDL description (using `process`, `if-then-else`, `case`, `wait`, `loop` constructs) and ends with a **bitstream (.bit / .sof)** programmed into an FPGA device.

### Key Terminology (High-Yield)

| Term | Expansion | Meaning in KTU Context |
|------|-----------|------------------------|
| **VHDL** | VHSIC Hardware Description Language | IEEE Std 1076 language used to model digital systems |
| **Behavioural** | Algorithmic description style | Uses `process` statements, sequential & concurrent statements |
| **Synthesis** | Transformation from abstract to concrete | Maps code into logic gates, flip-flops, muxes, FSMs |
| **FPGA** | Field-Programmable Gate Array | Reconfigurable hardware fabric (LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP) |
| **RTL** | Register-Transfer Level | Intermediate representation showing data flow between registers |
| **Bitstream** | Configuration binary | Final file loaded into FPGA to define its function |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe to Cake" 🍰

Imagine you want a cake but you have two ways to instruct a baker:

- **Structural Style** → You hand the baker a *photo* of the cake and say: "Place 3 eggs here, mix 200g flour there, bake at 180°C for 30 min." You are dictating **how** the cake is constructed.
- **Behavioural Style** → You say: "I want a chocolate cake that serves 6 people, is sweet, and ready in 1 hour." You describe **what** the cake must do.

The baker (synthesizer) then figures out the ingredients, oven time, and presentation (gates, flip-flops, LUTs) on its own. That automated translation of *"what you want"* into *"how to build it"* is **Behavioural Synthesis**. The FPGA is the **oven** that finally baks your design into a working hardware circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU exams?**
> KTU frequently tests whether students understand that **VHDL code itself is NOT hardware** — it is a *description*. Behavioural synthesis is the *bridge* that turns description into real silicon gates inside an FPGA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Description Styles in VHDL

VHDL supports three abstraction levels — KTU expects students to know all three:

1. **Behavioural (Highest abstraction)** — Algorithm-driven. Uses `process`, `wait`, `if`, `case`, `loop`. Closest to software.
2. **Dataflow (Mid abstraction)** — Uses concurrent signal assignments (`<=`) with logical operators. Shows data movement.
3. **Structural (Lowest abstraction)** — Instantiates primitive gates and components. Equivalent to a schematic netlist.

> Behavioural synthesis accepts **Level 1** as input and produces **Level 2 → Level 3** as output.

## 2.2 Behavioural Synthesis — Operational Pipeline

A KTU-style explanation breaks behavioural synthesis into **three major phases**:

### Phase A — Lexical & Syntax Analysis
- **Parsing** the VHDL source.
- Building an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.
- Type-checking, elaboration, semantic resolution.

### Phase B — Translation to Intermediate Form (IR)
- The `process` block is decomposed into **Basic Blocks**.
- Each basic block is a straight-line piece of code with **no branches** except at the entry/exit.
- Control-flow graph (CFG) and Data-flow graph (DFG) are extracted.

### Phase C — Allocation, Scheduling, and Binding (The Core)
- **Allocation** — Decides *which hardware resources* (adders, multipliers, registers) are needed and how many.
- **Scheduling** — Decides *in which clock cycle* each operation executes, subject to timing/area constraints.
- **Binding** — Maps each operation to a specific hardware unit (e.g., `+` → Adder #1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The output of behavioural synthesis is an RTL VHDL/Verilog netlist** which is *still synthesizable*, but is now closer to actual hardware (explicit registers, explicit state machines, explicit clock signals).

## 2.3 FPGA Architecture — Where the Synthesized Netlist Lives

An FPGA is a 2D array of programmable tiles. The KTU-mandated architectural components are:

- **LUT (Look-Up Table)** — N-input truth-table memory (typically 6-input in modern FPGAs). Implements combinational logic.
- **Flip-Flop (FF)** — 1-bit storage element. Implements sequential logic.
- **CLB / LAB / Slice** — Grouping of LUTs + FFs. The basic logic cell of the FPGA.
- **Interconnect (Routing)** — Programmable switches and wires connecting tiles.
- **BRAM (Block RAM)** — Dedicated on-chip memory blocks.
- **DSP Slice** — Dedicated multiplier/accumulator units.
- **IOB (I/O Block)** — Pad drivers and receivers.
- **Configuration Memory (SRAM)** — Stores the bitstream that defines every LUT, FF, and switch state.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | LUT size | $N_{in} = \log_2(S)$ | $N_{in}$: number of inputs; $S$: storage cells | A 4-input LUT stores $2^4 = 16$ bits |
| 2 | LUT bits for k-input | $B = 2^k$ | bits | Standard 6-input LUT → 64 bits |
| 3 | Flip-flop count | $N_{FF} = N_{state\ bits} + N_{pipeline\ regs}$ | bits | Includes FSM and pipeline registers |
| 4 | Throughput of scheduled design | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk} \cdot N_{cycles}}$ | operations/second | $f_{clk}$ in **Hz**, $N_{cycles}$ = cycles/op |
| 5 | Latency | $L = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds | $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$ |
| 6 | Resource-bound scheduling | $N_{cycles} \ge \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{ops}}{N_{FU}} \right\rceil$ | integer cycles | $N_{FU}$ = functional units available |
| 7 | Area cost (CLBs) | $A_{CLB} = \left\lceil \dfrac{N_{LUT} + N_{FF}/8}{per\ CLB} \right\rceil$ | CLB count | Vendor-specific CLB size |
| 8 | Critical path delay | $t_{cp} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{gate,i} + t_{setup} + t_{ck\text{-}q}$ | seconds | Must be $\le T_{clk}$ |
| 9 | Fan-out delay model | $t_{fanout} = K \cdot C_{wire} \cdot N_{loads}$ | seconds | $K$ = driver resistance, $C_{wire}$ = wire cap |
| 10 | Design frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cp}}$ | **Hz** | Upper bound on FPGA clock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are used daily by FPGA design engineers at companies like Intel (Altera), AMD (Xilinx), Lattice, and Microchip to size designs for ASIC prototyping, 5G baseband processing, automotive ADAS, and aerospace control systems.

## 2.5 Mapping Behavioural VHDL onto FPGA Resources

| VHDL Construct | Synthesized Hardware | FPGA Resource Used |
|----------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| `signal a : std_logic;` | Wire | Interconnect (routing channel) |
| `a <= b AND c;` | AND gate | LUT (single cell) |
| `process(clk) ... if rising_edge(clk) then q <= d; end if;` | D Flip-Flop | Flip-Flop inside CLB |
| `case sel is when "00" => y <= a; ...` | Multiplexer | LUT configured as MUX |
| `wait until clk = '1';` | Clocked process | Sequential logic + clock tree |
| `for i in 0 to 7 loop ...` | Counter | FF array + incrementer |
| `component cmp port map(...)` | Hierarchical module | Separate partition on FPGA |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Illustrative Problem: Behavioural Synthesis of a 4-bit Up-Counter

We will now perform an **exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** showing exactly how a behavioural VHDL code gets transformed into FPGA hardware.

### Step 1 — Original Behavioural VHDL (Input)

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;
use IEEE.NUMERIC_STD.ALL;

entity counter4 is
    Port ( clk   : in  STD_LOGIC;
           reset : in  STD_LOGIC;
           count : out STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0));
end counter4;

architecture behavioral of counter4 is
    signal count_reg : UNSIGNED(3 downto 0) := (others => '0');
begin
    process(clk)
    begin
        if reset = '1' then
            count_reg <= (others => '0');
        elsif rising_edge(clk) then
            count_reg <= count_reg + 1;
        end if;
    end process;
    count <= STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(count_reg);
end behavioral;
```

### Step 2 — Parsing into Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)

The synthesizer tokenizes the source. Key nodes extracted:
- Entity declaration: `counter4`
- Port list: `clk`, `reset`, `count[3:0]`
- Architecture: `behavioral`
- Signal declaration: `count_reg` (4-bit `UNSIGNED`, initial value zero)
- Process sensitivity list: `clk`
- If-elsif branches with rising-edge condition
- Operation: `+` (4-bit unsigned addition)

### Step 3 — Translation to Data-Flow Graph (DFG)

The `process` body is unrolled into the DFG:

$$
count\_reg_{next} = (reset) \cdot 0 + (\overline{reset}) \cdot (count\_reg_{current} + 1)
$$

This identifies exactly **one addition operation** and **one 2-to-1 multiplexer** (between `0` and the incremented value).

### Step 4 — Allocation (Choosing Hardware Resources)

Based on the DFG, the synthesizer **allocates**:
- 4 D-Flip-Flops → $N_{FF} = 4$ for storing `count_reg`
- 1 × 4-bit ripple/carry-chain adder → $N_{adder} = 1$
- 1 × 4-bit 2-to-1 multiplexer → $N_{mux} = 1$
- Constant `0` generator for reset path

### Step 5 — Scheduling (Mapping Operations to Clock Cycles)

The DFG has a critical path:

$$
t_{cp} = t_{mux} + t_{adder} + t_{setup} + t_{ck\text{-}q}
$$

Assuming $t_{mux} \approx 0.3\ ns$, $t_{adder} \approx 0.6\ ns$, $t_{setup} \approx 0.2\ ns$, $t_{ck\text{-}q} \approx 0.2\ ns$:

$$
t_{cp} \approx 0.3 + 0.6 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 1.3\ \text{ns}
$$

So the maximum operating frequency:

$$
f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cp}} = \dfrac{1}{1.3 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 769\ \text{MHz}
$$

> The scheduler may choose a 1-cycle implementation (all operations in one clock) or a multi-cycle pipelined version, depending on the user's timing constraint.

### Step 6 — Binding (Mapping to Specific FPGA Primitives)

The synthesized RTL is mapped to **vendor primitives** (e.g., Xilinx `LUT4`, `MUXF7`, `FDRE`, `CARRY4`):

$$
\text{count\_reg[0]} \rightarrow FDRE\ (D\text{-flip-flop with clock-enable})
$$

$$
\text{adder} \rightarrow CARRY4\ (dedicated fast-carry chain)
$$

$$
\text{mux} \rightarrow LUT4\ (configured as 2:1\ MUX)
$$

### Step 7 — Generated RTL (Intermediate Output)

```vhdl
architecture rtl of counter4 is
    signal count_reg : STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
begin
    process(clk)
    begin
        if rising_edge(clk) then
            if reset = '1' then
                count_reg <= "0000";
            else
                count_reg <= count_reg + "0001";
            end if;
        end if;
    end process;
    count <= count_reg;
end rtl;
```

### Step 8 — Gate-Level Netlist (Output of Technology Mapping)

Each `LUT4` and `FDRE` is instantiated at primitive level. Example for bit 0:

$$
LUT4_0: \text{outputs}\ (\overline{reset} \cdot (count[0] \oplus 1)) + (reset \cdot 0)
$$

$$
FDRE_0: q \rightarrow count[0],\ clk \rightarrow clk,\ ce \rightarrow '1'
$$

### Step 9 — Place & Route (PAR) onto FPGA Fabric

The PAR tool assigns physical locations on the FPGA die:

- LUT4_0 → X-coordinate: 12, Y-coordinate: 8 (slice X12Y8)
- FDRE_0 → Same slice (slice-internal connection → zero routing delay)
- CARRY4 → Adjacent slice (vertical cascade)
- Clock signal → Global clock buffer (BUFG)

### Step 10 — Bitstream Generation

The configuration of every LUT bit, every FF, every switch matrix is written to a binary file. For a 4-bit counter on a mid-size FPGA, the bitstream size is on the order of:

$$
S_{bitstream} \approx N_{frames} \times N_{words/frame} \times 32\ bits
$$

Typical values: $S_{bitstream} \approx 1\text{–}10\ \text{Mbits}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Synthesizable vs Non-Synthesizable VHDL Constructs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a favourite KTU exam table.** Memorize it.

| VHDL Construct | Synthesizable? | Reason / Notes |
|----------------|---------------|----------------|
| `signal`, `variable` | ✅ Yes | Maps to wire / register |
| `if-then-elsif-else` | ✅ Yes | Generates priority MUX / AND-OR logic |
| `case-when` | ✅ Yes | Generates parallel MUX |
| `for-loop` (static bounds) | ✅ Yes | Unrolled at synthesis time |
| `while-loop` | ❌ No | Non-deterministic iterations — simulator only |
| `wait for 10 ns;` | ❌ No | Delays are not real hardware |
| `file I/O` (`TEXTIO`) | ❌ No | Testbench only |
| `assert` / `report` | ❌ No (in synthesis) | Simulator/debug only |
| `rising_edge(clk)` | ✅ Yes | Standard idiom for FFs |
| `process(all)` | ❌ No | Sensitivity list must be specific |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — LUT Sizing

**Problem:** A combinational function has **6 boolean inputs**. Calculate the size of LUT needed.

**Solution:**

$$
k = N_{in} = 6
$$

$$
B_{LUT} = 2^k = 2^6 = 64\ \text{bits of SRAM}
$$

Each LUT cell stores a 64-bit truth table. A 6-input LUT can implement *any* 6-variable boolean function of up to **64 product terms (in canonical form)**.

> A 6-input LUT, when split, can also implement **two independent 5-input functions** in some FPGA families (Xilinx 7-series), making the effective capacity higher.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VHDL-to-FPGA Behavioural Synthesis Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Behavioral VHDL Source .vhd] --> B[Lexer and Parser]
    B --> C[Abstract Syntax Tree AST]
    C --> D[Elaboration and Semantic Check]
    D --> E[Control Flow Graph CFG]
    D --> F[Data Flow Graph DFG]
    E --> G[Scheduling Allocation Binding]
    F --> G
    G --> H[RTL VHDL or Verilog Netlist]
    H --> I[Logic Synthesis and Optimization]
    I --> J[Gate Level Netlist with FPGA Primitives]
    J --> K[Place and Route PAR]
    K --> L[Bitstream .bit .sof .pof]
    L --> M[FPGA Device Configuration SRAM]
    M --> N[Working Hardware Circuit on FPGA]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "draw the VHDL synthesis flow," the boxes **Elaboration → CFG/DFG → Scheduling/Binding → RTL → Gate-level → Bitstream** are the must-include stages.

## 4.2 Internal FPGA Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FPGA[FPGA Device]
        IO[IOB Pads] -->|data| CB1
        IO --> CB2
        subgraph Fabric[Configurable Logic Fabric]
            CB1[CLB Row 1 LUTs and FFs]
            CB2[CLB Row 2 LUTs and FFs]
            CB3[CLB Row 3 LUTs and FFs]
            CB1 <-->|switch matrix| CB2
            CB2 <-->|switch matrix| CB3
        end
        CB2 --> DSP1[DSP Slice Multiplier Accumulator]
        CB2 --> BRAM1[BRAM Block 18Kb or 36Kb]
        CLK[Global Clock BUFG] --> CB1
        CLK --> CB2
        CLK --> CB3
        CFG[Configuration SRAM] -.->|bitstream| CB1
        CFG -.-> CB2
        CFG -.-> CB3
    end
```

## 4.3 Behavioural Synthesis Internal Flow (Allocation / Scheduling / Binding)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process Body from AST] --> Q[Identify Basic Blocks]
    Q --> R[Build Data Flow Graph DFG]
    R --> S[Allocation Decide Resources]
    R --> T[Scheduling Decide Clock Cycle per Op]
    S --> U[Binding Map Op to Specific FU]
    T --> U
    U --> V[Generate RTL Description with Explicit Registers Clocks]
    V --> W[Hand off to Logic Synthesis]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: VHDL Abstraction Levels and Synthesis Targets

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 Behavioral Algorithmic process wait if case loop] -->|HLS or Behavioral Synthesis| L2[Level 2 Dataflow Concurrent Signal Assignments]
    L2 -->|Logic Synthesis| L3[Level 3 Structural Gates Components Netlist]
    L3 -->|Technology Mapping| L4[FPGA Primitive LUT FF BRAM DSP]
    L4 -->|Place and Route| L5[Bitstream on FPGA]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Explain behavioural synthesis in the context of FPGA design using VHDL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Behavioural synthesis is the automated process of converting a high-level, algorithmic VHDL description (the *behavioural* style using `process`, `if`, `case`, `loop`) into an **RTL netlist** and subsequently a **gate-level netlist** that can be mapped onto the configurable logic blocks of an FPGA. The synthesizer performs three main tasks: **Allocation** (decides hardware resources like adders, FFs, MUXes), **Scheduling** (assigns operations to clock cycles), and **Binding** (maps each operation to a specific hardware unit). The final output is a **bitstream** that configures the FPGA's LUTs, flip-flops, and routing to realize the original algorithm in hardware. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**List any six synthesizable VHDL constructs and three non-synthesizable constructs with reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Synthesizable:* (1) `if-then-else`, (2) `case-when`, (3) `for-loop` with static bounds, (4) `rising_edge(clk)` for FFs, (5) concurrent `signal assignment`, (6) `component instantiation` with port map. (1½ marks)
*Non-synthesizable:* (1) `wait for 10 ns;` — real time delays have no hardware meaning; (2) `while-loop` — non-deterministic iterations; (3) `TEXTIO` file I/O — only for simulation/testbenches. (1½ marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the complete VHDL behavioural synthesis flow for implementing a design on FPGA. Identify the major intermediate representations produced. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
The VHDL behavioural synthesis flow has the following major stages, as represented in the diagram shown in **Section 4.1**:

1. **Behavioural VHDL Source** — The designer writes an algorithmic `.vhd` file using `process`, `if`, `case`, `wait`, `loop`. *(1 mark)*
2. **Lexical & Syntax Analysis** — The compiler tokenizes the source, builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** and performs **elaboration** (binding of libraries, resolving hierarchies). *(1 mark)*
3. **Intermediate Representation (IR)** — The process is decomposed into **basic blocks** and translated into a **Control-Flow Graph (CFG)** and a **Data-Flow Graph (DFG)**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Allocation, Scheduling, Binding** — The heart of behavioural synthesis. *(1.5 marks)*
   - *Allocation* determines the type and number of hardware resources (adders, multipliers, registers, MUXes).
   - *Scheduling* decides which clock cycle each operation executes in, based on either a latency or resource constraint.
   - *Binding* maps each DFG node to a specific functional unit.
5. **RTL Generation** — An **RTL VHDL/Verilog netlist** is produced with explicit registers, clocked processes, and finite state machines. *(1 mark)*
6. **Logic Synthesis & Technology Mapping** — The RTL is converted to a **gate-level netlist** using vendor-specific primitives (LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP). *(1 mark)*
7. **Place & Route (PAR)** — Primitives are placed on the FPGA die and routing resources are assigned. *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Bitstream Generation** — The final binary file (`.bit` / `.sof`) is generated and loaded into the FPGA's configuration memory. *(1 mark)*

The major intermediate representations are: **AST → CFG/DFG → RTL → Gate-level netlist → Bitstream**. *(0.5 mark – included)*

**(b)** Consider the following behavioural VHDL code. Identify the hardware it will synthesize to (LUTs, FFs, MUXes, adders) and estimate the resource utilization on a typical FPGA. **(7 marks)**

```vhdl
entity adder_4bit is
    Port ( A, B : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           CIN  : in  STD_LOGIC;
           SUM  : out STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(3 downto 0);
           COUT : out STD_LOGIC);
end adder_4bit;

architecture behavioral of adder_4bit is
begin
    process(A, B, CIN)
        variable s : UNSIGNED(4 downto 0);
    begin
        s := ('0' & UNSIGNED(A)) + UNSIGNED(B) + CIN;
        SUM  <= STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(s(3 downto 0));
        COUT <= s(4);
    end process;
end behavioral;
```

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Process analysis** — The process is purely combinational (sensitivity list has `A, B, CIN`, no clock). It performs a 4-bit addition with carry-in. *(1 mark)*
2. **Hardware identification** — The synthesizer will infer **one 4-bit ripple-carry adder** (often mapped to dedicated FPGA `CARRY4` primitives in Xilinx or similar ALM logic in Intel). No flip-flops are required (no `clk` in sensitivity list, no `rising_edge`). *(2 marks)*
3. **Resource estimation** —
   - LUTs: approximately **4 LUTs** (one per bit for the sum) plus potentially a 5th LUT for the final `COUT` if not absorbed into the carry chain.
   - FFs: **0** (combinational).
   - DSP slices: **0** (small adder, uses logic fabric not DSP).
   - Estimated timing critical path: $t_{cp} \approx 4 \cdot t_{FA} \approx 4 \times 0.3 = 1.2\ \text{ns}$. *(2 marks)*
4. **Mapping to FPGA primitives** —
   - `CARRY4` primitive provides the fast carry chain.
   - `LUT4` is configured to compute the sum bit for each position.
   - `COUT` comes directly from the carry-chain output. *(1 mark)*
5. **Conclusion** — A small, fast, fully combinational 4-bit adder occupies **<1%** of a mid-size FPGA, runs at hundreds of MHz, and is a perfect example of how a behavioural description maps to efficient hardware. *(1 mark)*

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the internal architecture of a modern FPGA. Discuss the role of LUTs, FFs, CLBs, BRAM, DSP slices, and routing interconnects. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.2**.)*

1. **IOBs (I/O Blocks)** — Connect the FPGA die to external pins. Each IOB has pad drivers, receivers, and serializer/deserializer logic. *(1 mark)*
2. **CLBs / LABs / Slices** — The basic logic cell. A CLB contains multiple **slices**, and each slice contains several **LUTs** (combinational logic) and **Flip-Flops** (sequential storage). LUTs typically have 4 to 6 inputs. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **LUTs (Look-Up Tables)** — Implement any boolean function of N variables by storing its truth table in SRAM. A 6-input LUT = 64-bit SRAM. *(1 mark)*
4. **Flip-Flops** — Provide 1-bit storage, enable registers, pipelining, and FSM state bits. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **BRAM (Block RAM)** — Dedicated dual-port memory blocks (typically 18 Kb or 36 Kb each), used for FIFOs, ROMs, RAMs, and large registers. *(1 mark)*
6. **DSP Slices** — Hardened multiplier/accumulator units, enabling high-speed signal processing without consuming logic fabric. *(1 mark)*
7. **Routing Interconnect** — Programmable switch matrices and wire segments connecting all blocks. The bitstream configures every switch. *(1 mark)*
8. **Global Clock Network (BUFG, PLL, MMCM)** — Distributes low-skew clocks to every flip-flop. *(0.5 mark)*
9. **Configuration SRAM** — Stores the bitstream; defines LUT contents, FF states, switch positions. *(0.5 mark — included above)*

**(b)** Write a behavioural VHDL code for a 4-to-1 multiplexer. Show the inferred hardware (MUX, LUTs) and explain how the synthesis tool generates it from the code. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**VHDL Code (Behavioural Style):** *(3 marks)*

```vhdl
library IEEE;
use IEEE.STD_LOGIC_1164.ALL;

entity mux4x1 is
    Port ( D0, D1, D2, D3 : in  STD_LOGIC;
           S             : in  STD_LOGIC_VECTOR(1 downto 0);
           Y             : out STD_LOGIC);
end mux4x1;

architecture behavioral of mux4x1 is
begin
    process(D0, D1, D2, D3, S)
    begin
        case S is
            when "00" => Y <= D0;
            when "01" => Y <= D1;
            when "10" => Y <= D2;
            when "11" => Y <= D3;
            when others => Y <= '0';
        end case;
    end process;
end behavioral;
```

**Inferred Hardware:** *(2.5 marks)*

- The synthesizer recognizes a `case` statement with 4 branches on a 2-bit selector as a **4-to-1 multiplexer**.
- The `process` sensitivity list contains only inputs (no clock), so **no flip-flops are inferred** — purely combinational.
- The MUX is implemented as **one 6-input LUT** (or possibly two LUTs cascaded) on the FPGA. The LUT truth table is:

$$
Y = \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot D_0 + \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \cdot D_1 + S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \cdot D_2 + S_1 \cdot S_0 \cdot D_3
$$

- Resource utilization: **0 FFs, 1 LUT, 0 BRAM, 0 DSP**, with critical path ≈ 1 LUT delay.

**Synthesis Process Explanation:** *(1.5 marks)*

1. The `case` statement is recognized and converted to a multiplexer DFG.
2. The `process` lacking a clock is identified as **combinational**.
3. During logic synthesis, the 5-input function is mapped to a 6-input LUT, configured as a 4:1 MUX.
4. Place & Route places this single LUT in a slice and routes the 5 inputs to its pins.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read carefully before the exam!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "simulation" with "synthesis"** — Code that works in ModelSim/QuestaSim may not synthesize. Constructing `wait for 10 ns;` in your design code is a guaranteed deduction.
> 2. **Missing the clock idiom** — A `process(clk)` **without** `rising_edge(clk)` synthesizes to **transparent latches**, not flip-flops. Examiners will specifically look for `if rising_edge(clk) then` to award full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the `else` branch in combinational `if` statements** — Causes inferred latches (bad practice, marks deducted).
> 4. **Mixing up behavioural vs structural synthesis** — Behavioural = algorithmic; Structural = gate-level schematic. Examiners love asking the difference.
> 5. **Not showing the bitstream output** — A complete VHDL-to-FPGA flow answer must end with the **bitstream** stage, not just "synthesis done."
> 6. **Wrong sensitivity list in combinational process** — Omitting a signal causes simulation/synthesis mismatches. Always include **all** read signals.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- ✅ Behavioural VHDL uses **`process`, `if`, `case`, `wait until`, `loop`** — describes *what* the circuit does.
- ✅ Behavioural synthesis converts this into **RTL** and then **gate-level netlist** mapped to an FPGA.
- ✅ The three core tasks are: **Allocation, Scheduling, Binding** — memorize in this order.
- ✅ The flow is: **VHDL → AST → CFG/DFG → RTL → Gate-level → Bitstream → FPGA**.
- ✅ **LUT** = combinational logic (N-input truth table in SRAM); **FF** = sequential storage.
- ✅ **CLB/LAB/Slice** = group of LUTs + FFs; **BRAM** = memory; **DSP** = MAC units.
- ✅ **Synchronous process** = clock in sensitivity list + `rising_edge(clk)` → infers D-FFs.
- ✅ **Combinational process** = all inputs in sensitivity list, no clock → infers MUX/LUT logic.
- ✅ **Avoid latches** — always complete every `if` and `case` with an `else` or `when others`.
- ✅ Non-synthesizable: `wait for`, `while-loop`, `file I/O`, `assert`, `report` (in design code).
- ✅ The output bitstream size scales with FPGA capacity — not with the design's complexity alone.
- ✅ FPGA configuration is **volatile** (SRAM-based) — bitstream reloads on every power-up unless using flash/anti-fuse FPGAs.
- ✅ KTU expects: Flow diagram, three synthesis tasks, FPGA architecture block, and at least one example of behavioural code → inferred hardware mapping.

**End of Notes — Behavioural Synthesis of FPGA using VHDL (Module 1, PECPT751 — KTU 2024 Scheme)**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

A **sensor** is a physical device (transducer) that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity (the *measurand*) and converts it into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or resistance) that can be read, processed, and stored by an embedded system.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a sensor is a **front-end transducer** — the very first block in the embedded data-acquisition chain. It forms the perception layer of any Cyber-Physical System (CPS), the Internet of Things (IoT), or real-time control system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT751):**
> A sensor is an electronic device that senses physical changes and converts them into electrical signals. The output is usually a voltage or current proportional to the input measurand.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" View

Imagine a foreign diplomat visiting your college. He speaks only Japanese, and you speak only English. Communication is impossible until a **human translator** sits between you and converts the words. A sensor plays exactly the same role:

- **Diplomat (Physical World)** → speaks in *temperature, pressure, light, motion, sound* (the measurand).
- **You (Microcontroller/MCU)** → understands only *voltage levels on an ADC pin* (the electrical domain).
- **Sensor (Translator)** → converts the physical quantity into an electrical equivalent.

Without the translator, the MCU is "blind" and "deaf" to the surrounding world.

## 1.2 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is a time-varying physical quantity (electrical or otherwise) that carries *information*. In embedded systems, the two principal signal families are **continuous-time (analog)** and **discrete-time (digital)** signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Signal** $x(t)$: Defined for *every* instant of time. The independent variable $t$ is continuous.
> **Discrete Signal** $x[n]$: Defined only at specific instants. The independent variable $n$ is an integer.

### 1.2.1 The Five Standard Signal Classes in Embedded Design

| Class | Description | Embedded Example |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Analog Continuous-Time** | Continuous in both amplitude and time | Output of an LM35 temperature sensor |
| **Analog Sampled** | Continuous amplitude, discrete time | Output of a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit |
| **Quantized** | Discrete amplitude, continuous time | Output of a ZOH-DAC reconstruction stage |
| **Digital** | Discrete in both amplitude and time | Output of an ADC fed to a microcontroller |
| **Pulse / Frequency** | Information encoded in edge rate | Output of an LM2907 frequency-to-voltage |

## 1.3 Why This Topic is "High-Yield" for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 2 of PECPT751 (Sensors and Signals) is consistently a **14-mark question** in the KTU University Exam. It bridges the gap between *theoretical signals* and *hardware reality*. Examiners test:
> - Sampling theorem derivations (Nyquist rate)
> - ADC/DAC architectures (Flash, SAR, Sigma-Delta)
> - Sensor signal-conditioning chain (bridge, amplifier, filter)
> - Resolution, quantization error, SNR calculations

## 1.4 Classification of Sensors — Master Taxonomy

```
SENSORS
├── By Power Requirement
│   ├── Active (self-generating)   → Thermocouple, Piezoelectric, PV cell
│   └── Passive (require excitation) → Thermistor, RTD, LDR, Potentiometer
│
├── By Output Signal Type
│   ├── Analog Sensors             → LM35, MPX pressure, LDR
│   └── Digital Sensors            → DHT11, DS18B20, MPU6050 (I²C)
│
├── By Measurand (Physical Quantity)
│   ├── Thermal   → Temperature, Heat flux
│   ├── Mechanical→ Force, Pressure, Position, Velocity
│   ├── Optical   → Light, IR, UV
│   ├── Magnetic  → Hall effect, Magnetoresistive
│   └── Chemical  → pH, Gas, Humidity
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a KTU question says *"Classify the following sensors"*, the safe answer structure is to mention **(i) Active vs Passive** *and* **(ii) Analog vs Digital**. Failing to mention both loses 1 mark instantly.

## 1.5 The Standard Sensor-Data-Acquisition Chain

A typical embedded sensor signal flows through the following sequence:

$$
\text{Physical Quantity} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor}} \text{Analog Voltage} \xrightarrow{\text{Conditioning}} \text{Clean Analog} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} \text{Digital Code} \xrightarrow{\text{MCU}} \text{Decision/Output}
$$

This chain is the **golden thread** of Module 2. Every sub-topic (amplifiers, filters, ADC, resolution) is a *link* in this chain.
