# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Performance Parameters — The Six Pillars

Every sensor in a KTU exam must be characterized by these six metrics. Memorize the exact symbols and units.

### 2.1.1 Static Characteristics

1. **Range (Span)** — The difference between the maximum and minimum measurand the sensor can detect.
   $$\text{Range} = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$$

2. **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** — The algebraic difference between the output at the upper and lower range limits.
   $$\text{FSO} = V_{out,\max} - V_{out,\min}$$

3. **Accuracy** — The closeness of the measured value to the *true* value. Expressed as a percentage of FSO.
   $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Measured} - \text{True}}{\text{True}} \times 100\%$$

4. **Precision (Repeatability)** — The ability of the sensor to reproduce the same output for the same input under identical conditions. *Precision ≠ Accuracy*.

5. **Resolution** — The *smallest* change in measurand that produces a detectable change in output.
   $$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{FSO}}{2^N - 1}$$ for an N-bit ADC.

6. **Sensitivity (K)** — The ratio of output change to input change.
   $$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{Volt}}{\text{Unit of measurand}} \right]$$

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Characteristics

7. **Time Constant (τ)** — Time to reach 63.2% of the final value in a first-order system.
8. **Settling Time** — Time for output to settle within ±2% (or ±5%) of final value.
9. **Bandwidth** — The frequency range over which the sensor responds linearly.
10. **Dynamic Error** — Difference between true instantaneous value and sensor output for a time-varying input.

## 2.2 The Sampling Theorem (Whittaker–Nyquist–Shannon)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most heavily tested theorem in Module 2.** Appears in 100% of past KTU 2022/2023 papers.

**Statement:** A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ (Hz) can be *uniquely* reconstructed from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s$ is **strictly greater than twice** the highest frequency in the signal.

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$

- $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.
- $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$ causes **Aliasing** (spectral folding).
- $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the **Sampling Period**.

### 2.2.1 Why "Strictly Greater Than"?

Mathematically, equality $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ is borderline — it requires an *ideal* brick-wall low-pass filter that is unrealizable. Hence KTU boards accept $f_s > 2 f_{\max}$ as the safe condition, with $f_s \geq 5 f_{\max}$ used in practice for a guard band.

## 2.3 Quantization and Resolution

When an ADC samples an analog signal of range $[0, V_{ref}]$, each sample is rounded to the nearest of $2^N$ discrete levels.

$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \text{1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)}$$

This $Q$ is the **quantization step size** or **resolution**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** "An 8-bit ADC is used with $V_{ref} = 5$ V. What is the smallest voltage it can distinguish?"
> Answer: $Q = 5/2^8 = 19.53$ mV. NOT $5/256$ written as a decimal — show the *exact* units.

## 2.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and Quantization Noise

For an ideal N-bit ADC, the SNR (in dB) due solely to quantization is given by the celebrated formula:

$$\text{SNR}_{q} = 6.02N + 1.76 \quad [\text{dB}]$$

This is derived in Section 3. The "+1.76" term is a constant for a *full-scale sine wave* input.

> [!NOTE]
> Each additional bit of resolution adds **6.02 dB** of SNR. This is the "6 dB per bit" rule of thumb used by audio engineers (CDs use 16 bits → ~96 dB SNR; telephony uses 8 bits → ~50 dB).

## 2.5 Signal Conditioning — The "Signal Polish" Stage

Raw sensor outputs are rarely microcontroller-ready. The conditioning chain is:

| Stage | Purpose | Common Building Block |
|-------|---------|------------------------|
| **Amplification** | Boost weak signals (mV → V) | INA128, OP07 instrumentation amplifier |
| **Filtering** | Remove out-of-band noise | RC low-pass, Butterworth active filter |
| **Isolation** | Galvanic separation, safety | Optocoupler, transformer |
| **Linearization** | Correct non-linear response | Look-up table, polynomial fit |
| **Cold-Junction Compensation** | For thermocouples | On-chip temp sensor + software correction |

### 2.5.1 The Wheatstone Bridge

A classic active-sensor excitation circuit. When all four resistors are equal ($R$), the output is zero. As the active resistor (say a strain gauge) changes by $\Delta R$:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \quad \text{(for small }\Delta R\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **quarter-bridge**, **half-bridge**, and **full-bridge** configurations differ in the number of active elements and directly affect the sensitivity factor. A full bridge has 4× the sensitivity of a quarter bridge.

## 2.6 ADC Architectures — The "Big Three"

KTU Module 2 expects you to compare three ADC types:

| Parameter | Flash ADC | Successive Approximation (SAR) | Sigma-Delta (ΔΣ) |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------|
| **Speed** | Fastest (ns) | Medium (µs) | Slowest (ms) |
| **Resolution** | Low (≤8 bits) | Medium (8–18 bits) | High (16–24 bits) |
| **Cost per channel** | High (2ⁿ comparators) | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Use** | Oscilloscopes, radar | Microcontrollers (STM32, MSP430) | Audio, precision weighing |
| **Architecture Hint** | Resistor ladder + comparators | Binary search using DAC | Oversampling + noise shaping |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this table. It is the single most useful artifact for revision.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Sampling frequency, max signal frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | Sampling period | seconds |
| 3 | $Q = V_{ref} / 2^N$ | Quantization step (1 LSB) | Volts |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_q = 6.02N + 1.76$ | Quantization SNR | dB |
| 5 | $\text{Resolution} = \text{FSO}/(2^N - 1)$ | Smallest detectable change | Same as FSO |
| 6 | $V_{out} = K \cdot x + V_{offset}$ | Linear sensor model | V |
| 7 | $V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Quarter-bridge output | V |
| 8 | $\text{Accuracy} = \lvert \text{Measured} - \text{True}\rvert / \text{True} \times 100\%$ | Percentage inaccuracy | % |
| 9 | $H(s) = \frac{\omega_0^2}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$ | 2nd-order transfer function | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | RC low-pass cutoff frequency | Hz |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never confuse $2^N$ with $2^N - 1$ for resolution. The denominator is $(2^N - 1)$ *only* if you are counting the *number of intervals*; for *step size*, use $2^N$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **exhaustive derivations** that examiners love to assign as 7-mark sub-questions. Every algebraic step is explicitly written.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem (Reconstruction Argument)

We begin with an ideal sampling process: a continuous signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$.

### Step 1 — Express the sampled signal

The sampled signal is:
$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)
$$

### Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform

Using the modulation property of impulses, the spectrum is:
$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} X\!\left(f - \frac{k}{T_s}\right)
$$

This means **the spectrum of $x_s(t)$ is a periodic repetition of the original spectrum $X(f)$**, scaled by $1/T_s$, and centered at every integer multiple of $f_s = 1/T_s$.

### Step 3 — Impose the non-overlap condition

For the original spectrum to be extractable (recoverable) by a low-pass filter, the shifted copies must not overlap. The first two copies are centered at $0$ and at $\pm f_s$. To prevent overlap:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}
$$

### Step 4 — Reconstruction

A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$ where $f_{\max} \leq f_c \leq f_s - f_{\max}$ recovers $X(f)$ from $X_s(f)$. Taking the inverse Fourier transform yields $x(t)$. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> The bandwidth of the guard band between $f_{\max}$ and $f_s - f_{\max}$ is exactly $f_s/2 - f_{\max}$. A larger guard band allows for non-ideal (realizable) filters to be used.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Quantization Noise Formula

We treat the quantization error $e_q = x_q - x$ as a uniformly distributed random variable on $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$.

### Step 1 — Probability Density Function

The uniform PDF is:
$$
p(e_q) = \frac{1}{Q} \quad \text{for } -\frac{Q}{2} \leq e_q \leq \frac{Q}{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Mean-Square Value (Power) of the Error

By definition of mean-square for a uniform distribution:
$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-Q/2}^{Q/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{Q}\, de_q
$$

Evaluate the integral:
$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{Q} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-Q/2}^{Q/2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the limits

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{Q} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{Q}{2}\right)^3 \right]
$$

The bracketed term simplifies because the cube of a negative number is negative:
$$
= \frac{1}{3Q} \left[ \frac{Q^3}{8} + \frac{Q^3}{8} \right] = \frac{1}{3Q} \cdot \frac{Q^3}{4}
$$

Cancel one $Q$:
$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{Q^2}{12}
$$

This is the **average quantization noise power**.

### Step 4 — Signal Power for a Full-Scale Sine Wave

Let $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ with $A = V_{ref}/2$ (full-scale amplitude). The RMS value is $A/\sqrt{2}$, so the signal power is:
$$
P_{signal} = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{V_{ref}^2}{8}
$$

### Step 5 — Form the SNR

Substitute $Q = V_{ref}/2^N$ into the noise power:
$$
P_{noise} = \frac{1}{12} \left(\frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{ref}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}
$$

Divide and simplify:
$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{ref}^2/8}{V_{ref}^2/(12 \cdot 2^{2N})} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Decibels

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2)
$$

Using $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02$:
$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02N
$$

**This is the celebrated formula.** Q.E.D.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:**
> An audio system uses a 16-bit ADC to digitize a microphone signal whose bandwidth extends to 20 kHz. The ADC reference voltage is 3.3 V.
> **(a)** Calculate the smallest voltage change that the ADC can resolve.
> **(b)** Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.
> **(c)** Compute the theoretical signal-to-noise ratio in dB.
> **(d)** If the actual measured SNR is 92 dB, what is the **Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)**?

### Solution — Part (a)

The quantization step:
$$
Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{3.3}{2^{16}} = \frac{3.3}{65536}
$$

Compute the numerical value:
$$
Q = 5.035 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} \approx 50.35\ \mu\text{V}
$$

### Solution — Part (b)

By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem:
$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 20\text{ kHz} = 40\text{ kHz}
$$

The standard audio CD uses $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, satisfying the inequality with a small guard band.

### Solution — Part (c)

Substitute $N = 16$ into the SNR formula:
$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}
$$

### Solution — Part (d)

Set the measured SNR equal to the ideal formula and solve for $N$:
$$
92 = 6.02 \cdot \text{ENOB} + 1.76
$$

Rearrange:
$$
6.02 \cdot \text{ENOB} = 90.24
$$

Divide:
$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{90.24}{6.02} \approx 14.99 \approx 15 \text{ bits}
$$

So although the converter is nominally 16-bit, the effective resolution is 15 bits — the missing bit is consumed by real-world non-idealities (clock jitter, integral non-linearity, thermal noise).

## 3.4 Derivation — Sensor Sensitivity of a Quarter-Bridge

A strain gauge with nominal resistance $R$ changes by $\Delta R = G F \cdot R$ when subjected to strain $\epsilon = \Delta L / L$, where $G F$ is the **gauge factor**.

In a quarter-bridge configuration with three fixed resistors $R$:
$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)
$$

Combine the terms over a common denominator $2(2R + \Delta R)$:
$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}
$$

For small $\Delta R$ relative to $2R$, the denominator $\approx 4R$, giving:
$$
V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G F \cdot \epsilon
$$

This is the **linearized** output. The sensitivity of the bridge is therefore:
$$
K = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G F \quad \left[\frac{\text{V}}{\text{strain}}\right]
$$

> [!TIP]
> For a **full bridge** (four active gauges), the sensitivity becomes $K = V_{ex} \cdot GF$ — exactly four times higher. This is why precision load cells use full-bridge topology.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Sensor to Use?

The following decision tree is a quick reference for design viva and case-study questions.

| Application | Best Sensor | Why? | KTU Note |
|-------------|-------------|------|----------|
| Body temperature monitoring (medical) | RTD (PT100) or DS18B20 | High accuracy, linear, digital output | DS18B20 gives 1-Wire digital |
| Non-contact IR temperature | Thermopile / MLX90614 | Measures radiated IR; safe for moving targets | Output via I²C |
| Industrial pressure (high range) | Piezoelectric sensor | Fast dynamic response, no moving parts | Needs charge amplifier |
| Automotive airbag trigger | MEMS accelerometer (ADXL) | High g-range, fast digital response | Threshold-based interrupt |
| Air quality / VOC | Metal-oxide chemiresistor (MQ-135) | Low cost, detects specific gases | Needs heater stabilization |
| Liquid level in tank | Ultrasonic HC-SR04 | Non-contact, range 2–400 cm | TTL pulse-width output |
| Rotational speed | Hall-effect sensor | Contactless, immune to dust | Quadrature decoding |

## 4.1 The Complete Embedded Sensor Signal Chain

The following Mermaid diagram shows the full data path from physical phenomenon to digital decision. Every block is a hardware stage you may be asked to identify in an exam.

```mermaid
    phy[Physical Quantity\nTemperature Pressure Light] --> sens[Sensor\nTransducer]
    sens --> raw[Raw Analog Signal\nmV level noisy]
    raw --> cond[Signal Conditioning\nAmplification Filtering]
    cond --> sh[Sample and Hold\nS H Circuit]
    sh --> adc[ADC\nAnalog to Digital]
    adc --> mcu[Microcontroller\nDecision Logic]
    mcu --> act[Actuator Output\nMotor LED Buzzer]
    mcu --> comm[Communication\nUART I2C SPI WiFi]
```


## 4.3 Nyquist Sampling — Time and Frequency Domain Picture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph time_domain[Time Domain View]
        sigA[Continuous Signal x of t]
        sampA[Sampled Signal x of nTs]
        sigA --> sampA
    end

    subgraph freq_domain[Frequency Domain View]
        specA[Original Spectrum X of f\nbandlimited to fmax]
        specB[Replicated Spectrum at k fs]
        specA --> specB
        specB --> lpf[Low Pass Filter\ncutoff at fs slash 2]
        lpf --> recon[Reconstructed x of t]
    end

    classDef domain fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1px
    classDef process fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px

    class sigA,sampA,specA,specB,recon domain
    class lpf process
```

## 4.4 ADC Pipeline (SAR) — Internal Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Analog Input Vin] --> sh2[Sample and Hold]
    sh2 --> comp[Comparator]
    sar[SAR Logic Register] --> dacint[Internal DAC]
    dacint --> comp
    comp --> msb[Decision Bit]
    msb --> sar
    sar --> out[Digital Output N bits]

    classDef stage fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ctrl fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px
    class input,sh2,comp,dacint,out stage
    class sar,msb ctrl
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The SAR performs a *binary search*. In cycle 1, it sets the MSB and checks the comparator; in cycle 2, the next bit; and so on, for $N$ cycles total. Hence total conversion time is $N \cdot T_{clk}$ — a direct trade-off between resolution and speed.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This question bank strictly follows the **2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: 3-mark short answers in **Part A** and 14-mark analytical questions in **Part B** with internal choice.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Define a sensor. Differentiate between active and passive sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

A sensor is a device that converts a physical quantity such as temperature, pressure, or light into a corresponding electrical signal.

| Property | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Power source | Self-generating (no external excitation) | Requires external excitation voltage |
| Working principle | Direct energy conversion | Modulates an external signal |
| Example 1 | Thermocouple (Seebeck effect) | Thermistor (resistance change) |
| Example 2 | Piezoelectric crystal | LDR (light-dependent resistor) |

*Examiner's note:* A passive sensor acts as a transducer that *modifies* an external signal; an active sensor *generates* its own output.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing?**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist sampling theorem states that a continuous-time bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_{\max}$ can be reconstructed from its discrete samples provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ is called the Nyquist rate, and the corresponding interval $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period.

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which frequency components higher than $f_s/2$ "fold back" into the lower frequency range, distorting the reconstructed signal. It occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$. The aliased frequency is given by:
$$
f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - k f_s \vert \quad \text{for integer } k
$$

Aliasing is prevented by applying an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before sampling.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in decibels for an ideal N-bit ADC in terms of the quantization step size $Q$ and the full-scale input range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC has a reference voltage of 5 V and is used to digitize a signal with bandwidth 5 kHz. Calculate:
- (i) the resolution in volts
- (ii) the minimum sampling frequency
- (iii) the quantization noise power
- (iv) the theoretical SNR in dB

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of SNR Formula

**Step 1:** Model the quantization error $e_q$ as a uniform random variable on $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$ with PDF $p(e_q) = 1/Q$.

**Step 2:** Compute the mean-square (noise power):
$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \int_{-Q/2}^{Q/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{Q}\, de_q = \frac{Q^2}{12}
$$

**Step 3:** For a full-scale sine wave of amplitude $A = V_{ref}/2$, the signal power is $A^2/2 = V_{ref}^2/8$.

**Step 4:** Form the SNR ratio:
$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{ref}^2/8}{Q^2/12}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $Q = V_{ref}/2^N$:
$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{ref}^2/8}{V_{ref}^2/(12 \cdot 2^{2N})} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to dB:
$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2) \approx 1.76 + 6.02N
$$

**Therefore:** $\boxed{\text{SNR}_{q} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Resolution:**
$$
Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) Minimum sampling frequency:**
$$
f_s = 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 5000 = 10 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(iii) Quantization noise power:**
$$
P_q = \frac{Q^2}{12} = \frac{(1.221 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = \frac{1.491 \times 10^{-6}}{12}
$$
$$
P_q = 1.24 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V}^2
$$

**(iv) Theoretical SNR:**
$$
\text{SNR}_{q} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the various stages of a typical sensor signal-conditioning chain. Discuss the role of an instrumentation amplifier in this chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wheatstone bridge has three fixed 120 Ω resistors and one active strain gauge. The gauge factor is 2.1 and the applied strain is 1000 microstrain. The excitation voltage is 5 V. Calculate the bridge output voltage. If a full-bridge configuration is used instead, what will be the new output? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain

A typical sensor signal goes through the following stages before being fed to an ADC:

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Sensor → Amplification → Filtering → Isolation → Linearization → ADC
```

**Detailed explanation of each stage:**

1. **Amplification Stage:** Raw sensor output (e.g., thermocouple gives ~40 µV/°C) is too small to be digitized. An **instrumentation amplifier (INA)** provides high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR > 100 dB), high input impedance (GΩ), and adjustable gain from 1 to 10000 via a single external resistor.

2. **Filtering Stage:** Removes out-of-band noise and RF interference. Common types:
   - RC low-pass for simple anti-aliasing.
   - Active Butterworth for flat passband.
   - Notch filter at 50/60 Hz to remove mains hum.

3. **Isolation Stage:** Uses optocouplers or isolation amplifiers to break ground loops and protect the microcontroller from high-voltage transients (important in motor control and medical applications).

4. **Linearization Stage:** Compensates for non-linear sensor response using look-up tables (LUT) in firmware or analog function-fitting circuits.

5. **ADC Stage:** Converts the conditioned analog signal into digital codes for the microcontroller.

**Why an instrumentation amplifier?**
- Three op-amp topology provides symmetrical input impedances.
- Rejects common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains pickup along thermocouple wires).
- Single-resistor gain setting is convenient for embedded designers.

#### Part (b) — Bridge Output Calculation

**Given data:**
- $R = 120\ \Omega$
- $GF = 2.1$
- $\epsilon = 1000\ \mu\text{strain} = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$
- $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$

**Step 1:** Compute the resistance change:
$$
\Delta R = G F \cdot R \cdot \epsilon = 2.1 \times 120 \times 10^{-3} = 0.252\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the quarter-bridge formula:
$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \cdot \frac{0.252}{120}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$
V_{out} = 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 2.625 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4:** For a full bridge (four active gauges), the sensitivity is 4× higher:
$$
V_{out,\text{full}} = 4 \times 2.625\ \text{mV} = 10.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Quarter-bridge output: **2.625 mV**
- Full-bridge output: **10.5 mV**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of strain.** Microstrain is $10^{-6}$, not $10^{-3}$. Many students write $\epsilon = 1000$ and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Not writing the formula derivation.** A direct numerical answer without showing the bridge equation costs you 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the 4× factor for full bridge.** This is a common slip; the 4× comes from the fact that in a full bridge, *all four* arms change resistance.
> 4. **Mixing up gauge factor and sensitivity.** Gauge factor is dimensionless; sensitivity has units of V/strain.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for "Sensors and Signals":**

- ☐ A **sensor** converts a physical measurand into an electrical signal; an **actuator** does the reverse.
- ☐ **Active sensors** generate their own output (thermocouple, piezoelectric); **passive sensors** modulate an external excitation (thermistor, LDR).
- ☐ **Static characteristics** of a sensor: range, FSO, accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity.
- ☐ **Dynamic characteristics**: time constant, settling time, bandwidth, dynamic error.
- ☐ **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. Below this, **aliasing** occurs. Prevent it with an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter**.
- ☐ **Quantization step size** of an N-bit ADC: $Q = V_{ref} / 2^N$.
- ☐ **Quantization noise power**: $P_q = Q^2 / 12$.
- ☐ **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** of an ideal N-bit ADC: $\text{SNR}_{q} = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. The "6 dB per bit" rule.
- ☐ **Wheatstone bridge output (quarter)**: $V_{out} \approx (V_{ex}/4)(\Delta R / R)$. Full bridge = 4× quarter bridge.
- ☐ **Gauge factor** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \epsilon$ — dimensionless, typically 2.0 for constantan.
- ☐ **Three ADC architectures**: Flash (fast, low-res), SAR (mid-range, MCU-friendly), Sigma-Delta (high-res, audio/precision).
- ☐ **Signal conditioning chain**: Amplify → Filter → Isolate → Linearize → ADC.
- ☐ **Instrumentation amplifier** provides high CMRR; preferred for thermocouples, strain gauges, and ECG signals.
- ☐ **Smart sensors** (e.g., DHT22, MPU6050) integrate sensor + ADC + digital interface in a single IC package.
- ☐ **ENOB** (Effective Number of Bits): real ADCs lose bits due to thermal noise, jitter, and INL; solve via $\text{ENOB} = (\text{SNR}_{measured} - 1.76)/6.02$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Memorization Heuristic:** For any KTU numerical problem in this module, identify first whether the question is about **(a) Sampling**, **(b) Quantization/Resolution**, or **(c) Bridge/Transduction**. Then apply the corresponding formula from Section 2.7 directly. Most errors arise from mixing formulas across categories.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discretization of Signals and A/D Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discretization of Signals and A/D Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Discretization of Signals — Formal Definition

**Discretization** is the process of converting a **continuous-time, continuous-amplitude (analog) signal** into a **discrete-time, discrete-amplitude (digital) signal** so that it can be processed, stored, or transmitted by digital systems such as microcontrollers, DSPs, and FPGAs.

In an embedded systems context, the natural physical world is analog (temperature, pressure, vibration, sound, voltage, current). A digital processor cannot directly interpret these continuously varying quantities. Therefore, **discretization** acts as the *bridge* between the physical (analog) domain and the computational (digital) domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Discretization of a signal involves two fundamental operations: **Sampling** (converting continuous time to discrete time) and **Quantization** (converting continuous amplitude to discrete amplitude), followed by **Coding** (binary representation). The combined subsystem that performs all three is called an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.*

---

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Discretization

| Stage | Operation | Domain Change | Hardware Block |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sampling** | Reads the signal at discrete time instants | Continuous time → Discrete time | Sample-and-Hold (S/H) |
| **Quantization** | Maps each sample to the nearest discrete level | Continuous amplitude → Discrete amplitude | Comparator array / Quantizer |
| **Coding** | Converts the level index into binary code | Level → Binary word | Encoder / Logic network |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Staircase on a Hill"**
> Imagine standing at the bottom of a smooth, rolling hill. You cannot describe the hill's shape to a friend in another city using the smooth curve. Instead, you **measure its height at fixed horizontal intervals** (this is **sampling**). Now suppose your measuring stick only has markings every **1 meter** (this is **quantization**). The smooth hill is now reduced to a series of small **steps**. Those steps — represented as numbers — can be sent over a phone line (this is **coding**). The receiver reconstructs an approximate staircase, which is the **discretized version** of the smooth hill.
>
> - More frequent measurements (higher sampling) = smoother staircase.
> - Finer measuring stick (more bits) = shorter, less visible steps.

### 1.4 What is an A/D Converter (ADC)?

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is the integrated mixed-signal circuit that performs the entire discretization process — sampling, quantization, and coding — and produces a parallel or serial digital word representing the input analog voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight for KTU:** In a typical embedded system, the analog sensor signal is first conditioned (amplified, anti-aliased filtered), then held by a **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit**, and finally quantized by the ADC. The reverse operation is performed by a **D/A Converter (DAC)** during reconstruction.

---

### 1.5 Block-Level View of the Discretization Pipeline

The classical signal chain in any embedded data-acquisition system is:

$$
\text{Analog Input} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Anti-Alias Filter} \;\rightarrow\; \text{S/H} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Quantizer} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Encoder} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Digital Output}
$$

The block-level functional architecture is rendered in Mermaid in **Section 4**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sampling — The Nyquist–Shannon Theorem

**Statement (Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem):**
A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if, and only if, the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

- $f_s = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate** (minimum).
- $f_s > 2 \cdot f_{max}$ is called **oversampling** (used in practice with a safety margin).
- $f_s < 2 \cdot f_{max}$ leads to **aliasing**, an irreversible distortion.

**Why "≥ 2"?** Sampling in the time domain creates periodic replicas of the signal spectrum in the frequency domain, separated by $f_s$. To prevent these replicas from overlapping, the spacing $f_s$ must be at least twice the signal bandwidth.

**Practical Rule of Thumb:** Embedded designers typically choose $f_s = 5 \cdot f_{max}$ to $f_s = 10 \cdot f_{max}$ to allow for realizable (non-ideal) anti-alias filters with finite roll-off.

---

### 2.2 Aliasing — The Silent Enemy

**Aliasing** occurs when a high-frequency component $f_{in} > f_s/2$ "folds back" into the baseband and appears as a lower frequency $f_{alias}$ given by:

$$
f_{alias} = \left\vert f_{in} - n \cdot f_s \right\vert \quad \text{where } n = \left\lfloor \tfrac{f_{in}}{f_s} + \tfrac{1}{2} \right\rfloor
$$

The classic example: a **100 Hz sine wave sampled at 90 Hz** appears to the observer as a **10 Hz sine wave** (because $100 - 90 = 10$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing is irreversible.** Once the high-frequency content folds into the baseband, no amount of post-processing can recover the original information. **Prevention** (anti-alias filter) is the only cure.

**Anti-Alias Filter (AAF):** A low-pass filter placed *before* the sampler that band-limits the input to $f_s/2$ with sufficient stop-band attenuation.

---

### 2.3 Quantization — Mapping Amplitude to Discrete Levels

For an $n$-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS}$:

**Number of quantization levels:**

$$
L \;=\; 2^n
$$

**Quantization step size (LSB weight, mid-rise ADC):**

$$
\Delta \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
$$

Some texts use $2^n - 1$ in the denominator (mid-tread); both conventions are accepted in KTU. The KTU-preferred convention is $V_{FS}/2^n$.

**Maximum Quantization Error (mid-rise):**

$$
E_{q,\max} \;=\; \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} \;=\; \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**RMS Quantization Noise Voltage (assuming uniform distribution):**

$$
E_{q,rms} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a full-scale sinusoidal input:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02 \, n \;+\; 1.76 \quad \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The famous "6 dB per bit" rule:** Each additional bit of ADC resolution improves the SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**. This is the single most tested relationship in KTU analog-digital conversion problems.

---

### 2.4 Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Subsystem

A practical ADC requires a finite conversion time $T_{conv}$. If the input changes during this interval, the output code is ambiguous. A **Sample-and-Hold** circuit:

1. **Track (Sample) Mode** — A switch connects the input to a hold capacitor $C_H$ that charges to $v_{in}(t)$.
2. **Hold Mode** — The switch opens; $C_H$ retains the last sampled voltage, freezing it for the ADC.

**Key timing parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aperture time** | $t_{ap}$ | Delay between the hold command and the actual switch opening |
| **Acquisition time** | $t_{acq}$ | Time for $C_H$ to charge within $1/2$ LSB of the new input |
| **Settling time** | $t_s$ | Time for the output to settle to the final value within a tolerance |
| **Hold mode droop** | — | Slow discharge of $C_H$ due to leakage; expressed in V/s |

$$
t_{acq} \;\approx\; (n+1) \cdot R_s \cdot C_H \cdot \ln 2
$$

where $R_s$ is the source resistance and $n$ is the ADC resolution (in bits).

---

### 2.5 Types of ADCs — Architecture Comparison

| ADC Type | Resolution (typical) | Conversion Time | Relative Speed | Cost | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flash (Parallel)** | 6 – 8 bits | ~ ns (1 cycle) | Fastest | Highest | Oscilloscopes, high-speed video |
| **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | 8 – 18 bits | $n$ clock cycles | Medium | Low | Embedded data acquisition, microcontrollers |
| **Pipeline** | 10 – 16 bits | $k + n$ stages | Fast | Medium-High | Communications, CCD imaging |
| **Dual-Slope Integrating** | 16 – 24 bits | $2^{n+1}$ clock cycles | Very Slow | Low | Digital voltmeters, industrial sensors |
| **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$)** | 16 – 24 bits | Oversampled | Slow (high OSR) | Medium | Audio, precision instrumentation, weighing scales |
| **Counting / Tracking** | 8 – 12 bits | Variable | Slow | Very Low | Simple ramp-type converters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** The **SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADC** is the workhorse of embedded microcontrollers (e.g., the STM32, MSP430, LPC, and ATmega families). The exam will frequently test its working principle, conversion time, and the binary-search algorithm it uses.

---

### 2.6 ADC Key Specifications (Engineering Glossary)

- **Resolution ($n$):** Number of output bits; $2^n$ discrete levels.
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** The voltage span the ADC can quantize, often $V_{ref+}$ – $V_{ref-}$.
- **INL (Integral Non-Linearity):** Maximum deviation of the actual transfer function from the ideal straight line, in **LSB**.
- **DNL (Differential Non-Linearity):** Deviation of actual code width from the ideal 1 LSB.
- **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** Ratio of fundamental to total noise, in dB.
- **ENOB (Effective Number of Bits):** $\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$
- **Throughput Rate:** Samples per second (SPS), equal to $1/T_{conv}$ for single-channel operation.
- **Aperture Jitter ($\Delta t_{ap}$):** Uncertainty in sampling instant; causes $\Delta V = 2\pi f_{in} V_{pp} \Delta t_{ap}$.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Alias frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - n f_s \vert$ | Hz |
| 3 | Quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | LSB step | $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$ | Volts |
| 5 | Peak quantization error | $E_{q,\max} = \Delta/2$ | Volts |
| 6 | RMS quantization noise | $E_{q,rms} = \Delta/\sqrt{12}$ | Volts |
| 7 | SQNR (sine input) | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 8 | ENOB | $\text{ENOB} = (\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76)/6.02$ | Bits |
| 9 | S/H acquisition time | $t_{acq} = (n+1) R_s C_H \ln 2$ | Seconds |
| 10 | SAR conversion time | $T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| 11 | Flash conversion time | $T_{conv} \approx 1 \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| 12 | Dual-slope conv. time | $T_{conv} = 2^{n+1} \cdot T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| 13 | Aperture-jitter error | $\Delta V = 2 \pi f_{in} V_{pp} \Delta t_{ap}$ | Volts |
| 14 | Percentage resolution | $\%R = (1/2^n) \times 100$ | Percent |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Systems:** CD audio uses 16-bit, 44.1 kHz sampling → SQNR $\approx$ **98 dB** (dynamic range).
- **Industrial Process Control:** 12-bit SAR ADCs in PLCs sense temperature, pressure, flow.
- **Biomedical (ECG, EEG):** Sigma-delta ADCs in Holter monitors — 24-bit resolution at low sampling.
- **Automotive Sensors:** 10–12-bit SAR ADCs in ECU for throttle, MAP, oxygen sensing.
- **IoT Edge Nodes:** Low-power SAR / $\Sigma\Delta$ ADCs for battery-driven environmental sensing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the RMS Quantization Noise Voltage

Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ (mid-rise quantizer). The probability density function is:

$$
p(e_q) \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for } e_q \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]
$$

The mean-square value of the quantization error (which is the noise power normalized to 1 $\Omega$) is:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, p(e_q) \, de_q
$$

Substituting $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q \;=\; \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \;=\; \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot \Delta^3 / 8}{3} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^3}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

The RMS quantization noise voltage is the square root:

$$
E_{q,rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\overline{e_q^2}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

This is the **fundamental noise floor** that limits the dynamic range of any ideal $n$-bit ADC.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the SQNR Formula (6.02 n + 1.76 dB)

**Step 1 — Signal Power:** For a full-scale sinusoidal input $v(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ with peak $V_p = V_{FS}/2$, the RMS value is:

$$
V_{sig,rms} \;=\; \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

The signal power (across 1 $\Omega$) is:

$$
P_{sig} \;=\; V_{sig,rms}^2 \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}
$$

**Step 2 — Noise Power:** From the derivation above:

$$
P_{noise} \;=\; \overline{e_q^2} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

With $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$:

$$
P_{noise} \;=\; \frac{(V_{FS}/2^n)^2}{12} \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n}
$$

**Step 3 — SQNR ratio:**

$$
\text{SQNR} \;=\; \frac{P_{sig}}{P_{noise}} \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)} \;=\; \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} \;=\; 1.5 \cdot 4^n
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels:** Using $10 \log_{10}$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 4^n) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 n \log_{10}(4)
$$

Substituting standard log values $\log_{10}(1.5) = 0.1761$ and $\log_{10}(4) = 0.6021$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;=\; 10(0.1761) + 10 n (0.6021) \;=\; 1.761 + 6.021 n
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;\approx\; 6.02\,n \;+\; 1.76\;}
$$

This derivation **proves** the famous "6 dB per bit" rule.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Typical KTU Problem Style

**Problem [KTU-Style]:** An embedded sensor node uses a 12-bit SAR ADC with a 3.3 V reference. The input signal is a 50 Hz sine wave with peak-to-peak amplitude 2.5 V. The ADC is clocked at 1 MHz.

**Find:**
1. The LSB (resolution) in volts.
2. The peak quantization error.
3. The theoretical SQNR in dB.
4. The maximum conversion time.
5. The minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing (if the signal has 5th harmonic content).

**Step-by-step solution:**

**(a) LSB step:**

$$
\Delta \;=\; \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \;=\; \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{2^{12}} \;=\; \frac{3.3}{4096} \;\approx\; 8.06 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \;=\; 0.806 \text{ mV}
$$

**(b) Peak quantization error:**

$$
E_{q,\max} \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{2} \;\approx\; 0.403 \text{ mV}
$$

**(c) Theoretical SQNR:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \;=\; 72.24 + 1.76 \;=\; 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

**(d) SAR conversion time (n clock cycles):**

$$
T_{conv} \;=\; n \cdot T_{clk} \;=\; 12 \cdot \frac{1}{1 \times 10^6} \;=\; 12 \, \mu s
$$

**(e) Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist):** Highest frequency = 5th harmonic = $5 \times 50 = 250$ Hz.

$$
f_s \;\geq\; 2 \cdot 250 \;=\; 500 \text{ Hz} \quad (\text{Nyquist minimum})
$$

Applying the 5× safety factor rule of thumb:

$$
f_{s,\text{practical}} \;\geq\; 5 \cdot 250 \;=\; 1250 \text{ Hz}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing an ADC for Embedded Use

| Requirement / Constraint | Best ADC Architecture | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sampling rate > 100 MSPS (oscilloscope) | **Flash** | Single-cycle conversion; lowest latency |
| 12–16 bit precision, 1 MSPS (industrial) | **Pipeline** or **SAR** | Balance of speed and resolution |
| Ultra-low power (battery IoT) | **SAR** | No continuous comparators; gated operation |
| 20+ bits at low rate (weighing scale) | **Sigma-Delta** | Oversampling + noise shaping |
| DC voltmeter (rejects mains 50/60 Hz) | **Dual-slope** | Excellent normal-mode line rejection |
| Lowest cost, slow | **Counting / Single-slope** | Minimal hardware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse "Resolution" with "Accuracy." A 16-bit ADC may have only 12-bit **effective** accuracy (ENOB) due to noise, INL, and reference drift. Always check the datasheet's **ENOB** specification, not just the nominal bit count.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Discretization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal] --> B[Anti Alias Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold S and H]
    C --> D[Quantizer]
    D --> E[Digital Encoder]
    E --> F[n bit Digital Output Word]
    F --> G[Embedded Processor MCU or DSP]

    H[Clock Generator] --> C
    H --> E

    I[Voltage Reference Vref] --> D

    G --> J[Display or Actuator or Communication Link]
    G --> K[Memory Storage]

    style A fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#660000
    style C fill:#fff0cc,stroke:#664400
    style D fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#004400
    style E fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#220066
    style F fill:#cccccc,stroke:#000000
    style G fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#000066
```

**Interpretation:** The analog signal is first band-limited by the anti-alias filter, then frozen in time by the S/H, quantized to a discrete level, and finally encoded into a binary word that the MCU/DSP can store, analyze, or transmit.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[Analog Vin] --> SH[S and H Circuit]
    SH --> CMP[Comparator]

    REF[DAC with Vref] --> CMP

    CMP --> SAR[SAR Logic and Control]

    SAR -->|Sets MSB first| REF
    SAR -->|Controls trial bits| OUT[n bit Digital Output]

    CLK[Clock] --> SAR
    EOC[End of Conversion Signal] --> SAR

    style IN fill:#e0f0ff
    style SH fill:#fff0cc
    style CMP fill:#ffe0e0
    style SAR fill:#e0ffe0
    style REF fill:#f0e0ff
    style OUT fill:#cccccc
```

**How SAR Works (Binary Search Algorithm):**
1. MSB is set to 1; DAC produces $V_{ref}/2$.
2. If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, the bit is kept at 1; else, it is reset to 0.
3. The next bit is tried in the same fashion, halving the search range each step.
4. After $n$ cycles, all bits are resolved → **End of Conversion (EOC)** is asserted.

---

### 4.3 State Flow of a Flash ADC (for completeness)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> B[Resistor Ladder 2 to the n minus 1 taps]
    B --> C1[Comparator 1]
    B --> C2[Comparator 2]
    B --> C3[Comparator 3]
    B --> Cdots[Comparator dot dot dot 2 to the n minus 1]
    B --> Cend[Comparator 2 to the n minus 1]

    C1 --> P[Priority Encoder]
    C2 --> P
    C3 --> P
    Cdots --> P
    Cend --> P

    P --> OUT[n bit Binary Code]

    style A fill:#e0f0ff
    style B fill:#fff0cc
    style P fill:#e0ffe0
    style OUT fill:#cccccc
```

**Note:** A flash ADC needs $2^n - 1$ comparators — for a 10-bit flash, that is **1023 comparators**, which is why flash ADCs are typically limited to ≤ 8 bits.

---

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right ADC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the required resolution in bits?}

    Q1 -->|8 bits or less| Q2{Is speed the priority?}
    Q1 -->|10 to 16 bits| Q3{Throughput requirement?}
    Q1 -->|18 bits or more| Q4{Is it DC or low frequency?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[FLASH ADC]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[SAR ADC]

    Q3 -->|> 1 MSPS| R3[PIPELINE ADC]
    Q3 -->|< 1 MSPS| R4[SAR ADC]

    Q4 -->|Yes DC precision| R5[SIGMA DELTA ADC]
    Q4 -->|Yes line rejection| R6[DUAL SLOPE ADC]

    style Q1 fill:#fff0cc
    style R1 fill:#e0ffe0
    style R2 fill:#e0ffe0
    style R3 fill:#e0ffe0
    style R4 fill:#e0ffe0
    style R5 fill:#e0ffe0
    style R6 fill:#e0ffe0
```

This decision flowchart is a **frequently tested** concept at KTU — students are expected to justify the choice of ADC based on speed, resolution, cost, and noise requirements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**Define quantization error. Derive the expression for the RMS value of the quantization noise of an $n$-bit ADC with step size $\Delta$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the actual analog input voltage and the discrete quantized level assigned to it. For a mid-rise quantizer with step $\Delta$, the error is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with PDF $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$.

Mean-square value:

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

RMS value:

$$
E_{q,rms} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the Nyquist rate.

Aliasing is the phenomenon in which frequency components above $f_s/2$ fold back into the baseband and appear as spurious lower frequencies. Since it is irreversible, it is **prevented** by placing a **low-pass anti-alias filter** before the sampler, band-limiting the input to $f_s/2$ with sufficient stop-band attenuation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — 14 Marks — Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. How many clock cycles are required for an $n$-bit conversion? (7 marks)

**(b)** A 10-bit ADC has a full-scale input of 5 V. Calculate (i) the resolution (LSB size), (ii) the peak quantization error, and (iii) the theoretical SQNR in dB. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A **SAR ADC** uses a binary-search algorithm to find the digital code corresponding to the analog input. Its block diagram consists of:

1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — Freezes the analog input.
2. **Comparator** — Compares the held input with the output of an internal DAC.
3. **Internal DAC** (typically R-2R ladder) — Generates an analog trial voltage from the SAR register's bits.
4. **SAR Logic Block** — Performs the binary search, starting from the MSB.
5. **Clock and Control** — Sequences the trial bits and generates the End-of-Conversion (EOC) signal.

**Algorithm (binary search):**

- **Cycle 1 (MSB):** Set bit $b_{n-1} = 1$, all others 0. DAC output = $V_{ref}/2$.
  - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$: keep $b_{n-1} = 1$.
  - Else: reset $b_{n-1} = 0$.
- **Cycle 2:** Set $b_{n-2} = 1$. Compare and decide.
- ...
- **Cycle n (LSB):** Final bit decided.

After **$n$ clock cycles**, the complete $n$-bit code is latched and EOC is asserted.

$$
T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}
$$

**Advantages:** Low power, moderate speed (up to several MSPS), high resolution (up to 18 bits). **Disadvantage:** Throughput decreases linearly with resolution.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given: $n = 10$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5$ V.

**(i) Resolution (LSB size):**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.883 \text{ mV}
$$

**(ii) Peak quantization error:**

$$
E_{q,\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical SQNR:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 10 + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — 14 Marks — Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **dual-slope ADC** with a neat diagram. Why is it preferred for digital voltmeters? (7 marks)

**(b)** An audio signal is band-limited to 20 kHz. It is sampled by a 12-bit ADC operating at 48 kSPS. Determine (i) the sampling rate relative to the Nyquist rate (in %), (ii) the LSB weight if $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, and (iii) the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A **dual-slope integrating ADC** converts an analog voltage into a digital count using two integration phases:

1. **Run-Up Phase (Fixed time $T_1$):** The unknown input $V_{in}$ is integrated for a fixed duration $T_1 = 2^n \cdot T_{clk}$ via an op-amp integrator. The integrator output ramps up to $V_{in} \cdot T_1 / RC$.

2. **Run-Down Phase (Variable time $T_2$):** A reference voltage $-V_{ref}$ of opposite polarity is switched to the integrator input. The output ramps back down at a constant slope. A counter measures the time $T_2$ taken for the output to return to zero.

Since the slopes are linear, by charge balance:

$$
V_{in} \cdot T_1 \;=\; V_{ref} \cdot T_2
$$

$$
V_{in} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{N_2}{2^n}
$$

where $N_2$ is the counter reading during run-down.

**Why preferred for digital voltmeters:**
- Excellent rejection of **mains-frequency noise (50/60 Hz)** if $T_1$ is chosen as a multiple of the line period.
- High noise immunity (integrator averages out random noise).
- High resolution (16–24 bits) and excellent linearity.
- Insensitive to component tolerances (R and C drift cancels out in the ratio).

**Conversion time:**

$$
T_{conv} = (2^n + N_2) \cdot T_{clk} \approx 2^{n+1} \cdot T_{clk}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given: $f_{max} = 20$ kHz, $f_s = 48$ kSPS, $n = 12$ bits, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V.

**(i) Sampling rate relative to Nyquist rate:**

Nyquist rate $= 2 \cdot f_{max} = 40$ kHz.

$$
\frac{f_s}{f_{Nyquist}} \times 100\% = \frac{48}{40} \times 100\% = 120\%
$$

So the signal is oversampled by 20%.

**(ii) LSB weight:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV}
$$

**(iii) SQNR:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students frequently use $2^n - 1$ in the denominator for LSB calculation, but the KTU-accepted convention is $V_{FS} / 2^n$. Mixing up these two forms will cost you **1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **resolution (LSB step)** with **accuracy (ENOB)**. A 12-bit ADC does not always have 12-bit effective accuracy.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to use $\log_{10}$ (not $\ln$) when converting to decibels. SNR is **always** computed in dB using $10 \log_{10}$.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** For the dual-slope ADC, students often forget to **mention the noise-rejection property** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark ask in KTU valuation.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** In Nyquist problems, students do not specify the **safety margin** (typically $f_s = 5$–$10 \times f_{max}$) for practical filter design — examiners reward this insight.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Discretization of Signals and A/D Converters**

**1. Discretization Process**
- Discretization = Sampling + Quantization + Coding.
- Performed by an ADC; the reverse is performed by a DAC.
- An anti-alias filter **must** precede the sampler.

**2. Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)**
- $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist rate = $2 f_{max}$).
- Aliasing is irreversible; use an anti-alias low-pass filter.
- Oversampling by 5×–10× is standard in practice.

**3. Quantization Basics**
- $L = 2^n$ levels; $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^n$ is the LSB step.
- Peak error = $\Delta / 2$; RMS noise = $\Delta / \sqrt{12}$.
- **SQNR = 6.02 n + 1.76 dB** (sine input, full-scale).

**4. Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)**
- $\text{ENOB} = (\text{SNR}_{dB} - 1.76) / 6.02$
- Real ADCs always have ENOB < nominal bit count.

**5. Sample-and-Hold**
- Track and Hold modes; finite $t_{acq}$, $t_{ap}$, $t_s$.
- $t_{acq} \approx (n+1) R_s C_H \ln 2$.

**6. SAR ADC**
- Binary-search algorithm; $n$ clock cycles for $n$-bit conversion.
- $T_{conv} = n \cdot T_{clk}$. Workhorse of embedded MCUs.

**7. Flash ADC**
- $2^n - 1$ comparators; single-cycle conversion. Limited to ≤ 8 bits.

**8. Dual-Slope ADC**
- Run-up + run-down integration. $T_{conv} \approx 2^{n+1} T_{clk}$.
- Excellent **mains-noise rejection**. Used in digital voltmeters.

**9. Sigma-Delta ADC**
- Oversampling + noise shaping. 16–24 bit. Slow but very precise.

**10. Pipeline ADC**
- Multi-stage conversion; $k + n$ cycles. 10–16 bit, high speed.

**11. Aperture Jitter**
- $\Delta V = 2\pi f_{in} V_{pp} \Delta t_{ap}$. Limits high-frequency accuracy.

**12. Selection Criteria for Embedded ADCs**
- Resolution, speed, power, cost, reference stability, package, and interface (SPI, I²C, parallel).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:**
> *When asked to "explain with a block diagram," always draw a labeled diagram first, then describe each block's function. Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** purely for the diagram. Also, state the governing equation, assumptions, and a worked numerical example wherever possible — this guarantees a full-score response under KTU's stepwise marking scheme.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantization Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quantization Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantization Noise** is the inherent rounding error generated when a continuous-amplitude (analog) signal is mapped onto a finite set of discrete amplitude levels by the quantization process inside an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). It is the difference between the true input analog sample value and the reconstructed digital output value assigned by the quantizer.

In formal mathematical terms, if $x(t)$ is the continuous input to an ideal N-bit ADC and $x_q[n]$ is the quantized output, the **quantization error** (noise) is defined as:

$$e_q[n] = x[n] - x_q[n]$$

where $x[n]$ is the sampled (discrete-time) version of $x(t)$.

For a uniform mid-tread quantizer, the quantization step size (also called the **Least Significant Bit - LSB**) is given by:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^N}$$

where:
- $V_{FS}$ = Full-Scale voltage range
- $N$ = Number of bits of the ADC
- $2^N$ = Total number of quantization levels

The quantization error is bounded within the half-interval:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q[n] \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Broken Weighing Scale"**

Imagine a digital bathroom scale that displays your weight only in whole kilograms (e.g., 68 kg, 69 kg, 70 kg). If your **true weight is 68.6 kg**, the scale will *round* and show **69 kg**. The difference (0.4 kg) is the **quantization error**, and this error is what we call **quantization noise** in signal processing.

- **Resolution of the scale** ↔ Number of bits ($N$) of an ADC
- **Step size (1 kg)** ↔ Quantization step $\Delta$
- **Rounding error** ↔ Quantization error $e_q[n]$
- **More kg intervals (a more precise scale)** ↔ Higher $N$ → Lower $\Delta$ → Less quantization noise

The key insight: the scale cannot show fractional values because it has limited "steps" — similarly, an ADC with a finite number of bits cannot capture every infinitesimal variation in the analog signal. This unavoidable limitation creates noise.

### 1.3 Why Quantization Noise Matters in Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU's PECPT751 (Embedded Systems Design) syllabus, quantization noise is a **fundamental design constraint** when selecting ADCs for sensors (temperature, pressure, audio, biomedical) because:
> 1. It sets the **theoretical lower limit** on the dynamic range of a digitised signal.
> 2. It directly determines the achievable **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.
> 3. It governs the **bit-width selection** in DSP pipelines and data converters.
> 4. It is non-removable through calibration — it is a fundamental physical/architectural limitation.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

- **Number of quantization levels** $L = 2^N$ (for N-bit binary ADC)
- **Quantization step / LSB size** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$
- **Peak-to-peak quantization noise** = $\Delta$
- **Maximum instantaneous quantization error** = $\pm \Delta/2$
- **Standard derivation of quantization noise (uniformly distributed)** — see Section 2

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions Underlying the Quantization Noise Model

For the standard KTU-level analysis, the following assumptions are made:

1. **Uniform Quantization** — All step sizes $\Delta$ are equal across the entire input range.
2. **High-Resolution Approximation** — The input signal is assumed to vary over *many* quantization levels, so the quantization error is approximately **statistically independent** of the input signal.
3. **Uniform Probability Distribution** — The quantization error $e_q$ is assumed to be uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
4. **Zero Mean** — The expected value $E[e_q] = 0$ (no DC bias).
5. **Wide-Sense Stationary** — The error variance does not change with time.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Input Sampling (Already Performed before Quantization):**
The continuous signal $x(t)$ is first sampled to obtain $x[n] = x(nT_s)$, where $T_s$ is the sampling period.

**Step 2 — Quantization (Mapping to Discrete Levels):**
Each sample $x[n]$ is mapped to the nearest representable digital level $x_q[n]$ by the quantizer function $Q(\cdot)$.

$$x_q[n] = Q(x[n]) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\left(\frac{x[n]}{\Delta}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Error Generation:**
The quantization error (noise) is the residue:

$$e_q[n] = x[n] - x_q[n]$$

**Step 4 — Why "Noise"?**
Although $e_q$ is deterministic given a specific input, when the input is a complex signal (e.g., audio, sensor data with randomness), $e_q$ behaves like an **additive white noise** component that is statistically uncorrelated with the signal. This is the **Bennett's Theorem** assumption (1948).

**Step 5 — Modelling the Error as Additive Noise:**
The quantized signal can be written as:

$$x_q[n] = x[n] + e_q[n]$$

This additive noise model is the foundation for **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** calculations.

### 2.3 Derivation of RMS Quantization Noise Power

The mean-square value of the uniformly distributed quantization error over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$:

$$E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e) \, de$$

where $p(e) = 1/\Delta$ is the uniform PDF.

This gives the **mean-square quantization noise power**:

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

The **RMS quantization noise voltage** is:

$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ | Quantization step size / LSB voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $L = 2^N$ | Total number of quantization levels | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $e_q = x - x_q$ | Instantaneous quantization error | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \Delta/2$ | Maximum quantization error magnitude | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$ | Mean-square quantization noise power | V² |
| 6 | $e_{q,\text{rms}} = \Delta/\sqrt{12}$ | RMS quantization noise voltage | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $P_{\text{signal}} = A^2/2$ | Power of a full-scale sine wave (peak $A$) | V² |
| 8 | $P_{\text{noise}} = \Delta^2/12$ | Quantization noise power (uniform) | V² |
| 9 | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | SQNR (in dB) for full-scale sine input | Decibels (dB) |
| 10 | $\text{SQNR} = 1.76 + 6.02N$ | Same as #9 (alternate form) | Decibels (dB) |
| 11 | $\text{ENOB} = (\text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} - 1.76)/6.02$ | Effective Number of Bits | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** The famous $6.02$ dB-per-bit rule comes from $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.0206$ dB. Each additional bit of resolution **halves the quantization noise voltage** and adds approximately **6 dB** of SQNR.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Systems (CD = 16-bit, DVD-Audio = 24-bit):** Determines audible dynamic range.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT, ECG):** Critical for diagnostic accuracy.
- **Automotive Sensors (TPMS, LiDAR):** Quantization noise sets minimum detectable signal.
- **Telecommunications (5G base-station ADCs):** Drives bit-width and power trade-offs.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Designers must balance power consumption vs. ADC bit-width to meet noise budgets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power $\sigma_{e_q}^2$

**Statement to Prove:**
For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$, the mean-square quantization error is $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$.

**Assumption:** The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.

**Step 1 — Define the Uniform Probability Density Function (PDF):**
Since the error is uniformly distributed over an interval of width $\Delta$, the PDF is:

$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for} \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Set up the Mean-Square Integral:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the PDF:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q$$

**Step 4 — Pull out the constant $1/\Delta$:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral using the antiderivative $\int e_q^2 \, de_q = e_q^3/3$:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the upper and lower limits:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right]$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the cubes (note the sign change):**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \left[ \frac{\Delta^3}{8} - \left(-\frac{\Delta^3}{8}\right) \right] = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \left[ \frac{\Delta^3}{8} + \frac{\Delta^3}{8} \right]$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the sum inside the brackets:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \frac{2\Delta^3}{8} = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{4}$$

**Step 9 — Cancel $\Delta$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is the **fundamental result** used in every SQNR calculation.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoidal Input

**Statement to Prove:**
For an N-bit ADC digitising a full-scale sine wave $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$, the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio is:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Signal Power:**
For a sinusoidal signal with peak amplitude $A$ and $1\Omega$ reference resistance:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

For a **full-scale** input, the peak amplitude is half the full-scale range: $A = V_{FS}/2$.

**Step 2 — Express the Signal Power in terms of $\Delta$ and $N$:**

$$A = \frac{V_{FS}}{2} = \frac{\Delta \cdot 2^N}{2} = \Delta \cdot 2^{N-1}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{(\Delta \cdot 2^{N-1})^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-2}}{2} = \Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-3}$$

**Step 3 — Recall the Quantization Noise Power:**

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Signal-to-Noise Power Ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-3}}{\Delta^2/12}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{2^{2N-3}}{1/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2N-3} = 12 \cdot \frac{2^{2N}}{8} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2N}}{2} = 3 \cdot 2^{2N-1}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Decibels:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(3 \cdot 2^{2N-1})$$

**Step 7 — Apply logarithm properties ($10 \log(ab) = 10 \log a + 10 \log b$):**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(3) + 10 \log_{10}(2^{2N-1})$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate each logarithm:**
- $10 \log_{10}(3) = 10 \times 0.4771 = 4.771 \text{ dB}$
- $10 \log_{10}(2^{2N-1}) = 10(2N-1)\log_{10}(2) = 10(2N-1)(0.30103) = 6.0206(2N-1)$

**Step 9 — Combine:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 4.771 + 6.0206(2N-1)$$

**Step 10 — Expand and re-group:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 4.771 + 12.0412N - 6.0206 = 12.0412N - 1.2496$$

**Step 11 — Final Form (Standard Textbook Expression):**

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} \approx 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An 8-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5.12$ V is used to digitise a 1 kHz sine wave of peak amplitude 2.5 V. Calculate:
(a) The LSB step size $\Delta$
(b) The RMS quantization noise voltage
(c) The SQNR in dB

**Solution (a):**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5.12}{2^8} = \frac{5.12}{256} = 0.02 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.02}{3.464} = 5.77 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 5.77 \text{ mV}$$

**Solution (c):** Using the standard formula for N = 8:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

**Verification via direct power calculation:**
- Signal power: $P_s = A^2/2 = (2.5)^2/2 = 3.125$ V²
- Noise power: $P_n = \Delta^2/12 = (0.02)^2/12 = 3.33 \times 10^{-5}$ V²
- Ratio: $3.125 / 3.33 \times 10^{-5} = 93,750$
- In dB: $10 \log_{10}(93,750) = 49.72$ dB ✓ (matches within rounding)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quantization Noise vs. Bit Resolution

| N (bits) | $\Delta$ (assuming $V_{FS} = 1$ V) | $e_{q,\text{rms}}$ | SQNR (dB) | Approx. Dynamic Range |
|:--------:|:----------------------------------:|:-------------------:|:---------:|:---------------------:|
| 4 | 0.0625 V | 18.04 mV | 25.84 dB | ~19:1 |
| 8 | 3.906 mV | 1.128 mV | 49.92 dB | ~313:1 |
| 10 | 0.977 mV | 0.282 mV | 61.96 dB | ~1,250:1 |
| 12 | 244.1 µV | 70.46 µV | 74.00 dB | ~5,012:1 |
| 16 | 15.26 µV | 4.40 µV | 98.08 dB | ~80,000:1 |
| 24 | 59.6 nV | 17.2 nV | 146.24 dB | ~20,500,000:1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern observed:** Each additional bit reduces the quantization noise RMS by a factor of **2** and increases SQNR by approximately **6 dB**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Quantization Process in an ADC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal x t] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Filter LPF]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    C --> D[Quantizer N-bit]
    D --> E[Digital Encoder Binary Output]
    D --> F[Quantization Error eqn]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Additive White Noise Model]
    G --> H[SQNR Calculation]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Signal xq t]

    classDef ana fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#0a58ca
    classDef dig fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132
    classDef noise fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#842029

    class A,B,C ana
    class D,E dig
    class F,G,H,I noise
```

### 4.2 Mid-Tread Quantizer Transfer Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Input Domain xn]
        i1[Range -2.5 to -1.5 Delta]
        i2[Range -1.5 to -0.5 Delta]
        i3[Range -0.5 to +0.5 Delta]
        i4[Range +0.5 to +1.5 Delta]
        i5[Range +1.5 to +2.5 Delta]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Level xqn]
        o1[Level -2 Delta]
        o2[Level -1 Delta]
        o3[Level 0 Delta]
        o4[Level +1 Delta]
        o5[Level +2 Delta]
    end

    i1 --> o1
    i2 --> o2
    i3 --> o3
    i4 --> o4
    i5 --> o5

    classDef range fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#664d03
    classDef level fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160,color:#055160

    class i1,i2,i3,i4,i5 range
    class o1,o2,o3,o4,o5 level
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **mid-tread quantizer** has a "tread" (flat plateau) at zero — ideal for audio and DC signals. A **mid-rise quantizer** has a "rise" (no plateau) at zero — often used for signed data with guaranteed non-zero transitions.

### 4.3 Quantization Error Waveform (Sawtooth Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Continuous Input xn]
        A1[Smooth Ramp Signal]
    end

    subgraph B[Quantization Error eqn vs n]
        B1[Sawtooth Waveform]
        B2[Amplitude from -Delta per 2 to +Delta per 2]
        B3[Period equals 1 sample for monotonic input]
    end

    subgraph C[Statistical Model]
        C1[Uniform PDF over minus Delta per 2 to plus Delta per 2]
        C2[Mean equals 0]
        C3[Variance equals Delta squared over 12]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3

    classDef sig fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132
    classDef err fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#842029
    classDef stat fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#0a58ca

    class A1 sig
    class B1,B2,B3 err
    class C1,C2,C3 stat
```

### 4.4 End-to-End SQNR Computation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Start: Identify N bits and VFS] --> S2[Compute Delta equals VFS over 2 to the power N]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Noise Power Pn equals Delta squared over 12]
    S3 --> S4[Identify Signal Type: Sine / Speech / Random]
    S4 --> S5{Is it a full-scale sine?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Use SQNR equals 6.02N plus 1.76 dB]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Compute Signal Power Ps from waveform]
    S7 --> S8[Compute SQNR equals 10 log10 of Ps over Pn]
    S6 --> S9[Result: SQNR in dB]
    S8 --> S9

    classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03,color:#664d03
    classDef dec fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#0a58ca
    classDef endNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#0f5132

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S7,S8 proc
    class S5,S6 dec
    class S9 endNode
```
