<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define quantization noise. Derive the expression for the RMS value of quantization noise for a uniform quantizer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantization Noise** is the error introduced when a continuous-amplitude signal is approximated by a finite number of discrete levels in an ADC.

**Derivation:**
For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$, the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with PDF $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$.

The mean-square noise power is:

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right]$$

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

Therefore, the RMS quantization noise is:

$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{e_q}^2} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** State and explain the expression for Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of an N-bit ADC for a full-scale sinusoidal input.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an N-bit ADC with a full-scale sine wave input, the **SQNR** is given by:

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

**Explanation:** The constant **6.02** comes from $20 \log_{10}(2)$ — meaning every additional bit improves SQNR by approximately 6 dB. The constant **1.76 dB** comes from $10 \log_{10}(3/2)$, which is the ratio of the peak-to-rms correction for a sinusoidal waveform. For N = 16 (CD-quality audio), SQNR ≈ 98 dB, giving a dynamic range of about 63,000:1.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the quantization process in an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). Define quantization error and quantization noise. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of an N-bit ADC for a full-scale sinusoidal input signal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Quantization Process in an ADC (7 marks):**

The quantization process is the heart of an ADC. The functional block diagram is:

```
[Analog Input] → [Anti-Aliasing LPF] → [Sample & Hold] → [Quantizer] → [Digital Encoder] → [Digital Output]
                                                       ↓
                                                  [Quantization Error eq]
```

**Step 1 — Sampling:** The continuous input $x(t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1/T_s$ to produce $x[n]$.

**Step 2 — Quantization:** The quantizer $Q(\cdot)$ maps each sample $x[n]$ to the nearest of $2^N$ discrete amplitude levels separated by step size:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

**Step 3 — Encoding:** The selected level index is converted to an N-bit binary code word (e.g., natural binary or two's complement).

**Quantization Error** $e_q$ is the difference between the actual sample value and the quantized value:

$$e_q[n] = x[n] - x_q[n], \quad \text{where} \quad -\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Quantization Noise** is the statistical characterisation of the quantization error when treated as an additive random noise component uncorrelated with the signal.

**Step 4 — Modelling:** The quantized signal is modelled as:

$$x_q[n] = x[n] + e_q[n]$$

where $e_q$ is treated as a uniformly distributed random variable with zero mean and variance $\Delta^2/12$.

**(b) Derivation of SQNR (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Signal Power for a full-scale sine wave:**
For a sine wave with peak amplitude $A$:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

For a full-scale input, $A = V_{FS}/2 = \Delta \cdot 2^{N-1}$:

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{(\Delta \cdot 2^{N-1})^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-2}}{2} = \Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-3}$$

**Step 2 — Noise Power:**

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Power Ratio:**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-3}}{\Delta^2/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2N-3} = 3 \cdot 2^{2N-1}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dB:**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(3 \cdot 2^{2N-1}) = 10\log_{10}(3) + (2N-1) \cdot 10\log_{10}(2)$$

$$= 4.77 + (2N-1)(3.01) = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the uniform quantization process. Derive the expression for the mean-square quantization noise power for a uniform mid-tread quantizer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit ADC has a full-scale voltage of 10 V. Calculate:
- (i) The quantization step size $\Delta$
- (ii) The RMS quantization noise voltage
- (iii) The maximum possible quantization error
- (iv) The SQNR in dB for a full-scale sine input

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Uniform Quantization (7 marks):**

In a **uniform quantizer**, the entire input voltage range $V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min}$ is divided into $2^N$ equal-width intervals of size $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$. The output of the quantizer for input $x$ is:

$$Q(x) = \Delta \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{x}{\Delta} + \frac{1}{2} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(mid-tread)}$$

**Assumptions:** The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, zero-mean, and statistically independent of the input.

**Derivation of mean-square noise power:**

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, de_q$$

$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^3 \right] = \frac{1}{3\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{4} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 10$, $V_{FS} = 10$ V.

**(i) Quantization step size:**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{10}{1024} = 9.766 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 9.766 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) RMS quantization noise voltage:**

$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464} = 2.819 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Maximum quantization error:**

$$\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{9.766 \text{ mV}}{2} = 4.883 \text{ mV}$$

**(iv) SQNR for full-scale sine input:**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02N + 1.76 = 6.02(10) + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $A = V_{FS}/2$** — Many students incorrectly use $A = V_{FS}$ in the signal power formula, giving a result 6 dB too high.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ with $\Delta = V_{FS}/(2^N - 1)$** — For mid-tread quantizers, the $2^N$ form is standard. For mid-rise quantizers, some textbooks use $2^N - 1$. Verify from the question's wording.
> 3. **Using natural logs instead of $\log_{10}$** — The 6.02 factor comes specifically from $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02$. Using $20 \ln(2) = 13.8$ is a common but catastrophic mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting the 1.76 dB additive constant** — Half the marks for SQNR questions are lost here. Always include the 1.76 dB correction for sine inputs.
> 5. **Not specifying input type for SQNR** — The $6.02N + 1.76$ formula is **only for full-scale sine waves**. For random/voice signals, you must compute powers directly.
> 6. **Unit confusion** — Always state units (V, mV, dB) explicitly in numerical answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Quantization Noise**

- ✅ **Definition:** Quantization noise is the rounding error in ADC between the true analog sample and the assigned digital level.
- ✅ **Step size (LSB):** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ — smaller for higher N.
- ✅ **Error bound:** $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$ — peak-to-peak error = $\Delta$.
- ✅ **Mean-square noise power:** $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \Delta^2/12$ — the most-used formula.
- ✅ **RMS noise voltage:** $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$.
- ✅ **SQNR for sine input:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB.
- ✅ **6 dB-per-bit rule:** Each extra bit halves noise voltage and adds ~6 dB SQNR.
- ✅ **Assumptions:** Uniform distribution, zero mean, uncorrelated with signal.
- ✅ **ENOB:** Effective Number of Bits = $(\text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} - 1.76)/6.02$.
- ✅ **Overload condition:** If $\vert x \vert > V_{FS}/2$, the quantizer saturates — error is no longer bounded by $\Delta/2$ and the noise model breaks down.
- ✅ **Dithering:** Adding small random noise before quantization can linearise the quantizer and decorrelate the error from the signal — important for low-bit-depth audio.
- ✅ **Non-uniform quantization (µ-law, A-law):** Used in telephony to reduce noise for speech signals where small amplitudes are more frequent.
- ✅ **Differential Non-Linearity (DNL) and Integral Non-Linearity (INL):** Real ADCs deviate from the ideal uniform model — practical noise is slightly higher than the theoretical $\Delta^2/12$.
- ✅ **KTU board exam focus areas:**
  1. Derivation of $\Delta^2/12$ — very frequently asked.
  2. Derivation of $6.02N + 1.76$ — high-yield.
  3. Numerical problems computing $\Delta$, RMS noise, and SQNR.
  4. Conceptual difference between quantization error and quantization noise.
  5. Effect of oversampling on quantization noise (noise spectral density = $\sigma_{e_q}/\sqrt{2f_s}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Trick:** *"Quantization noise power is Delta-squared-over-twelve, SQNR is six-point-oh-two-N plus one-point-seven-six, and every bit you add gives you six dB."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SNR and D/A Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SNR and D/A Converters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) — Formal Definition

**Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the engineering metric that quantifies the ratio of the *desired signal power* to the *unwanted noise power* in any electronic, digital, or embedded system. It is universally expressed in **decibels (dB)**, making it a logarithmic, perceptually meaningful indicator of signal fidelity.

For a continuous-time signal $v(t)$ corrupted by an additive noise source $n(t)$, the SNR is defined as:

$$
\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) \;\;\text{dB}
$$

where $P_{\text{signal}} = \overline{v(t)^2}$ and $P_{\text{noise}} = \overline{n(t)^2}$ represent the average signal and noise powers respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why SNR Matters in Embedded Systems**
> In embedded data acquisition chains (sensors → ADC → DSP → DAC), SNR dictates *how many meaningful bits* survive the round-trip of conversion. A low-SNR DAC at the actuator output can render an otherwise perfect algorithm useless in the field.

### 1.2 Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) — Formal Definition

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a mixed-signal subsystem that accepts a discrete, binary-coded digital word of $N$ bits and reconstructs a corresponding **continuous-amplitude, continuous-time analog voltage or current** at its output. The output is held, ramped, or interpolated depending on architecture.

> [!NOTE] **Core Definition — Resolution**
> The **resolution** of an $N$-bit DAC is the number of distinct analog output levels it can produce:
> $$ \text{Number of levels} = 2^N $$
> For example, a 12-bit DAC produces $2^{12} = 4096$ discrete steps. The smallest step, called the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)**, is:
> $$ \Delta V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^N - 1} \approx \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^N} $$
> where $V_{\text{FSR}}$ is the full-scale output range (typically $V_{\text{REF}}$ or $2 \times V_{\text{REF}}$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Staircase and the Orchestra

Imagine an orchestra playing a violin solo, and a recording engineer is converting the live analog sound into a digital MP3 file:

* The **violin sound** is the *signal* — smooth, continuous, infinitely detailed.
* The **recording** is sampled — like a **staircase** of snapshots; the stairs' height is the **LSB (Δ)** of our DAC/ADC.
* The **gaps between steps** create a "stairstep error" — this is **quantization noise**.
* The **SNR** is essentially *"How tall is the violin note compared to the jaggedness of the staircase?"*

If the staircase has **tall risers** (few bits → large LSB), the violin sound gets *chopped up* and you hear hissing/buzzing noise — a **low SNR**. If the staircase has **tiny risers** (many bits → small LSB), the violin is reproduced faithfully — a **high SNR**.

A **DAC is the reverse staircase**: it takes the digital MP3 and rebuilds the smooth violin sound from a set of held voltages, often using a low-pass **reconstruction filter** to smooth the staircase back into a continuous waveform.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| Thermal noise voltage density | $\overline{e_n^2}$ | **$4kTR$ V²/Hz** |
| Quantization step (LSB) | $\Delta$ | **Volts** |
| Standard SNR per bit | — | **$\approx 6.02$ dB/bit** |
| SNR offset (sine wave) | — | **$+1.76$ dB** |

> [!NOTE] **For a KTU Exam:** The phrase *"increasing resolution by 1 bit improves SNR by 6 dB"* is a direct consequence of the **6.02 N + 1.76 dB** formula derived in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Noise in a DAC-Reconstructed Signal

When a DAC outputs a digital code, the resulting analog signal contains several noise/error components:

1. **Quantization Noise** — the *fundamental* noise floor imposed by finite resolution.
2. **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist)** — $4kTR$ from resistive elements in the DAC and op-amp.
3. **Nonlinearity Noise** — caused by Integral Non-Linearity (INL) and Differential Non-Linearity (DNL).
4. **Glitch Impulses** — transient spikes during code transitions (especially in current-steering DACs).
5. **Clock Jitter Noise** — uncertainty in the update timing that translates to amplitude noise.
6. **Power-Supply Rejection (PSRR) issues** — ripple coupling into the output.

The **total noise** is the **root-sum-square (RSS)** of all uncorrelated components:

$$
P_{\text{noise,total}} = \sqrt{P_{\text{quant}}^2 + P_{\text{thermal}}^2 + P_{\text{glitch}}^2 + \ldots}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **In a well-designed 12-bit DAC, quantization noise dominates, and the SNR approaches the theoretical 6.02·N + 1.76 dB limit. Thermal and glitch noise merely "eat" a fraction of a dB from this ideal.**

### 2.2 Architecture Categories of DAC

| Architecture | Working Principle | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Resistor DAC** | Binary-weighted resistor network (R, 2R, 4R…); currents sum at op-amp | Simple concept, fast | Wide resistor spread (2^N : 1); hard to match for N > 8 |
| **R-2R Ladder DAC** | Recursive R-2R network; only two resistor values | Easy to manufacture, monotonic, good for N up to 16 | Needs precision matched resistors |
| **String DAC** | Series of $2^N$ equal resistors acting as a tapped voltage divider | Inherently monotonic, simple | Requires $2^N$ resistors and switches; large for high N |
| **PWM DAC** | Low-pass filtering a PWM waveform | Cheapest, ideal for microcontrollers | Limited by PWM frequency & filter settling |
| **Sigma-Delta (Σ-Δ) DAC** | Oversampling + noise shaping | Very high resolution (16–24 bit), excellent SNR | Requires digital filter, higher latency |
| **Current-Steering DAC** | Array of matched current sources switched by thermometer/binary decoders | Very fast, low glitch | Complex; needs careful layout |

### 2.3 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

A **binary-weighted resistor DAC** works in five conceptual stages:

1. **Digital input** — An $N$-bit binary word $b_{N-1}\,b_{N-2}\ldots b_1\,b_0$ enters the DAC.
2. **Bit-to-switch mapping** — Each bit drives a single-pole switch: $b_k = 1$ connects resistor $R_k$ to $V_{\text{REF}}$; $b_k = 0$ connects it to ground.
3. **Current summation** — The currents through all resistors sum at the inverting input of an op-amp acting as a current-to-voltage converter.
4. **Binary weighting** — $R_k = R / 2^k$ ensures the MSB produces twice the current of the next bit, four times of the next, etc.
5. **Output voltage** — The op-amp produces:
   $$ V_{\text{out}} = -V_{\text{REF}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R}\left(\frac{b_{N-1}}{1} + \frac{b_{N-2}}{2} + \frac{b_{N-3}}{4} + \cdots + \frac{b_0}{2^{N-1}}\right) $$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility — Real-world use**
> R-2R ladder DACs are the *workhorse* of audio codecs, motor control (PWM reference generation), and embedded instrumentation. Sigma-Delta DACs dominate professional audio (96 kHz/24-bit) and precision sensor excitation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **All high-yield equations for SNR and DACs — must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SNR (general) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | dB scale |
| 2 | Quantization step (LSB) | $\Delta = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^N$ | Approximation for large $N$ |
| 3 | Quantization noise power | $P_q = \Delta^2 / 12$ | Uniform distribution over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ |
| 4 | Full-scale sine power | $P_s = V_{\text{FSR}}^2 / 8$ | Peak = FSR/2 |
| 5 | **Ideal SNR (full-scale sine)** | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | **THE** famous DAC/ADC formula |
| 6 | SINAD | $\text{SINAD} = 10 \log_{10}[(P_s)/(P_n + P_d)]$ | Includes distortion |
| 7 | ENOB (Effective Number of Bits) | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits |
| 8 | Dynamic range | $DR = 6.02\,N$ dB | For random/noise-like signals |
| 9 | Thermal (Johnson) noise | $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTRB$ | $B$ = bandwidth |
| 10 | R-2R output voltage | $V_{\text{out}} = -V_{\text{REF}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \dfrac{b_k}{2^{N-1-k}}$ | Standard polarity |
| 11 | Settling time (DAC) | $t_s \approx \tau \cdot \ln(2^N / \text{accuracy})$ | For $1/2$ LSB |
| 12 | Glitch energy | $E_g = V_{\text{glitch}} \cdot \Delta t$ | Joule-equivalent metric |

> [!TIP] **KTU Exam Tip — Memorize the 6.02 N + 1.76 dB formula by heart.** It is the single most-tested equation across SNR/DAC/ADC problems in KTU ESE and model exams. The constants $6.02 = 20\log_{10}(2)$ and $1.76 = 10\log_{10}(3/2)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Master Derivation — SNR of an Ideal N-Bit DAC (Full-Scale Sine Input)

This derivation produces the **cornerstone equation** of data converter theory.

#### Step 1 — Define the Quantization Step (LSB)

For an $N$-bit DAC spanning a full-scale range $V_{\text{FSR}}$:

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^N}
$$

*Logic:* The total analog range is divided into $2^N$ equally spaced discrete levels; the spacing between adjacent levels is the LSB size.

---

#### Step 2 — Model the Quantization Error

The quantization error $e(t)$ is the difference between the ideal continuous signal and the staircase output. It is bounded and approximately **uniformly distributed** in the range:

$$
-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e(t) \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

*Logic:* At best, a sample lands exactly on a step; at worst, it is $\Delta/2$ away. The error is bounded by half an LSB on either side.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute the Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power

For a uniform probability density function $p(e) = 1/\Delta$ over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, the variance is:

$$
P_q = \overline{e^2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot p(e)\, de
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
P_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2\, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

$$
P_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left(\frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot \Delta^3 / 8}{3}
$$

$$
P_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

*Logic:* The $\Delta^2$ comes from the $e^2$ term, the $12$ emerges from $3 \times 4$ (cube expansion plus symmetry factor).

---

#### Step 4 — Compute the Full-Scale Sine Wave Signal Power

A sine wave that just reaches the rails has a peak amplitude $V_p = V_{\text{FSR}}/2$. Its RMS value is $V_p / \sqrt{2} = V_{\text{FSR}} / (2\sqrt{2})$, and the average power delivered to a 1 Ω load is:

$$
P_s = \left(\frac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}^2}{8}
$$

*Logic:* Average power of a sine wave = (peak)²/2; here peak = FSR/2, so the 2 and the 4 combine into an 8.

---

#### Step 5 — Form the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}^2 / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{\text{FSR}}^2}{\Delta^2}
$$

Substituting $\Delta = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^N$, so $V_{\text{FSR}} / \Delta = 2^N$:

$$
\text{SQNR} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot (2^N)^2 = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N} = 1.5 \cdot 4^N
$$

*Logic:* This is the raw power ratio — a huge number, like $9.6 \times 10^{9}$ for $N=16$.

---

#### Step 6 — Convert to Decibels

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N} \right)
$$

Splitting the logarithm:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2^{2N}\right)
$$

Evaluating each term:
* $10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 10 \times 0.17609 = 1.761$ dB
* $10 \log_{10}(2^{2N}) = 20 N \cdot \log_{10}(2) = 20 N \times 0.30103 = 6.0206\,N$ dB

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\;}
$$

*Logic:* Every additional bit of resolution *multiplies* the number of levels by 2, *squares* the ratio, and adds $6.02$ dB — a **6 dB improvement per bit**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 12-bit DAC has a full-scale output of $V_{\text{FSR}} = 4.096$ V. A full-scale sine wave is reconstructed. Calculate:
(a) The LSB size.
(b) The quantization noise power.
(c) The theoretical SNR in dB.
(d) The ENOB if measured SINAD is 70 dB.

#### Solution

**(a) LSB size:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{4.096}{2^{12}} = \frac{4.096}{4096} = 1 \text{ mV/step}
$$

**(b) Quantization noise power:**

$$
P_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(10^{-3})^2}{12} = \frac{10^{-6}}{12} \approx 83.3 \text{ nW} \;\;(\text{per }\Omega)
$$

**(c) Theoretical SNR:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \text{ dB}
$$

**(d) Effective Number of Bits (ENOB):**

$$
\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{68.24}{6.02} \approx 11.33 \text{ bits}
$$

*Interpretation:* Even though the DAC is 12-bit, real-world noise and distortion drop the effective resolution to ~11.3 bits — about **0.7 bits of loss**, typical for a well-engineered CMOS DAC.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Different DAC Architectures vs SNR

| Architecture | Achievable Resolution | Typical SNR | Settling Time | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weighted Resistor | 6 – 8 bits | 38 – 50 dB | < 100 ns | High-speed pulse generation |
| R-2R Ladder | 8 – 14 bits | 50 – 84 dB | 100 ns – 1 µs | Audio, motor control |
| String DAC | 6 – 10 bits | 38 – 62 dB | 10 – 100 ns | Bias generators, trim DACs |
| PWM + LPF | 8 – 12 bits (effective) | 50 – 74 dB | ms range | Low-cost MCUs, LED dimming |
| Sigma-Delta | 16 – 24 bits | 96 – 120+ dB | µs – ms range | Professional audio, sensors |
| Current-Steering | 8 – 16 bits | 50 – 98 dB | < 10 ns | Video, RF, comms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete DAC Signal Chain in an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["MICROCONTROLLER CORE"] --> REG["DATA REGISTER 8 OR 16 BIT"]
    REG --> SW["DIGITAL SWITCH MATRIX"]
    SW --> NET["RESISTOR NETWORK R-2R OR WEIGHTED"]
    NET --> I2V["OPAMP I TO V CONVERTER"]
    I2V --> LPF["RECONSTRUCTION LOW PASS FILTER"]
    LPF --> OUT["ANALOG OUTPUT V OUT"]
    VREF["VOLTAGE REFERENCE 2.5V OR 4.096V"] --> NET
    CLK["CLOCK"] --> REG
    NOISE1["QUANTIZATION NOISE"] -.-> I2V
    NOISE2["THERMAL NOISE 4KTR"] -.-> NET
    NOISE3["GLITCH IMPULSE"] -.-> SW
    OUT --> SNR["SNR METER IN DB"]
    LPF --> SNR
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid — Functional Architecture of a 4-Bit R-2R Ladder DAC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B3["BIT B3 MSB SWITCH"] --> R3A["RESISTOR R 22K"]
    B2["BIT B2 SWITCH"] --> R2A["RESISTOR 2R 44K"]
    B1["BIT B1 SWITCH"] --> R1A["RESISTOR 2R 44K"]
    B0["BIT B0 LSB SWITCH"] --> R0A["RESISTOR 2R 44K"]
    R3A --> NODE1["LADDER NODE N3"]
    R2A --> NODE1
    R1A --> NODE2["LADDER NODE N2"]
    NODE1 --> NODE2
    R0A --> NODE3["LADDER NODE N1 OUTPUT"]
    NODE2 --> NODE3
    NODE3 --> OPAMP["OPAMP SUMMING INVERTING INPUT"]
    OPAMP --> VOUT["V OUT ANALOG SIGNAL"]
    VREF["VREF 5V"] --> OPAMP
    RF["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R 22K"] --> OPAMP
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid — Bit-Weighting Bar Chart Showing DAC Code-to-Voltage Mapping (4-bit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["CODE 0000 = 0V"] --- A1["CODE 0001 = 1 LSB"]
    A1 --- A2["CODE 0010 = 2 LSB"]
    A2 --- A3["CODE 0011 = 3 LSB"]
    A3 --- A4["CODE 0100 = 4 LSB"]
    A4 --- A5["CODE 0101 = 5 LSB"]
    A5 --- A6["CODE 0110 = 6 LSB"]
    A6 --- A7["CODE 0111 = 7 LSB"]
    A7 --- A8["CODE 1000 = 8 LSB MSB ONLY"]
    A8 --- A9["CODE 1111 = 15 LSB FULL SCALE"]
```

---

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Binary-Weighted Resistor DAC (Conceptual)

```
        b3   b2   b1   b0     (Digital Input Bits)
        |    |    |    |
        |    |    |    |
        SW   SW   SW   SW     (Single-Pole Switches)
        |    |    |    |
   +----+----+----+----+
   |    |    |    |    |
  [R] [2R] [4R] [8R]        (Binary-Weighted Resistors)
   |    |    |    |
   +----+----+----+--------+--------->  Inverting input of Op-Amp
                                  |
                                 [Rf]
                                  |
   ----------------------- Vout  (Analog Output)
        Non-inverting (+)  --|
                             |
                            GND

   Vref  ------------------------>  Reference voltage source
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the schematic:** The MSB (b3) drives the *smallest* resistor (R) and hence contributes the *largest* current. Each successive bit drives a resistor *twice as large* and contributes *half* the current — a perfect binary weighting.

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — SNR Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SIN["ANALOG SINE INPUT V PEAK = FSR BY 2"] --> POWS["P SIGNAL = VFSR SQUARED BY 8"]
    DAC["DAC RECONSTRUCTS STAIRCASE"] --> ERR["QUANTIZATION ERROR E T"]
    ERR --> POWQ["P NOISE = DELTA SQUARED BY 12"]
    POWS --> RATIO["RATIO P SIGNAL BY P NOISE"]
    POWQ --> RATIO
    RATIO --> DB["SNR DB = 10 LOG10 RATIO"]
    DB --> FINAL["SNR DB = 6.02 N PLUS 1.76"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
> Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in the context of a Digital-to-Analog Converter. State the formula for the theoretical SNR of an ideal $N$-bit DAC when reconstructing a full-scale sine wave.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **(1 mark) Definition:** SNR is the ratio of the desired signal power to the quantization noise power at the DAC output, expressed in decibels.
* **(1 mark) Formula:** $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_q)$ dB.
* **(1 mark) Theoretical result:** For a full-scale sine input, $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB, where $N$ is the number of bits.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Supplementary]**
> List any **three** specifications of a DAC. Briefly explain the term "monotonicity" with one sentence.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **(2 marks) Three specifications** (any three of): Resolution, Settling Time, Linearity (INL/DNL), Slew Rate, Full-Scale Range, Output Type (current/voltage), Glitch Energy, Power Dissipation.
* **(1 mark) Monotonicity:** A DAC is *monotonic* if its analog output **never decreases** as the digital input code increases from zero to full scale (i.e., no missing codes or downward steps).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**
> **Question A:**
> (a) Derive the expression for the theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio of an ideal $N$-bit DAC for a full-scale sinusoidal input. Show every step clearly. **(7 marks)**
> (b) An 8-bit DAC has a full-scale output of 5 V. Compute (i) the LSB size, (ii) the quantization noise power (assuming 1 Ω load), and (iii) the theoretical SNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

* **Step 1 (1 mark) — Define LSB:**
$$ \Delta = \dfrac{V_{\text{FSR}}}{2^N} $$

* **Step 2 (1 mark) — Quantization error is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$** with PDF $p(e) = 1/\Delta$.

* **Step 3 (2 marks) — Noise power:**
$$ P_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} $$

* **Step 4 (1 mark) — Signal power for full-scale sine ($V_p = V_{\text{FSR}}/2$):**
$$ P_s = \dfrac{V_p^2}{2} = \dfrac{V_{\text{FSR}}^2}{8} $$

* **Step 5 (1 mark) — Ratio:**
$$ \text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{P_q} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}}{1} = 1.5 \cdot 4^N $$

* **Step 6 (1 mark) — Convert to dB:**
$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2) = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \;\text{dB} $$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

* **(i) LSB (2 marks):**
$$ \Delta = \dfrac{5}{2^8} = \dfrac{5}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV} $$

* **(ii) Quantization noise power (2 marks):**
$$ P_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{(0.01953)^2}{12} = \dfrac{3.815 \times 10^{-4}}{12} \approx 31.8 \;\mu\text{W} $$

* **(iii) Theoretical SNR (3 marks):**
$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \approx 49.9 \text{ dB} $$

---

> **Q3(b). [Internal Choice — Alternative to Q3(a) | 14 Marks]**
> **Question B:**
> (a) Explain the working of a **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its analog output voltage. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Compare **R-2R ladder**, **weighted-resistor**, and **sigma-delta** DAC architectures in terms of (i) resolution, (ii) speed, (iii) component matching requirement, and (iv) typical SNR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) R-2R Ladder DAC Working (7 marks):**

* **Block diagram (2 marks):** Show the ladder with bits $b_3, b_2, b_1, b_0$ each driving a SPDT switch that connects the corresponding node to either $V_{\text{REF}}$ or GND. The ladder's leftmost node feeds the inverting input of a summing op-amp; $V_{\text{REF}}$ provides the reference.
* **Working principle (2 marks):** At every node, the Thevenin resistance looking into the ladder is exactly $R$, irrespective of switch positions. This recursive property causes each successive bit to contribute exactly *half* the current of the previous bit — perfect binary weighting with only two resistor values.
* **Derivation of output (3 marks):**
$$ V_{\text{out}} = -V_{\text{REF}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R} \left( b_3 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right) $$

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | R-2R Ladder | Weighted Resistor | Sigma-Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 8 – 14 bit | 6 – 8 bit | 16 – 24 bit |
| Speed | 100 ns – 1 µs | < 100 ns (fast) | µs – ms (slow) |
| Matching need | Moderate (only R, 2R) | Severe ($2^N$ : 1 spread) | Minimal (mostly digital) |
| Typical SNR | 50 – 84 dB | 38 – 50 dB | 96 – 120+ dB |

* **(1 mark) Awarded** for the table itself and **1 mark** for at least one sentence of explanation per row.

---

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | 14 Marks]**
> **Question A:**
> (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the major **sources of noise** in a reconstructed DAC output. How does each one affect the achievable SNR? **(7 marks)**
> (b) A 16-bit audio DAC is specified to deliver 96 dB SINAD. Calculate its (i) theoretical maximum SNR, (ii) Effective Number of Bits (ENOB), and (iii) the SNR loss in dB compared to the ideal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Noise sources (7 marks):**
* **(1 mark each) — Six sources:** Quantization noise, Thermal (Johnson) noise, Glitch impulses, Clock jitter, Integral Non-Linearity (INL), Power-supply ripple (PSRR).
* **(1 mark)** for stating that the *total* noise is the RSS of all components and that quantization noise is the theoretical floor.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
* **(i) Theoretical SNR (2 marks):**
$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB} $$

* **(ii) ENOB (3 marks):**
$$ \text{ENOB} = \frac{96 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{94.24}{6.02} = 15.65 \text{ bits} $$

* **(iii) SNR Loss (2 marks):**
$$ \Delta\text{SNR} = 98.08 - 96 = 2.08 \text{ dB} $$

---

> **Q4(b). [Internal Choice — Alternative to Q4(a) | 14 Marks]**
> **Question B:**
> (a) Explain the **sigma-delta (Σ-Δ) DAC** architecture. How does *noise shaping* help achieve very high SNR? **(7 marks)**
> (b) Design a 4-bit **binary-weighted resistor DAC** with $V_{\text{REF}} = 5$ V and $R = 10$ kΩ for the MSB branch. Calculate the output voltage for input code `1010`. Use $R_f = 10$ kΩ. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sigma-Delta DAC (7 marks):**
* **(3 marks) — Architecture:** It uses a *low-resolution* (often 1-bit) DAC running at a *very high oversampling rate* ($64\times$ to $512\times$), followed by a digital decimation filter. The quantization noise is *pushed out* of the audio band by a feedback loop containing an integrator and comparator (the "sigma" and "delta" modulators).
* **(2 marks) — Noise shaping:** The loop filter shapes the quantization noise spectrum so that it is suppressed in the low-frequency band and amplified at very high frequencies, where the decimation filter removes it. Result: in-band SNR improves by 6 dB per octave of oversampling.
* **(2 marks) — Real-world utility:** Used in 24-bit/192 kHz audio DACs, precision sensor excitation, and instrumentation.

**(b) Binary-weighted design (7 marks):**
* **Resistor values (2 marks):** $R_3 = 10$ kΩ (MSB), $R_2 = 20$ kΩ, $R_1 = 40$ kΩ, $R_0 = 80$ kΩ.
* **Output formula (2 marks):**
$$ V_{\text{out}} = -V_{\text{REF}} \cdot \frac{R_f}{R} \left( \frac{b_3}{1} + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_1}{4} + \frac{b_0}{8} \right) $$
* **Substitute (2 marks):** For code `1010` ($b_3=1, b_2=0, b_1=1, b_0=0$):
$$ V_{\text{out}} = -5 \cdot 1 \cdot \left( 1 + 0 + \frac{1}{4} + 0 \right) = -5 \cdot 1.25 = -6.25 \text{ V} $$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where students lose marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.76 dB offset** — writing SNR = 6.02N loses you 1 mark every time. The 1.76 comes from $10\log_{10}(3/2)$ and the sine-wave assumption.
> 2. **Using wrong FSR convention** — some textbooks use $V_{\text{FSR}} = 2 V_{\text{REF}}$ (bipolar). Always state the assumption clearly.
> 3. **Mixing ENOB with N** — ENOB accounts for *real-world* noise; it is **always less than** the nominal bit count.
> 4. **Confusing dynamic range (6.02 N dB)** with **SNR (6.02 N + 1.76 dB)**. Dynamic range is for noise-like signals; SNR is for sine waves.
> 5. **Forgetting the feedback resistor $R_f$ in the R-2R output equation** — it is *not* a passive divider; the op-amp sets the gain.
> 6. **Treating quantization error as random white noise** — the assumption is valid only when the input is "busy" enough (rule of thumb: at least a few LSBs of dither). For DC or slowly varying signals, the error contains spurious tones, not noise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist — SNR and D/A Converters**

* ☐ **Master formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ — derived from $P_s = V_{\text{FSR}}^2 / 8$ and $P_q = \Delta^2 / 12$ with $\Delta = V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^N$.
* ☐ **6 dB per bit rule:** every additional bit *doubles* the number of levels, *squares* the ratio, and adds **6.02 dB** to the SNR.
* ☐ **Quantization noise is uniformly distributed** between $\pm \Delta/2$ with variance $\Delta^2 / 12$. This is the *fundamental* noise floor of any N-bit system.
* ☐ **DAC resolution = $2^N$ levels, LSB = $V_{\text{FSR}} / 2^N$** (approximately, when $N$ is large).
* ☐ **Binary-weighted resistor DAC** uses resistors of values $R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{N-1}R$ — suffers from resistor-spread problem for $N > 8$.
* ☐ **R-2R ladder DAC** uses only two resistor values (R and 2R) — recursive Thevenin resistance property gives binary weighting.
* ☐ **Sigma-delta DAC** achieves ultra-high SNR (16–24 bit) via *oversampling* and *noise shaping*; in-band noise drops by 6 dB per octave of oversampling ratio.
* ☐ **SINAD vs SNR:** SINAD includes *distortion*; SNR is noise-only. ENOB = (SINAD − 1.76) / 6.02.
* ☐ **Dynamic range = $6.02\,N$ dB** (no 1.76 offset, used for random/noise-like signals).
* ☐ **Settling time** of a DAC is approximately $\tau \ln(2^N)$ for ½-LSB accuracy; it is a key spec for high-speed control loops.
* ☐ **Monotonicity** is a *guaranteed* property of R-2R ladder and string DACs but *not* of all binary-weighted designs.
* ☐ **Glitch energy** $E_g = V_{\text{glitch}} \cdot \Delta t$ quantifies code-transition transients — critical for audio and video DACs.
* ☐ **Thermal noise** sets a hard physical floor: $\overline{v_n^2} = 4kTRB$; at room temperature ($T = 300$ K), $kT \approx 4 \times 10^{-21}$ J.
* ☐ **In a real system**, total noise = RSS of quantization, thermal, glitch, and jitter contributions. Quantization noise dominates only for **slow, low-resolution** designs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arduino Uno

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Arduino Uno** is an open-source microcontroller development board based on the **ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) microcontroller fabricated by Microchip Technology (formerly Atmel). It serves as the foundational hardware reference platform for prototyping embedded systems, IoT devices, and real-time control applications in the KTU 2024 Embedded Systems Design curriculum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Arduino Uno is studied under Module 2 of PECPT751 as a representative **single-board microcontroller platform** that bridges low-level hardware concepts (registers, timers, ADC) with high-level abstracted programming (sketches, libraries, APIs).

### 1.1.1 Formal Architectural Classification

| Parameter | Classification |
|-----------|---------------|
| Architecture | Harvard (separate program and data buses) |
| CPU Type | 8-bit RISC AVR |
| Operating Voltage | **5 V** |
| Input Voltage (recommended) | **7 V – 12 V** |
| Digital I/O Pins | **14** (6 PWM-capable) |
| Analog Input Pins | **6** (10-bit ADC) |
| Flash Memory | **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader) |
| SRAM | **2 KB** |
| EEPROM | **1 KB** |
| Clock Speed | **16 MHz** |
| Form Factor | Open-source hardware reference design |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of Embedded Prototyping"**

Imagine the **Arduino Uno** as a **fully-equipped, ready-to-drive compact car chassis** in a mechanical engineering workshop:

- The **ATmega328P chip** is the *engine* — it does all the computational "heavy lifting."
- The **PCB board, headers, and power circuitry** are the *chassis, seats, and dashboard* — they make the engine usable, observable, and interfaceable.
- The **Arduino IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is the *car's instruction manual* — it translates human-friendly English-like commands (`digitalWrite(13, HIGH)`) into the engine's native machine language.
- The **USB port** is the *fuel pump and diagnostic port* combined — it both powers the board and allows software upload.

Just as a student mechanic can use the chassis to test different engines and accessories without rebuilding from scratch, an embedded systems student uses Arduino Uno to **focus on application logic** (sensors, actuators, control algorithms) rather than wrestling with low-level hardware bring-up. The platform's value lies in **abstraction without losing access** — you can ignore the complexity until you need it.

## 1.3 Why Arduino Uno is Central to KTU 2024 ESD Pedagogy

> [!NOTE]
> The Uno was selected as the KTU reference board because it offers **complete register-level transparency** (unlike Raspberry Pi's Linux abstraction layer) while still providing **beginner-friendly APIs** (unlike raw 8051 or bare AVR-GCC development).

### Key Pedagogical Strengths
1. **Open-source hardware** — schematics, gerber files, and BOM are public domain.
2. **Cross-platform toolchain** — Windows, macOS, Linux.
3. **Massive library ecosystem** — over 4,000 community-contributed libraries.
4. **Industry adoption** — used in prototyping at Bosch, Siemens, NASA, and Adafruit.
5. **Bridge to bare-metal AVR** — students can graduate to direct register manipulation using the same chip.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions on Arduino Uno typically test three layers — (1) **board-level features** (voltage, pin count), (2) **on-chip peripherals** (ADC, timers, PWM), and (3) **sketch programming model** (`setup()` and `loop()`). Memorize the ATmega328P datasheet highlights — they reappear every exam cycle.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ATmega328P — The On-Board MCU: Architectural Deep Dive

The ATmega328P is an 8-bit CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) microcontroller based on the **enhanced RISC AVR architecture**. It executes most instructions in a **single clock cycle**, achieving throughputs approaching **1 MIPS per MHz** (Million Instructions Per Second per Megahertz).

### 2.1.1 Core Subsystems Breakdown

#### A. Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs arithmetic, logic, and bit-manipulation operations in a single cycle.
- **32 × 8-bit General Purpose Registers:** Directly accessible in a single cycle — this is why AVR is "register-rich" compared to 8051's accumulator-based design.
- **Status Register (SREG):** Contains the ALU flags — Carry (C), Zero (Z), Negative (N), Overflow (V), Sign (S), Half-Carry (H), Transfer Bit (T), Global Interrupt Enable (I).

#### B. Memory Architecture (Harvard)
The ATmega328P uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with three distinct memory spaces:

| Memory Type | Size | Persistence | Purpose |
|-------------|------|-------------|---------|
| Flash (Program) | 32 KB | Non-volatile | Stores the sketch + bootloader |
| SRAM (Data) | 2 KB | Volatile | Stack, heap, variables at runtime |
| EEPROM | 1 KB | Non-volatile | Long-term parameter storage (e.g., calibration data) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for KTU:** Of the **32 KB Flash**, the bootloader occupies the top **512 bytes** (0.5 KB), leaving **31.5 KB** for user sketches. Exceeding this triggers a compile-time error in the Arduino IDE.

#### C. Power Management Subsystem
- **Operating Voltage Range:** 1.8 V – 5.5 V
- **Active Mode Current:** ~12 mA at 16 MHz, 5 V
- **Power-Save Mode Current:** < 1 µA (RTC running)
- **Brown-out Detector (BOD):** Triggers reset if Vcc drops below threshold (default 4.3 V on Uno).

### 2.1.2 Clock System

The Arduino Uno provides **three clock source options** selectable via fuse bits or software:

1. **External Crystal Oscillator — 16 MHz (default):** Connected to pins 9 (XTAL1) and 10 (XTAL2) of the ATmega328P, providing the master clock via the on-chip oscillator circuit.
2. **Internal RC Oscillator — 8 MHz:** Calibrated, with ±10% accuracy.
3. **External 32.768 kHz Crystal:** For RTC applications (Timer2 in asynchronous mode).

## 2.2 Pin Configuration Functional Analysis

The Arduino Uno exposes **20 functional pins** in addition to power and analog inputs. Understanding the **dual-mapping** (Arduino pin number vs. ATmega328P port pin) is critical for KTU register-level questions.

| D3 | PD3 | INT1, **OC2B (PWM)** | — | Timer2 |
| D4 | PD4 | T0 (Timer0 Input) | — | — |

## 2.3 ADC Subsystem Theory

The Arduino Uno features a **10-bit successive approximation register (SAR) ADC** with **6 multiplexed input channels** (A0–A5) plus internal temperature sensor channels.

### 2.3.1 ADC Conversion Mathematics

The fundamental ADC quantization formula:

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{1023}$$

For the **default configuration** (AVcc = 5 V reference, 10-bit resolution):

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \cdot 5}{1023} \;\text{Volts}$$

The **ADC step size** (resolution voltage, LSB) is:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.883\;\text{mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Insight:** When Vref = 5 V, the smallest measurable voltage change is **4.883 mV**. If a sensor outputs 2.5 V, the expected ADC reading is: ADC = (2.5 × 1023) / 5 = **511.5 → rounds to 512**.

### 2.3.2 ADC Prescaler & Sampling Rate

The ADC clock is derived from the system clock via a prescaler:

$$f_{ADC} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{PS}$$

For optimal accuracy, the ATmega328P datasheet mandates:

$$50\;\text{kHz} \leq f_{ADC} \leq 200\;\text{kHz}$$

At $f_{CLK} = 16\;\text{MHz}$, the prescaler options that satisfy this constraint are **PS = 128** (125 kHz) or **PS = 64** (250 kHz — slightly above recommended max).

The **single conversion time** is:

$$T_{conv} = \frac{13}{f_{ADC}}$$

For PS = 128: $T_{conv} = 13 / 125000 = 104\;\mu s$, giving a maximum sampling rate of **~9,615 samples/second**.

## 2.4 PWM Subsystem Theory

The Arduino Uno provides **6 hardware PWM channels** based on three 8/16-bit timers.

### 2.4.1 PWM Frequency Calculations

| Timer | Resolution | Arduino PWM Pins | PWM Frequency |
|-------|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Timer0 (8-bit) | 8-bit | 5, 6 | **976.5625 Hz** (≈ 1 kHz) |
| Timer1 (16-bit) | 16-bit | 9, 10 | **490.196 Hz** (≈ 490 Hz) |
| Timer2 (8-bit) | 8-bit | 3, 11 | **490.196 Hz** (≈ 490 Hz) |

**Timer0 PWM Frequency Derivation (Fast PWM mode, default):**

$$f_{PWM0} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{N \cdot (TOP + 1)} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{64 \cdot 256} = 976.5625\;\text{Hz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Timer0 is also used by `millis()`, `delay()`, and `Servo` library internals. Modifying its prescaler will break timing functions — this is a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.4.2 Duty Cycle Equation

For an 8-bit PWM channel with compare value $OCR$:

$$D = \frac{OCR}{2^{n} - 1} \times 100\% = \frac{OCR}{255} \times 100\%$$

For a 16-bit channel (Timer1):

$$D = \frac{OCR1x}{65535} \times 100\%$$

## 2.5 Communication Peripherals

| Protocol | Pins Used | Max Speed | KTU Exam Relevance |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **USART** | D0 (RX), D1 (TX) | Up to 2 Mbps (Uno typically 115200 baud) | Serial monitor, GPS, GSM modules |
| **I²C (TWI)** | A4 (SDA), A5 (SCL) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz | Sensors (MPU6050, BMP280), RTC (DS3231) |
| **SPI** | D10 (SS), D11 (MOSI), D12 (MISO), D13 (SCK) | Up to fCLK/2 = 8 MHz | SD cards, displays, high-speed sensors |
| **USB** | Via ATmega16U2 → UART bridge | 12 Mbps (Full Speed) | Programming, serial monitor |

- **Rapid Prototyping:** 10× faster development vs. bare 8051 for proof-of-concept demos.
- **Education:** Used in 100+ universities worldwide as a teaching tool (IITs, NITs, KTU-affiliated colleges).
- **Industry:** Bosch automotive sensor validation, NASA CubeSat prototypes, medical device R&D.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{1023}$ | ADC voltage conversion |
| 2 | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | ADC step size (LSB voltage) |

| 7 | $f_{UART} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ | USART baud rate (normal mode) |
| 8 | $f_{I^2C} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 + 2 \cdot TWBR \cdot 4^{TWPS}}$ | I²C SCL frequency |
| 9 | $T_{delay} = \dfrac{N \cdot (OCR + 1)}{f_{CLK}}$ | Timer CTC delay generation |
| 10 | $I_{total} = 5V \cdot 200mA = 1W$ | Power budget of 5 V rail |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: ADC Reading to Voltage Conversion

**Problem Statement:** A potentiometer is connected to A0 of an Arduino Uno. The IDE's `analogRead(A0)` returns a value of **723**. Compute the actual analog voltage at the pin, given that the default analog reference is **AVcc = 5.0 V**, and determine the theoretical percentage error introduced by quantization.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation

From the KTU Formula Sheet (Item 1), the ADC voltage reconstruction formula is:

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

where $n = 10$ for the ATmega328P, so $2^{n} - 1 = 1023$.

### Step 2: Substitute the Known Values

Given: $ADC = 723$, $V_{ref} = 5.0\;\text{V}$, $n = 10$.

$$V_{in} = \frac{723 \cdot 5.0}{1023}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator

The numerator product is:

$$723 \cdot 5.0 = 3615.0$$

### Step 4: Perform the Division

$$V_{in} = \frac{3615.0}{1023} = 3.5337\ldots \;\text{V}$$

### Step 5: Round to Engineering Precision

To **3 significant figures**:

$$V_{in} \approx 3.534\;\text{V}$$

### Step 6: Quantization Error Analysis

The maximum quantization error for an ideal ADC is **±½ LSB**:

$$\Delta V_{max} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} = \pm \frac{5.0}{2048} = \pm 2.4414\;\text{mV}$$

Therefore the **true voltage** lies in the interval:

$$V_{in,true} = 3.534 \pm 0.00244\;\text{V} = [3.5316,\; 3.5364]\;\text{V}$$

### Step 7: Percentage Error Computation

$$\% \text{Error}_{max} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{V_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.00244}{3.534} \times 100\% = 0.0691\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The Arduino Uno's 10-bit ADC introduces a maximum relative error of **~0.069%** at this voltage level — a key metric when comparing it to 12-bit (0.024%) or 16-bit (0.0015%) ADCs in industrial applications.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: PWM Frequency for Timer1 in Fast PWM Mode (16-bit)

**Problem Statement:** Configure Timer1 of the ATmega328P in **Fast PWM, 16-bit mode** with a prescaler of **64**. Compute the resulting PWM frequency on pins D9 and D10.

### Step 1: Recall the Fast PWM Frequency Formula

For Fast PWM, the PWM frequency is:

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{N \cdot (TOP + 1)}$$

where $N$ is the prescaler and $TOP = 65535$ for 16-bit mode (the maximum count of the 16-bit timer).

### Step 2: Identify Parameters

- $f_{CLK} = 16{,}000{,}000\;\text{Hz}$ (Arduino Uno's 16 MHz crystal)
- $N = 64$ (Timer1 prescaler)
- $TOP = 65535 = 2^{16} - 1$

### Step 3: Compute $(TOP + 1)$

$$TOP + 1 = 65535 + 1 = 65536 = 2^{16}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator Product

$$N \cdot (TOP + 1) = 64 \cdot 65536 = 4{,}194{,}304$$

### Step 5: Compute the PWM Frequency

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{4{,}194{,}304}$$

### Step 6: Perform the Division

$$f_{PWM} = 3.814697\ldots\;\text{Hz}$$

### Step 7: Round and Interpret

$$f_{PWM} \approx 3.815\;\text{Hz} \;\;( \approx 262\;\text{ms period})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Such a low PWM frequency is **useless for motor control** (audible noise, jerky motion). The Arduino IDE's default `analogWrite()` on pins 9/10 uses a prescaler of **64** with $TOP = 255$ (8-bit mode), yielding **~490 Hz** — the practical default.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: USART Baud Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Configure the ATmega328P's USART to communicate with a PC at **9600 baud** in normal (asynchronous) mode. Determine the value of the **UBRR0 (USART Baud Rate Register)** register.

### Step 1: Recall the USART Baud Rate Equation (Normal Mode, Double Speed OFF)

$$f_{baud} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to Solve for UBRR

$$UBRR + 1 = \frac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot f_{baud}}$$

$$UBRR = \frac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot f_{baud}} - 1$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Parameters

- $f_{CLK} = 16{,}000{,}000\;\text{Hz}$
- $f_{baud} = 9600\;\text{bits/second}$

$$UBRR = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \cdot 9600} - 1$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator

$$16 \cdot 9600 = 153{,}600$$

### Step 5: Compute the Quotient

$$UBRR = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{153{,}600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 104.1666\ldots - 1 = 103.1666\ldots$$

### Step 6: Round to Nearest Integer

The register must hold an integer. We choose **UBRR = 103**.

### Step 7: Compute the Actual Baud Rate with UBRR = 103

$$f_{baud,actual} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \cdot (103 + 1)} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{1664} = 9615.38\;\text{baud}$$

### Step 8: Compute the Baud Rate Error

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{f_{baud,actual} - f_{baud,target}}{f_{baud,target}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \times 100\% = 0.160\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The error of **0.160%** is well within the ±2% tolerance specified by the ATmega328P datasheet for reliable asynchronous communication.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Arduino Uno Peripherals vs. ATmega328P Registers

| Arduino Function | ATmega328P Register(s) | Reset Value | Direction | KTU Significance |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------|-----------|------------------|
| `pinMode(p, OUTPUT)` | `DDRx` (Data Direction Reg.) | 0x00 | R/W | Sets pin as input (0) or output (1) |
| `digitalWrite(p, HIGH)` | `PORTx` | 0x00 | R/W | Writes HIGH/LOW to output pin |
| `digitalRead(p)` | `PINx` | Hi-Z | R | Reads logic level on pin |
| `analogRead(p)` | `ADMUX`, `ADCSRA`, `ADC` | 0x00 | Mixed | 10-bit ADC read |
| `analogWrite(p, v)` | `TCCRnA`, `TCCRnB`, `OCRnx` | 0x00 | R/W | PWM generation |
| `millis()` | `TCNT0`, `TIMSK0` | 0x00 | R | 1 ms tick from Timer0 overflow |
| `attachInterrupt()` | `EICRA`, `EIMSK` | 0x00 | R/W | External interrupt config |
| `Serial.begin(9600)` | `UBRR0H/L`, `UCSR0B/C` | 0x00 | R/W | USART configuration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** A common **14-mark question** asks students to draw the register configuration to, for example, generate a 1 kHz PWM on pin 6 (Timer0, OC0A). The above matrix is your mental shortcut — practice writing out the bits in `TCCR0A` and `TCCR0B` by hand.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Content: Power Budget Calculation

**Problem Statement:** An Arduino Uno controls 4 LEDs (each 20 mA, Vf = 2.0 V) through 220 Ω current-limiting resistors from 5 V pins. Calculate the total current drawn from the 5 V rail and the resistor power dissipation.

### Step 1: Current Through Each LED (Ohm's Law)

$$I_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{R} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{220} = \frac{3.0}{220} = 0.01364\;\text{A} = 13.64\;\text{mA}$$

### Step 2: Total LED Current

$$I_{total} = 4 \cdot 13.64\;\text{mA} = 54.55\;\text{mA}$$

### Step 3: Power Dissipated in Each Resistor

$$P_R = I^2 \cdot R = (0.01364)^2 \cdot 220 = 0.0001860 \cdot 220 = 0.0409\;\text{W} \approx 41\;\text{mW}$$

### Step 4: Total Resistor Power

$$P_{R,total} = 4 \cdot 41\;\text{mW} = 164\;\text{mW}$$

### Step 5: Comparison with Arduino's 5 V Rail Limit

> [!WARNING]
> The Arduino Uno's **5 V pin** can source a maximum of **~200 mA** (limited by the on-board voltage regulator and trace current capacity). The calculated **54.55 mA** is safe. However, if 20 LEDs were added, the current would exceed 270 mA, causing the regulator to overheat and trigger thermal shutdown.

## 4.4 Programming Model Flow Diagram

The Arduino sketch execution model uses a super-loop architecture with two mandatory functions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWRON[Board Powered or Reset] --> BL[Optiboot Bootloader Runs<br>500 ms Window]
    BL -->|New sketch via UART| DLT[Erase Flash and Write Sketch]
    BL -->|No upload timeout| JMP[Jump to Application<br>Address 0x0000]
    DLT --> JMP
    JMP --> SETUP[setup Function<br>Runs ONCE]
    SETUP --> INIT[Initialize pins, Serial, peripherals]
    INIT --> LOOP[loop Function<br>Runs INFINITELY]
    LOOP --> T1{Execute Body<br>of Sketch}
    T1 --> END1[Return to top of loop]
    END1 --> LOOP
    T1 -.->|ISR Fires| ISR[Interrupt Service Routine<br>Preempt loop]
    ISR --> T1
    style SETUP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347,stroke-width:2px
    style LOOP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:3px
    style ISR fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Subsystem Interconnection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["ATmega328P Core"]
        CPU[CPU Core<br>AVR RISC]
        ALU[ALU]
        REG[Register File<br>32 x 8 bit]
        CPU <--> ALU
        CPU <--> REG
    end
    subgraph PERIPH["On-Chip Peripherals"]
        T0[Timer0 8 bit]
        T1[Timer1 16 bit]
        T2[Timer2 8 bit]
        ADC[ADC 10 bit SAR]
        UART[USART]
        I2C[TWI I2C]
        SPI[SPI]
        WDT[Watchdog Timer]
        EE[EEPROM 1 KB]
    end
    CPU <--> BUS[Internal Data Bus]
    BUS <--> T0
    BUS <--> T1
    BUS <--> T2
    BUS <--> ADC
    BUS <--> UART
    BUS <--> I2C
    BUS <--> SPI
    BUS <--> WDT
    BUS <--> EE
    FLASH[Flash 32 KB] -->|Program Fetch| CPU
    SRAM[SRAM 2 KB] <--> BUS
    style CORE fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px
    style PERIPH fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
```
