# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of the bootloader in an Arduino Uno. What is the default bootloader used and how much Flash memory does it occupy?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **bootloader** is a small pre-programmed firmware stored in the ATmega328P's Flash memory that enables the Arduino Uno to accept new sketches via the UART interface (over USB) without requiring an external hardware programmer.

**Key points:**
1. **(1 Mark)** The default bootloader is **Optiboot**, an open-source firmware maintained by Bill Westfield, optimized for small size and fast boot times.
2. **(1 Mark)** It occupies the **top 512 bytes (0.5 KB)** of the 32 KB Flash, leaving 31.5 KB for user sketches. The bootloader resides in the high-address region: **0x7E00 – 0x7FFF**.
3. **(1 Mark)** Upon reset, the bootloader runs for approximately **500 ms** (watchdog-driven) checking the DTR (Data Terminal Ready) line for an upload request. If no upload is initiated, control transfers to address **0x0000** where the user's sketch begins execution.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*List the differences between Arduino Uno's analog pins A4 and A5 and the remaining analog pins A0–A3. Why are A4 and A5 special?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Pins **A4 and A5** serve **dual functions** unlike A0–A3, which are exclusively ADC channels.

| Feature | A0 – A3 | A4 | A5 |
|---------|---------|----|----|
| ADC Function | ✓ (ADC0–ADC3) | ✓ (ADC4) | ✓ (ADC5) |
| I²C Function | None | **SDA** (Data line) | **SCL** (Clock line) |
| On-board Pull-ups | No | Weak internal pull-ups disabled by Wire library | Same as A4 |

**(1 Mark)** A4 = I²C **SDA** (Serial Data line)
**(1 Mark)** A5 = I²C **SCL** (Serial Clock line)
**(1 Mark)** When using the I²C bus (e.g., for connecting RTC DS3231, MPU6050, BMP280), these pins cannot simultaneously be used as analog inputs. The I²C bus requires **external pull-up resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ)** to 5 V for reliable communication.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the **Arduino Uno** board, identifying the major subsystems and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ADC subsystem** of the ATmega328P microcontroller used in Arduino Uno. Derive the formula to convert the digital ADC output to the equivalent analog input voltage. A sensor produces an output of **1.25 V**. Determine the corresponding digital ADC value, assuming **AVcc = 5.0 V** as the reference. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Subsystem Description (7 Marks)

The Arduino Uno board is organized into the following subsystems:

1. **(1.5 Marks) Power Supply Subsystem:**
   - Three input methods: **DC barrel jack (7–12 V)**, **VIN pin**, and **USB Type-B (5 V)**.
   - **On-board LDO regulator** (AP2112K or NCP1117) steps down to a stable **5 V** rail.
   - A second regulator produces **3.3 V** at up to 50 mA.
   - Reverse-polarity protection diode and polyfuse for overcurrent.

2. **(1.5 Marks) Microcontroller Core:**
   - **ATmega328P** running at **16 MHz** from an external crystal.
   - 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM.
   - 14 digital I/O, 6 analog inputs, 6 PWM outputs.

3. **(1.5 Marks) USB Interface Subsystem:**
   - **ATmega16U2** programmed as a USB-to-UART bridge.
   - Provides serial communication and 5 V power input.
   - Can be reprogrammed to act as a USB HID device (keyboard, mouse, MIDI).

4. **(1.5 Marks) I/O Headers and Connectors:**
   - **POWER header** (5V, 3.3V, GND, VIN, IOREF, RESET)
   - **DIGITAL header** (D0–D13, including PWM-marked pins)
   - **ANALOG IN header** (A0–A5)
   - **ICSP header** for in-circuit serial programming

5. **(1 Mark) Miscellaneous:**
   - Power LED (green), built-in LED on pin 13 (orange), RESET button, **16U2 activity LEDs** (TX/RX).

> [!TIP]
> **Block Diagram Reference:** Use SECTION 4.1's Mermaid diagram as the basis for your hand-drawn version. Make sure to label the ATmega16U2 bridge — many students forget it.

##### Part (b) — ADC Subsystem & Numerical (7 Marks)

**Theoretical Description (3 Marks):**

The ATmega328P features a **10-bit successive approximation register (SAR) ADC** with the following characteristics:
- 6 multiplexed single-ended input channels (ADC0–ADC5)
- 0 to Vcc input range
- Optional 1.1 V internal bandgap reference
- Selectable prescaler: 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128
- Conversion time: 13 ADC clock cycles
- Free-running or single-conversion modes

**Derivation (2 Marks):**

The ADC quantizes an analog voltage $V_{in}$ into one of $2^{n}$ discrete digital codes. For an ideal ADC, the input voltage is reconstructed as:

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

The **LSB step voltage** (resolution) is:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} = 4.883 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V}$$

**Numerical Solution (2 Marks):**

Rearranging for the ADC value:

$$ADC = \frac{V_{in} \cdot (2^{n} - 1)}{V_{ref}} = \frac{1.25 \cdot 1023}{5.0}$$

$$ADC = \frac{1278.75}{5.0} = 255.75$$

Rounding to the nearest integer (since ADC output is integer-valued):

$$\boxed{ADC = 256}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $V_{in,reconstructed} = (256 \cdot 5.0) / 1023 = 1.2512\;\text{V}$, which is within 0.0012 V of the true 1.25 V — well within the ADC's ±½ LSB tolerance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **PWM generation mechanism** of the Arduino Uno. With suitable diagrams, describe how the ATmega328P's Timer/Counter peripheral generates PWM signals on pins 6, 9, and 11. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a sketch to generate a **PWM signal of approximately 50% duty cycle** on pin 9 using Timer1. Calculate the actual PWM frequency obtained. What is the period of the waveform? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — PWM Mechanism (7 Marks)

**Theory (4 Marks):**

The Arduino Uno generates **hardware PWM** using the three on-chip timers:

- **Timer0 (8-bit):** Drives PWM on **pins 5 (OC0B)** and **pin 6 (OC0A)**. Default PWM frequency **~976 Hz**.
- **Timer1 (16-bit):** Drives PWM on **pins 9 (OC1A)** and **pin 10 (OC1B)**. Default PWM frequency **~490 Hz**.
- **Timer2 (8-bit):** Drives PWM on **pins 3 (OC2B)** and **pin 11 (OC2A)**. Default PWM frequency **~490 Hz**.

In **Fast PWM mode** (used by default), the timer counts from 0 to the **TOP** value (255 for 8-bit, 65535 for 16-bit), then resets. The output compare pin **OCnx** is driven HIGH when the timer is **below** the compare value `OCRnx` and LOW otherwise, creating a rectangular pulse train.

**The duty cycle for an 8-bit channel:**

$$D = \frac{OCRnx + 1}{256} \times 100\%$$

**The PWM frequency for an 8-bit channel with prescaler N=64:**

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{N \cdot 256} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{64 \cdot 256} = 976.5625\;\text{Hz}$$

**Pin-Specific Explanation (3 Marks):**

- **Pin 6 (PD6 / OC0A):** Driven by Timer0's Channel A. Configured via `TCCR0A` register — set bits **COM0A1=1, COM0A0=0** for non-inverting mode, and **WGM01=1, WGM00=1** for Fast PWM 8-bit mode. `TCCR0B` sets prescaler bits **CS01=1, CS00=1** for N=64.
- **Pin 9 (PB1 / OC1A):** Driven by Timer1's Channel A. `TCCR1A` controls Compare Output Mode and Waveform Generation Mode bits. `TCCR1B` controls the prescaler.
- **Pin 11 (PB3 / OC2A):** Driven by Timer2's Channel A. `TCCR2A` and `TCCR2B` registers provide configuration. Note that Timer2 can be clocked from an **external 32.768 kHz crystal** for RTC applications.

> [!TIP]
> **For sketch-level work:** `analogWrite(9, 128)` produces **~50% duty cycle** on pin 9. The `128` value is loaded into `OCR1A` (after shifting by 8 in some modes).

##### Part (b) — Design Calculation (7 Marks)

**Design Specification:**
- Target: **~50% duty cycle** on pin 9
- Pin 9 → Timer1 Channel A → 16-bit timer
- Arduino's default `analogWrite()` uses **8-bit PWM** with TOP = 255, so `analogWrite(9, 127)` gives **127/255 = 49.8%** ≈ 50% duty cycle.

**Sketch (3 Marks):**

```cpp
void setup() {
    pinMode(9, OUTPUT);
    // Configure Timer1 for Fast PWM, 8-bit mode, prescaler 64
    TCCR1A = _BV(COM1A1) | _BV(WGM10);     // Non-inverting, 8-bit Fast PWM
    TCCR1B = _BV(WGM12)  | _BV(CS11) | _BV(CS10);  // Prescaler 64
    OCR1A  = 127;                          // ~50% duty cycle
}

void loop() {
    // No CPU action required - hardware generates PWM
}
```

**Frequency Calculation (2 Marks):**

Using the default Arduino configuration for pin 9 (Fast PWM, 8-bit, prescaler 64):

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{N \cdot 256} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{64 \cdot 256} = 976.5625\;\text{Hz}$$

**Period Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$T_{PWM} = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{976.5625} = 1.024 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{s} = 1.024\;\text{ms}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often state that Arduino has **separate hardware PWM modules** for each pin. **Reality:** The 6 PWM pins share **3 timers**, so changing the frequency of pin 5 also changes pin 6 (both use Timer0).
> 
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that **Timer0 is used by `millis()` and `delay()`**. Modifying `TCCR0B` will silently break all timekeeping functions. KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for not mentioning this.
> 
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **analogWrite()** (which outputs a steady PWM duty cycle) with **analogRead()** (which samples an analog voltage). These are **fundamentally different** operations.
> 
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Using the wrong prescaler value in calculations. Always verify from the **Arduino `wiring_analog.c` core file** or the **ATmega328P datasheet Table 16-9** (Timer/Counter0 Prescaler Select).
> 
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In the baud-rate derivation, students forget that `UBRR` is a **16-bit register** split into `UBRR0H` (high byte) and `UBRR0L` (low byte) on the ATmega328P.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Arduino Uno is built on ATmega328P** (8-bit AVR RISC, 16 MHz)
- [x] **Operating voltage:** 5 V; **Input voltage range:** 7–12 V (recommended)
- [x] **Memory:** 32 KB Flash (0.5 KB bootloader), 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM
- [x] **I/O Count:** 14 digital (6 PWM), 6 analog (10-bit ADC)
- [x] **ADC resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / 1024$ ≈ **4.883 mV at 5 V**
- [x] **Default PWM frequencies:** Pins 5, 6 → 976.56 Hz (Timer0); Pins 3, 9, 10, 11 → 490.20 Hz (Timer1/2)
- [x] **USART:** Default baud 9600, UBRR = 103, error 0.16%
- [x] **I²C bus:** A4 (SDA), A5 (SCL), typically 100/400 kHz, requires 4.7 kΩ pull-ups
- [x] **SPI bus:** D10 (SS), D11 (MOSI), D12 (MISO), D13 (SCK)
- [x] **USB bridge:** ATmega16U2 (separate chip on the board, NOT the ATmega328P)
- [x] **Bootloader:** Optiboot, 512 bytes at top of Flash, 500 ms wait
- [x] **Sketch structure:** `setup()` runs once, `loop()` runs forever — super-loop architecture
- [x] **Built-in LED on pin 13** — connect a multimeter to see the voltage toggle
- [x] **3.3 V pin max current:** ~50 mA — do NOT power hungry sensors from this
- [x] **Power budget of 5 V pin:** ~200 mA total (regulator-limited)
- [x] **Six interrupt sources:** INT0, INT1, PCINT0–PCINT23 (pin change interrupts)
- [x] **Three timers:** Timer0 (8-bit, used by millis/delay), Timer1 (16-bit, for servo), Timer2 (8-bit, supports async 32.768 kHz)
- [x] **Watchdog timer:** Independent 128 kHz oscillator, configurable timeout 16 ms – 8 s

### 🧠 Memory Aids

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| **"P**orts **D**igital, **B**oth, **C**onfig" | Arduino digital pins 0–7 = Port D; 8–13 = Port B; analog A0–A5 = Port C |
| **"**5**/**6** Faster, **9**/**10**/**11** Slower" | Timer0 PWM pins (5, 6) run at ~976 Hz; Timer1/2 PWM pins (9, 10, 11, 3) at ~490 Hz |
| **"A4-A5 = I²C"** | A4=SDA, A5=SCL — never forget! |

### 📊 Exam Strategy Tips

1. **Always draw a labeled block diagram** for "Explain the architecture" type questions — this guarantees partial marks even if your text is incomplete.
2. **Memorize the ATmega328P pin map** (Arduino pin → ATmega port pin) for register-level questions.
3. **Always show the units** in your final numerical answers (e.g., "T_PWM = 1.024 ms" rather than "T = 1.024").
4. **Verify ADC voltage ranges** before computing. If the question states Vref = 1.1 V (internal bandgap), the calculations change drastically.
5. **Mention the bootloader explicitly** in any question about the boot process — this is a KTU favorite.
6. **For PWM questions,** always state the **timer source, prescaler, TOP value, and compare value** — these four parameters fully specify the waveform.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Communication and Timer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Serial Communication and Timer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Serial Communication — Formal Definition

**Serial Communication** is a data transmission methodology in which bits of a data word are transmitted sequentially over a single communication channel (wire or wireless link), one bit at a time, as opposed to parallel communication where multiple bits are sent simultaneously across multiple channels. In the context of embedded systems, serial communication is the principal mechanism used to interface microcontrollers with sensors, actuators, displays, computers, and other microcontrollers.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the topic encompasses three primary protocols:

- **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** — Asynchronous, full-duplex, no shared clock.
- **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)** — Synchronous, full-duplex, 4-wire master-slave.
- **I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)** — Synchronous, half-duplex, 2-wire multi-master.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Students must master the **frame format, baud rate calculation, register-level configuration** of UART, the **clock polarity/phase and master-slave topology** of SPI, and the **start/stop conditions, addressing, and acknowledge** mechanism of I2C. Timers must be understood as **counters driven by a clock source** with modes such as normal, CTC, PWM, and fast PWM.

### 1.2 Timers/Counters — Formal Definition

A **Timer** is a hardware peripheral that increments (or decrements) an internal register (counter) at a frequency derived from the system clock (or an external clock source). A **Counter** is functionally identical to a timer, except that the clock source is an external event (pin toggle) rather than the internal oscillator. Timers are used for:
- Precise time delays
- Event counting
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** generation
- Input capture (frequency measurement)
- Triggering ADC samples
- Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) tick generation

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Serial vs. Parallel Communication:**
Imagine a highway. A **parallel communication** highway has 8 lanes (for an 8-bit data bus); 8 cars carrying one byte travel together and arrive simultaneously. A **serial communication** highway has only **1 lane**, but the cars travel at a much higher speed so that all 8 bits still arrive in roughly the same total time — and the highway costs 8× less to build (fewer pins/wires).

**Analogy 2 — UART (Asynchronous):**
Two strangers agree in advance (via a fixed **baud rate**) to exchange letters. Neither sends a clock signal with the letter. The receiver knows exactly when to "open the envelope" because both agreed on a timing schedule beforehand. If the schedules drift (clock mismatch), letters get jumbled.

**Analogy 3 — SPI (Synchronous, Master-Slave):**
A teacher (master) dictates notes to students (slaves) in a classroom. The teacher provides both the **content (MOSI)** and a **rhythm signal (SCK)**. A **chalk duster (SS̄)** is used to point to a specific student before speaking to them. Multiple slaves = multiple chalk dusters (one per student).

**Analogy 4 — I2C (Two-wire, Addressed):**
A hostel notice board (the **SDA line**) with a bell (the **SCL clock**). A warden (master) rings the bell, then announces a **room number (address)**. Only the student in that room reads the message and raises a hand to **ACK**nowledge. Multiple wardens can take turns (multi-master).

**Analogy 5 — Timer:**
A digital stopwatch built into the MCU. The stopwatch increments every microsecond (or every clock tick). When the count hits a **threshold value**, it "rings a bell" (raises an **interrupt flag**) and can either reset to zero or be reloaded with a value.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Baud Rate (Bd):** Symbols per second. For binary signalling, **1 Baud = 1 bit/second**.
- **Standard Baud Rates:** **1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200** bits/second.
- **System Clock (f<sub>OSC</sub>):** For ATmega328P, typically **16 MHz**. For ARM Cortex-M (STM32), up to **72 MHz** or higher.
- **Tick Frequency (f<sub>tick</sub>):** The rate at which a timer's counter register increments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standards:**
> - **UART Standard:** RS-232, RS-422, RS-485 (voltage levels: ±12 V for RS-232, 0–3.3 V/5 V for TTL UART).
> - **I2C Speeds:** Standard Mode **100 kHz**, Fast Mode **400 kHz**, Fast Mode Plus **1 MHz**, High Speed Mode **3.4 MHz**.
> - **SPI Speeds:** Application-dependent, typically **1 MHz – 50 MHz** (no fixed standard).
> - **Pull-up Resistors for I2C:** Typically **2.2 kΩ to 10 kΩ** (calculated using $\tau = R \cdot C$ for bus capacitance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 UART — Operational Breakdown

**Why UART?** Because it requires only **2 wires (TX, RX)** and is fully asynchronous, making it ideal for low-cost, point-to-point communication like PC-MCU links via USB-to-UART bridges (CP2102, CH340, FTDI).

**How UART works (logical steps):**

1. **Idle State:** The line is held at logic **HIGH (1)** when no transmission occurs.
2. **Start Bit:** The transmitter pulls the line **LOW (0)** for **1 bit period** to signal the receiver that a new frame is beginning.
3. **Data Bits:** **5, 6, 7, or 8 bits** are transmitted, **LSB first** (least significant bit first). This is a KTU favourite point.
4. **Parity Bit (Optional):** Even or odd parity for error detection.
5. **Stop Bit(s):** The line returns to **HIGH** for **1, 1.5, or 2 bit periods** to mark the end of the frame.
6. **Baud Rate Agreement:** Both transmitter and receiver must be configured to the **exact same baud rate** (within ±2–3% tolerance typically).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** *"Why is the LSB transmitted first in UART?"* — Answer: UART was originally designed for telegraphy and electromechanical teletypewriters (Baudot code), where the LSB-first convention was used. UART hardware shifts bits in/out of a shift register starting from the LSB position.

### 2.2 SPI — Operational Breakdown

**Why SPI?** Because it is **synchronous, full-duplex, and very fast** (no protocol overhead like I2C's start/stop/ACK). Used for SD cards, displays, flash memory, ADCs/DACs.

**SPI Signals:**
- **SCLK (Serial Clock):** Generated by the **master only**.
- **MOSI (Master Out, Slave In):** Data from master to slave.
- **MISO (Master In, Slave Out):** Data from slave to master.
- **SS̄ (Slave Select, Active LOW):** One dedicated line per slave.

**How SPI works (logical steps):**

1. The master pulls the **SS̄ pin of the selected slave LOW** to "activate" it.
2. The master generates the **SCLK** clock signal.
3. On each clock edge (configurable), **1 bit is shifted out from the master on MOSI** AND **1 bit is shifted in from the slave on MISO** simultaneously (true full-duplex).
4. After 8 clock cycles, one full byte has been exchanged.
5. The master pulls **SS̄ HIGH** to release the bus.

**Clock Configuration (CPOL and CPHA):**
- **CPOL (Clock Polarity):** Defines the idle state of SCLK (0 = LOW, 1 = HIGH).
- **CPHA (Clock Phase):** Defines whether data is sampled on the **leading edge (0)** or **trailing edge (1)**.
- This gives **4 SPI modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)** — a high-yield KTU topic.

### 2.3 I2C — Operational Breakdown

**Why I2C?** Because it uses only **2 wires (SDA, SCL)** and supports **multiple masters and multiple slaves** on the same bus, making it ideal for board-level sensor networks (EEPROM, RTC, accelerometers, temperature sensors).

**I2C Signals:**
- **SDA (Serial Data):** Bidirectional data line.
- **SCL (Serial Clock):** Clock generated by the **current master**.
- Both lines require **pull-up resistors to V<sub>CC</sub>** (open-drain configuration).

**How I2C works (logical steps):**

1. **Idle State:** Both SDA and SCL are **HIGH** (released by pull-ups).
2. **START Condition (S):** SDA transitions from **HIGH to LOW** while SCL is **HIGH**. (A unique "Start" marker, never confused with data.)
3. **Address Frame:** Master transmits **7-bit slave address** + **1 R/W̄ bit** (1 = read, 0 = write), MSB first.
4. **ACK Bit:** Receiver pulls SDA **LOW** for one SCL cycle to acknowledge. (If no ACK, master knows slave is missing.)
5. **Data Frames:** 8-bit data bytes are transmitted, each followed by an **ACK**.
6. **STOP Condition (P):** SDA transitions from **LOW to HIGH** while SCL is **HIGH**.
7. **Repeated START (Sr):** A new START without a preceding STOP — used to switch direction (read/write) without releasing the bus.

### 2.4 Timers — Operational Breakdown

**Why Timers?** Because software `delay()` loops are inaccurate (depend on compiler optimization and CPU clock) and **block** the CPU. Hardware timers run independently of the CPU and can trigger interrupts precisely.

**How Timers work (logical steps):**

1. **Clock Source:** The timer is fed from the system clock (f<sub>OSC</sub>) optionally divided by a **prescaler**.
2. **Prescaler:** A divider that reduces the input clock frequency. Common prescaler values: **1, 8, 64, 256, 1024** (ATmega328P).
3. **Counter Register (TCNT):** An 8-bit or 16-bit register that increments on each tick.
4. **Compare Register (OCR):** A register holding a threshold value. When TCNT == OCR, an event occurs (interrupt, reset, or PWM output).
5. **Overflow:** When TCNT wraps from its max value (e.g., 255 for 8-bit, 65535 for 16-bit) back to 0, an overflow interrupt is generated.

**Timer Operating Modes (AVR family, commonly asked in KTU):**

- **Normal Mode:** Counter counts up, overflows, resets to 0, generates overflow interrupt. Used for simple timing.
- **CTC Mode (Clear Timer on Compare Match):** Counter resets to 0 when TCNT == OCR. Used for precise frequency generation.
- **Fast PWM Mode:** Used to generate PWM where OCR controls the duty cycle. Counter counts up only, resets on TOP (255 or OCR).
- **Phase Correct PWM Mode:** Counter counts up **and** down, producing a center-aligned PWM (less harmonic distortion).
- **Input Capture Mode:** Captures the TCNT value when an external event occurs on the ICP pin.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bit Period | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | T in seconds, Baud in bps | Fundamental relationship |
| UART Baud Rate (AVR) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ | f<sub>OSC</sub> in Hz, UBRR integer | UBRR = U2X=0 normal mode |
| UART Baud Rate (U2X=1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{8 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ | f<sub>OSC</sub> in Hz | Double speed mode |
| UBRR Value | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot \text{Baud}} - 1$ | Integer | Round to nearest |
| Timer Tick Frequency | $f_{tick} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{\text{Prescaler}}$ | f in Hz, Prescaler unitless | f<sub>tick</sub> must be ≤ CPU clock / 2 |
| Timer Overflow Period (Normal) | $T_{OVF} = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{Prescaler}}{f_{OSC}}$ | N = 2<sup>n</sup> for n-bit timer | For 8-bit: N = 256; 16-bit: N = 65536 |
| Timer Overflow Frequency | $f_{OVF} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{N \cdot \text{Prescaler}}$ | f in Hz | Inverse of T<sub>OVF</sub> |
| CTC Frequency | $f_{CTC} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{2 \cdot (\text{OCR} + 1) \cdot \text{Prescaler}}$ | OCR integer | When using Timer0 in CTC |
| PWM Duty Cycle (Fast PWM) | $D\% = \dfrac{\text{OCR} + 1}{N} \times 100$ | N = 2<sup>n</sup> | N-bit timer; OCR in [0, N−1] |
| PWM Frequency | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{N \cdot \text{Prescaler}}$ | f in Hz | Fast PWM, non-phase-correct |
| Phase Correct PWM Frequency | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{2 \cdot N \cdot \text{Prescaler}}$ | f in Hz | Note the factor of 2 |
| I2C Bus Capacitance Limit | $C_{bus} \leq 400 \text{ pF}$ (standard) | Capacitance | Limits bus length and speed |
| I2C Pull-up Resistor | $R_{min} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - 0.4}{3 \text{ mA}}$, $R_{max} = \dfrac{t_r}{0.8473 \cdot C_{bus}}$ | R in Ω, t<sub>r</sub> = rise time | From NXP I2C spec |
| SPI Clock Division | $f_{SCK} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128}$ | Divisor selectable | AVR-specific |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** UART is used in GPS modules, Bluetooth (HC-05), and debug consoles. SPI dominates in SD cards, TFT displays, and high-speed ADCs. I2C is the go-to for low-speed sensor arrays (BMP280, MPU6050). Timers underpin motor control (PWM), servo actuation, audio synthesis, and OS task scheduling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: UART Baud Rate Register (UBRR) Calculation

**Problem:** An ATmega328P microcontroller is operating with a system clock of **f<sub>OSC</sub> = 16 MHz**. The system must communicate with a PC at **9600 baud** in normal (U2X = 0) mode. Calculate the required value of the **UBRR register** and the **actual achieved baud rate**.

**Given:**
- $f_{OSC} = 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{Desired Baud} = 9600 \text{ bps}$
- $U2X = 0$ (normal asynchronous mode, 16× oversampling)

**Step 1: Write the AVR baud rate formula (normal mode).**

$$
\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for UBRR.**

$$
UBRR + 1 = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot \text{Baud}}
$$

$$
UBRR = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot \text{Baud}} - 1
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 9600} - 1
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the denominator.**

$$
16 \cdot 9600 = 153600
$$

**Step 5: Perform the division.**

$$
\frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{153600} = \frac{16000000}{153600} = 104.166\overline{6}
$$

**Step 6: Subtract 1.**

$$
UBRR = 104.166\overline{6} - 1 = 103.166\overline{6}
$$

**Step 7: Round to the nearest integer (UBRR is an unsigned 12-bit register, so only integers allowed).**

$$
UBRR = 103
$$

**Step 8: Calculate the actual achieved baud rate with UBRR = 103.**

$$
\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot (103 + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 104} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{1664}
$$

$$
\text{Baud}_{actual} = 9615.38 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 9: Calculate the percentage error.**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \left| \frac{\text{Baud}_{actual} - \text{Baud}_{desired}}{\text{Baud}_{desired}} \right| \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \left| \frac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \right| \times 100 = \left| \frac{15.38}{9600} \right| \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = 0.16 \%
$$

**Step 10: Verify acceptability.**

A baud rate error of **±2%** is generally acceptable for UART communication. Our error of **0.16%** is well within tolerance. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** UBRR is rounded DOWN to 103 (not up to 104) because rounding UP would produce a baud rate of 9615.38 → wait, let me recheck. If UBRR = 104, baud = 16000000 / (16 × 105) = 9523.8 bps (error = 0.79% UPWARDS). If UBRR = 103, baud = 16000000 / (16 × 104) = 9615.38 bps (error = 0.16% UPWARDS). **Both are acceptable, but UBRR = 103 gives lower error.** KTU examiners love this numerical "which side to round" question.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Timer Overflow Delay Calculation

**Problem:** Using Timer0 (8-bit) of an ATmega328P with **f<sub>OSC</sub> = 16 MHz** and a **prescaler of 64**, calculate:
(a) The time for **one** timer overflow in normal mode.
(b) The number of overflows required to generate a delay of **exactly 100 ms**.
(c) The initial value to preload into TCNT0 to achieve exactly **50 ms** with the same prescaler.

**Given:**
- $f_{OSC} = 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- Prescaler = 64
- Timer is 8-bit → N = 2<sup>8</sup> = **256** (counts from 0 to 255 then overflows)

**Part (a) — Time for one overflow:**

**Step 1: Compute the timer tick frequency.**

$$
f_{tick} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{\text{Prescaler}} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{64} = 250000 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the time per tick (instruction period for the timer).**

$$
T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}} = \frac{1}{250000} = 4 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the time for one full overflow cycle (256 ticks).**

$$
T_{OVF} = N \cdot T_{tick} = 256 \cdot 4 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_{OVF} = 1024 \text{ μs} = 1.024 \text{ ms}
$$

**Part (b) — Number of overflows for 100 ms:**

**Step 1: Divide the desired delay by the overflow period.**

$$
\text{Number of overflows} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{OVF}} = \frac{100 \text{ ms}}{1.024 \text{ ms}}
$$

$$
\text{Number of overflows} = 97.66
$$

**Step 2: Round down to the nearest integer (since 98 full overflows overshoot, we may need a preload for the last one).**

$$
\text{Full overflows} = 97
$$

**Step 3: Compute the remaining delay after 97 full overflows.**

$$
T_{97} = 97 \cdot 1.024 \text{ ms} = 99.328 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{remaining} = 100 \text{ ms} - 99.328 \text{ ms} = 0.672 \text{ ms} = 672 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 4: To achieve the remaining 672 μs, the last overflow will need a preload (as in part c).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Software timers always count full overflows, and the "last partial" is generated by preloading TCNT0 with a starting value other than 0. This is a standard KTU viva question.

**Part (c) — Preload value for 50 ms:**

**Step 1: Compute the total number of timer ticks required for 50 ms.**

$$
\text{Total ticks} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{50000 \text{ μs}}{4 \text{ μs}} = 12500 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 2: Determine how many full overflows fit into 12500 ticks.**

$$
\text{Full overflows} = \left\lfloor \frac{12500}{256} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 48.828 \right\rfloor = 48
$$

**Step 3: Compute the ticks covered by 48 full overflows.**

$$
\text{Ticks from full overflows} = 48 \cdot 256 = 12288
$$

**Step 4: Compute the remaining ticks needed.**

$$
\text{Remaining ticks} = 12500 - 12288 = 212 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the preload value.**

The timer must count **212 ticks** before overflowing, so it must start at:

$$
\text{TCNT0}_{initial} = 256 - 212 = 44
$$

**Step 6: Verify.**

Starting from TCNT0 = 44, the timer will count: 44 → 45 → ... → 255 → 0 (overflow). Total ticks = 256 − 44 = 212. ✓

Total delay = (48 × 1.024 ms) + (212 × 4 μs) = 49.152 ms + 0.848 ms = **50.000 ms** ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: PWM Duty Cycle for Motor Speed Control

**Problem:** Using Timer2 (8-bit) of an ATmega328P in **Fast PWM mode** with **f<sub>OSC</sub> = 16 MHz** and a **prescaler of 64**, compute:
(a) The PWM frequency.
(b) The value of OCR2 required to produce a **40% duty cycle**.

**Given:**
- $f_{OSC} = 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- Prescaler = 64
- Timer is 8-bit → N = 256

**Part (a) — PWM frequency:**

$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{N \cdot \text{Prescaler}} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{256 \cdot 64}
$$

$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16384} = 976.56 \text{ Hz} \approx 977 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Part (b) — OCR2 for 40% duty cycle:**

In Fast PWM (non-inverting mode), the duty cycle formula is:

$$
D\% = \frac{\text{OCR} + 1}{N} \times 100
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\text{OCR} = \frac{D \cdot N}{100} - 1 = \frac{40 \cdot 256}{100} - 1
$$

$$
\text{OCR} = 102.4 - 1 = 101.4
$$

**Step 4: Round to nearest integer.**

$$
\text{OCR2} = 101
$$

**Step 5: Verify duty cycle.**

$$
D\% = \frac{101 + 1}{256} \times 100 = \frac{102}{256} \times 100 = 39.84\% \approx 40\% \checkmark
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I2C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wires Required | 2 (TX, RX) | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS̄) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
| Clock | Asynchronous (no shared clock) | Synchronous (master provides SCLK) | Synchronous (master provides SCL) |
| Duplex | Full-duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex |
| Speed | Up to ~1 Mbps (practical) | Up to ~50 MHz (high) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz |
| Number of Devices | Point-to-point (1-to-1) | 1 master, N slaves (using N SS̄ pins) | Multi-master, up to 127 slaves (7-bit addr) |
| Addressing | None (physical point-to-point) | Hardware (SS̄ pin per slave) | Software (7 or 10-bit address in frame) |
| Hardware Cost | Low (no clock, no addressing) | Medium (one pin per slave) | Low (only 2 wires for any number) |
| Overhead | Start + Stop + Parity bits | None (pure data stream) | Start + Address + R/W̄ + ACK + Stop |
| Typical Use | PC debug, GPS, Bluetooth | SD card, TFT, high-speed ADC | Sensors, EEPROM, RTC, GPIO expanders |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UART Frame Format (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Idle State - Line HIGH] --> B[Start Bit - LOW for 1 Bit Period]
    B --> C[Data Bit D0 - LSB]
    C --> D[Data Bit D1]
    D --> E[Data Bit D2]
    E --> F[Data Bit D3]
    F --> G[Data Bit D4]
    G --> H[Data Bit D5]
    H --> I[Data Bit D6]
    I --> J[Data Bit D7 - MSB]
    J --> K{Parity Enabled?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Parity Bit]
    K -- No --> M[Stop Bit - HIGH for 1 to 2 Bit Periods]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Return to Idle State]
    N --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block represents **one bit period** (T<sub>bit</sub> = 1/BaudRate). The line is sampled at the **center of each bit period** by the receiver for maximum noise margin.

### 4.2 SPI Master-Slave Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Master[Master MCU]
    S1[Slave 1 - e.g., ADC]
    S2[Slave 2 - e.g., Flash]
    S3[Slave 3 - e.g., SD Card]
    Master -- SCLK --> S1
    Master -- SCLK --> S2
    Master -- SCLK --> S3
    Master -- MOSI --> S1
    Master -- MOSI --> S2
    Master -- MOSI --> S3
    S1 -- MISO --> Master
    S2 -- MISO --> Master
    S3 -- MISO --> Master
    Master -. SS1 Active LOW .-> S1
    Master -. SS2 Active LOW .-> S2
    Master -. SS3 Active LOW .-> S3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** Only **one SS̄ line is LOW at a time**. The other slaves ignore the bus because their SS̄ is HIGH (inactive). MISO is a high-impedance state when a slave's SS̄ is HIGH, allowing shared bus use.

### 4.3 I2C Bus with Multi-Master, Multi-Slave

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Master 1 - MCU A]
    M2[Master 2 - MCU B]
    S1[Slave A - EEPROM 0x50]
    S2[Slave B - RTC 0x68]
    S3[Slave C - Temp Sensor 0x48]
    Bus1((SDA Line - Open Drain))
    Bus2((SCL Line - Open Drain))
    Rp1[Pull-up to VCC] --- Bus1
    Rp2[Pull-up to VCC] --- Bus2
    M1 --- Bus1
    M1 --- Bus2
    M2 --- Bus1
    M2 --- Bus2
    S1 --- Bus1
    S1 --- Bus2
    S2 --- Bus1
    S2 --- Bus2
    S3 --- Bus1
    S3 --- Bus2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Drain Note:** Every device on the I2C bus can only pull the line **LOW** (sink current) or **release** it. The pull-up resistors bring the line HIGH. This is called a **wired-AND** configuration — if any device pulls LOW, the bus is LOW. This prevents bus contention.

### 4.4 Timer/Counter Block Diagram (AVR-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[System Clock fOSC] --> PS[Prescaler 1, 8, 64, 256, 1024]
    EXT[External Pin T0] --> CS[Clock Select Mux]
    PS --> CS
    CS --> CNT[Counter Register TCNT]
    CNT --> CMP{TCNT == OCR?}
    CMP -- Yes --> EVT[Event: Reset, Interrupt, or PWM Output]
    CMP -- No --> CNT
    CNT --> OVF{TCNT Overflow to 0?}
    OVF -- Yes --> OVFINT[Overflow Interrupt]
    OVF -- No --> CNT
    EVT --> CNT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The prescaler reduces the clock frequency before the counter increments. The OCR register holds a compare value that, when matched by TCNT, triggers an event (CTC reset, PWM edge, or compare-match interrupt). The overflow is detected when the counter wraps from 0xFF (for 8-bit) or 0xFFFF (for 16-bit) back to 0.

### 4.5 PWM Waveform Generation (Fast PWM Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Counter
        C0[TCNT = 0] --> C1[TCNT = 64]
        C1 --> C2[TCNT = 128]
        C2 --> C3[TCNT = 192]
        C3 --> C4[TCNT = 255 - TOP]
        C4 --> C5[TCNT = 0 - Overflow]
    end
    C0 --> OUTF[Output: HIGH]
    C1 --> OUTF
    C2 --> OUTM{TCNT == OCR?}
    C3 --> OUTL[Output: LOW]
    C4 --> OUTL
    C5 --> OUTF
    OUTM -- Yes at OCR = 128 --> OUTL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PWM Output Behaviour:** In non-inverting Fast PWM, the output is **HIGH** from BOTTOM (0) until the counter reaches OCR, then **LOW** until the next overflow (TOP = 255 for 8-bit). The duty cycle is the fraction of the period spent HIGH.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the UART frame format for an 8-bit data transmission with even parity and one stop bit.*

**Model Answer:**

A UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter) frame in idle state holds the line **HIGH (logic 1)**. The frame begins with a **Start Bit** (logic 0) of duration 1 bit period, signalling the receiver that data transmission is commencing. Following the start bit, **8 data bits** are transmitted **LSB first** (D0 to D7). For even parity, a **Parity Bit** is appended such that the total number of 1s in the data + parity bits is even. Finally, **one Stop Bit** (logic 1) of 1 bit period marks the end of the frame. The receiver samples each bit at the **center of the bit period** to maximize noise immunity.

| Bit | Start | D0 | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 | D7 | Parity | Stop |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | 0 | b0 | b1 | b2 | b3 | b4 | b5 | b6 | b7 | P | 1 |

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- 1 mark: Correct idle/start sequence with line state.
- 1 mark: Correct ordering (LSB first) and labelling of 8 data bits.
- 1 mark: Parity logic + stop bit description.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between SPI and I2C protocols.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SPI | I2C |
|---|---|---|
| **Wires Required** | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS̄ per slave) | 2 (SDA, SCL) regardless of device count |
| **Communication Type** | Full-duplex (simultaneous TX/RX) | Half-duplex (one direction at a time) |
| **Addressing** | Hardware-based (one SS̄ pin per slave) | Software-based (7-bit or 10-bit address in frame) |
| **Speed** | Higher (up to tens of MHz) | Lower (100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz) |
| **Acknowledgement** | None | Mandatory ACK after every byte |

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark per valid difference with correct technical detail.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of the I2C protocol. Describe the START condition, STOP condition, and the data acknowledgement mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ATmega328P operates at 16 MHz. Configure its UART to communicate at 19200 baud with 8 data bits, no parity, and 1 stop bit (8N1). Calculate the UBRR value and the actual achieved baud rate. Also explain the role of the UBRR register. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave** serial protocol developed by Philips (now NXP) in 1982. It uses only **two bidirectional lines**: SDA (Serial Data) and SCL (Serial Clock), both configured as **open-drain** with external pull-up resistors to V<sub>CC</sub>.

**Architecture (Block Diagram):**

```
[Master 1]   [Master 2]   [Slave A]   [Slave B]   [Slave C]
    \           |            /            \            /
     \          |           /              \          /
      ------- SDA Line (Open-Drain) ----------------
      ------- SCL Line (Open-Drain) ----------------
                    |
              Pull-up Resistors to VCC
```

**START Condition (S):** A HIGH-to-LOW transition on **SDA** while **SCL is HIGH** signals the beginning of a transmission. This is unique because data changes normally occur when SCL is LOW.

**STOP Condition (P):** A LOW-to-HIGH transition on **SDA** while **SCL is HIGH** signals the end of a transmission. After STOP, the bus returns to the idle state (both lines HIGH).

**Repeated START (Sr):** A new START issued without a preceding STOP. Used by masters to change direction (read ↔ write) without releasing the bus to other masters.

**Data Acknowledgement (ACK/NACK):**
- After every **8 data bits** (one byte), the receiver must pull SDA **LOW for the 9th SCL clock cycle** → this is an **ACK (acknowledge = 0)** confirming successful receipt.
- If SDA remains **HIGH** during the 9th clock cycle, it is a **NACK (not acknowledge = 1)**, indicating the receiver is busy, the addressed slave is missing, or the master (in read mode) has signalled the end of data.
- The transmitter (which releases SDA after the 8th bit) reads back the SDA line during the 9th clock cycle to determine if the byte was received.

**Address Frame:** The master transmits a **7-bit slave address** (MSB first) followed by a **1-bit R/W̄ direction flag** (1 = master reads from slave, 0 = master writes to slave). Only the slave whose address matches the transmitted address responds with an ACK.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Block diagram with SDA, SCL, and open-drain pull-up concept.
- 2 marks: START/STOP condition definition with timing detail.
- 1 mark: ACK/NACK mechanism.
- 1 mark: Address frame structure.
- 1 mark: Multi-master/multi-slave architectural advantage.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Recall the UBRR formula for normal mode (U2X = 0).**

$$
UBRR = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot \text{Baud}} - 1
$$

**Step 2: Substitute f<sub>OSC</sub> = 16 MHz and Baud = 19200.**

$$
UBRR = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 19200} - 1
$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$
UBRR = \frac{16000000}{307200} - 1 = 52.083 - 1 = 51.083
$$

**Step 4: Round to nearest integer.**

$$
UBRR = 51
$$

**Step 5: Compute the actual achieved baud rate.**

$$
\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot (51 + 1)} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 52} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{832}
$$

$$
\text{Baud}_{actual} = 19230.77 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the percentage error.**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \left| \frac{19230.77 - 19200}{19200} \right| \times 100 = \frac{30.77}{19200} \times 100 = 0.16\%
$$

**Step 7: Role of UBRR register.**
The **UBRR (USART Baud Rate Register)** is a 12-bit register (in ATmega328P) that holds the integer divisor for the baud rate generator. The baud rate generator divides the system clock (f<sub>OSC</sub>) by 16 × (UBRR + 1) to produce the baud rate clock. The receiver uses this clock to sample the incoming data at the center of each bit period. UBRRH holds the upper 4 bits and UBRRL holds the lower 8 bits. It must be configured before enabling the transmitter/receiver to avoid garbage data on initialization.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1 mark: Writing the correct formula.
- 2 marks: Correct arithmetic and UBRR = 51.
- 1 mark: Actual baud rate calculation.
- 1 mark: Error percentage calculation and verification.
- 2 marks: Role of UBRR with depth (12-bit, H/L split, configuration timing).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the four SPI modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) with reference to CPOL and CPHA. Illustrate with timing waveforms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Timer0 of ATmega328P (8-bit timer) with f<sub>OSC</sub> = 8 MHz and prescaler = 256, design a delay routine in **CTC mode** to generate an **exact 1 ms** interrupt tick. Compute the required OCR0 value and verify your design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The SPI protocol supports **four modes** based on two configuration bits: **CPOL (Clock Polarity)** and **CPHA (Clock Phase)**. These must be matched between the master and slave, or data will be sampled at the wrong clock edge, resulting in corrupted communication.

**CPOL (Clock Polarity):**
- CPOL = 0 → SCLK idles at **LOW (0)**.
- CPOL = 1 → SCLK idles at **HIGH (1)**.

**CPHA (Clock Phase):**
- CPHA = 0 → Data is sampled on the **leading (first) clock edge** and shifted on the **trailing (second) edge**.
- CPHA = 1 → Data is sampled on the **trailing (second) clock edge** and shifted on the **leading (first) edge**.

**The Four Modes:**

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Idle SCLK | Sample Edge | Setup Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising (leading) | Falling (trailing) |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling (trailing) | Rising (leading) |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling (leading) | Rising (trailing) |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising (trailing) | Falling (leading) |

**Mode 0 Timing Waveform (most common — used by SD cards):**

```
SCLK:    ___   ‾‾‾‾‾   ___   ‾‾‾‾‾   ___   ‾‾‾‾‾   ___
         ‾  ‾‾‾     ‾‾‾   ‾  ‾‾‾     ‾‾‾   ‾  ‾‾‾     ‾
         (Leading) (Trailing)  (Leading) (Trailing)
                  ↑ Sample              ↑ Sample
MOSI:    ‾‾‾‾‾b6‾b5‾b4‾b3‾b2‾b1‾b0‾‾‾‾‾
         ←  Setup    →
```

In Mode 0, SCLK starts LOW. The master sets up data on MOSI just before the rising edge, the slave samples MOSI on the **rising edge (leading)**, and the data is shifted to the next bit position on the falling edge.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1 mark: CPOL definition.
- 1 mark: CPHA definition.
- 2 marks: Table of all 4 modes with idle state and sample edge.
- 2 marks: Waveform for at least one mode.
- 1 mark: Consequence of mismatch (e.g., data corruption).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $f_{OSC} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- Prescaler = 256
- Timer0 is 8-bit (0 to 255)
- Mode = CTC (Clear Timer on Compare Match)
- Desired tick = 1 ms = $1 \times 10^{-3}$ s

**Step 1: Compute the timer tick frequency.**

$$
f_{tick} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{\text{Prescaler}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6}}{256} = 31250 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the time per tick.**

$$
T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}} = \frac{1}{31250} = 32 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the number of ticks required for 1 ms.**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{tick}} = \frac{1000 \text{ μs}}{32 \text{ μs}} = 31.25 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 4: In CTC mode, the counter resets when TCNT0 == OCR0. The number of ticks from 0 to OCR0 is OCR0 + 1. So we need:**

$$
OCR0 + 1 = 31.25
$$

**Step 5: Compute OCR0.**

$$
OCR0 = 31.25 - 1 = 30.25
$$

**Step 6: Round to nearest integer.**

$$
OCR0 = 30
$$

**Step 7: Verify the actual time achieved.**

With OCR0 = 30, the counter counts 31 ticks (0 → 30 inclusive, then resets). Actual time:

$$
T_{actual} = 31 \cdot 32 \text{ μs} = 992 \text{ μs} = 0.992 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 8: Calculate the error.**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \left| \frac{992 - 1000}{1000} \right| \times 100 = 0.8\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **If the examiner insists on EXACT 1 ms:** Since the tick time (32 μs) does not divide 1000 μs exactly into an integer, **no integer OCR0 can produce exact 1 ms**. The closest is OCR0 = 31 → 32 ticks × 32 μs = 1024 μs (error 2.4%). So OCR0 = 30 is the **best approximation**. The KTU-correct answer is OCR0 = 30 with explicit error acknowledgement.

**Step 9: Design summary.**
- Set TCCR0A register: WGM01 = 1 (CTC mode), COM0A bits = 00 (normal pin operation, OC0A not toggling).
- Set TCCR0B register: CS02 = 1, CS01 = 0, CS00 = 0 (prescaler = 256).
- Load OCR0 = 30.
- Enable OCIE0A interrupt in TIMSK0 register.
- In the ISR, toggle a software counter to accumulate multiples of 1 ms (if a longer delay is needed).

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1 mark: f<sub>tick</sub> and T<sub>tick</sub> computation.
- 1 mark: Setting up the CTC equation (OCR0 + 1 = N).
- 1 mark: Correct OCR0 = 30 with rounding justification.
- 2 marks: Verification with error percentage.
- 2 marks: Register-level configuration (TCCR0A, TCCR0B, TIMSK0).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Writing UBRR = (f<sub>OSC</sub> / Baud) - 1 **without the factor of 16**. The divisor is 16 × (UBRR + 1), not just (UBRR + 1), in normal mode. This is a **favourite 2-mark trap**.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that UART transmits **LSB first**. Students often write MSB first, which is **wrong for UART** (but **correct for SPI/I2C**).
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In CTC timer problems, computing the number of ticks as OCR0 instead of **OCR0 + 1**. The counter includes 0, so it counts (OCR0 + 1) values before resetting.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In PWM duty cycle, using **OCR / N** instead of **(OCR + 1) / N**. The non-inverting Fast PWM output goes HIGH at BOTTOM (TCNT = 0) and goes LOW at MATCH (TCNT = OCR), so the HIGH duration is (OCR + 1) ticks.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Confusing I2C **START** (SDA falls while SCL is HIGH) with a regular data transition. Data can only change when SCL is LOW; any change when SCL is HIGH is a **control condition**.

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **UART** is **asynchronous, full-duplex, 2-wire (TX, RX), LSB first**, no shared clock. Baud rate must match exactly (±2%).
- **UBRR formula (normal mode):** $UBRR = \frac{f_{OSC}}{16 \cdot \text{Baud}} - 1$. Round to nearest integer and **verify error**.
- **SPI** is **synchronous, full-duplex, 4-wire**, master provides SCLK. **4 modes (CPOL/CPHA)** must match master and slave.
- **I2C** is **synchronous, half-duplex, 2-wire (SDA, SCL)**, open-drain with **pull-up resistors**, **7-bit address + R/W̄**, mandatory **ACK** bit.
- **I2C Start/Stop:** SDA transition while SCL is HIGH. SDA falling = START; SDA rising = STOP.
- **Timers** are hardware counters driven by a prescaled clock. They generate **delays, PWM, and capture events** without blocking the CPU.
- **Normal Mode:** counts up, overflows at MAX (255 / 65535), generates overflow interrupt.
- **CTC Mode:** counts up, resets on TCNT == OCR, generates compare match interrupt. Use for **precise periodic interrupts**.
- **Fast PWM:** OCR controls duty cycle. $D\% = \frac{\text{OCR} + 1}{N} \times 100$, $f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{N \cdot \text{Prescaler}}$.
- **Phase Correct PWM:** $f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{2 \cdot N \cdot \text{Prescaler}}$ (note the **factor of 2** — KTU trap question).
- **Prescaler values for ATmega Timer0/1/2:** 1, 8, 64, 256, 1024 (each is a power of 2).
- **Open-drain** is the I2C physical layer — devices can only sink, never source. Pull-ups are mandatory.
- **Full-duplex** = simultaneous TX and RX (UART, SPI). **Half-duplex** = one direction at a time (I2C).
- **Standard I2C addresses are 7-bit** (the 8th bit is R/W̄), allowing up to **128 unique addresses** (theoretically 127 slaves + 1 reserved).
- **Timer Resolution:** 8-bit timer = 256 steps; 16-bit timer = 65536 steps. Higher prescaler = lower resolution but longer delays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controller Design Using Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Controller Design Using Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Arduino** is an open-source electronics prototyping platform based on **flexible, easy-to-use hardware and software**. It comprises a microcontroller-based physical programmable circuit board (often referred to as a *microcontroller unit* or *Single Board Computer – SBC* derivative for embedded control) and a ready-made Integrated Development Environment (IDE) that simplifies the writing, compilation, and uploading of C/C++ code (called **sketches**) to the board.

For the purpose of **Controller Design**, an Arduino board acts as the **decision-making core** of an embedded system. It reads data from input peripherals (sensors, switches, communication modules), processes the data through pre-programmed logic, and produces control signals for output peripherals (actuators, LEDs, motors, displays).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight (Module 2):** *Arduino-based controller design* refers to the systematic process of selecting an appropriate Arduino variant (e.g., **Uno, Mega, Nano**), mapping the Input–Process–Output (IPO) model to its hardware pins, configuring peripherals using registers (via abstracted `digitalRead`, `analogRead`, `analogWrite` functions), and implementing real-time control logic for embedded applications.

The brain of the most commonly used Arduino board is the **Atmel/Microchip ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Arduino is a Customizable Smart Switchboard"**
> 
> Imagine a highly intelligent electrical switchboard in a modern home:
> - The **ATmega328P chip** is the *brain* sitting inside this switchboard.
> - The **digital/analog pins** are the *sockets and switches* on the front panel.
> - The **USB port / power jack** is the *main power and data cable* feeding the board.
> - The **Arduino IDE** is the *remote control app* you use to tell the brain what to do.
> - The **sketch (program)** is the *set of rules* you load into the brain.

When you want the light to turn on only when it is dark *AND* a person is present, you upload a simple "rule" to the brain. The brain then continuously checks the sensors and toggles the relay. The Arduino platform abstracts complex microcontroller register-level programming into intuitive function calls like `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH)`, allowing rapid prototyping.

## 1.3 Key Hardware Variants Used in KTU Curriculum

| Variant | MCU | Digital I/O | Analog Inputs | PWM | Flash | Ideal Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arduino Uno R3** | ATmega328P | 14 (6 PWM) | 6 | 6 | 32 KB | Default academic/beginner projects |
| **Arduino Mega 2560** | ATmega2560 | 54 (15 PWM) | 16 | 15 | 256 KB | Complex multi-sensor systems |
| **Arduino Nano** | ATmega328P | 14 (6 PWM) | 8 | 6 | 32 KB | Breadboard-friendly compact designs |
| **Arduino Due** | ATSAM3X8E | 54 | 12 | 12 | 512 KB | 32-bit ARM Cortex-M3 high-speed control |

## 1.4 Important Physical / Electrical Constants (Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Logic Voltage Level:** **5 V DC** (for Uno/Nano/Mega). **3.3 V DC** (for Due, MKR series).
> - **Clock Frequency ($f_{CLK}$):** **16 MHz** (crystal oscillator on Uno/Nano).
> - **ADC Resolution:** **10-bit** → $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete steps.
> - **Default ADC Reference Voltage ($V_{REF}$):** **5.0 V** (internal AVcc).
> - **Max Digital Pin Current (per pin):** **40 mA** (absolute max).
> - **Max Total Current (all I/O):** **200 mA** for ATmega328P.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AVR ATmega328P Architecture (Why It Matters for Controller Design)

The ATmega328P uses a **Modified Harvard architecture** with separate program and data memories. For a KTU 2024 controller design problem, you must understand the pin mapping and the role of built-in peripherals.

### 2.1.1 Memory Map (KTU-Favorite Topic)

| Memory Type | Size | Volatile? | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash (Program)** | 32 KB | No | Stores the uploaded sketch (code) |
| **SRAM (Data)** | 2 KB | Yes | Runtime variables, stack, heap |
| **EEPROM** | 1 KB | No (electrically erasable) | Long-term storage of parameters/config |

### 2.1.2 Pin Multiplexing – The "Why" of Pin Functions

Each pin on the Arduino Uno serves a **primary GPIO** function and **alternate peripheral** functions. A single pin (say, Digital Pin 6) might also be the **OC0A (Timer 0 Compare Match A)** output. This multiplexing is what allows the ATmega328P to offer PWM, I²C, SPI, UART, and interrupts despite having only 28 physical pins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware Trick:** When you call `analogWrite(6, 128)`, the Arduino library silently configures Pin 6's internal timer (Timer 0) by writing to TCCR0A and OCR0A registers. The student does NOT write to registers directly — the library abstracts this. In KTU exams, expect a question asking you to explain what happens at the register level.

## 2.2 The Arduino Programming Model (Sketch Structure)

Every Arduino sketch follows a strict two-function structure:

```cpp
void setup() {
    // Runs ONCE after power-up or reset.
    // Used for: pinMode(), Serial.begin(), attachInterrupt()
}

void loop() {
    // Runs FOREVER, repeatedly, as fast as possible.
    // Used for: reading sensors, control logic, updating actuators
}
```

- The `setup()` function is executed **exactly one time** when the board is powered up or reset. It is used to *initialize* hardware states.
- The `loop()` function is executed **infinitely**. After completion, the microcontroller's Program Counter (PC) returns to the top of `loop()`. The time taken for one iteration is the **loop period ($T_{loop}$)**.

## 2.3 Digital I/O – The Foundation of Control

A digital pin operates in one of two states: **HIGH (5 V)** or **LOW (0 V)**. Internally, this corresponds to setting/clearing a bit in the **PORT register** (when configured as OUTPUT) and reading the **PIN register** (when configured as INPUT).

| Function | Purpose | Key Argument |
|---|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | Configures pin direction | `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `INPUT_PULLUP` |
| `digitalWrite(pin, val)` | Drives pin HIGH/LOW | `HIGH` or `LOW` |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | Reads pin state | Returns `HIGH` or `LOW` |

## 2.4 Analog Input – ADC Operation

Arduino's analog input uses the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** built into the ATmega328P. The input voltage ($V_{in}$) is converted to a digital value ($ADC$) using:

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{REF}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$$

For the default $V_{REF} = 5.0$ V:

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{5.0} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution (Step Size):** The smallest detectable voltage change = $\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx$ **4.88 mV**. This means an input of 2.5 V gives ADC = 512; a 1.0 V input gives ADC = 204.

## 2.5 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) – Analog Output via Digital Means

PWM is **not true analog output**; it is a high-frequency square wave whose **duty cycle** is varied to control average power delivered to a load (e.g., LED brightness, DC motor speed).

The Arduino's `analogWrite(pin, value)` accepts an 8-bit duty cycle value (0–255):

$$Duty\ Cycle\ (\%) = \frac{value}{255} \times 100$$

The actual voltage averaged by an RC filter or perceived by a slow load is:

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \frac{value}{255}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Digital Value | $ADC = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{REF}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$ | $n = 10$ bits, range 0–1023 |
| 2 | Voltage from ADC | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{REF}}{1024}$ | Volts |
| 3 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Volts/step (4.88 mV for 5V ref) |
| 4 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{value}{255} \times 100\%$ | value range 0–255 |
| 5 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \dfrac{value}{255}$ | Volts |
| 6 | PWM Frequency (Pins 5,6) | $f_{PWM0} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{510 \times N}$ where N=prescaler (default 64) → **~980 Hz** | Hz |
| 7 | PWM Frequency (Pins 9,10,11,3) | $f_{PWM} \approx$ **490 Hz** (default prescaler 64) | Hz |
| 8 | Baud Rate Formula | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \times Baud} - 1$ | For 16 MHz, 9600 baud → UBRR = 103 |
| 9 | Max Current per Pin | $I_{pin,max} = 40$ mA | Absolute maximum rating |
| 10 | Instruction Cycle Time | $T_{inst} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \dfrac{1}{16\,\text{MHz}} = 62.5$ ns | One cycle per clock for AVR |
| 11 | ADC Conversion Time | $T_{ADC} = 13 \times \dfrac{1}{f_{ADC}}$ | $f_{ADC}$ between 50–200 kHz |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Arduino-based controllers are deployed in:
> - **Home Automation:** Smart light/fan controllers with PIR + LDR sensors.
> - **Agriculture:** Automatic irrigation with soil moisture sensors and relay-driven pumps.
> - **Robotics:** Line-follower and obstacle-avoidance robots with IR and ultrasonic sensors.
> - **Industrial Monitoring:** Temperature data logging with LM35 / DHT11 over serial.
> - **Wearable Prototypes:** Health monitoring (heartbeat, SpO₂) on Nano/MKR boards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting Analog Sensor Voltage to Digital ADC Value

**Problem:** An LM35 temperature sensor outputs **0.750 V** at **75 °C** (LM35 scaling = 10 mV/°C). Read this into Arduino Uno (default 5 V reference, 10-bit ADC). What digital value will `analogRead()` return?

**Given:**
- $V_{in} = 0.750\ \text{V}$
- $V_{REF} = 5.0\ \text{V}$
- $n = 10\ \text{bits} \Rightarrow 2^{10} = 1024$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{REF}} \times 2^{n} \right\rfloor$$

Substitute the values:

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{0.750}{5.0} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$$

Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{0.750}{5.0} = 0.1500$$

Multiply by 1024:

$$ADC = \lfloor 0.1500 \times 1024 \rfloor = \lfloor 153.6 \rfloor$$

Final result (floor since ADC is integer):

$$ADC = 153$$

**Reverse Calculation (Verification):**

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{REF}}{1024} = \frac{153 \times 5.0}{1024} = \frac{765}{1024}$$

$$V_{in} \approx 0.7471\ \text{V} \quad (\text{Quantization error} = 0.750 - 0.7471 = 2.9\ \text{mV})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 2.9 mV error corresponds to $\pm 0.29\,^\circ\text{C}$ in temperature — perfectly acceptable for an LM35-based air monitoring system.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PWM Duty Cycle for Required LED Brightness

**Problem:** Design the PWM output such that an LED (with current-limiting resistor) glows at **60% perceived brightness** when supplied with $V_{CC} = 5$ V. What value should be passed to `analogWrite()`?

**Given:** Desired duty cycle $D = 60\% = 0.60$, $V_{CC} = 5$ V.

**Derivation:**

$$D = \frac{value}{255} \Rightarrow value = D \times 255$$

$$value = 0.60 \times 255 = 153$$

The `analogWrite(pin, 153)` call produces a square wave with:

$$V_{avg} = 5.0 \times \frac{153}{255} = 3.0\ \text{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The PWM frequency is **~490 Hz** (default), which is fast enough that human eyes cannot perceive the flicker — they integrate the light and perceive the *average brightness*. But a slow load (e.g., a heater coil) will still see a pulsating voltage; for true DC, you must add an **RC low-pass filter**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: UART Baud-Rate Register Calculation

**Problem:** For Arduino Uno ($f_{CLK} = 16\ \text{MHz}$), calculate the UBRR register value for a baud rate of **9600 bps** in normal asynchronous mode (U2X = 0).

**Given:** $f_{CLK} = 16{,}000{,}000\ \text{Hz}$, Baud = 9600.

**Formula (Normal Mode, U2X = 0):**

$$UBRR = \frac{f_{CLK}}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$$

**Substitution:**

$$UBRR = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{153{,}600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 104.1666... - 1 = 103.1666...$$

Since UBRR must be an integer, round to nearest:

$$UBRR = 103$$

**Resulting Actual Baud Rate:**

$$Baud_{actual} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \times (103 + 1)} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{1664} \approx 9615.38\ \text{bps}$$

**Error Percentage:**

$$\%Error = \frac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \times 100 \approx 0.16\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A baud-rate error below **±2%** is acceptable for reliable UART communication. The 0.16% error here is excellent.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ADC Conversion Time and Sampling Rate

**Problem:** Calculate the maximum ADC sampling rate for Arduino Uno when the ADC prescaler is set to **128** (default). Clock = 16 MHz.

**Given:** $f_{CLK} = 16\ \text{MHz}$, Prescaler = 128, ADC conversion takes **13 ADC clock cycles**.

**Step 1: ADC Clock Frequency**

$$f_{ADC} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{Prescaler} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{128} = 125{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 125\ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 2: ADC Conversion Time**

$$T_{ADC} = \frac{13}{f_{ADC}} = \frac{13}{125{,}000} = 1.04 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}$$

$$T_{ADC} = 104\ \mu s$$

**Step 3: Maximum Sampling Rate**

$$f_{sample,max} = \frac{1}{T_{ADC}} = \frac{1}{104\ \mu s} \approx 9615\ \text{samples/sec}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A practical `analogRead()` call takes ~100 µs (library overhead included). For sampling audio up to ~5 kHz bandwidth (Nyquist), the Arduino ADC is borderline; for slowly varying sensor signals (temperature, light, soil moisture), it is more than sufficient.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Pin-to-Peripheral Mapping (Uno R3)

| Pin | Digital | PWM | Analog In | Special Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0, 1 | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | **UART** (RX, TX) |
| 2, 3 | ✓ | Pin 3 only | ✗ | **External Interrupts** (INT0, INT1) |
| 4, 5 | ✓ | Pin 5, 6 | ✗ | **TWI (I²C)** SDA, SCL |
| 6, 7 | ✓ | Pin 6 only | ✗ | – |
| 8, 9 | ✓ | Pin 9, 10 | ✗ | – |
| 10, 11 | ✓ | Pin 11 | ✗ | **SPI** (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK) |
| 12, 13 | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | SPI (MISO, SCK), Built-in LED on 13 |
| A0–A5 | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Analog inputs (also digital) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of Arduino-Based Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]
        pwr1["USB 5V / DC Barrel Jack 7-12V"]
        pwr2["On-board 5V LDO Regulator LM1117"]
        pwr3["On-board 3.3V Regulator"]
        pwr1 --> pwr2
        pwr1 --> pwr3
    end

    subgraph MCU["CORE - ATmega328P MCU"]
        cpu["AVR 8-bit RISC CPU @ 16 MHz"]
        flash["Flash 32KB - Sketch Code"]
        sram["SRAM 2KB - Variables/Stack"]
        eeprom["EEPROM 1KB - Config Data"]
        peri["Peripherals: ADC, Timers, UART, SPI, I2C"]
        cpu --> flash
        cpu --> sram
        cpu --> eeprom
        cpu --> peri
    end

    subgraph IN["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        s1["Digital Sensors: PIR, IR, Push Button"]
        s2["Analog Sensors: LM35, LDR, Soil Moisture"]
        s3["Communication: HC-05 BT, ESP8266 WiFi"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        a1["Visual: LEDs, 16x2 LCD, OLED"]
        a2["Actuators: Relay, DC Motor, Servo, Buzzer"]
        a3["Communication: UART to PC/Serial Monitor"]
    end

    PWR --> MCU
    IN --> peri
    peri --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Arduino Sketch Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Power ON / Reset Triggered"] --> init1["Initialize Hardware Abstraction Layer"]
    init1 --> setup["Execute setup Function - ONCE"]
    setup --> loopStart["Enter loop Function"]
    loopStart --> readInput["Read Sensors: digitalRead / analogRead"]
    readInput --> process["Process Logic: Decision / Control Algorithm"]
    process --> writeOutput["Drive Actuators: digitalWrite / analogWrite"]
    writeOutput --> commCheck["Check for Serial / Interrupt Events"]
    commCheck --> loopStart
```

## 4.3 ADC Internal Block Diagram (SAR Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Analog Input Pin A0-A5"] --> mux["Analog Multiplexer 8-to-1"]
    aref["VREF 5V / 1.1V / External"] --> mux
    mux --> sample["Sample and Hold Capacitor"]
    sample --> sar["SAR - Successive Approximation Register"]
    clk["ADC Clock from Prescaler"] --> sar
    sar --> result["10-bit Digital Output ADCH ADCL"]
    result --> cpuReg["AVR CPU Data Bus"]
```

## 4.4 PWM Generation – Timer Compare Match

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tclk["16 MHz System Clock"] --> presc["Timer Prescaler: 1, 8, 64, 256, 1024"]
    presc --> tcntr["Timer/Counter TCNTn: 8 or 16-bit"]
    ocrn["OCRnA / OCRnB Compare Value 0-255"] --> comp["Comparator"]
    tcntr --> comp
    comp --> flip["Toggle OCnA / OCnB Pin on Match"]
    flip --> pwmout["PWM Square Wave on Pin 5, 6, 9, 10, 3, 11"]
    tcntr --> overflow["Overflow at TOP: 255 for 8-bit"]
    overflow --> pwmout
```

## 4.5 Typical KTU Project: Automatic Street Light Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ldr["LDR + Voltage Divider"] --> a0["Arduino A0 - Analog Input"]
    ldr2["PIR Motion Sensor"] --> d2["Arduino D2 - Digital Input INT0"]
    a0 --> mcu["ATmega328P Decision Logic"]
    d2 --> mcu
    mcu --> d9["Arduino D9 - PWM Output"]
    d9 --> led["LED Array via MOSFET Driver"]
    mcu --> d13["Arduino D13 - Status LED"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A – 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three features of the ATmega328P microcontroller used in Arduino Uno. Explain the role of the `setup()` function in an Arduino sketch.*

**Model Answer:**

Three features of ATmega328P:
1. **8-bit AVR RISC architecture** with most instructions executing in **1 clock cycle** (62.5 ns @ 16 MHz).
2. **32 KB In-System Self-Programmable Flash** memory for program storage.
3. **10-bit Successive Approximation ADC** with 6 (Uno) / 8 (Nano) analog input channels and **6 PWM output channels**.
4. Built-in peripherals: **UART, SPI, I²C (TWI)**, three Timers, Watchdog Timer, and 23 programmable I/O lines.

**Role of `setup()`:**
The `setup()` function is executed **exactly once** when the Arduino board is powered up or reset. It is used to **initialize hardware settings** such as `pinMode()`, `Serial.begin()`, and `attachInterrupt()`. After completion, control passes permanently to the `loop()` function.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the function of `analogRead()` and `analogWrite()` in Arduino. Why is PWM output not a true analog signal?*

**Model Answer:**

- **`analogRead(pin)`:** Reads the voltage at the specified analog pin (A0–A5) and returns a **10-bit integer** (0–1023) proportional to the input voltage. Internally, it triggers the SAR ADC with the configured reference voltage.

- **`analogWrite(pin, value)`:** Outputs a **PWM square wave** on a PWM-capable pin (3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 on Uno). The `value` is 8-bit (0–255), setting the **duty cycle**.

- **Why PWM is NOT true analog:** PWM is a digital signal that switches between **HIGH (5 V) and LOW (0 V)** at a fixed frequency (~490 Hz). The output pin is never at an intermediate voltage like 2.5 V — only the *time-averaged* value simulates a lower voltage. A true DAC would maintain a constant intermediate voltage.

---

### Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the **ATmega328P microcontroller** used in Arduino Uno. Explain its memory organization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an Arduino-based **temperature monitoring and alerting system** using an **LM35 sensor** and a **buzzer**. The system should sound the buzzer when temperature exceeds **40 °C**. Provide the complete circuit connection, sketch (code), and explain the ADC calculation used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** in Arduino. Derive the expression for **average output voltage** and **duty cycle**. List the PWM-capable pins on Arduino Uno along with their default frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an Arduino-based **automatic street light controller** that uses an **LDR** (light sensor) and a **PIR motion sensor**. The street light should:
- Turn ON automatically when ambient light falls below threshold.
- Increase brightness via PWM when motion is detected.
- Remain OFF during daytime.

Provide the circuit, sketch, and explain the logic. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solutions (Step-by-Step)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a): ATmega328P Architecture**

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1 — Core MCU subgraph).**

**Architecture Description:**

The ATmega328P is an **8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller** based on a **Modified Harvard architecture** with separate buses for program and data memory.

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** | Performs arithmetic, logic, and bit operations in a single clock cycle. |
| **Program Counter & Stack Pointer** | Manages instruction sequencing; the stack resides in SRAM. |
| **General Purpose Registers (32 × 8-bit)** | Ultra-fast access registers R0–R31. |
| **Flash Program Memory (32 KB)** | Non-volatile storage of the Arduino sketch. In-system programmable. |
| **SRAM (2 KB)** | Volatile runtime data, stack, and heap memory. |
| **EEPROM (1 KB)** | Non-volatile byte-addressable storage for configuration parameters. |
| **I/O Ports (PORTB, PORTC, PORTD)** | 23 programmable I/O lines. |
| **Peripherals** | 3 × Timers (8-bit, 16-bit, 8-bit), 10-bit ADC (6/8 channels), UART, SPI, I²C, Analog Comparator, Watchdog. |

**Memory Organization:**

```
+----------------------+ 0x0000
|  Interrupt Vectors   |
+----------------------+ 0x002A
|  Sketch Program      |   (32 KB Flash)
+----------------------+ 0x7FFF
|  Bootloader Section  |   (512 bytes)
+----------------------+ 0x7FFF

+----------------------+ 0x0100
|  I/O & Periph Regs   |   (64 bytes for Uno)
+----------------------+ 0x01FF
|  Extended I/O        |
+----------------------+ 0x00FF
|  SRAM Data (2 KB)    |   (variables, stack, heap)
+----------------------+ 0x08FF
|  EEPROM (1 KB)       |   (separate address space, accessed via EEAR/EEDR/EECR)
+----------------------+
```

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b): Temperature Alert System with LM35**

**Circuit Connections:**

| Component | Arduino Pin | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| LM35 Vout | **A0** | 10 mV/°C output |
| LM35 V+ | **5V** | Power supply |
| LM35 GND | **GND** | Common ground |
| Buzzer (+) | **D8** | Through NPN transistor (BC547) for current amplification |
| Buzzer (-) | **GND** | – |

**Complete Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
const int sensorPin = A0;     // LM35 output
const int buzzerPin = 8;      // Buzzer via transistor
const float threshold = 40.0; // °C

void setup() {
    pinMode(buzzerPin, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(9600);
    digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW);
}

void loop() {
    int raw = analogRead(sensorPin);
    float voltage = (raw * 5.0) / 1024.0;
    float temperatureC = voltage * 100.0;   // LM35: 10 mV/°C

    Serial.print("Temp: ");
    Serial.print(temperatureC);
    Serial.println(" C");

    if (temperatureC > threshold) {
        digitalWrite(buzzerPin, HIGH);
    } else {
        digitalWrite(buzzerPin, LOW);
    }

    delay(500);
}
```

**ADC Calculation Explanation (Derivation):**

The LM35 outputs **10 mV per °C**. At 40 °C:

$$V_{in} = 40 \times 10\ \text{mV} = 0.400\ \text{V}$$

Corresponding ADC value:

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{0.400}{5.0} \times 1024 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 81.92 \rfloor = 81$$

So whenever `analogRead()` returns a value **> 81**, the buzzer is activated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark loss trap):**
> - Students often write `temperatureC = voltage * 1000` (wrong) instead of `* 100`. The factor 100 converts **Volts to milliVolts divided by 10** → i.e., directly to °C.
> - The LM35 needs a decoupling capacitor (0.1 µF) close to its power pins for stable ADC readings — students ignore this in diagrams.
> - The buzzer's current (often > 20 mA) **must be driven through a transistor**; directly connecting to the Arduino pin can damage the MCU.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a): PWM Derivation**

**Concept:** PWM is a modulation technique where the **width (duration) of a rectangular pulse** is varied while keeping the **period constant**. The duty cycle represents the fraction of time the signal is HIGH.

**Derivation:**

Let $T$ be the period of the PWM waveform, and $T_{ON}$ the duration for which the signal is HIGH.

$$Duty\ Cycle,\ D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} \times 100\%$$

**Average Output Voltage:**

The average voltage delivered to a load over one period is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T_{ON}} V_{CC}\, dt + \frac{1}{T} \int_{T_{ON}}^{T} 0\, dt$$

$$V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \frac{T_{ON}}{T}$$

Substituting $D$:

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \frac{D}{100}}$$

For Arduino, $D$ is set using an 8-bit value (0–255):

$$D = \frac{value}{255} \times 100\%$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times \frac{value}{255}}$$

**PWM Pins and Frequencies (Arduino Uno):**

| Pins | Timer Used | Default Frequency | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5, 6 | Timer 0 | 980 Hz | 8-bit |
| 9, 10 | Timer 1 | 490 Hz | 8-bit (16-bit timer) |
| 3, 11 | Timer 2 | 490 Hz | 8-bit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Timer 0 is used by the `millis()`, `delay()`, and `Servo` library functions. Manipulating its PWM frequency can disrupt system timing. Always use Timer 1 (pins 9, 10) for custom-frequency PWM.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b): Automatic Street Light Controller**

**Components:**
- LDR + 10 kΩ resistor (voltage divider on A0)
- PIR motion sensor (digital output on D2)
- LED / Lamp array driven by MOSFET (PWM on D9)
- Arduino Uno

**Circuit Logic (refer Mermaid diagram in Section 4.5):**

| Sensor/Actuator | Arduino Pin | Type |
|---|---|---|
| LDR divider output | A0 | Analog input |
| PIR OUT | D2 (INT0) | Digital input |
| MOSFET Gate | D9 (PWM) | PWM output |
| Status LED | D13 | Digital output |

**Complete Arduino Sketch:**

```cpp
const int ldrPin   = A0;
const int pirPin   = 2;
const int ledPin   = 9;
const int statusLed= 13;

const int ldrThreshold = 500;   // ADC value below = dark
const int dimLevel     = 80;    // ~31% duty cycle
const int brightLevel  = 255;   // 100% duty cycle

void setup() {
    pinMode(pirPin, INPUT);
    pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(statusLed, OUTPUT);
    Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
    int ldrValue = analogRead(ldrPin);
    int motion   = digitalRead(pirPin);

    if (ldrValue < ldrThreshold) {
        // Night time
        if (motion == HIGH) {
            analogWrite(ledPin, brightLevel);
        } else {
            analogWrite(ledPin, dimLevel);
        }
        digitalWrite(statusLed, HIGH);
    } else {
        // Day time
        analogWrite(ledPin, 0);
        digitalWrite(statusLed, LOW);
    }

    delay(100);
}
```

**Logic Explanation:**

1. The LDR forms a voltage divider with a 10 kΩ fixed resistor. As ambient light increases, the LDR resistance drops, and the voltage at A0 increases.
2. In bright light, `analogRead(A0) > 500` → the LED is turned OFF (PWM = 0).
3. In dark conditions (`analogRead(A0) < 500`), the LED is set to **dim mode** (PWM = 80).
4. If the PIR sensor detects motion (HIGH), the LED brightness jumps to **full** (PWM = 255) for the duration of the loop.
5. A 100 ms delay ensures the PIR is not over-sampled (PIR outputs settle for ~2–3 s after detection).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (5-mark loss trap):**
> - Students often forget the **voltage divider resistor** for the LDR; without it, the analog pin floats.
> - The PIR sensor needs a **warm-up time of 20–60 seconds** after power-on — mention this in viva.
> - The MOSFET/relay must be used for the lamp; students sometimes connect the lamp directly to the Arduino pin → **short circuit / fire risk**.
