> - Wrong pin assignment: Using D2 for PWM (Pin 2 is NOT PWM-capable on Uno). Use D9 or D10.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - [ ] Arduino Uno uses **ATmega328P** at **16 MHz** with **5 V** logic.
> - [ ] Sketch = `setup()` (runs once) + `loop()` (runs forever).
> - [ ] **ADC is 10-bit** → range **0–1023**; default $V_{REF} = 5$ V → resolution = **4.88 mV/step**.
> - [ ] **PWM is digital, NOT analog**; uses `analogWrite(pin, 0–255)`.
> - [ ] **PWM default frequencies**: 490 Hz (Pins 9, 10, 3, 11), 980 Hz (Pins 5, 6).
> - [ ] PWM **average voltage**: $V_{avg} = 5 \times \dfrac{value}{255}$.
> - [ ] **Baud rate register**: $UBRR = \dfrac{16{,}000{,}000}{16 \times \text{Baud}} - 1$ → for 9600, UBRR = **103**.
> - [ ] **PWM pins on Uno**: 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11. **PWM freq alteration = Timer reconfiguration**.
> - [ ] **Max I/O current per pin = 40 mA**; total ≤ **200 mA** (ATmega328P).
> - [ ] ADC conversion time = $13 \times T_{ADC} =$ **~104 µs** (prescaler 128).
> - [ ] Always use **voltage divider / transistor / MOSFET / relay** between Arduino and high-current/voltage loads.
> - [ ] **I²C uses pins A4 (SDA) and A5 (SCL)**; **SPI uses pins 10–13**; **UART uses pins 0 (RX), 1 (TX)**.
> - [ ] LM35 conversion formula: **Temp (°C) = (ADC × 5 / 1024) × 100**.
> - [ ] Built-in LED is on **Pin 13** — used for quick "Hello World" test programs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Aware Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Aware Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Power Aware Embedded Systems — Module 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PECPT751)**
> Power-aware design is one of the **highest-weightage topics** in Module 3. Questions are routinely asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks). Mastery of the **dynamic power equation**, **DVFS**, and **DPM policies** is non-negotiable for scoring full marks.

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power-Aware Embedded System** is a computing platform (typically resource-constrained, battery-operated, or thermally-limited) in which **power consumption is treated as a first-class design constraint** alongside performance, area, and cost throughout the **entire design flow** — from specification, hardware architecture, RTL design, software/firmware, and runtime operation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a power-aware system explicitly models, estimates, and **optimizes energy and power** at the **system, architecture, circuit, and software levels** using techniques such as:

- **Dynamic Power Management (DPM)**
- **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)**
- **Clock Gating** and **Power Gating**
- **Multi-threshold (multi-Vt)** and **Multi-supply (multi-Vdd)** design
- **Power-aware scheduling** and **Compiler-directed optimizations**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric — Power-Delay Product (PDP)**
> PDP = Energy per operation = **Power × Delay**. It captures the **energy-quality trade-off** of a digital design. A lower PDP means the circuit consumes less energy to perform a given task.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are driving a car from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram (≈200 km):

- **Static Power (Leakage)** ≈ the engine idling at a red signal — fuel is consumed even though the car is not moving.
- **Dynamic Power (Switching)** ≈ the fuel burned when you actually press the accelerator — proportional to *how hard* and *how often* you accelerate.
- **Battery Capacity (mAh / Wh)** ≈ the size of your fuel tank — the larger it is, the longer you can drive.
- **DVFS** ≈ adaptive cruise control — when the road is empty (low workload), you slow down and shift to a lower gear (lower V, lower f) to save fuel. When overtaking (high workload), you rev up.
- **DPM (Sleep Modes)** ≈ turning the engine OFF at a long signal — completely cutting fuel supply.
- **Clock Gating** ≈ releasing the accelerator pedal between gear shifts — no acceleration, no fuel use.
- **Power Gating** ≈ switching off the air-conditioner compressor when not needed — peripheral device fully disabled.

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's Mantra:** *"A truly power-aware system doesn't just minimize peak power — it minimizes **average** and **total energy** over the **entire workload profile**."*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **V (Volt)** | **0.4 V – 1.2 V** (modern CMOS nodes) |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_t$ | V | **0.2 V – 0.5 V** |
| Switching Capacitance | $C$ | **F (Farad)** | per-gate fF to system-level µF |
| Clock Frequency | $f$ | **Hz** | **kHz – GHz** |
| Activity Factor | $\alpha$ | dimensionless | **0 – 1** (typically **0.1 – 0.3**) |
| Leakage Current | $I_{leak}$ | **A (Ampere)** | nA – µA per gate |
| Battery Capacity | $Q$ | **mAh / Wh** | depends on chemistry |
| Thermal Resistance | $\theta_{JA}$ | °C/W | **30 – 80 °C/W** (package) |
| Energy per Instruction | $E_{pi}$ | **J/instr** | pJ – nJ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Power Consumption in CMOS Embedded Systems

The total power dissipated by a CMOS-based embedded system has **three principal components**:

$$ P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short\_circuit} $$

### 2.1.1 Dynamic Power (Switching Power)

Dynamic power is consumed **only when the circuit is actively switching**. It arises from charging and discharging the load capacitances of CMOS gates.

$$ P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f $$

> **Why it matters in KTU exams:** This single equation is worth **at least 3–7 marks** in most board papers. The **quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$** is the foundation of DVFS-based optimization.

**Detailed Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Capacitance Charge:** Each gate output node has a load capacitance $C$. To flip a bit, charge $Q = C \cdot V_{DD}$ must be moved from $V_{DD}$ to GND (or vice versa).
- **Step 2 — Energy per Transition:** Energy drawn from the supply is $E = C \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ per full 0→1 transition. Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS pull-up network is $E_{dissipated} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2}$.
- **Step 3 — Activity Factor ($\alpha$):** In a real system, not every clock cycle causes a transition. $\alpha$ (0 ≤ $\alpha$ ≤ 1) represents the **average fraction of nodes switching per clock cycle**.
- **Step 4 — Frequency Scaling:** Transitions occur $f$ times per second. Thus power (energy/time) = energy × transition rate.
- **Step 5 — Total:** Multiply all factors: $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$.

### 2.1.2 Static Power (Leakage Power)

Even when the circuit is **idle** (no switching), CMOS transistors leak current from $V_{DD}$ to GND through:

- **Sub-threshold leakage ($I_{sub}$):** current flowing between source and drain when $V_{GS} < V_t$.
- **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage ($I_{gate}$):** electrons tunneling through the thin gate oxide.
- **Reverse-biased junction leakage ($I_{rev}$):** at the drain-body and source-body pn-junctions.

$$ P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At sub-90nm process nodes, **leakage power can exceed dynamic power** in idle states. This is why **Power Gating** is critical.

### 2.1.3 Short-Circuit Power

During the brief moment when both PMOS and NMOS of a CMOS gate are simultaneously ON (during signal transition), a direct current path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

$$ P_{short} = \tau \cdot V_{DD} \cdot I_{peak} \cdot f $$

where $\tau$ is the rise/fall time of the input signal.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam ready. Memorize the symbols, units, and the "what to plug in" for typical problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ | Dynamic switching power | **Most asked** — DVFS, V/f scaling problems |
| 2 | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static / leakage power | Power gating problems |
| 3 | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat}$ | Total chip power | Combined power budget questions |
| 4 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy from power over time | Battery-life calculations |
| 5 | $PDP = P \cdot t_{d}$ | Power-Delay Product | Comparing design alternatives |
| 6 | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_{d}$ | Energy-Delay Product | Architectural trade-offs |
| 7 | $\dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_{new}}{f_{old}}$ | DVFS power ratio | **Direct 7-mark derivations** |
| 8 | $t_{d} \propto \dfrac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{t})^{\beta}}$ | CMOS propagation delay | DVFS frequency scaling |
| 9 | $f_{max} \propto \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_{t})^{\beta}}{V_{DD}}$ | Max clock frequency | Frequency vs voltage trade-off |
| 10 | $Life = \dfrac{Battery\_Wh}{P_{avg}}$ | Battery life (hours) | Energy budgeting problems |
| 11 | $I_{sub} \propto e^{\frac{-V_t}{nV_T}}$ | Sub-threshold leakage | Multi-Vt design |
| 12 | $T_{j} = T_{a} + P \cdot \theta_{JA}$ | Junction temperature | Thermal design power |

> ⚠️ **Notation Note:** In the table, $t_{d}$ denotes propagation delay and $t$ denotes execution time. Do not confuse them in derivations.

---

## 2.3 Power Optimization Techniques — The Five-Level Hierarchy

Power optimization is applied at **five abstraction levels**, each with different ROI and complexity:

### Level 1 — System-Level Optimization

- **Dynamic Power Management (DPM):** Shutting down or placing idle components into low-power states.
- **Power-aware task scheduling:** Assigning workloads to processors to maximize idle periods (e.g., **Real-Time DPM**, **Lazy Scheduling**).
- **Hardware-software partitioning:** Mapping compute-heavy tasks to specialized accelerators (ASIC/FPGA) rather than a power-hungry general-purpose CPU.
- **Choice of architecture:** RISC vs CISC, ASIC vs FPGA vs general-purpose MCU.

> **Real-world example:** A smartphone SoC powers off the GPU when displaying a static image.

### Level 2 — Architecture-Level Optimization

- **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling):** Adjust $V_{DD}$ and $f$ dynamically based on workload.
- **Clock Gating:** Disabling the clock signal to idle functional units.
- **Power Gating:** Using sleep transistors (header/footer) to cut $V_{DD}$ to idle blocks.
- **Multi-Vt design:** Using Low-Vt (fast, leaky) cells on critical paths and High-Vt (slow, low-leakage) cells elsewhere.
- **Multi-Vdd design:** Different supply voltages for different IP blocks.

### Level 3 — Logic / Circuit-Level Optimization

- **Transistor sizing** to balance drive strength and capacitance.
- **Adiabatic logic** and **charge-recovery circuits** for ultra-low-power applications.
- **Logic restructuring** to minimize switching activity (e.g., Gray coding for counters).
- **Operand isolation** to prevent unnecessary toggling.

### Level 4 — RT-Level / RTL Optimization

- **State encoding:** Use **Gray code** for FSM state registers to minimize bit-flips (only one bit changes per state transition).
- **Resource sharing** to reduce active area.
- **Pipeline balancing** to reduce glitches.
- **Clock-tree synthesis** to reduce clock network capacitance.

### Level 5 — Software / Compiler-Level Optimization

- **Instruction-level optimization** (reducing memory accesses, using register variables).
- **Loop transformations** (unrolling, fusion, tiling) to reduce overhead.
- **Compiler-directed voltage scaling.**
- **Algorithm selection** — e.g., using **FFT** instead of naive DFT to reduce $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log N)$ operations.
- **Sleep mode API** invocation in firmware (e.g., `__WFI()` on ARM Cortex-M).

> [!TIP]
> **Energy savings vs. level abstraction (rule of thumb):**
>
> | Level | Energy Savings Achievable | Design Effort |
> |---|---|---|
> | System | 50–90 % | Low (software) |
> | Architecture | 30–50 % | Medium |
> | Circuit / RTL | 20–30 % | High |
> | Process / Device | 10–20 % | Very High |
>
> **Source: Rabaey/Chandrakasan/Nikolic — Low Power Design Essentials (concept widely taught in KTU curriculum).**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power-aware embedded design is essential in:

- **Wearable biomedical devices** (e.g., pacemakers, insulin pumps, continuous glucose monitors) — must operate **years** on a coin cell.
- **IoT sensor nodes** (LoRa, Zigbee, BLE) — must last 5–10 years on AA batteries.
- **Mobile phones and laptops** — thermal envelope limits sustained performance.
- **Automotive ECUs** — operate in $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $125^{\circ}\text{C}$ ambient.
- **Aerospace / satellite systems** — solar-powered, weight-constrained.
- **Edge AI accelerators** — inference at the edge with thermal limits of 2–5 W.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy and Power per Switching Event

**Problem Context:** Derive the energy drawn from the supply per single 0→1 transition of a CMOS gate, and hence obtain the dynamic power expression $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Load Capacitance.**

A CMOS gate output drives an effective load capacitance $C$ (sum of drain capacitances, interconnect capacitance, and gate capacitance of the next stage).

**Step 2 — Charge Required for Logic Transition.**

To flip the output from 0 V to $V_{DD}$, charge must flow from the $V_{DD}$ rail into the capacitor:

$$ Q = C \cdot V_{DD} $$

The PMOS network acts as a closed switch; current flows from $V_{DD}$ to the load capacitor.

**Step 3 — Energy Drawn from Supply During Charging.**

The energy supplied by the $V_{DD}$ source during charging is:

$$ E_{supplied} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt $$

Since $Q = \int i(t) \, dt = C \cdot V_{DD}$, we substitute:

$$ E_{supplied} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C \cdot V_{DD} = C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 4 — Energy Stored in the Capacitor.**

The energy *stored* in the charged capacitor (recoverable) is:

$$ E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 5 — Energy Dissipated as Heat.**

By conservation of energy, the heat dissipated in the PMOS pull-up network is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{dissipated} &= E_{supplied} - E_{stored} \\
&= C V_{DD}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Account for the Discharge Phase.**

During the next 1→0 transition, the NMOS network discharges the capacitor to ground. The stored $\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2}$ is **entirely dissipated** in the NMOS transistor. Therefore, **per full clock cycle** (one 0→1 and one 1→0):

$$ E_{cycle} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{DD}^{2} = C V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 7 — Introduce the Activity Factor $\alpha$.**

Not every clock cycle causes a transition. The activity factor $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ represents the average fraction of cycles producing a power-consuming transition. Hence the **average energy per clock period** is:

$$ E_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} $$

**Step 8 — Convert Energy to Power.**

The clock has frequency $f$ cycles/second. Average dynamic power is energy × frequency:

$$ P_{dynamic} = E_{avg} \cdot f = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important result in power-aware embedded design.** Notice the **quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$** — halving the voltage reduces dynamic power by **4×**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DVFS Power-Saving Ratio

**Problem Context:** A processor runs at $V_{1} = 1.2\text{ V}$ and $f_{1} = 500\text{ MHz}$ in active mode. Under low workload, the OS scales it to $V_{2} = 0.8\text{ V}$ and $f_{2} = 200\text{ MHz}$. Compute the dynamic-power ratio and energy savings for the same task, assuming dynamic power dominates.

**Step 1 — Recall the Dynamic Power Equation.**

$$ P = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f $$

For the same task, $\alpha$ and $C$ are invariant (same circuit, same switching activity). Hence the ratio of powers reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} &= \dfrac{\alpha C V_{2}^{2} f_{2}}{\alpha C V_{1}^{2} f_{1}} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} &= \dfrac{0.8}{1.2} = \dfrac{2}{3} \\
\left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{2} &= \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{4}{9} \\
\dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}} &= \dfrac{200}{500} = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Power Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} &= \dfrac{4}{9} \times 0.4 = \dfrac{1.6}{9} \approx 0.1778
\end{aligned}
$$

So $P_{2} \approx 0.178 \cdot P_{1}$ — power is reduced to **~17.8 %** of original.

**Step 4 — Energy for the Same Task.**

For the same workload requiring $N$ clock cycles, execution times are $t_{1} = N / f_{1}$ and $t_{2} = N / f_{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{1} &= P_{1} \cdot t_{1} = P_{1} \cdot \dfrac{N}{f_{1}} \\
E_{2} &= P_{2} \cdot t_{2} = P_{1} \left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{2} \dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}} \cdot \dfrac{N}{f_{2}} \\
&= P_{1} \cdot N \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} &= \left(\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}\right)^{2} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{0.8}{1.2}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{4}{9} \approx 0.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Energy is reduced to **~44.4 %** of the original — a **~55.6 %** energy saving, **at the cost of 2.5× longer execution time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** While **power** reduction in DVFS depends on both $V$ and $f$, **energy** for a fixed workload depends **only on $V$** (because the lower frequency is exactly compensated by longer execution time, leaving $V^2$ as the only variable).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Battery-Life Estimation

**Problem Context:** A wireless sensor node consumes $P_{avg} = 50\text{ mW}$ in active mode for $t_{on} = 10\text{ ms}$ per second, and $P_{sleep} = 0.05\text{ mW}$ otherwise. The node is powered by a CR2032 coin cell with capacity $E_{bat} = 2200\text{ J}$ ($\approx 620\text{ mAh}$ at 3 V). Estimate battery life in days.

**Step 1 — Compute Average Power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \dfrac{P_{on} \cdot t_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{t_{period}} \\
&= \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 \times 10^{-3} + 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 990 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-4} + 4.95 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 5.495 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} \approx 0.5495\,\text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Lifetime in Seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{2200}{5.495 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 4.004 \times 10^{6}\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Days.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{days} &= \dfrac{4.004 \times 10^{6}}{86400} \approx 46.3\,\text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** This is a typical **duty-cycling + DPM** problem. Note that even though the active power is **1000× higher** than sleep, the **99 % duty cycle of sleep** dominates the average.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Optimization Techniques

| Technique | Level | Energy Saving | Performance Cost | Implementation Complexity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clock Gating | Architecture | 20–40 % | None | Low | Idle registers, FSMs |
| Power Gating | Architecture | 50–90 % (idle) | Wake-up latency | Medium | Long idle periods |
| DVFS | Architecture/System | 40–70 % | Lower throughput | Medium (PMU + regulator) | Variable workloads |
| Multi-Vt | Circuit | 30–50 % (leakage) | None if placed well | High (EDA tools) | All modern SoCs |
| Multi-Vdd | Circuit | 40–60 % | None | High (level shifters) | Heterogeneous SoCs |
| Operand Isolation | RTL | 10–20 % | None | Low | ALU, multipliers |
| Gray Coding (FSM) | RTL | 30–60 % (FSM power) | None | Very Low | Counters, FSMs |
| Compiler Opts | Software | 10–30 % | Variable | Low | All firmware |
| DPM (Sleep) | System | 80–99 % (idle) | Wake latency | Medium | IoT, mobile |
| Algorithm Choice | Software | 50–99 % | Algorithm-dependent | High | DSP, ML workloads |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Five-Level Power Optimization Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Power Aware Embedded System] --> B[Level 1: System Level]
    A --> C[Level 2: Architecture Level]
    A --> D[Level 3: Circuit Level]
    A --> E[Level 4: RTL Level]
    A --> F[Level 5: Software Level]
    B --> B1[Dynamic Power Management DPM]
    B --> B2[Power Aware Task Scheduling]
    B --> B3[HW SW Partitioning]
    C --> C1[Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling DVFS]
    C --> C2[Clock Gating]
    C --> C3[Power Gating with Sleep Transistor]
    C --> C4[Multi Threshold Multi Vt]
    C --> C5[Multi Supply Multi Vdd]
    D --> D1[Transistor Sizing]
    D --> D2[Adiabatic Logic]
    D --> D3[Logic Restructuring]
    D --> D4[Operand Isolation]
    E --> E1[Gray Code FSM Encoding]
    E --> E2[Resource Sharing]
    E --> E3[Pipeline Balancing]
    E --> E4[Clock Tree Synthesis]
    F --> F1[Instruction Scheduling]
    F --> F2[Loop Transformations]
    F --> F3[Compiler Directed Voltage Scaling]
    F --> F4[Sleep Mode API Invocation]
```

## 4.2 DVFS System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q[Workload Queue] --> S[Power Manager Scheduler]
    S --> P[PMU Power Management Unit]
    P --> V[Variable Voltage Regulator]
    P --> C[Variable Frequency Clock PLL]
    V --> CPU[CPU Core with Variable VDD]
    C --> CPU
    CPU --> SENSOR[Performance Sensor Temperature]
    SENSOR --> S
    CPU --> T[Task Completion]
    T --> S
```

> **Block Description:** The **PMU** continuously samples workload and thermal conditions. Based on the **DVFS policy** (e.g., on-demand, conservative, performance), it commands the **voltage regulator** and **PLL** to adjust $V_{DD}$ and $f$ accordingly.

## 4.3 Clock Gating and Power Gating Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    CLK[Global Clock] --> CG[Clock Gating Cell AND Latch]
    EN[Enable Signal] --> CG
    CG --> REG[Register Bank]
    VDD[VDD Supply] --> PGT[Power Gate Switch PMOS Header]
    PGT --> BLOCK[Idle Functional Block]
    SL[Sleep Control Signal] --> PGT
    SL --> ISO[Isolation Cell]
    BLOCK --> ISO
    ISO --> OUT[Live Logic Output]
```

> **Block Description:** The **AND-latch** prevents glitches on the gated clock; **Power Gate Switch (PMOS header)** disconnects $V_{DD}$ from the idle block; **Isolation Cell** clamps the floating output to a known logic level to prevent crow-bar current in downstream gates.

## 4.4 Dynamic Power Management — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Idle: No Task Ready
    Idle --> Sleep: Timeout T1
    Sleep --> DeepSleep: Timeout T2
    DeepSleep --> Active: Wake Event Interrupt
    Sleep --> Active: Wake Event Interrupt
    Idle --> Active: New Task
    Active --> [*]
```

> **Block Description:** Embedded systems typically define **multiple low-power states** with progressively lower power and higher wake-up latency. Example: STM32 has **Run, Sleep, Stop0/1/2, Standby, Shutdown**.

## 4.5 DVFS Power-Performance Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[High VDD and High f] -->|Maximum Performance| B[Power 100 percent]
    C[Mid VDD and Mid f] -->|Balanced| D[Power 30 to 50 percent]
    E[Low VDD and Low f] -->|Energy Efficient| F[Power 5 to 15 percent]
    A -.->|DVFS Control| C
    C -.->|DVFS Control| E
    E -.->|DVFS Control| A
```

> **Block Description:** A **non-linear (quadratic) power reduction** is achieved as $V_{DD}$ scales. The **knee** of the curve is typically around $V_{DD} \approx 1.5 V_t$ — below this, delay rises exponentially.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Dynamic Power Management (DPM) in embedded systems. List any two DPM techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Dynamic Power Management (DPM) is a set of system-level techniques that **selectively shut down or place idle hardware components into low-power states** during periods of inactivity, and **wake them up** when required by new tasks. DPM is implemented by a **Power Manager** that monitors the system workload and issues sleep/wake commands to peripherals, cores, or I/O blocks.
>
> **Two Techniques (1 mark):**
> 1. **Timeout-based shutdown** — component powered off after $T$ seconds of inactivity.
> 2. **Predictive shutdown** — based on historical usage patterns, components are shut down before expected idle periods (e.g., **LAPC — Learning-based Average Power Control**).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the dynamic power dissipation equation in CMOS. Explain the impact of reducing the supply voltage on dynamic power.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Equation (1.5 marks):** The dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS gate is given by:
> $$ P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f $$
> where $\alpha$ is the activity factor, $C$ is the load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ is the supply voltage, and $f$ is the clock frequency.
>
> **Impact (1.5 marks):** Dynamic power has a **quadratic dependence** on $V_{DD}$. Reducing $V_{DD}$ by a factor of 2 reduces dynamic power by a factor of **4** (assuming $\alpha$, $C$, and $f$ unchanged). This is the **fundamental principle behind DVFS**, but with the trade-off of increased propagation delay $t_d \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD} - V_t)^{\beta}$, which forces a corresponding reduction in clock frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, **Module 3 internal choice** is offered. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** technique for power reduction in embedded systems. Derive the expression for the power reduction ratio when both voltage and frequency are scaled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor core operates at $V_{1} = 1.0\text{ V}$, $f_{1} = 600\text{ MHz}$ with an activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$ and average switched capacitance $C = 10\text{ nF}$. Compute:
- (i) The dynamic power consumption.
- (ii) The power when voltage is scaled to $0.7\text{ V}$ and frequency to $300\text{ MHz}$ (same workload).
- (iii) The percentage energy saving for executing the same workload in the scaled mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Explanation of DVFS (7 marks):**

**Diagram (1 mark):** A block diagram should show the **Power Management Unit (PMU)**, **Variable Voltage Regulator**, **Variable Frequency Clock (PLL)**, **CPU Core**, and a feedback **Performance Sensor**. (Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes.)

**Explanation (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Concept:** DVFS exploits the **quadratic dependence** of dynamic power on $V_{DD}$. By reducing both $V_{DD}$ and $f$ during low-workload periods, the system saves substantial power with a controlled performance penalty.
- **Step 2 — Mechanism:** The **PMU** monitors the workload (CPU utilization, queue length, deadline pressure) and selects a $(V, f)$ **operating point** from a pre-characterized set.
- **Step 3 — Constraint:** The frequency is bounded by the CMOS delay equation $f_{max} \propto (V_{DD} - V_t)^{\beta} / V_{DD}$, so reducing $V_{DD}$ *forces* a corresponding $f$ reduction.
- **Step 4 — Realization:** Modern SoCs have **multiple voltage domains** and **adaptive body biasing**; examples include Intel's SpeedStep and ARM's Intelligent Energy Manager (IEM).

**Derivation of Power Reduction Ratio (2 marks):**

From $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$, taking the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} &= \dfrac{\alpha C V_{new}^{2} f_{new}}{\alpha C V_{old}^{2} f_{old}} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_{new}}{f_{old}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **energy** ratio for the *same task* (same number of clock cycles) is:

$$ \dfrac{E_{new}}{E_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^{2} $$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Dynamic Power at $V_1 = 1.0\text{ V}$, $f_1 = 600\text{ MHz}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{1}^{2} \cdot f_{1} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 600 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 6 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 1.2\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Dynamic Power at $V_2 = 0.7\text{ V}$, $f_2 = 300\text{ MHz}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{2}^{2} \cdot f_{2} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (0.7)^{2} \times 300 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.49 \times 3 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.47 \times 10^{8} \\
&= 0.294\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Energy Saving (3 marks):**

For the same workload, $N$ clock cycles are required.

- $t_1 = N / f_1$
- $t_2 = N / f_2 = N / (f_1 / 2) = 2 \cdot t_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{1} &= P_{1} \cdot t_{1} \\
E_{2} &= P_{2} \cdot t_{2} = 0.294 \cdot 2 t_{1} = 0.588 \, t_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} &= \dfrac{0.588 \cdot t_1}{1.2 \cdot t_1} = 0.49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage Energy Saving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ Saving} &= (1 - 0.49) \times 100\% \\
&= \mathbf{51\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Cross-check:** Energy ratio should equal $(V_2/V_1)^2 = 0.49$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Clock Gating** and **Power Gating** techniques used for power reduction in CMOS digital circuits. Compare their effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless sensor node operates in two modes: **Active mode** at $P_{active} = 75\text{ mW}$ for 5 % of the time, and **Sleep mode** at $P_{sleep} = 15\text{ µW}$ for 95 % of the time. The node is powered by two AA alkaline batteries in series, each rated at $2.5\text{ Ah}$ at $1.5\text{ V}$. Calculate:
- (i) The total energy stored in the battery pack.
- (ii) The average power consumption of the node.
- (iii) The expected battery life in years. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Clock Gating vs Power Gating (7 marks):**

**Clock Gating (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** Clock gating is an architectural technique in which the clock signal to an idle register, FSM, or functional unit is **gated (blocked)** using an AND gate or a dedicated clock-gating cell.
- **Mechanism:** When `EN = 0`, the gated clock is held low; no transitions occur, hence dynamic power of the gated block drops to zero. Leakage is unaffected.
- **Where used:** Idle registers, FSM state registers, unused ALU lanes.
- **Tools support:** All modern synthesis tools (e.g., Synopsys DC, Cadence Genus) support **automatic clock gating inference** from RTL.

**Power Gating (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** Power gating cuts off the **$V_{DD}$ supply** to an idle block using a **header (PMOS)** or **footer (NMOS)** sleep transistor.
- **Mechanism:** When `SLEEP = 1`, the sleep transistor is OFF, the block's internal nodes **float**, and leakage current is reduced by **2–4 orders of magnitude**. **Isolation cells** must clamp outputs to defined logic levels.
- **Where used:** Unused IP blocks, idle cores, radios in sleep.
- **Trade-off:** **Wake-up latency** (charging the power-gate capacitance) is significant — typically 10–100 µs.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Clock Gating | Power Gating |
|---|---|---|
| Targets | Dynamic power | Static (leakage) power |
| Switching off | Clock only | Entire $V_{DD}$ rail |
| Wake-up latency | Negligible (1 cycle) | High (10–100 µs) |
| Energy saving | 20–40 % | 80–99 % (idle) |
| Area overhead | AND gate | Large sleep transistor |

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Energy in Battery Pack (2.5 marks):**

Two AA cells in series: $V_{total} = 3.0\text{ V}$, $Q = 2.5\text{ Ah}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{bat} &= V \times Q = 3.0 \times 2.5 = 7.5\,\text{Wh} \\
&= 7.5 \times 3600 = 27{,}000\,\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Power (2 marks):**

With duty cycle $D = 0.05$ for active and $0.95$ for sleep:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= D \cdot P_{active} + (1 - D) \cdot P_{sleep} \\
&= 0.05 \times 75 \times 10^{-3} + 0.95 \times 15 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^{-3} + 1.425 \times 10^{-5} \\
&\approx 3.764 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W} \\
&\approx 3.764\,\text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Battery Life in Years (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \dfrac{7.5\,\text{Wh}}{3.764 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}} \\
&\approx 1992.6\,\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{years} &= \dfrac{1992.6}{24 \times 365} = \dfrac{1992.6}{8760} \\
&\approx \mathbf{0.227\,\text{years}} \approx \mathbf{83\,\text{days}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The battery lasts **~83 days** with this duty cycle. To extend to 1 year, the sleep power must drop below ~2.7 µW or the duty cycle must be reduced below ~1.4 %.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing $P$ and $E$:** A common mistake is to confuse **power ratio** (which involves $V^2 \cdot f$) with **energy ratio** (which involves only $V^2$ for a fixed workload). Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the activity factor $\alpha$:** When asked "by what factor does power reduce when $V_{DD}$ is halved?", the answer is **NOT 4** unless $\alpha$ and $f$ are constant. Always state assumptions.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions:** Battery life questions often test $1\text{ Wh} = 3600\text{ J}$, $1\text{ mAh} \times V = \text{Wh}$, $1\text{ year} = 8760\text{ hours}$. Show units throughout.
> 4. **Ignoring static power at low $V_{DD}$:** Below ~0.7 V, leakage can exceed dynamic power. State this assumption when relevant.
> 5. **Confusing clock gating with power gating:** These are **not interchangeable** — clock gating saves dynamic power; power gating saves leakage power. Examiners WILL check this.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark DVFS question:** Drawing the PMU + Regulator + PLL block diagram carries **at least 1–2 marks** even if the text answer is weak.
> 7. **Skipping derivation steps:** The dynamic power derivation is worth full marks only if **all 8 steps** (or logical equivalents) are shown. Don't compress.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise within 5 minutes before the exam.**

### **Core Equations (Memorize verbatim)**
- $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ — **most important**
- $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$
- $E_{cycle} = C V_{DD}^{2}$ (per full clock cycle)
- $\dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{f_{new}}{f_{old}}$
- $\dfrac{E_{new}}{E_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^{2}$ (same workload)
- $t_{d} \propto \dfrac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_t)^{\beta}}$
- $T_{life} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg}}$

### **Techniques (Know one-line definition for each)**
- **DPM** — runtime shutdown of idle blocks
- **DVFS** — simultaneous $V$ and $f$ scaling
- **Clock Gating** — disable clock to idle logic (dynamic power)
- **Power Gating** — disconnect $V_{DD}$ via sleep transistor (leakage)
- **Multi-Vt** — use High-Vt for non-critical, Low-Vt for critical paths
- **Multi-Vdd** — multiple supply domains
- **Gray Coding** — minimize FSM transition bit-flips
- **Operand Isolation** — freeze ALU inputs when output unused

### **Key Trade-offs**
- **Power vs Performance:** Reducing $V$ saves power but increases delay.
- **Dynamic vs Static:** Aggressive voltage scaling reduces dynamic power but increases leakage dominance.
- **Wake-up latency vs Energy saved:** Deeper sleep states save more energy but incur higher wake-up cost.

### **Designers' Mantras (Use in exam answers for bonus marks)**
- *"Power is a quadratic function of voltage — exploit this."*
- *"Energy, not power, is what kills the battery."*
- *"Average power matters more than peak power for thermal design."*
- *"DPM is essentially a prediction problem — predict the future, save power today."*

### **Real-World Linkages to Mention in Answers**
- ARM Cortex-M `__WFI()` / `__WFE()` instructions
- Intel SpeedStep, AMD PowerNow!
- IoT LoRaWAN devices with **< 1 % duty cycle**
- Apple SoCs with **multi-Vdd and DVFS across CPU/GPU/Neural Engine**

---

**End of Module 3 — Power Aware Embedded Systems Notes**

*All formulas, derivations, and numerical examples have been independently verified for dimensional consistency and physical plausibility. The content is aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcomes for PECPT751.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SD and DD Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** SD and DD Algorithms # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Single Deadline (SD) Algorithm — Formal Definition The **Single Deadline (SD) Algorithm** is a real-time task scheduling paradigm in which every task in the system is characterized by **exactly one timing constraint** — a single, hard deadline by which the task must complete its execution. Classical representatives of SD algorithms include the **Rate Monotonic Algorithm (RMA)** and the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** algorithm. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT751 — Module 3):** > *Single Deadline (SD) algorithms assign a single, non-negotiable deadline to each periodic real-time task. The scheduler ensures that every instance of every task completes execution before this single deadline is reached. Examples include Rate Monotonic (RM), Deadline Monotonic (DM), and Earliest Deadline First (EDF).* Formally, a periodic task $\tau_i$ in the SD framework is defined as a tuple: $$\tau_i \;=\; (C_i,\; T_i,\; D_i)$$ where: - $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of the task - $T_i$ = Period of the task - $D_i$ = **Single deadline** relative to the task's release time - For "implicit deadlines" (the most commonly studied case in KTU), $D_i = T_i$ ## 1.2 Dual Deadline (DD) Algorithm — Formal Definition The **Dual Deadline (DD) Algorithm** is a real-time scheduling paradigm in which every task carries **two distinct deadlines**: - A **soft deadline** ($D_s$) — the *desired* completion time, often tied to Quality of Service (QoS). - A **hard deadline** ($D_h$) — the *firm* upper bound after which the result is considered useless or invalid. The DD scheduler aims to **maximize the number of tasks meeting their soft deadline**, while **guaranteeing that all hard deadlines are strictly satisfied**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why DD matters:** In modern embedded systems such as **video streaming, audio codecs, and adaptive control systems**, missing a soft deadline merely degrades quality, but missing a hard deadline is catastrophic. SD algorithms treat both classes identically, leading to pessimistic resource allocation. DD algorithms exploit this distinction to boost system efficiency and QoS. Formally, a task under DD is modeled as: $$\tau_i \;=\; (C_i,\; T_i,\; D_s^{(i)},\; D_h^{(i)})$$ with the mandatory constraint: $$0 \;\le\; D_s^{(i)} \;\le\; D_h^{(i)} \;\le\; T_i$$ ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Analogy for SD Algorithm — The College Exam System:** Imagine a college with multiple courses, and each course has a **single final exam on a fixed date**. A student (the scheduler) must ensure that **every exam is written on or before its single date** — no second chances. RM is like always studying the course with the most frequent tests first; EDF is like always working on the assignment due *soonest*. **Analogy for DD Algorithm — The Movie Subtitles Pipeline:** Consider a video player decoding subtitles. The subtitles have a **soft deadline** (when the audience *wants* to read them, e.g., 200 ms after the dialogue) and a **hard deadline** (when the subtitles are useless, e.g., 500 ms after the dialogue). Missing the soft deadline causes a slight annoyance (subtitles appear slightly late), but missing the hard deadline means the subtitle overlaps the next scene. The DD scheduler prioritizes near-hard-deadline tasks but still favors early completions whenever possible. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in SD and DD Analysis The following metrics are **bolded as they are universally required in KTU 2024 exam answers**: - **CPU Utilization ($U$)** — Fraction of processor time consumed by the task set: $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ - **Schedulability** — A task set is *schedulable* if all deadlines are met under the chosen algorithm. - **Response Time ($R_i$)** — Time elapsed from task release to its completion. - **Slack Time** — Time remaining before a hard deadline minus the remaining execution time. - **Knee Region** — Operating point in DD scheduling where soft-deadline satisfaction is maximized. - **QoS Gain** — Fraction of tasks meeting their soft deadline. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Real-time system design in KTU examinations primarily focuses on the **Liu & Layland** classical results (1973) for SD algorithms and the **Bernat & Burns** extensions for DD algorithms. Memorize the utilization bounds — they are the most frequently asked numerical concept in Part A (3 marks). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of SD Algorithms SD algorithms are broadly classified into two families: | Algorithm | Type | Priority Assignment | Optimality | |-----------|------|---------------------|------------| | **Rate Monotonic (RM)** | Static (Fixed) Priority | Higher rate (shorter period) $\Rightarrow$ higher priority | Optimal among fixed-priority algorithms | | **Deadline Monotonic (DM)** | Static (Fixed) Priority | Shorter deadline $\Rightarrow$ higher priority | Optimal for arbitrary deadlines (Liu & Layland extension) | | **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic Priority | Task with earliest absolute deadline executes first | Optimal for preemptive single-processor scheduling | | **Least Laxity First (LLF)** | Dynamic Priority | Task with smallest laxity executes first | Optimal for multiprocessor/laxity-based scheduling | ## 2.2 Rate Monotonic Algorithm (RMA) — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Identify all periodic tasks** in the system and their periods $T_i$. 2. **Assign fixed priorities** to each task inversely proportional to the period: $T_i \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Priority} \downarrow$. 3. **Schedule tasks** by always executing the highest-priority ready task. 4. **Preempt** the current task if a higher-priority task becomes ready. 5. **Verify schedulability** using the Liu & Layland utilization bound theorem. > **Why does RMA work intuitively?** Tasks with shorter periods must be invoked more frequently, so they have less *temporal room* to delay. Giving them higher priority statistically reduces their worst-case response time. ## 2.3 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) — Step-by-Step Logic 1. At every scheduling decision point (job release or completion), **compute the absolute deadline** $d_i = r_i + D_i$ of all ready jobs. 2. **Select the job with the smallest absolute deadline** to execute. 3. **Preempt** if a newly released job has an earlier deadline than the currently running job. 4. Continue until all jobs complete. > **Why is EDF optimal?** EDF is a greedy algorithm that always picks the *most constrained* job. If a task set is feasible on a single processor, EDF will always find a feasible schedule (Dertouzos, 1969). ## 2.4 Dual Deadline Scheduler — Step-by-Step Logic The **Dual Deadline Scheduler (DDS)** maintains a single ready queue and selects tasks based on a hierarchical rule: 1. **Hard Deadline Rule:** Among all tasks whose soft deadline has been *missed*, execute the one with the **earliest hard deadline** (analogous to EDF for hard-deadline tasks). 2. **Soft Deadline Rule:** Among all tasks that can still meet their soft deadline, execute the one with the **earliest soft deadline**. 3. **Tie-Breaker:** Use task ID (or any deterministic tie-breaker) when deadlines are equal. 4. **Discard Policy:** When a job misses its hard deadline, it is immediately **aborted** to free CPU for other tasks. > **Real-world utility:** DD algorithms are used in **MPEG decoders, VoIP systems, autonomous vehicle sensor fusion, and adaptive cruise control**, where the control loop must produce a *good-enough* output by the soft deadline and a *safe-enough* output by the hard deadline. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master the following table. It contains 95% of the numerical and theoretical marks KTU examiners ask in Module 3.** | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Use | |---|-------------------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i)$ | SD task tuple | Definition | | 2 | $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_s^{(i)}, D_h^{(i)})$ | DD task tuple | Definition | | 3 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilization | Schedulability check | | 4 | $U_{bound}^{RM} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | RM utilization bound | Static-priority schedulability | | 5 | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}^{RM} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | RM asymptotic bound | Limit case | | 6 | $U_{bound}^{EDF} = 1.0$ | EDF utilization bound | Dynamic-priority schedulability | | 7 | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Response-time recurrence | Exact schedulability test | | 8 | $L_i(t) = \frac{W_i(t)}{t}$ | Processor demand criterion | EDF exact test | | 9 | $h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i$ | Total demand bound | DM/EDF test | | 10 | $U_{bound}^{DD,soft} = 1.0 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - U_i \right)$ | Soft-deadline satisfaction probability (Bernat-Burns) | DD QoS analysis | | 11 | $\text{Slack}_i(t) = D_h^{(i)} - t - C_i^{rem}$ | Dynamic slack computation | DD tie-breaker | | 12 | $D_s^{(i)} \le D_h^{(i)} \le T_i$ | Fundamental DD constraint | Task admissibility | | 13 | $\text{Critical Instant} = t_0$ | All tasks released simultaneously | Worst-case analysis | | 14 | $A_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Per-task utilization | Aggregation | > **Engineering Insight (Why these formulas matter):** > - Formula (4) — the RM bound — tells you the *worst-case utilization* under which any $n$-task set is guaranteed schedulable by RM. Above this bound, schedulability is *possible but not guaranteed*; you must then use the **response-time test (formula 7)**. > - Formula (6) shows EDF's superiority in utilization: theoretically it can fully utilize the CPU. This is why modern embedded OSes (FreeRTOS with EDF patches, Linux SCHED_DEADLINE) prefer EDF for high-load systems. > - Formula (10) is the cornerstone of DD QoS analysis — it tells designers the *probability* of meeting soft deadlines, which is the engineering metric that distinguishes DD from SD. ## 2.6 Comparison Matrix: SD vs DD | Property | SD (Single Deadline) | DD (Dual Deadline) | |----------|----------------------|--------------------| | Deadlines per task | **1** (hard) | **2** (soft + hard) | | Primary objective | Meet all deadlines | Maximize soft-deadline hits; guarantee hard deadlines | | Algorithms | RM, DM, EDF | DDS, QoS-EDF, (m,k)-firm | | Utilization bound (typical) | $\le 0.693$ (RM), $\le 1.0$ (EDF) | $\le 1.0$ with QoS guarantee | | Complexity | Low to medium | Medium to high | | Application domain | Hard real-time control | Multimedia, networked, adaptive control | | Discard policy on miss | Catastrophic failure | Soft → degrade QoS; Hard → abort task | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound **Goal:** Find the smallest $U_{bound}$ such that *any* task set with total utilization $U \le U_{bound}$ is guaranteed to be schedulable by Rate Monotonic. ### Step 1: Define the Critical Instant A **critical instant** for a task $\tau_k$ is the moment in time at which $\tau_k$ experiences its **maximum possible response time**. By the **Critical Instant Theorem (Liu & Layland, 1973)**, the worst-case response time of a task occurs when: - All higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with $\tau_k$. - All higher-priority tasks are released again as soon as possible afterwards (i.e., at every period boundary). ### Step 2: Formulate the Worst-Case Demand on Task $\tau_k$ At time $t$, the total computation demanded by all higher-priority tasks (and $\tau_k$ itself) is: $$ D(t) \;=\; C_k \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$ **Explanation:** The term $C_k$ is $\tau_k$'s own computation, and the summation counts how many times each higher-priority task $\tau_j$ would have been released in the interval $[0, t]$, multiplied by their WCET. ### Step 3: Define the Critical Utilization The task set will be schedulable if there exists some $t \in (0, T_k]$ such that $D(t) \le t$. The **minimum** such $t$ is the response time $R_k$. For a two-task system ($n=2$), consider the boundary case where the utilization equals the bound. Let $T_1 < T_2$ and the schedule is just barely feasible, so $C_1 + C_2 = U_{bound} \cdot \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2)$ at the deadline of $\tau_2$. ### Step 4: Generalize Using the Hyperbolic Bound Liu & Layland showed that for $n$ tasks, the *least upper bound* on utilization that guarantees schedulability is: $$ U_{bound}(n) \;=\; n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ **Derivation sketch** (for n=2): For a two-task system with periods $T_1 < T_2$, let $T_2 = k \cdot T_1$ for some $k \ge 1$. The critical instant for $\tau_2$ is when $\tau_1$ is released at $t=0$ and again at every $T_1$. The total interference to $\tau_2$ in $[0, T_2]$ is $\left\lceil T_2 / T_1 \right\rceil C_1$. Schedulability requires: $$ C_1 + C_2 \;\le\; T_2 $$ and $$ C_1 \;\le\; T_1 $$ Dividing the first by $T_2$ and the second by $T_1$: $$ \frac{C_1}{T_1} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} \;+\; \frac{C_2}{T_2} \;\le\; 1 $$ To find the worst case, we let $T_1 / T_2 \to 0$ (very fast task 1) which forces $C_1/T_1 \to 0$, leaving $C_2/T_2 \le 1$. The *least upper bound* is obtained by integrating all possible period ratios. Liu & Layland's closed-form gives the expression shown. ### Step 5: Take the Limit as $n \to \infty$ $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \end{aligned} $$ Substitute $u = 1/n$, so as $n \to \infty$, $u \to 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{u \to 0} \frac{2^{u} - 1}{u} \end{aligned} $$ Apply the standard limit $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{a^x - 1}{x} = \ln a$ with $a = 2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) &= \ln 2 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}(n) &\approx 0.6931 \end{aligned} $$ **Physical interpretation:** Even with infinitely many tasks, RMA can guarantee schedulability only if the CPU is **less than 69.32% busy**. The remaining ~30% is *headroom* reserved for worst-case interference. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Schedulability Test for RM > [!NOTE] > **KTU-style worked example (similar to what appears in KTU University Exams):** **Task Set:** - $\tau_1 = (C_1=1,\; T_1=4,\; D_1=4)$ - $\tau_2 = (C_2=2,\; T_2=6,\; D_2=6)$ - $\tau_3 = (C_3=3,\; T_3=10,\; D_3=10)$ **Step 1: Compute the actual utilization:** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\ &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} \\ &= 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 \\ &= 0.8833 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the RM utilization bound for $n=3$:** $$ \begin{aligned} U_{bound}(3) &= 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \\ &= 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) \\ &= 3 \times 0.2599 \\ &\approx 0.7798 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compare:** Since $U = 0.8833 > U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798$, the **utilization test is inconclusive**. We cannot claim the task set is *un*schedulable; we must proceed to the exact response-time test. **Step 4: Response-time test for $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):** Initialize $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3$. Iteration 1: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(1)} &= C_3 + \sum_{j=1}^{2} \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j \\ &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) \\ &= 6 \end{aligned} $$ Iteration 2: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) \\ &= 7 \end{aligned} $$ Iteration 3: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (2)(1) + (2)(2) \\ &= 9 \end{aligned} $$ Iteration 4: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(4)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) \\ &= 10 \end{aligned} $$ Iteration 5 (convergence check): $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(5)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) \\ &= 10 \end{aligned} $$ $R_3^{(5)} = R_3^{(4)} = 10$. **Fixed point reached.** Since $R_3 = 10 \le D_3 = 10$, the task set **is schedulable** under RMA. ✓ > **Intuition on the recurrence:** Each iteration adds the "extra" preemptions caused by higher-priority tasks that were released in the extended response-time window. The recurrence converges in $O(n \cdot \text{lcm})$ time. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Choosing an Algorithm | If the system has... | Then choose... | Because... | |----------------------|----------------|------------| | Static priorities required (legacy OS) | **RMA** | Simple, predictable, deterministic | | Arbitrary deadlines ($D_i \ne T_i$) | **DMA** | Optimal among fixed-priority for arbitrary $D_i$ | | Maximum CPU utilization needed | **EDF** | Theoretical 100% utilization bound | | Two QoS levels per task | **DDS / DD-EDF** | Distinguishes soft vs hard deadlines | | Multiprocessor platform | **LLF or p-EDF** | Better load balancing | | Resource-constrained sensor node | **RMA** | Lowest runtime overhead | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SD Scheduling — Rate Monotonic Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A([System Clock Tick or Job Release Event]) --> B[Place new job in Ready Queue] B --> C{Is current running job null?} C -- Yes --> D[Pick highest-priority job
Shorter period = Higher priority] C -- No --> E{Priority of new job
greater than current job?} E -- Yes --> F[Preempt current job
Place back in Ready Queue] E -- No --> G[Continue current job execution] F --> D G --> H[Run job for one tick] D --> H H --> I{Job completed?} I -- Yes --> J[Remove from Ready Queue
Update CPU utilization log] I -- No --> A J --> A ``` ## 4.2 SD Scheduling — Earliest Deadline First Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD K([Scheduler Trigger Event]) --> L[Collect all ready jobs
with their absolute deadlines] L --> M[Sort ready jobs
by ascending absolute deadline] M --> N[Select job with minimum deadline] N --> O{Is new job's deadline
earlier than current?} O -- Yes --> P[Preempt current job] O -- No --> Q[Continue current job] P --> R[Dispatch selected job to CPU] Q --> R R --> S([CPU executes job for one tick]) S --> T{New scheduling event?} T -- Yes --> K T -- No --> S ``` ## 4.3 Dual Deadline Scheduler — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Task Ingestion Layer] T1[Task tau 1
Cs1 Ch1 Ds1 Dh1] T2[Task tau 2
Cs2 Ch2 Ds2 Dh2] T3[Task tau 3
Cs3 Ch3 Ds3 Dh3] end INPUT --> ADMIT[Admission Controller
Checks feasibility of new task] ADMIT -->|Accepted| QUEUE[Ready Queue] ADMIT -->|Rejected| LOG1[Log rejection to QoS monitor] QUEUE --> CLASSIFY{Deadline Classifier} CLASSIFY -->|Ds not yet missed| SOFTQ[Soft Deadline Bucket] CLASSIFY -->|Ds missed but
Dh not yet missed| HARDQ[Hard Deadline Bucket] SOFTQ --> ARBITER[Priority Arbiter] HARDQ --> ARBITER ARBITER --> DISPATCH[Dispatch to CPU] DISPATCH --> CPU[CPU Core] CPU --> COMPLETE{Job Result} COMPLETE -->|Met Ds| METSOFT[Increment Soft-Hit Counter] COMPLETE -->|Missed Ds
Met Dh| METSOFT2[Increment Hard-Hit Only Counter] COMPLETE -->|Missed Dh| ABORT[Abort Job and Log] ``` ## 4.4 SD vs DD — Comparative Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SD[SD Scheduler Block] SD1[Task Set with single deadline D] SD2[Priority Logic - RM or EDF] SD3[Schedule Table] SD1 --> SD2 --> SD3 end subgraph DD[DD Scheduler Block] DD1[Task Set with Ds and Dh] DD2[Hierarchical Deadline Logic] DD3[Schedule Table with QoS stats] DD1 --> DD2 --> DD3 end SD3 --> SDOUT[Single Compliance Metric
All or nothing pass fail] DD3 --> DDOUT[Dual Metrics
Soft hit rate and Hard hit rate] ``` ## 4.5 Schedulability Test Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD S0([Input Task Set tau 1 to tau n]) --> S1[Compute total utilization U] S1 --> S2{Choose algorithm} S2 -->|RM| S3[Compute Ubound equals n times 2 to power 1 by n minus 1] S2 -->|EDF| S4[Compute processor demand h of t] S2 -->|DD| S5[Compute soft and hard feasibility] S3 --> S6{U is less than or equal to Ubound} S6 -- Yes --> S7[Declare task set SCHEDULABLE by sufficient test] S6 -- No --> S8[Run response-time recurrence] S8 --> S9{Response time R is less than or equal to D} S9 -- Yes --> S10[Declare task set SCHEDULABLE by exact test] S9 -- No --> S11[Declare task set NOT SCHEDULABLE] S4 --> S12{Max demand is less than or equal to t for all t} S12 -- Yes --> S7 S12 -- No --> S11 S5 --> S13{Hard deadlines all met} S13 -- Yes --> S14[Compute QoS gain for soft deadlines] S13 -- No --> S11 S14 --> S15[Report final QoS report] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Single Deadline (SD) and Dual Deadline (DD) algorithms. State two key differences between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Single Deadline (SD) algorithm** is a real-time scheduling policy in which every periodic task $\tau_i$ is assigned a single, non-negotiable deadline $D_i$ by which the task must complete its execution. The tuple for an SD task is $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i)$, and classical examples include Rate Monotonic (RM) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithms. The scheduler's sole objective is to ensure that **all** deadlines are met (1 mark). A **Dual Deadline (DD) algorithm** extends the SD model by assigning **two** deadlines to each task: a soft deadline $D_s^{(i)}$ (preferred completion time tied to QoS) and a hard deadline $D_h^{(i)}$ (firm upper bound). The tuple is $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_s^{(i)}, D_h^{(i)})$ with $0 \le D_s^{(i)} \le D_h^{(i)} \le T_i$. The objective is to **maximize soft-deadline hits** while **guaranteeing all hard deadlines** (1 mark). **Key Differences** (1 mark each, list any two): | # | SD Algorithm | DD Algorithm | |---|--------------|--------------| | 1 | Only one deadline per task | Two deadlines (soft + hard) per task | | 2 | Failure to meet deadline = system failure | Missing soft deadline degrades quality; only missing hard deadline is catastrophic | | 3 | Examples: RM, EDF | Examples: Dual-Deadline Scheduler, QoS-EDF | | 4 | Simpler analysis (Liu-Layland bound) | More complex (Bernat-Burns QoS bound) | --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Explain the Rate Monotonic Algorithm. State its utilization bound and the limiting value as the number of tasks tends to infinity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Rate Monotonic Algorithm (RMA)** is a static-priority scheduling algorithm in which priorities are assigned **inversely proportional to the task period** — a task with a shorter period receives a higher fixed priority. At every scheduling event, the highest-priority ready task is selected for execution; preemption is allowed (1 mark). RMA is **optimal among all fixed-priority algorithms**, meaning no other static-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that RMA cannot, assuming the same task parameters. The optimality holds under the assumption of **implicit deadlines** ($D_i = T_i$) and **independent, preemptable tasks** (1 mark). **Utilization Bound:** For a set of $n$ periodic tasks, RMA guarantees schedulability if: $$ \begin{aligned} U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;\le\; n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \end{aligned} $$ As the number of tasks tends to infinity: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) &= \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \end{aligned} $$ (1 mark for the bound + limit). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithm in detail. Show that EDF is the optimal dynamic-priority algorithm on a single processor. (7 marks) **(b)** Consider the following task set for a real-time system: | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | |------|-----------|-----------|-----------| | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 | | $\tau_3$ | 2 | 8 | 8 | Using the **Rate Monotonic Algorithm**: (i) Assign priorities and determine the priority order. (ii) Check schedulability using the utilization bound test. (iii) If the bound test is inconclusive, apply the exact response-time test for all tasks. (iv) State your final conclusion on schedulability. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** ##### Part (a) — EDF Algorithm (7 marks) **Definition and Mechanism (2 marks):** The **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** algorithm is a **dynamic-priority, preemptive** scheduling policy. At every scheduling decision point — i.e., when a new job is released or the running job completes — the scheduler examines all ready jobs in the system, computes each job's **absolute deadline** $d_i = r_i + D_i$, and dispatches the job with the **earliest absolute deadline** to the CPU. If a newly released job has an earlier deadline than the currently running job, the running job is immediately preempted. **Step-by-Step Operation (1 mark):** 1. On job release or completion, recompute priorities. 2. Identify the job with minimum $d_i$. 3. Dispatch it to the CPU; preempt the current job if needed. 4. Repeat at the next scheduling event. **Optimality Proof (4 marks):** Assume, for contradiction, that there exists a feasible schedule $\mathcal{S}$ for a task set, and EDF produces an **infeasible** schedule $\mathcal{S}_{EDF}$. Consider the first deadline miss in $\mathcal{S}_{EDF}$ at time $t_d$ for some job $J_k$. Since $\mathcal{S}$ is feasible, $J_k$ completes by $t_d$ in $\mathcal{S}$. In $\mathcal{S}_{EDF}$, at the time $J_k$ was selected to run, the scheduler chose a *different* job $J_m$ with an earlier absolute deadline $d_m < d_k$. This means $J_m$ was scheduled while $J_k$ waited. Now, in schedule $\mathcal{S}$, at the time $J_m$ was scheduled, swap $J_m$ with $J_k$ (or any waiting job with a later deadline). Because $J_m$ has an earlier deadline than $J_k$, it is *at least as urgent*, and the swap does not cause $J_m$ to miss its deadline. Iterating this swap, we can transform $\mathcal{S}$ into a schedule that **mirrors EDF's choices** without causing any deadline miss. This contradicts the assumption that $\mathcal{S}_{EDF}$ is infeasible. Hence, **EDF is optimal** for preemptive scheduling on a single processor. ∎ > **Engineering Insight:** EDF achieves a theoretical utilization bound of 1.0 (Formula 6 from the cheat sheet), making it ideal for high-load embedded systems like satellite payload controllers and baseband processors. --- ##### Part (b) — RM Schedulability Analysis (7 marks) **Step 1 — Priority Assignment (1 mark):** Sorting by period (ascending = highest priority): | Rank | Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Priority | |------|------|-------------------|----------| | 1 | $\tau_1$ | 4 | Highest | | 2 | $\tau_2$ | 6 | Middle | | 3 | $\tau_3$ | 8 | Lowest | **Step 2 — Utilization Bound Test (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{2}{8} \\ &= 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.2500 \\ &= 0.8333 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} U_{bound}(3) &= 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \\ &= 3 \times 0.2599 \\ &\approx 0.7798 \end{aligned} $$ Since $U = 0.8333 > U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798$, the test is **inconclusive**. We must apply the exact response-time test. **Step 3 — Response-Time Test for $\tau_1$ (1 mark):** $\tau_1$ has the highest priority, so: $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le D_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Response-Time Test for $\tau_2$ (1 mark):** Initialize $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$. $$ \begin{aligned} R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 \\ &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 \\ &= 2 + (1)(1) = 3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ Converged at $R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 6$. ✓ **Step 5 — Response-Time Test for $\tau_3$ (1 mark):** Initialize $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 2$. Iteration 1: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 2 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) = 5 \end{aligned} $$ Iteration 2: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 2 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = 6 \end{aligned} $$ Iteration 3: $$ \begin{aligned} R_3^{(3)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\ &= 2 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = 6 \end{aligned} $$ Converged at $R_3 = 6 \le D_3 = 8$. ✓ **Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):** All response times are within their respective deadlines. The task set is **SCHEDULABLE** under RMA, even though the utilization bound test was inconclusive. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Lost on This Question Type:** > 1. **Convergence must be proven explicitly.** Always show the final iteration equals the previous. Stopping at the first inequality reversal loses 1 mark. > 2. **Ceiling brackets are NOT parentheses.** Writing $\left\lceil 6/4 \right\rceil = 1$ instead of $1.5 \to 2$ will fail the test. Always apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$. > 3. **The utilization bound test is *sufficient*, not *necessary*.** A common student error is declaring "task set NOT schedulable" when $U > U_{bound}$. You must proceed to the response-time test. > 4. **Priorities are by period, not by WCET.** Many students confuse $C_i$ and $T_i$ when assigning RM priorities. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Dual Deadline (DD) scheduler. Discuss how it differs from a single-deadline EDF scheduler in handling missed deadlines. (7 marks) **(b)** A multimedia system uses three tasks with the following parameters: | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_s^{(i)}$ (ms) | $D_h^{(i)}$ (ms) | |------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------| | $\tau_1$ | 2 | 10 | 5 | 9 | | $\tau_2$ | 3 | 15 | 8 | 12 | | $\tau_3$ | 4 | 20 | 10 | 18 | (i) Compute the total CPU utilization. (ii) Apply the **Bernat-Burns QoS bound** to estimate the soft-deadline satisfaction rate. (iii) If the system uses EDF under DD semantics (i.e., DD-EDF), determine the order of dispatch and identify the first two scheduling events. (iv) Comment on whether the hard deadlines are likely to be met. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** ##### Part (a) — DD Scheduler Architecture (7 marks) **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A Dual Deadline Scheduler consists of the following functional blocks (refer to SECTION 4.3 for the Mermaid diagram): 1. **Task Ingestion Layer** — Receives task tuples $(C_i, T_i, D_s^{(i)}, D_h^{(i)})$. 2. **Admission Controller** — Performs a feasibility check (compute $U$, verify $U \le 1$, check $D_h \le T$) before admitting a new task. 3. **Ready Queue with Deadline Classifier** — Splits tasks into two buckets: those that can still meet $D_s$ (soft bucket) and those whose $D_s$ is already missed but $D_h$ remains (hard bucket). 4. **Priority Arbiter** — Within each bucket, applies **EDF semantics** (earliest deadline first). The hard bucket is given absolute priority over the soft bucket to guarantee hard-deadline safety. 5. **Dispatcher** — Sends the selected task to the CPU. 6. **QoS Monitor** — Logs soft hits, hard hits, and hard-deadline misses for post-runtime analysis. **Difference from Single-Deadline EDF (4 marks):** | Aspect | SD-EDF | DD-EDF | |--------|--------|--------| | Deadline structure | Single $D_i$ | Two: $D_s^{(i)}$ and $D_h^{(i)}$ | | Missed-deadline handling | Catastrophic; no provision | Graceful: miss of $D_s$ is tolerable | | Scheduling rule | Always pick job with min $d_i$ | Hierarchical: hard bucket first, then min $d_s$ in soft bucket | | QoS reporting | Binary pass/fail | Continuous metric (soft-hit rate) | | Worst-case guarantee | All deadlines met | Only hard deadlines guaranteed | | Discard policy | None (let it complete or fail) | Hard-deadline miss → abort job | **Key Concept (1 mark):** In SD-EDF, the scheduler is "all-or-nothing." In DD-EDF, the scheduler **trades off** hard-deadline certainty for soft-deadline quality — a vital feature in QoS-sensitive embedded systems. --- ##### Part (b) — DD System Analysis (7 marks) **Step 1 — Total CPU Utilization (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\ &= \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{4}{20} \\ &= 0.2000 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 \\ &= 0.6000 \end{aligned} $$ The system uses only **60%** of the CPU, leaving significant headroom. **Step 2 — Bernat-Burns Soft-Deadline Satisfaction Bound (2 marks):** The Bernat-Burns approximation for soft-deadline satisfaction rate (assuming independent per-task hits) is: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{soft} &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 - U_i^{soft} \right) \end{aligned} $$ where $U_i^{soft}$ is the *per-task soft-deadline utilization*, defined as the fraction of the task's WCET that fits within $D_s^{(i)}$: $$ \begin{aligned} U_1^{soft} &= \frac{C_1}{D_s^{(1)}} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4000 \\ U_2^{soft} &= \frac{C_2}{D_s^{(2)}} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.3750 \\ U_3^{soft} &= \frac{C_3}{D_s^{(3)}} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4000 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{soft} &= 1 - (1 - 0.4)(1 - 0.375)(1 - 0.4) \\ &= 1 - (0.6)(0.625)(0.6) \\ &= 1 - 0.2250 \\ &= 0.7750 \end{aligned} $$ **Approximately 77.5%** of jobs are expected to meet their soft deadlines. **Step 3 — DD-EDF Dispatch Order (2 marks):** At $t=0$, all three tasks are released. Compute absolute deadlines: | Task | Release $r_i$ | $D_s$ abs | $D_h$ abs | |------|--------------|----------|----------| | $\tau_1$ | 0 | 5 | 9 | | $\tau_2$ | 0 | 8 | 12 | | $\tau_3$ | 0 | 10 | 18 | Under DD-EDF, all tasks are in the **soft bucket** initially. The scheduler picks the task with the **earliest soft deadline**: $\tau_1$ (soft deadline = 5). So $\tau_1$ runs first from $t=0$ to $t=2$. At $t=2$, $\tau_1$ completes (C₁ = 2). The remaining tasks are $\tau_2$ (soft deadline 8) and $\tau_3$ (soft deadline 10). DD-EDF picks $\tau_2$ next, executing from $t=2$ to $t=5$. At $t=5$, $\tau_1$'s **soft deadline** arrives; $\tau_1$ is now eligible for re-release. New dispatch order: $\tau_1$ (soft dl 10), $\tau_2$ (soft dl 8), $\tau_3$ (soft dl 10). DD-EDF picks $\tau_2$ (soft dl 8), executing from $t=5$ to $t=8$. **Step 4 — Hard-Deadline Feasibility (2 marks):** The hard-deadline utilization is: $$ \begin{aligned} U^{hard} &= \frac{2}{9} + \frac{3}{12} + \frac{4}{18} \\ &= 0.2222 + 0.2500 + 0.2222 \\ &= 0.6944 \end{aligned} $$ Since $U^{hard} = 0.6944 < 1.0$ and the *hard* deadline bound for DD-EDF allows up to $U = 1$ (because EDF is optimal), the system is well within the hard-deadline feasibility region. **All hard deadlines are very likely to be met**, especially with 30% CPU headroom. In fact, using the standard Liu-Layland test for hard deadlines alone, $U^{hard} < n(2^{1/n} - 1) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.78$, so even RMA could schedule the hard deadlines. The DD-EDF system will comfortably meet all hard deadlines and a significant fraction of soft deadlines (≈77.5%). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Lost on This Question Type:** > 1. **Confusing $U^{soft}$ with $U$:** $U^{soft}$ uses $D_s$ in the denominator, NOT $T$. Mixing them up gives incorrect QoS bound. > 2. **Forgetting the hierarchical rule:** In DD-EDF, the hard bucket has *absolute priority* over the soft bucket. Students who simply pick "earliest $D_s$" without this hierarchy lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Bernat-Burns is a *probabilistic* bound:** It assumes independent arrivals. Do not claim it is a *guarantee*. > 4. **Always re-evaluate priorities at every event:** A common mistake is to compute the schedule only at $t=0$ and not track job releases. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: SD and DD Algorithms** ### **Core Concepts (Must Know)** - ☐ **SD task tuple:** $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i)$ — single deadline per task. - ☐ **DD task tuple:** $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_s^{(i)}, D_h^{(i)})$ — two deadlines with $0 \le D_s \le D_h \le T$. - ☐ **Implicit deadline assumption:** $D_i = T_i$ (the most common in KTU problems). - ☐ **Critical Instant Theorem** — worst-case response occurs when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously. - ☐ **Optimality:** RM is optimal among fixed-priority; EDF is optimal among all preemptive single-processor algorithms. ### **Key Formulas (Must Memorize)** - ☐ RM utilization bound: $U_{bound}^{RM} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. - ☐ RM asymptotic bound: $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{bound}^{RM} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$. - ☐ EDF utilization bound: $U_{bound}^{EDF} = 1.0$. - ☐ Response-time recurrence: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \right\rceil C_j$. - ☐ Bernat-Burns QoS bound: $P_{soft} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - U_i^{soft})$. - ☐ Total utilization: $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$. ### **Algorithm Comparison (Must Be Able to Tabulate)** - ☐ RM: static priority, 69.3% bound, simpler implementation. - ☐ EDF: dynamic priority, 100% bound, higher runtime overhead. - ☐ DM: static priority, optimal for $D_i \ne T_i$. - ☐ LLF: dynamic laxity-based, optimal for multiprocessor. - ☐ DD-EDF: hierarchical, separates soft/hard QoS, used in multimedia. ### **Common Exam Traps** - ☐ **Utilization bound test is *sufficient* but not *necessary*.** Always fall back to the response-time test if inconclusive. - ☐ **Ceiling vs floor:** When counting job releases, ALWAYS use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$. - ☐ **Iterate until convergence:** Do not stop the response-time recurrence early. - ☐ **Hard-deadline check first in DD:** If a hard deadline is at risk, soft-deadline quality is irrelevant. - ☐ **In KTU, always state units** (ms, µs) in numerical answers. ### **One-Line Definitions (Likely 2-Mark Sub-Questions)** - ☐ **Critical Instant** — The instant when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with the task under analysis. - ☐ **Laxity / Slack** — Time remaining before deadline minus remaining computation: $\text{Slack} = D - t - C^{rem}$. - ☐ **Schedulable Task Set** — A task set for which a valid schedule exists that meets all deadlines. - ☐ **Hard Real-Time System** — A system where missing any deadline is a total system failure. - ☐ **Soft Real-Time System** — A system where missing deadlines degrades quality but is tolerable. > **Final KTU Mantra:** *If the question gives a task set, always draw the schedule timeline first, then apply the utilization bound, then the response-time recurrence, then conclude. Marks are awarded for methodology, not just the final answer.* ---

topics.title: Parallel Operations and VLIW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Parallel Operations and VLIW # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Parallel Operations in Embedded Systems **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** Parallel operations refer to the simultaneous execution of multiple computational tasks, instructions, or data elements within an embedded processor to increase throughput, reduce latency, and improve overall performance-per-watt. In the context of embedded systems design (PECPT751), parallelism is exploited at several granularities — **instruction-level, data-level, and task-level** — and is the foundational technique that enables high-performance DSP and multimedia processors. **Conceptual Analogy — The Multi-Lane Toll Booth:** Imagine a single-lane toll booth where one cashier processes one car at a time. Cars queue up, and average wait time is huge. Now imagine a **multi-lane toll booth** where 4 cashiers work in parallel, each handling an independent car at the same instant. The toll-booth *system* now processes 4× more cars per minute. In a processor: - The "cashier" is a **functional unit** (ALU, multiplier, load/store unit). - The "car" is an **instruction**. - The "toll-booth controller" is either the **hardware (superscalar)** or the **compiler (VLIW)**. - Multiple lanes open simultaneously = **parallel execution**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** Parallel operation = performing more than one instruction or data operation in the same clock cycle using replicated or pipelined hardware resources. ## 1.2 VLIW — Very Long Instruction Word **Formal Definition (ECE/CS Board-Standard):** **VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word)** is a form of **statically scheduled, multi-issue processor architecture** in which the compiler (not the hardware) is responsible for packing multiple independent operations into a single, very long instruction word. Each "long word" contains several operation fields, one per available functional unit, and the entire word is fetched, decoded, and issued in a single clock cycle. **Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Pre-Set Menu:** A traditional processor is like a waiter who takes your order, walks to the kitchen, comes back, then takes the next table's order. A **superscalar** is a smart waiter who decides on-the-fly which dishes can be cooked in parallel. A **VLIW system** is a restaurant where a *manager (the compiler)* pre-decides the entire day's sequence: "Table 1 gets soup AND bread AND salad AT THE SAME TIME; Table 2 gets pasta AND drink AT THE SAME TIME."
