At execution, the cook simply follows the preset schedule — no on-the-fly decisions, no scheduling hardware needed. > [!NOTE] > **VLIW Mantra:** *"Move the complexity from hardware (run-time) to the compiler (compile-time)."* ## 1.3 Key KTU Terminology You MUST Know | Term | Meaning | One-line Memory Hook | |------|---------|---------------------| | **ILP** | Instruction-Level Parallelism | How many independent instructions exist in a window | | **DLP** | Data-Level Parallelism | Same op on many data items (SIMD) | | **TLP** | Thread/Task-Level Parallelism | Independent tasks on different cores | | **Static Scheduling** | Scheduling done by compiler | Used in VLIW | | **Dynamic Scheduling** | Scheduling done by hardware | Used in superscalar | | **EPIC** | Explicitly Parallel Instruction Computing | Intel/HP label for VLIW philosophy | | **Multi-issue** | Issuing > 1 instruction per cycle | Prerequisite for VLIW | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics in This Module:** > - **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)** — ideal VLIW target = **N**, where N = number of functional units. > - **Clock frequency ($f_{clk}$)** is measured in **MHz/GHz**. > - **Speedup ($S$)** = $T_{serial} / T_{parallel}$ (Amdahl). > - **Code size inflation** for VLIW is typically **2× to 4×** over RISC. --- # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchy of Parallelism in Embedded Processors Parallelism in embedded processors (especially DSP and VLIW cores) is exploited in the following ordered layers: 1. **Bit-level parallelism** — widening the data path (8 → 16 → 32 bits). 2. **Instruction-level parallelism (ILP)** — multiple instructions per cycle (this is what VLIW exploits). 3. **Data-level parallelism (DLP)** — vector/SIMD operations on arrays. 4. **Task/thread-level parallelism (TLP)** — multiple independent threads or cores. > [!NOTE] > VLIW belongs to **ILP exploitation** combined with **static (compile-time) scheduling**. ## 2.2 Forms of Parallel Operations (Syllabus Focus) ### A. Pipelining A pipeline overlaps the execution of several instructions by splitting each instruction into stages (Fetch → Decode → Execute → Memory → Write-Back). Throughput increases, though latency per instruction remains. ### B. Superscalar Execution Hardware checks dependencies at run-time and issues multiple independent instructions per cycle from a conventional instruction stream. Complexity is in the hardware scheduler. ### C. VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) The compiler pre-schedules multiple operations into a single long instruction word. Hardware is simplified; compiler does the dependency analysis. ### D. SIMD / Vector Processing A single instruction operates on multiple data items simultaneously (e.g., 4-wide 16-bit MAC in a single cycle). Strong DLP, weak ILP. ### E. Multi-core / Multiprocessor Two or more independent CPU cores execute different threads. This is TLP, beyond the scope of single-core VLIW. ## 2.3 Anatomy of a VLIW Processor A VLIW architecture consists of the following sub-blocks: - **Instruction Memory** holding *long instruction words* (e.g., 256-bit or 512-bit wide). - **Fetch Unit** that brings one full long word per cycle. - **Decoder/Dispatch Unit** that splits the long word into N operation fields. - **N Parallel Functional Units** (ALU1, ALU2, MUL, LSU, BR — branch). - **Register File** (often multi-ported, with **N read ports and M write ports**). - **Interconnect / Crossbar** that routes operands from register file to functional units. > [!IMPORTANT] > The hallmark of VLIW: **The instruction word is split into N explicit operation slots, each containing opcode + destination + source1 + source2. The hardware does not need dependency-checking logic because the compiler guarantees independence.** ### Structural Example — A 4-issue VLIW A typical 4-issue VLIW long word layout (used in Texas Instruments TMS320C6x family): | Slot 0 (ALU1) | Slot 1 (ALU2) | Slot 2 (MUL) | Slot 3 (LSU/BR) | |:--------------|:--------------|:-------------|:----------------| | `ADD .L1 …` | `SUB .S1 …` | `MPY .M1 …` | `LDW .D1 …` | All four execute **in the same clock cycle**, on independent functional units, with no hardware arbitration. ## 2.4 VLIW vs Superscalar — The Core Distinction | Feature | VLIW | Superscalar | |---------|------|-------------| | Scheduling | **Compile-time (static)** | **Run-time (dynamic, hardware)** | | Instruction word | Long word = N operations | Conventional narrow instructions | | Dependency check | **In compiler** | **In hardware (scoreboard/ROB)** | | Hardware complexity | Low | High | | Power efficiency | High (no scheduler) | Lower | | Code size | Large (NOPs inserted) | Compact | | Compiler complexity | Very high | Moderate | | Binary compatibility | Poor (schedule baked in) | Good | | Example | TI C6x, Itanium | Intel Core, ARM Cortex-A | > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners **love** this comparison. Memorize the table — at least 7-mark questions are framed on it. ## 2.5 Advantages of VLIW 1. **Simpler hardware** → lower cost, lower power — ideal for embedded SoCs. 2. **Higher clock frequency possible** (less combinational logic per stage). 3. **Predictable timing** — crucial for hard real-time embedded systems (e.g., engine control, modems). 4. **Compiler can do global optimization** beyond what hardware can see. 5. **Scalable**: adding more functional units only widens the instruction word. ## 2.6 Limitations of VLIW 1. **Code-size explosion** — NOP slots inflate binaries by 2×–4×. 2. **Compiler is the bottleneck** — poor schedules lose performance. 3. **Cache misses disrupt static schedule** — variable memory latency breaks assumptions. 4. **Binary incompatibility** across implementations with different issue widths. 5. **Branch prediction harder** — wrong-path code was statically scheduled too. 6. **No dynamic load balancing** when functional units are unequal in capability. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are direct KTU 2024 Module-3 exam-relevant. Memorize them with units.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $S = \dfrac{T_{serial}}{T_{parallel}}$ | Speedup factor | dimensionless | | 2 | $S_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | Amdahl's Law (f = parallel fraction, N = processors/units) | dimensionless | | 3 | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f)N + f}$ | Parallel efficiency | dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 4 | $CPI_{effective} = \dfrac{\text{Total cycles}}{\text{Total instructions}} = \dfrac{1}{IPC}$ | Cycles per instruction | cycles/instr | | 5 | $T_{CPU} = N_{instr} \times CPI \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{N_{instr} \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | CPU execution time | seconds | | 6 | $IPC_{VLIW} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i$ | Total IPC = sum of utilizations of N units | instr/cycle | | 7 | $Throughput = \dfrac{IPC \times f_{clk}}{10^6}$ | MIPS rate (millions of instr/sec) | MIPS | | 8 | $T_{pipeline} = T_{stage} + (k-1) \cdot T_{stage}$ | Pipelined execution time for k instructions, n stages | seconds | | 9 | $S_{pipeline} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ | Pipeline speedup (n stages, k instructions) | dimensionless | | 10 | $NOP_{overhead} = \dfrac{N - u_{avg}}{N}$ | Fraction of VLIW slots wasted on NOPs | 0 to 1 | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **DSP VLIW cores** (TMS320C67x, SHARC) power audio codecs, modems, baseband processing in 4G/5G handsets. - **VLIW in image processors** handle 4K video decode, computer-vision pipelines in ADAS. - **Itanium (IA-64 EPIC)** — server-grade VLIW descendant used in HP Integrity systems. - **Modern ML accelerators** borrow VLIW's "static-schedule, wide-issue" philosophy to reduce silicon area and energy. --- # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (High-Yield for 14-mark problems) **Statement:** Amdahl's Law gives the theoretical maximum speedup of a parallel system when only a fraction $f$ of the work can be parallelized over $N$ processors/units, and the remaining $(1-f)$ is inherently serial. ### Step-by-step proof **Step 1 — Define total work.** Let the total execution time on a single processor be $T_{serial} = 1$ (normalized unit of work). **Step 2 — Split into serial and parallel parts.** $$ T_{serial} = T_{serial\,part} + T_{parallel\,part} = (1 - f) + f $$ where $f$ is the parallelizable fraction and $(1 - f)$ is the serial fraction. **Step 3 — Apply N-way parallelism.** With $N$ processors, the parallel part runs in $f / N$ time, but the serial part cannot be parallelized and still takes $(1 - f)$ time. $$ T_{parallel} = (1 - f) + \frac{f}{N} $$ **Step 4 — Compute speedup $S = T_{serial} / T_{parallel}$.** $$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} $$ This is **Amdahl's Law** ✅. **Step 5 — Limiting case as $N \to \infty$.** $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - f} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial KTU Conclusion:** Even with infinite processors, speedup is bounded by $1/(1-f)$. For example, if just **5%** of the code is serial ($f = 0.95$), maximum speedup = $\frac{1}{0.05} = 20\times$. This is why VLIW compilers work hard to **reduce serial bottlenecks** such as memory dependencies. **Step 6 — Derivation of Parallel Efficiency.** Efficiency $E$ is defined as speedup per processor: $$ E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{1}{N(1 - f) + f} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Amdahl's Law Application (KTU-style) **Problem:** A VLIW DSP core has 8 parallel functional units. An embedded audio codec spends 75% of its execution time in parallelizable FIR/FFT loops and 25% in serial control code. Compute: (a) Speedup achieved, and (b) Efficiency, and (c) Maximum theoretical speedup if the number of units were infinite. ### Step-by-step solution **Given:** $N = 8$, $f = 0.75$, $1 - f = 0.25$. **(a) Speedup S:** $$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{8}} $$ $$ \frac{0.75}{8} = 0.09375 $$ $$ S = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.09375} = \frac{1}{0.34375} \approx 2.91 $$ **(b) Efficiency E:** $$ E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{2.909}{8} \approx 0.3636 \quad \text{(i.e., 36.36%)} $$ **(c) Max speedup as $N \to \infty$:** $$ S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 $$ > [!NOTE] > Going from 8 units to infinity only improves speedup from **2.91× to 4×**. This is the *diminishing return* insight Amdahl's Law exposes. ## 3.3 VLIW Performance Derivation — IPC, Throughput, NOP Overhead **Problem:** A VLIW processor issues 4 operations per long word. In a benchmark loop of 1000 long-word instructions, profiler data shows: - ALU1 utilized in 950 long-words - ALU2 utilized in 800 - MUL utilized in 700 - LSU utilized in 600 Find: **(a) Average IPC, (b) NOP overhead, (c) Effective throughput at 200 MHz clock.** **(a) Average IPC:** $$ IPC = \frac{\sum u_i}{N} = \frac{950 + 800 + 700 + 600}{1000} = \frac{3050}{1000} = 3.05 $$ **(b) NOP overhead:** $$ NOP_{overhead} = \frac{N - IPC}{N} = \frac{4 - 3.05}{4} = 0.2375 \quad (23.75\%) $$ **(c) Throughput at $f_{clk} = 200$ MHz:** $$ Throughput = IPC \times f_{clk} = 3.05 \times 200 \times 10^6 = 6.10 \times 10^8 \text{ instr/sec} = 610 \text{ MIPS} $$ ## 3.4 Pipeline Speedup Derivation (k instructions, n-stage pipeline) **Without pipeline** (sequential): $T_{seq} = k \cdot n \cdot T_{stage}$. **With pipeline**: first instruction takes $n \cdot T_{stage}$, each of the next $(k-1)$ takes $T_{stage}$: $$ T_{pipe} = n \cdot T_{stage} + (k - 1) \cdot T_{stage} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_{stage} $$ **Speedup:** $$ S_{pipe} = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{k \cdot n \cdot T_{stage}}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_{stage}} = \frac{k \cdot n}{n + k - 1} $$ **Limiting case** as $k \to \infty$: $S \to n$ (the ideal, classic pipeline result). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — VLIW Scheduling Example A VLIW long word has 3 slots: `[ALU | MUL | LSU]`. Compiler output for 5 sequential source statements: | Src # | Source Statement | Independent? | Cycle | Long Word `[ALU \| MUL \| LSU]` | |:-----:|:-----------------|:------------|:-----:|:------------------------------| | 1 | `a = b + c` | yes | 1 | `ADD a,b,c \| — \| —` | | 2 | `d = e * f` | yes | 1 | `ADD a,b,c \| MUL d,e,f \| —` | | 3 | `Mem[g] = h` | yes | 1 | `ADD a,b,c \| MUL d,e,f \| ST g,h` | | 4 | `i = a + d` | depends on 1,2 | 2 | `ADD i,a,d \| — \| —` | | 5 | `j = d * a` | depends on 1,2 | 2 | `ADD i,a,d \| MUL j,d,a \| —` | **Analysis:** - **3 source instructions in cycle 1** (perfectly packed) → IPC = 3. - **2 source instructions in cycle 2** (true data dependency forces stall) → IPC = 2. - **Average IPC over 5 instructions, 2 cycles** = $5 / 2 = 2.5$. - **Sequential baseline** would have taken 5 cycles → **Speedup = 5 / 2 = 2.5×**. > [!TIP] > This kind of scheduling trace is **exactly the kind of 7-mark sub-question KTU sets**. Practice drawing these. --- # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Generic 4-issue VLIW Processor ```mermaid flowchart TB IMem["Instruction Memory
Wide Long Words (e.g., 256 bits)"] Fetch["Fetch Unit
1 long word per cycle"] Decode["Decode / Dispatch Unit
Splits long word into N op fields"] RF["Multi-ported Register File
(N read, M write ports)"] CB["Operand Crossbar / Interconnect"] ALU1["Functional Unit 1
ALU / Integer"] ALU2["Functional Unit 2
ALU / Integer"] MUL["Functional Unit 3
Multiplier"] LSU["Functional Unit 4
Load / Store Unit"] BR["Branch Unit (optional)"] DMem["Data Memory
(cache or SRAM)"] IMem --> Fetch Fetch --> Decode Decode -->|"Op 0"| ALU1 Decode -->|"Op 1"| ALU2 Decode -->|"Op 2"| MUL Decode -->|"Op 3"| LSU Decode -->|"Op 4 (if present)"| BR RF --> CB CB --> ALU1 CB --> ALU2 CB --> MUL CB --> LSU CB --> BR LSU --> DMem ALU1 --> RF ALU2 --> RF MUL --> RF LSU --> RF BR --> RF ``` ## 4.2 VLIW Long-Word Layout (Bit-Field View) ```mermaid block-beta columns 4 block:longword s0["Slot 0
Opcode + Dest + Src1 + Src2
(~64 bits)"] s1["Slot 1
Opcode + Dest + Src1 + Src2
(~64 bits)"] s2["Slot 2
Opcode + Dest + Src1 + Src2
(~64 bits)"] s3["Slot 3
Opcode + Dest + Src1 + Src2
(~64 bits)"] end s0 --> s1 s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 ``` > [!NOTE] > Each slot is essentially a RISC-style instruction. The long word is simply $N$ of them concatenated. The compiler's job is to **fill each slot with an operation independent of the others in the same word**. ## 4.3 VLIW vs Superscalar — Architectural Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Source Program
(C / C++)"] --> B["Compiler"] B --> C1["VLIW Path
Static Schedule
(long instruction words)"] B --> C2["Superscalar Path
Narrow RISC instructions"] C1 --> D1["VLIW Hardware
Simple fetch + decode
No dependency check
N parallel FUs"] C2 --> D2["Superscalar Hardware
Out-of-order engine
Scoreboard / ROB
Renaming logic"] D1 --> E1["Low power, low cost,
predictable timing,
large code"] D2 --> E2["Higher power,
complex hardware,
compact code"] ``` ## 4.4 VLIW Pipeline Timing — 4-issue, 2-cycle Execution ```mermaid gantt title VLIW Pipeline Trace — 4-issue core, 5 long words dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Long Word 1 F1 (Fetch) :a1, 0, 1 D1 (Decode/Issue) :a2, 1, 1 X1 (Execute) :a3, 2, 1 section Long Word 2 F2 :b1, 1, 1 D2 :b2, 2, 1 X2 :b3, 3, 1 section Long Word 3 F3 :c1, 2, 1 D3 :c2, 3, 1 X3 :c3, 4, 1 section Long Word 4 F4 :d1, 3, 1 D4 :d2, 4, 1 X4 :d3, 5, 1 section Long Word 5 F5 :e1, 4, 1 D5 :e2, 5, 1 X5 :e3, 6, 1 ``` > [!NOTE] > After the first long word, the pipeline is **fully filled** and the issue rate of 1 long word/cycle (i.e., 4 ops/cycle) is sustained. This is the **steady-state throughput** KTU examiners refer to. ## 4.5 Compile-Time Scheduling Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Source Instruction i"] --> DepCheck{"Dependencies with
previous i-1, i-2 ... ?"} DepCheck -- "No, all slots empty" --> PackA["Pack into current long word"] DepCheck -- "Yes, dependency exists" --> Stall["Move to next long word"] PackA --> Next["i = i + 1"] Stall --> Next Next --> SlotsFull{"All N slots
of current word full?"} SlotsFull -- Yes --> Emit["Emit long word, open new word"] SlotsFull -- No --> DepCheck Emit --> DepCheck ``` --- # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Define VLIW architecture. How does it differ from a superscalar processor in terms of scheduling responsibility? **Model Answer (3 marks):** **VLIW Definition (1 mark):** VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) is a multi-issue processor architecture in which a single long instruction word contains multiple operation fields, each meant for a different functional unit. The compiler statically schedules these operations to execute in parallel within the same clock cycle, eliminating the need for hardware-based dynamic scheduling. **Scheduling Responsibility Difference (2 marks):** | Aspect | VLIW | Superscalar | |--------|------|-------------| | Who schedules? | **Compiler (static)** | **Hardware (dynamic)** | | Where is complexity? | In the compiler | In the issue logic / scoreboard | | Decision time | At compile-time | At run-time, per cycle | In VLIW, the hardware merely fetches and dispatches the long word; it does **not check for dependencies** — that is the compiler's job. In a superscalar, the hardware reads conventional RISC-like instructions and decides on-the-fly which ones can issue together. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** List any **four advantages** of VLIW processors in embedded system design. **Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):** 1. **Lower hardware complexity** — no dynamic scheduler, scoreboard, or register renamer; smaller die area and lower cost. 2. **Lower power consumption** — fewer switching transistors per cycle, ideal for battery-powered embedded devices. 3. **Higher achievable clock frequency** — the critical path is shorter because complex scheduling logic is removed. 4. **Deterministic / predictable timing** — same input gives the same execution schedule every run, essential for hard real-time embedded systems (engine control, industrial PLCs). 5. *(Bonus, if asked)* **Compiler can perform global optimization** with full visibility of the program. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):** > - **Do NOT** confuse VLIW with superscalar in scheduling diagrams. Marks are deducted when students say "VLIW issues instructions dynamically" — it does not. > - In Amdahl's Law numericals, **always show the formula substitution step explicitly**. A bare "= 2.91×" without the denominator arithmetic loses 2 marks. > - When drawing VLIW long-word packing, **label every slot with a specific unit** (ALU, MUL, LSU) — vague "unit1, unit2" answers lose marks. > - Mentioning **at least one real example** (TMS320C6x, Itanium) is worth a bonus mark on most KTU answer sheets. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a VLIW processor with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the compiler in achieving instruction-level parallelism. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A multimedia embedded processor adopts VLIW with 6 parallel functional units. A video decoding benchmark shows that 80% of the code is parallelizable. Compute: (i) The speedup achieved with the 6 units. (ii) The maximum speedup possible with infinite units. (iii) The parallel efficiency. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) VLIW Architecture & Compiler Role — 7 marks** **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A VLIW processor consists of: - A **wide instruction memory** storing long instruction words (e.g., 6 × 32 bits = 192 bits per long word). - A **fetch unit** that retrieves one long word per cycle. - A **decode/dispatch unit** that splits the long word into 6 operation fields. - A **multi-ported register file** providing operands to all functional units simultaneously. - **6 parallel functional units** — typically a mix of integer ALUs, multipliers, and load/store units. - An **operand crossbar** that routes source operands from the register file to the requested functional unit. The diagram should resemble Section 4.1 of these notes (4-issue version, easily extended to 6). **Compiler's Role (4 marks):** The VLIW compiler performs tasks that a superscalar does in hardware: 1. **Dependency Analysis** — Builds a data-dependence graph for the procedure. RAW, WAR, WAW hazards are identified. 2. **Instruction Scheduling** — Using techniques like *trace scheduling*, *software pipelining*, or *modulo scheduling*, independent instructions are packed into the same long word. 3. **NOP Insertion** — Slots in a long word that cannot be filled get explicit NOPs (this is what causes code-size inflation). 4. **Register Allocation** — Since multiple operations write to the register file in the same cycle, the compiler must ensure no two slots write to the same architectural register. 5. **Loop Optimization** — Software pipelining interleaves iterations of a loop to keep all functional units busy every cycle. > The compiler's goal: **maximize the number of independent operations packed per long word → maximize IPC.** **(b) Amdahl's Law Numerical — 7 marks** Given: $N = 6$ functional units, $f = 0.80$, $1 - f = 0.20$. **(i) Speedup with 6 units (3 marks):** $$ S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{6}} $$ $$ \frac{0.80}{6} = 0.1333 $$ $$ S = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.1333} = \frac{1}{0.3333} = 3.0 $$ **Speedup = 3.0× ✅** **(ii) Maximum speedup as $N \to \infty$ (2 marks):** $$ S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0 $$ **Max speedup = 5.0× ✅** **(iii) Parallel efficiency (2 marks):** $$ E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{3.0}{6} = 0.50 \quad \text{or} \quad 50\% $$ **Efficiency = 50% ✅** > [!NOTE] > Even though we have 6 units, half the potential is wasted on the 20% serial portion. The lecturer can be asked: "Can we make the serial 20% faster?" → This is precisely what a good VLIW compiler tries to do via instruction reordering. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** *(Internal alternative to Question A)* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Compare VLIW and superscalar architectures across **at least six parameters**. Why is VLIW preferred for embedded DSP applications? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-issue VLIW processor runs at 250 MHz. In a benchmark of 500 long-word instructions, the four functional units (ALU1, ALU2, MUL, LSU) are utilized in 480, 420, 380, and 350 long words respectively. Calculate: (i) The average IPC. (ii) The NOP overhead percentage. (iii) The execution time of the benchmark in microseconds, assuming each long word takes 1 cycle to fetch, decode, and issue. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) VLIW vs Superscalar Comparison — 7 marks** **Comparison Table (5 marks)** — students should present a clean table: | Parameter | VLIW | Superscalar | |-----------|------|-------------| | Instruction format | Long word with N op slots | Conventional RISC instructions | | Scheduling | Compile-time (static) | Run-time (dynamic) | | Hardware complexity | Low | High (issue logic, renaming, ROB) | | Power consumption | Lower | Higher | | Code size | Large (NOP padding) | Compact | | Compiler complexity | Very high | Moderate | | Binary portability | Poor (schedule baked in) | Good | | Predictability | Deterministic timing | Variable timing | | Issue rate | Fixed at design time | Adaptive per cycle | | Example | TI TMS320C6x, Itanium | Intel Core i-series | **Why VLIW is preferred for embedded DSP (2 marks):** 1. **DSP workloads are highly regular** — tight loops over arrays (FIR filters, FFTs) have abundant ILP that a compiler can statically extract. 2. **Deterministic timing** is critical for real-time audio/video pipelines. 3. **Lower power and cost** — essential for portable/battery-powered devices like handsets, hearing aids, drones. 4. **High compute density per watt** — DSP VLIWs deliver multi-GFLOP performance at fractions of a watt. **(b) VLIW Performance Numerical — 7 marks** Given: $N = 4$ slots, $f_{clk} = 250$ MHz, $L = 500$ long words, $u_1 = 480, u_2 = 420, u_3 = 380, u_4 = 350$. **(i) Average IPC (3 marks):** $$ IPC = \frac{u_1 + u_2 + u_3 + u_4}{L} = \frac{480 + 420 + 380 + 350}{500} $$ $$ = \frac{1630}{500} = 3.26 $$ **Average IPC = 3.26 ✅** **(ii) NOP overhead (2 marks):** $$ NOP_{overhead} = \frac{N - IPC}{N} = \frac{4 - 3.26}{4} = \frac{0.74}{4} = 0.185 = 18.5\% $$ **NOP overhead = 18.5% ✅** **(iii) Execution time (2 marks):** Each long word issues in 1 cycle → total cycles = 500. $$ T_{exec} = \frac{\text{Total cycles}}{f_{clk}} = \frac{500}{250 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2\ \mu s $$ **Execution time = 2 µs ✅** > [!TIP] > The same benchmark on a scalar processor would have taken $500 \times 3.26 = 1630$ cycles, i.e., $6.52\ \mu s$. The VLIW is **3.26× faster** for the same clock — a beautiful illustration of IPC gain. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Re-read this section the night before the exam):** - **Definition to memorize verbatim:** *VLIW is a multi-issue architecture where the compiler statically packs multiple independent operations into a single long instruction word for parallel execution in one cycle.* - **The 3 pillars of VLIW:** *(i) static scheduling, (ii) wide instruction word, (iii) multiple parallel functional units.* - **VLIW ≠ Superscalar.** VLIW = compile-time decision; Superscalar = run-time hardware decision. - **Key formula 1:** $S_{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/N}$ - **Key formula 2:** $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$ (as $N \to \infty$) - **Key formula 3:** $E = S / N$ - **Key formula 4:** $IPC = \sum u_i / L$ (VLIW-specific) - **Key formula 5:** $NOP_{overhead} = (N - IPC)/N$ - **Real-world examples to quote:** TI **TMS320C6x** family (DSP), Intel **Itanium** (IA-64 / EPIC). - **Advantages to list:** simple hardware, low power, predictable timing, high clock rate, compiler does global optimization. - **Disadvantages to list:** large code size, compiler is critical, cache-miss sensitivity, poor binary portability, no dynamic load balancing. - **Compiler techniques to name:** dependency analysis, trace scheduling, software pipelining, modulo scheduling, register allocation. - **Amdahl's Law insight:** Even with infinite parallelism, speedup is bounded by the **serial fraction**. This is why a 5% serial portion caps speedup at 20×. - **VLIW is a form of ILP exploitation**, not DLP or TLP. SIMD = DLP, Multi-core = TLP. - **Number to remember:** VLIW code-size inflation is typically **2×–4×** compared to RISC. - **Diagram must-have:** A block diagram with *Instruction Memory → Fetch → Decode → Multi-ported Register File → Crossbar → N parallel FUs.* Drawing this single diagram can fetch you 3–4 marks on its own. - **Avoid in your answer:** Saying "VLIW does dynamic scheduling" or "VLIW checks dependencies at runtime" — these are the **two most penalized wrong statements** on KTU answer sheets.

topics.title: Code Efficiency DSP Application and Address Generation Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Efficiency DSP Application and Address Generation Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Code Efficiency in Embedded Systems

### Formal Academic Definition
**Code efficiency** in embedded systems refers to the optimal utilization of processor resources—execution time, memory footprint, and power consumption—achieved through disciplined programming techniques, compiler optimizations, and architecture-aware coding practices. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, code efficiency encompasses both **spatial efficiency** (reduced memory usage) and **temporal efficiency** (reduced execution cycles), which are critical for resource-constrained embedded platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Code efficiency is not merely "fast code" — it is the *engineering discipline* of balancing **three competing constraints**: speed, size, and power. The 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on writing code that is simultaneously optimized for the target MCU/DSP architecture.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Chef in a Tiny Kitchen
Imagine a chef working in a very small kitchen (the embedded processor's limited resources). 

- A **regular kitchen** has unlimited counter space, multiple ovens, and helpers. The chef can be messy. → This is like a **desktop PC** where resources are abundant.
- A **tiny galley kitchen** on a ship has *one* burner, *one* counter, and a strict time limit for every dish. The chef must pre-measure everything, reuse the same pan, and move with surgical precision. → This is **embedded code efficiency**.

In this analogy:
- **Pre-measuring ingredients** = Pre-computing values in tables (lookup tables)
- **Reusing the same pan** = Reusing registers and memory locations
- **Surgical precision** = Eliminating redundant instructions
- **Strict time limit** = Hard real-time deadlines in embedded systems

### Why Code Efficiency Matters in DSP Applications
Digital Signal Processors (DSPs) are specialized embedded processors designed to perform mathematical operations on streaming data in real-time. DSP code efficiency determines:

| Metric | Standard | Typical DSP Target |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) | High-level | **< 100 MIPS** for most real-time DSP |
| **Code Size** | KB–MB | **< 64 KB** for on-chip memory |
| **Power** | Watts | **< 1 W** for portable DSP |
| **Latency** | ms | **µs or less** for real-time audio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Most DSP algorithms (FIR filters, FFTs, convolutions) execute the same inner loop *billions* of times per second. Even saving **one cycle per loop iteration** can save millions of cycles per second of audio/video processing.

---

## 1.2 Address Generation Unit (AGU)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Address Generation Unit (AGU)** is a dedicated hardware sub-block within a microprocessor or DSP that computes the memory addresses required for operand fetches and result stores—*in parallel* with the main ALU's arithmetic operations. By offloading address arithmetic from the main execution unit, the AGU enables **single-cycle memory accesses** with non-trivial addressing patterns (e.g., circular buffers, bit-reversed indexing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Memorize for KTU):** An AGU is a *special-purpose hardware unit* that performs address calculations (addition, subtraction, modulo, bit-reverse) **concurrently** with the main datapath, allowing zero-overhead memory addressing in DSP loops.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Librarian's Assistant
Consider a librarian in a vast library (the system memory) who must fetch books (data) requested by a researcher (the CPU).

- **Without an AGU:** The researcher (CPU) must personally calculate which shelf, row, and section each book is in, write down the location, walk to the shelf, and fetch the book—*all by himself*. Every fetch involves calculation overhead.
- **With an AGU:** The researcher hires a **dedicated assistant** (the AGU). The researcher simply says, *"Get me the next book in the sequence,"* and the assistant instantly knows exactly where to go, calculates the next location in parallel, and even pre-fetches the *next-next* book. The researcher's main job (computation) never pauses.

The **AGU** is this dedicated assistant. While the CPU multiplies and accumulates (MAC) one sample, the AGU is already computing the address of the *next* sample.

### The Fundamental Need for an AGU in DSP
DSP algorithms exhibit highly regular memory access patterns:

1. **FIR Filter:** `y[n] = Σ h[k] · x[n-k]` — needs *consecutive* samples from a sliding window
2. **IIR Filter:** Needs *circular* buffer access to avoid copying
3. **FFT:** Requires **bit-reversed** address sequencing for the input/output
4. **Convolution:** Requires two parallel streams of *advancing* pointers

Without an AGU, the CPU would burn 2–3 cycles per memory access just calculating the address, halving or thirding the throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** Modern DSPs like the **TMS320C6000**, **SHARC ADSP-21xx**, and **ARM Cortex-M** variants include 1–2 dedicated AGUs precisely because DSP workloads are *address-bound* rather than *compute-bound* for many practical kernels.

### Key Constants and Architectural Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **AGU clock cycles per address** | **1** | Single-cycle address generation |
| **Number of AGUs per DSP core** | 1–2 | Dual AGU enables dual-memory fetch |
| **Circular buffer size** | **N** (power of 2) | Modulo arithmetic is bit-mask |
| **Modulo operation cost** | 1 cycle (with AGU) | Free with dedicated hardware |
| **Bit-reversal latency** | 1 cycle (with AGU) | Computed during address calc |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Why is an AGU necessary in DSP?"* — your answer must mention *three* things: **(1) parallel address calculation, (2) non-trivial addressing modes (circular, bit-reverse), and (3) sustained single-cycle memory throughput in inner loops**.

---

## 1.3 DSP Application Characteristics (Why DSP ≠ General-Purpose)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of DSP Workloads:**
> 1. **Repetitive inner loops** — the same MAC (multiply-accumulate) is executed millions of times.
> 2. **Streaming data access** — input samples arrive at a fixed rate and must be processed before the next sample arrives.
> 3. **Real-time deadlines** — missing a deadline is a *system failure*, not just slow performance.
> 4. **Math-intensive operations** — convolution, correlation, transforms, filtering.

These four pillars drive the architectural choices in DSPs, including the **mandatory presence of an AGU**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Code Efficiency in DSP

### 2.1.1 The Three Dimensions of Code Efficiency

Efficient DSP code is optimized along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Dimension | What is Minimized | Typical Techniques |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Temporal (Speed)** | Execution cycles | Loop unrolling, inlining, MAC, AGU |
| **Spatial (Memory)** | Code/data bytes | Lookup tables, fixed-point, overlays |
| **Energy (Power)** | Joules per operation | Clock gating, low-power modes, code density |

### 2.1.2 The MAC Operation — The Heart of DSP

The **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC)** is the single most important operation in DSP. It computes:

$$y = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a[k] \cdot b[k]$$

A dedicated **MAC unit** in a DSP executes `a*b + accumulator` in a **single cycle**, with the AGU providing the two operand addresses in parallel.

```
[Register A] ──┐
               ├──► [Multiplier] ──► [Adder] ──► [Accumulator]
[Register B] ──┘
```

The AGU supplies the address of A from one memory bank, and the address of B from a second memory bank — all in the *same cycle*.

### 2.1.3 Code Optimization Strategies (KTU-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> These techniques are *specifically* tested in KTU Module 3 questions.

**1. Loop Unrolling**
Reduces loop overhead by replicating the loop body. Trade-off: increases code size.

**2. Software Pipelining**
Re-orders instructions so that the next iteration's address calculation overlaps with the current iteration's computation.

**3. Use of Intrinsics & Inline Assembly**
Replaces a function call with a single hardware instruction (e.g., `__builtin_mac()`).

**4. Fixed-Point Arithmetic**
Replaces expensive floating-point with integer arithmetic, often **4–10× faster** on low-end DSPs.

**5. Circular Buffer Avoids Memory Copy**
Instead of shifting an array, a circular buffer with AGU support slides a "window" through memory with **zero data movement**.

**6. Lookup Tables (LUT)**
Pre-computes expensive functions (sin, log, sqrt) at compile time, trading memory for cycles.

### 2.1.4 The Memory Bandwidth Bottleneck

Many DSP kernels are **memory-bound**, not compute-bound. The **Roofline Model** captures this:

$$
\text{Attainable Performance} = \min\left( \text{Peak Compute}, \; \text{Memory Bandwidth} \times \text{AI} \right)
$$

Where **AI** is the *Arithmetic Intensity* (operations per byte loaded). For most DSP inner loops, increasing the AI through **block processing** and **data reuse** is more effective than adding compute hardware.

---

## 2.2 The Address Generation Unit — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Architecture of an AGU

A typical DSP AGU consists of:

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Address Register File** | Holds base pointers (e.g., `R0`, `R1`, …) |
| **Address Arithmetic Unit (AAU)** | Performs add/subtract/modulo on addresses |
| **Modify Registers** | Specify the step size for each pointer |
| **Index/Offset Registers** | Provide displacement into arrays |
| **Buffer Control Logic** | Manages circular buffer wrap-around |

The **AGU outputs the address to the data memory in parallel** with the ALU/MAC executing on data already in registers.

### 2.2.2 Addressing Modes Supported by a Typical DSP AGU

| Addressing Mode | Equation | Use Case |
|----------------|----------|----------|
| **Register Indirect** | `EA = R` | Pointer-based access |
| **Post-increment** | `EA = R; R = R + 1` | Linear streaming, array traversal |
| **Post-decrement** | `EA = R; R = R - 1` | Reverse traversal |
| **Indexed** | `EA = R + offset` | Filter taps with offset |
| **Modulo (Circular)** | `EA = (R + step) mod N` | FIR/IIR circular buffers |
| **Bit-Reversed** | `EA = bitrev(R)` | FFT butterfly I/O |
| **Pre-modify** | `R = R + step; EA = R` | Synchronized advance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulo Addressing Equation** (memorize for KTU):
> $$\text{New Address} = (R + \text{step}) \bmod N$$
> For power-of-2 N, this is implemented as a *bitwise AND*:
> $$\text{New Address} = (R + \text{step}) \; \& \; (N-1)$$
> This makes modulo addressing **free** in hardware.

### 2.2.3 Circular Buffer Mechanism

A circular buffer of size N (power of 2) is implemented with a **buffer start register (BSA)** and **buffer end register (BEA)**. When an address falls outside the range:

$$\text{If } R > BEA: \quad R = R - N$$
$$\text{If } R < BSA: \quad R = R + N$$

The DSP checks this **every memory access, in hardware, in 1 cycle** — no software branch needed.

### 2.2.4 Bit-Reversed Addressing for FFT

The **Cooley–Tukey FFT** requires the input (or output) to be in **bit-reversed order**. For an N = 8-point FFT, the bit-reversal of index `k = b₂b₁b₀` is `b₀b₁b₂`.

| Index `k` | Binary | Bit-Reversed | Reordered `k` |
|----------|--------|--------------|---------------|
| 0 | `000` | `000` | 0 |
| 1 | `001` | `100` | 4 |
| 2 | `010` | `010` | 2 |
| 3 | `011` | `110` | 6 |
| 4 | `100` | `001` | 1 |
| 5 | `101` | `101` | 5 |
| 6 | `110` | `011` | 3 |
| 7 | `111` | `111` | 7 |

A DSP AGU with **bit-reversal hardware** can read/write these addresses in *natural incrementing order* while the data appears in *bit-reversed order* in memory. This eliminates a separate shuffling pass — saving **N·log₂(N) cycles** for an N-point FFT.

### 2.2.5 The Modified Harvard Architecture

DSPs (and modern MCUs) use a **Modified Harvard architecture** with separate program and data memories, allowing the AGU to fetch an operand while the program counter advances the next instruction — a **simultaneous two-memory access** pattern.

```
         ┌─────────────────────┐
         │   Program Memory    │◄──── PC (Instruction Fetch)
         └──────────┬──────────┘
                    │
                    ▼
              ┌───────────┐
              │    CPU    │◄────► [ MAC / ALU ]
              └─────┬─────┘
                    │ Address bus
                    ▼
              ┌───────────┐
              │    AGU    │──────► Data Memory (X, Y banks)
              └───────────┘
```

The AGU feeds **two independent data address buses** (DA1, DA2), enabling *dual-data fetch* in one cycle.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your exam-day cheat sheet. Memorize it.**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---------|---------------------|-------|
| **MAC Operation** | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ | Single-cycle in DSP MAC unit |
| **FIR Filter Output** | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k \cdot x[n-k]$ | Requires N MACs per output sample |
| **Modulo Address** | $\text{EA} = (R + \text{step}) \bmod N$ | Power-of-2 N ⇒ bitwise AND |
| **Bit-Reverse Address** | $\text{EA} = \text{bitrev}(R)$ | Used in FFT I/O |
| **Circular Buffer Wrap** | $R = R - N \; \text{if} \; R > BEA$ | Hardware-managed |
| **Cycles Saved by AGU** | $\Delta T = N_{\text{loop}} \times (T_{\text{addr}} - T_{\text{AGU}})$ | $T_{\text{AGU}} = 1$ cycle |
| **FFT Computational Cost** | $T_{\text{FFT}} = N \cdot \log_2(N)$ MAC ops | Reduced by 2 for radix-2 |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | $BW = f_{\text{CPU}} \times W_{\text{bus}} \times N_{\text{access}}$ | Bytes/second |
| **Arithmetic Intensity** | $\text{AI} = \text{Ops} / \text{Bytes Loaded}$ | Higher ⇒ compute-bound |
| **Loop Unroll Speedup** | $S = \frac{N_{\text{orig}} \cdot T_{\text{loop}}}{N_{\text{unroll}} \cdot T_{\text{unroll}}}$ | Bounded by code size |
| **Fixed-Point Speedup** | $S_{\text{FP}} = T_{\text{Float}} / T_{\text{Fix}}$ | Typically 4–10× |
| **Cycles per FIR Tap** | $T_{\text{tap}} = 1$ (with MAC + AGU) | 2 without AGU |
| **Buffer Size Constraint** | $N = 2^k$ | Enables fast modulo via mask |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When asked to compute cycles saved by an AGU, students often forget to *subtract* the AGU's own cost. The net saving is:
> $$\text{Saved} = (\text{software addr calc}) - (\text{AGU cost})$$
> where **AGU cost = 1 cycle** (parallel with MAC). If software calculation is 2 cycles, net saving = 1 cycle *per access*. Multiply by N accesses.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why AGU is Indispensable |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC)** | FIR/IIR filters on 48 kHz streams need real-time throughput |
| **5G Baseband Processing** | FFT/IFFT on thousands of subcarriers every millisecond |
| **Medical Imaging (MRI, Ultrasound)** | 2D FFT reconstruction requires bit-reversed + linear addressing |
| **Radar/Echo Cancellation** | Adaptive LMS filters need circular sample buffers |
| **Motor Control (FOC)** | Park/Clarke transforms with fast sample rotation |
| **Image Processing Pipelines** | 2D convolution with sliding window + line buffer wrap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Stat:** A single SHARC ADSP-21489 (with 2 AGUs) can execute an N=256 tap FIR filter at a 1 MSPS sample rate, while a generic ARM Cortex-M4F (no AGU) can barely sustain 100 kSPS for the same filter. That is a **10× throughput advantage**, purely from architecture.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cycles Per Output Sample for a Block FIR Filter

### Problem Statement
Compute the number of clock cycles required to compute one output sample of an N-tap FIR filter:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

**Compare three implementations:**
- (A) Generic RISC MCU, no MAC, no AGU
- (B) DSP with MAC but no AGU
- (C) DSP with MAC + dedicated AGU

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Implementation A — Generic RISC, no MAC, no AGU**

Each tap requires:
- 1 cycle: load `h[k]`
- 1 cycle: load `x[n-k]`
- 1 cycle: address update (next tap)
- 1 cycle: multiply
- 1 cycle: add to accumulator
- Loop overhead: 1 cycle per tap

Total cycles per tap: $5 + 1 = 6$ cycles
Total cycles for N taps:
$$T_A = 6N \text{ cycles per output sample}$$

**Implementation B — DSP with MAC, no AGU**

Each tap:
- 1 cycle: MAC operation (`a*b + acc` in one cycle)
- 2 cycles: address calculation (add step, fetch, update)
- Loop overhead: 1 cycle per tap

Total per tap: $1 + 2 + 1 = 4$ cycles
Total for N taps:
$$T_B = 4N \text{ cycles per output sample}$$

**Implementation C — DSP with MAC + AGU**

The AGU computes the next address *in parallel* with the MAC. So:
- 1 cycle: MAC + simultaneous address update (AGU hidden in MAC)
- Loop overhead: 1 cycle per tap

Total per tap: $1 + 1 = 2$ cycles
Total for N taps:
$$T_C = 2N \text{ cycles per output sample}$$

### Speedup Analysis

$$\text{Speedup}_{C/A} = \frac{T_A}{T_C} = \frac{6N}{2N} = 3\times$$

$$\text{Speedup}_{C/B} = \frac{T_B}{T_C} = \frac{4N}{2N} = 2\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Adding an AGU to a MAC-equipped DSP **doubles** the FIR throughput. For a 100-tap FIR at 48 kHz audio rate, this is the difference between *needing a 9.6 MHz CPU* and *needing a 4.8 MHz CPU*. In battery-powered devices, this halves the dynamic power (since $P \propto f$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Circular Buffer Wrap-Around Logic

### Problem Statement
A circular buffer of size $N = 8$ is allocated at base address `BUF_START = 0x1000`. The current pointer is at `R = 0x1006`, and a post-increment by `step = 1` is requested. Show that the AGU wraps the address correctly.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the next raw address:**
$$R_{\text{next,raw}} = R + \text{step} = 0x1006 + 1 = 0x1007$$

**Step 2 — Check against buffer end:**
The buffer occupies addresses `0x1000` to `0x1007` (8 elements, BUF_END = 0x1007).
Since $0x1007 \le BEA = 0x1007$, wrap is *not* yet triggered.

**Step 3 — Output the address:**
$$\text{EA} = 0x1007$$

**Step 4 — Next access (this one wraps):**
$$R_{\text{next,raw}} = 0x1007 + 1 = 0x1008$$

Since $0x1008 > BEA = 0x1007$:
$$\text{AGU wraps: } R_{\text{next}} = 0x1008 - N = 0x1008 - 8 = 0x1000$$

**Result:** The pointer correctly wraps to `0x1000`, and the next sample reads the *oldest* element — perfect for a sliding-window FIR.

### Generalized Wrap Formula

For any buffer size $N$ and step $s$:
$$R_{\text{next}} = \begin{cases} (R + s) \bmod N & \text{if power-of-2} \\ (R + s) - N & \text{if } R+s > BEA \\ (R + s) + N & \text{if } R+s < BSA \end{cases}$$

For power-of-2 $N = 2^k$, the modulo collapses to a **bit-mask**:
$$R_{\text{next}} = (R + s) \; \& \; (N-1)$$

This single AND-gate makes the wrap **free** in hardware.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: FFT Bit-Reversal Address Generation

### Problem Statement
For an $N = 8$ point FFT, the input data is stored in natural order. Show how the AGU's bit-reversed addressing mode reads the data in bit-reversed order without any data shuffling.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — AGU starts with R = 0, in bit-reversal mode:**

The AGU's natural increment is `R = R + 1` (normal order), but the *emitted address* is `bitrev(R)`.

**Step 2 — Table of AGU outputs:**

| Step `i` | $R = i$ | Binary of $i$ | $\text{bitrev}(R)$ | Emitted Address |
|---------|---------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 0 | 0 | `000` | `000` | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | `001` | `100` | 4 |
| 2 | 2 | `010` | `010` | 2 |
| 3 | 3 | `011` | `110` | 6 |
| 4 | 4 | `100` | `001` | 1 |
| 5 | 5 | `101` | `101` | 5 |
| 6 | 6 | `110` | `011` | 3 |
| 7 | 7 | `111` | `111` | 7 |

**Step 3 — Compare to required bit-reversed order:**

The required bit-reversed sequence is: $\{0, 4, 2, 6, 1, 5, 3, 7\}$.

The AGU's emitted addresses are *exactly* this sequence. ✓

**Step 4 — Cost analysis:**

Without AGU support, a separate bit-reversal shuffle pass would require:
- An auxiliary bit-reversed index table: $N \cdot \log_2(N)$ memory accesses
- OR: in-place bit-reversal with swap operations: $\frac{N}{2} \log_2(N)$ swaps

With AGU bit-reverse mode, **the shuffle costs 0 extra cycles** — it is "free."

### Generalized Cost Saving

For an N-point radix-2 FFT with separate bit-reversal pass:
$$T_{\text{shuffle}} = N \cdot \log_2(N) \text{ cycles (without AGU)}$$
$$T_{\text{shuffle}} = 0 \text{ cycles (with AGU bit-reverse)}$$

For $N = 1024$ points: $T_{\text{shuffle}} = 10{,}240$ cycles saved per FFT — significant at 100 kHz FFT rates.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Code-Size vs. Speed Tradeoff for Loop Unrolling

### Problem Statement
A loop of $N$ iterations has a body of $B$ instructions and loop overhead of $L$ instructions per iteration (branch, counter decrement, etc.).

**Compare:**
- (i) Original loop: code size = $B + L$, cycles = $N \cdot (B + L)$
- (ii) Unrolled by factor $k$: code size = $k \cdot B + L'$, cycles = $(N/k) \cdot (k \cdot B + L')$

Assume $L' \approx L$ (unrolled loop still has some overhead).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Speedup formula:**
$$S = \frac{N(B+L)}{N/k \cdot (kB + L)} = \frac{k(B+L)}{kB + L}$$

**Step 2 — Limit as $N \to \infty$:**
$$S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{k(B+L)}{kB + L} = 1 + \frac{L}{B + L/k}$$

**Step 3 — As $k \to \infty$ (fully unrolled):**
$$S_{\infty} = 1 + \frac{L}{B}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:**
For $B = 10$ (loop body) and $L = 3$ (overhead):
$$S_{\infty} = 1 + \frac{3}{10} = 1.3 = 30\% \text{ speedup}$$

**Step 5 — Code-size cost:**
$$\text{Size Ratio} = \frac{k \cdot B + L}{B + L}$$

For $k = 4, B = 10, L = 3$:
$$\text{Size Ratio} = \frac{40 + 3}{10 + 3} = 3.31\times$$

### Tradeoff Summary Table

| Unroll Factor $k$ | Speedup $S$ | Code Size Ratio | When to Use |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 (no unroll) | 1.00× | 1.0× | Tight memory, low performance need |
| 2 | ~1.18× | ~1.9× | Moderate |
| 4 | ~1.25× | ~3.3× | Tight loop, ample code memory |
| 8 | ~1.28× | ~6.4× | Performance critical |
| $\infty$ | $1 + L/B$ | $\infty$ | Theoretical maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unrolling is most effective when **loop overhead $L$ is a large fraction of body $B$**. For DSP inner loops, $B$ is large (the MAC) and $L$ is small (just the branch), so unrolling yields only modest gains. **The MAC + AGU combination is more effective than unrolling for DSP**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Total Cycles for an N-Tap FIR with Circular Buffer + AGU

### Problem Statement
Compute the total cycles to process $M$ output samples of an N-tap FIR on a DSP with **MAC + AGU + circular buffer support**. Each tap:
- Loads $h[k]$ from program memory (P-bus)
- Loads $x[n-k]$ from data memory (D-bus, via AGU)
- Performs MAC
- AGU updates $x$-pointer with modulo wrap

Assume: 1 cycle for the MAC+AGU pair, 1 cycle for loop overhead.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cycles per output sample:**
$$T_{\text{per-sample}} = N \cdot T_{\text{MAC+AGU}} + T_{\text{loop}} = N \cdot 1 + 1 = N + 1 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Cycles for M output samples (block processing):**

For M output samples, the loop runs M times with inner N-tap loop. With perfect pipelining:
$$T_{\text{total}} = M \cdot (N + 1) \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Add block-level overhead (e.g., pre-loading initial pointer):**
$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{setup}} + M(N + 1)$$

For a typical setup of 4 cycles:
$$T_{\text{total}} = 4 + M(N + 1) \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check:**
For $N = 64, M = 1024$:
$$T_{\text{total}} = 4 + 1024 \cdot 65 = 4 + 66{,}560 = 66{,}564 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 5 — At 100 MHz DSP clock:**
$$t_{\text{process}} = \frac{66{,}564}{100 \times 10^6} = 665.64 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum sample rate:**
$$f_{\text{s,max}} = \frac{1}{N+1} \cdot f_{\text{clock}} = \frac{100 \text{ MHz}}{65} \approx 1.538 \text{ MSPS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Without AGU**, each tap would need 2 extra cycles for address calculation:
> $$f_{\text{s,max,no-AGU}} = \frac{100 \text{ MHz}}{(2 \cdot 64) + 1} \approx 775 \text{ kSPS}$$
> AGU nearly **doubles** the achievable sample rate.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DSP Core with AGU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU [DSP Core CPU]
        PC[Program Counter]
        IF[Instruction Fetch Unit]
        DEC[Instruction Decoder]
        CTRL[Control Unit]
    end

    subgraph DP [Main Datapath]
        MAC[MAC Unit]
        ALU[ALU]
        ACC[Accumulator Register File]
    end

    subgraph AGU1 [AGU 1 - X Memory]
        ARF1[Address Register File]
        AAU1[Address Arithmetic Unit]
        MOD1[Modulo Logic]
        OUT1[Address Output 1]
    end

    subgraph AGU2 [AGU 2 - Y Memory]
        ARF2[Address Register File]
        AAU2[Address Arithmetic Unit]
        MOD2[Modulo Logic]
        OUT2[Address Output 2]
    end

    subgraph MEM [Memory Subsystem]
        PMEM[Program Memory]
        XMEM[X Data Memory]
        YMEM[Y Data Memory]
    end

    PC --> IF
    IF --> PMEM
    PMEM --> DEC
    DEC --> CTRL
    CTRL --> MAC
    CTRL --> ALU
    CTRL --> ARF1
    CTRL --> ARF2
    ARF1 --> AAU1
    AAU1 --> MOD1
    MOD1 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> XMEM
    ARF2 --> AAU2
    AAU2 --> MOD2
    MOD2 --> OUT2
    OUT2 --> YMEM
    XMEM --> MAC
    YMEM --> MAC
    MAC --> ACC
    ACC --> ALU
    ALU --> XMEM
    ALU --> YMEM
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU** fetches instructions from program memory via the PC.
- The decoded instruction triggers the **MAC** and **both AGUs in parallel**.
- **AGU 1** addresses the X-memory; **AGU 2** addresses the Y-memory.
- Both memories feed operands to the **MAC** in a single cycle.
- This is the **dual-memory Modified Harvard** architecture used in TMS320C6x, SHARC, etc.

---

## 4.2 Address Generation Flow for Circular Buffer Access

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Current Address R] --> B[Add Step Size]
    B --> C{Raw Sum Within Buffer Range}
    C -- Yes --> D[Emit Address EA]
    C -- No E1[Below BUF_START] --> F[Wrap Forward - Add N]
    C -- No E2[Above BUF_END] --> G[Wrap Back - Subtract N]
    F --> D
    G --> D
    D --> H[Update Register R equals EA]
    H --> I[Memory Access - Read Write Sample]
    I --> J[AGU Returns to Idle - 1 cycle total]
```

**Operation:**
- The AGU adds the step, checks bounds, and wraps *all in one cycle*.
- The CPU/MAC simultaneously processes the *current* data — no stall.

---

## 4.3 AGU Addressing Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[AGU Receives Addressing Mode Bits] --> Q1{Addressing Mode?}
    Q1 -- Mode 0 --> M0[Register Indirect - EA = R]
    Q1 -- Mode 1 --> M1[Post Increment - EA = R, R = R + 1]
    Q1 -- Mode 2 --> M2[Post Decrement - EA = R, R = R - 1]
    Q1 -- Mode 3 --> M3[Indexed - EA = R + Offset]
    Q1 -- Mode 4 --> M4[Modulo - EA = R + Step mod N]
    Q1 -- Mode 5 --> M5[Bit Reverse - EA = bitrev R]
    Q1 -- Mode 6 --> M6[Pre Modify - R = R + Step, EA = R]
    Q1 -- Mode 7 --> M7[Indexed with Post Increment]

    M0 --> OUT[Emit EA to Memory Bus]
    M1 --> OUT
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
    M5 --> OUT
    M6 --> OUT
    M7 --> OUT
```

---

## 4.4 FIR Filter Execution Timeline with AGU + MAC

```mermaid
gantt
    title FIR Filter Inner Loop - MAC and AGU Parallelism
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section MAC Unit
    Load h0 and x n     :a1, 0, 1
    MAC iteration 1     :a2, 1, 1
    MAC iteration 2     :a3, 2, 1
    MAC iteration 3     :a4, 3, 1
    MAC iteration N     :a5, 6, 1
    section AGU 1
    Compute addr x n 0  :b1, 0, 1
    Compute addr x n 1  :b2, 1, 1
    Compute addr x n 2  :b3, 2, 1
    Compute addr x n 3  :b4, 3, 1
    Compute addr x n N  :b5, 6, 1
    section AGU 2
    Compute addr h 0    :c1, 0, 1
    Compute addr h 1    :c2, 1, 1
    Compute addr h 2    :c3, 2, 1
    Compute addr h 3    :c4, 3, 1
    Compute addr h N    :c5, 6, 1
    section Loop Ctrl
    Branch overhead     :d1, 0, 1
    Counter update      :d2, 1, 1
    Branch overhead N   :d3, 6, 1
```

**Reading the Gantt chart:**
- The **MAC** and **AGUs** execute *in the same cycles* — they are pipelined.
- **No cycle is wasted** on address calculation.
- The **loop overhead** appears only *between* iterations.

---

## 4.5 Memory Map: Circular Buffer Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CB [Circular Buffer - Size 8, Base 0x1000]
        A0[0x1000 - Oldest Sample]
        A1[0x1001]
        A2[0x1002]
        A3[0x1003]
        A4[0x1004]
        A5[0x1005]
        A6[0x1006]
        A7[0x1007 - Newest Sample]
    end
    subgraph PTR [Pointer State]
        R[Register R - points to 0x1006]
    end
    R -.-> A6
    A7 -.->|next access wraps to 0x1000| A0
```

**Key insight:** When the AGU advances from `0x1007` to `0x1008`, it detects the overflow and subtracts $N=8$, yielding `0x1000` — the *oldest* sample, which will now be overwritten. This is the "sliding window" in zero software overhead.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Address Generation Unit (AGU) and list its key roles in a DSP processor.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**

**Model Answer:**

An **Address Generation Unit (AGU)** is a dedicated hardware sub-block in a DSP that computes memory operand addresses in parallel with the main datapath (ALU/MAC), thereby enabling single-cycle memory accesses for complex addressing patterns.

**Key roles:**
1. Computes effective addresses using registers, offsets, and step sizes.
2. Implements **modulo/circular** addressing for sliding-window filters.
3. Implements **bit-reversed** addressing for FFT I/O.
4. Runs **in parallel** with the MAC, so address computation does not stall the datapath.
5. Reduces loop overhead and supports **zero-overhead loops** in DSP kernels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word count guideline:** For a 3-mark question, target ~80–100 words. Mention parallelism (1 mark), circular addressing (1 mark), bit-reverse (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: List any three code-efficiency techniques used in embedded DSP programming.
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Sample Q2]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Loop unrolling** — replicates the loop body to amortize branch/counter overhead.
2. **Use of MAC and AGU intrinsics** — replaces general code with single-cycle hardware operations.
3. **Lookup tables (LUTs)** — pre-computes expensive functions (sin, log) at compile time.
4. **Fixed-point arithmetic** — replaces floating-point with integer math, ~4–10× faster.
5. **Circular buffer reuse** — eliminates data-copy overhead in sliding-window algorithms.

*(Any three well-explained techniques earn full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions for Module 3 typically span 14 marks with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each is split into (a) and (b), worth 7 marks each.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Question on AGU and Code Efficiency
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Question A(a):** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Address Generation Unit (AGU) in a DSP processor. List the various addressing modes supported. **(7 marks)**

**Question A(b):** Explain the role of AGU in implementing a circular buffer for an N-tap FIR filter. Show with a diagram how pointer wrap-around is achieved. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Architecture of the AGU (Block Diagram Description):**

The AGU consists of the following sub-blocks:

1. **Address Register File** — holds base/pointer registers (e.g., `R0` to `R7` in TMS320C6x).
2. **Address Arithmetic Unit (AAU)** — performs add, subtract, and shift on address operands.
3. **Modify Registers** — each address register has a paired modify register holding the step size.
4. **Index/Offset Registers** — provide a constant displacement for indexed addressing.
5. **Buffer Control Logic** — detects circular buffer bounds and applies wrap-around.
6. **Bit-Reverse Logic** — reverses the bit pattern of the index for FFT addressing.

**Addressing modes (must list at least 6 for full marks):**

| # | Mode | Formula | Application |
|---|------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Register Indirect | `EA = R` | Generic pointer access |
| 2 | Post-increment | `EA = R; R += step` | Linear streaming |
| 3 | Post-decrement | `EA = R; R -= step` | Reverse traversal |
| 4 | Indexed | `EA = R + offset` | Filter tap addressing |
| 5 | Modulo (Circular) | `EA = (R + step) mod N` | FIR/IIR circular buffer |
| 6 | Bit-reversed | `EA = bitrev(R)` | FFT |
| 7 | Pre-modify | `R += step; EA = R` | Synchronized advance |

**Key exam points to mention:**
- The AGU operates **in parallel** with the main datapath → no pipeline stall.
- The address computed in cycle T is used to fetch data in cycle T+1 (pipelined).
- Two AGUs in modern DSPs (e.g., SHARC) enable **dual-data fetch** in one cycle.

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Circular Buffer for N-tap FIR:**

A circular buffer holds the last N input samples $\{x[n], x[n-1], \dots, x[n-N+1]\}$. As each new sample arrives, the oldest is overwritten — without any data shifting.

**Pointer wrap-around mechanism:**

1. Allocate buffer of size $N = 2^k$ at base address `BUF_START`.
2. Initialize pointer `R = BUF_START`.
3. Each FIR access performs `EA = *R++` (post-increment by 1).
4. **Hardware check** in AGU:
   - If `R > BUF_END` (i.e., past end of buffer), wrap: `R = R - N`.
   - For power-of-2 $N$, this is `R = R & (N-1)` (bit-mask).
5. The wrap is **invisible to the software** — the FIR code simply reads `*R++` and the AGU ensures the address is always valid.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.5):** The pointer `R` cycles through `0x1000 → 0x1007 → 0x1000`, always reading the *correct* oldest sample when it wraps.

**Benefit:** Zero data movement, single-cycle access per tap → enables $T_{\text{FIR}} = N$ cycles per output sample.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3 Alt]**

**Question B(a):** Explain the concept of code efficiency in embedded DSP systems. Discuss at least four techniques with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Question B(b):** With a worked example, show how bit-reversed addressing in an AGU is used in an 8-point radix-2 FFT. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Code Efficiency — Definition:**
Code efficiency in embedded DSP is the optimization of program code to simultaneously minimize **execution time, memory footprint, and power consumption** while meeting real-time deadlines.

**Four Techniques:**

**1. Use of MAC Instruction + AGU (Single-Cycle MAC):**
A DSP's MAC with parallel AGU executes `y += a*b` *and* fetches the next operands in **one cycle**. Without it, a generic RISC needs 4–6 cycles per MAC — a **4–6× slowdown**.

**Example:** For a 64-tap FIR at 48 kHz audio:
- With MAC+AGU: $64 + 1 = 65$ cycles/sample → ~770 kSPS at 50 MHz
- Without: $64 \times 6 = 384$ cycles/sample → ~130 kSPS at 50 MHz

**2. Circular Buffer with AGU:**
Avoids the need to shift samples in memory after every new input. AGU's modulo logic handles the wrap-around in hardware.

**Example:** A 100-tap LMS adaptive filter uses one circular buffer; no `memmove()` call needed → saves $(N-1) \times 2$ memory ops per sample.

**3. Fixed-Point Arithmetic:**
Replaces 32-bit float with 16-bit (Q15) or 32-bit (Q31) integer math. Fits more data in cache, uses integer MAC units, and avoids IEEE 754 overhead.

**Example:** On TMS320C5509, Q15 MAC is 1 cycle; float MAC (via software library) is ~20 cycles. **~20× speedup**.

**4. Loop Unrolling & Software Pipelining:**
Unroll inner loops to expose instruction-level parallelism. The compiler/software-pipeliner can then schedule independent MACs to overlap.

**Example:** A loop with branch overhead $L=3$ and body $B=10$ sees a speedup of $1 + L/B = 1.3\times$ when fully unrolled.

**5. *(Optional) Lookup Tables:***
For non-linear functions like `sin(x)` in a Goertzel filter, an 8-bit LUT gives 1-cycle lookup instead of 30+ cycle Taylor expansion.

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**8-Point Radix-2 FFT with Bit-Reversed AGU:**

The Cooley-Tukey radix-2 FFT computes an 8-point DFT in 3 stages (log₂8 = 3) of butterflies. The input data must be in **bit-reversed order** (or the output must be — depending on the convention).

**Step 1 — Natural-order indices and their bit-reversal:**

| Index `k` (binary) | `bitrev(k)` | Reordered `k` |
|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 0 (`000`) | `000` | 0 |
| 1 (`001`) | `100` | 4 |
| 2 (`010`) | `010` | 2 |
| 3 (`011`) | `110` | 6 |
| 4 (`100`) | `001` | 1 |
| 5 (`101`) | `101` | 5 |
| 6 (`110`) | `011` | 3 |
| 7 (`111`) | `111` | 7 |

**Step 2 — AGU's role:**
The AGU's counter increments naturally from 0 to 7, but the **emitted address** is `bitrev(counter)`. This means the FFT's input read pattern (which *requires* bit-reversed order) is satisfied *automatically* by the AGU.

**Step 3 — Worked example — first two reads:**

- Cycle 1: AGU counter = 0 → emitted address = `bitrev(0) = 0` → reads `x[0]`
- Cycle 2: AGU counter = 1 → emitted address = `bitrev(1) = 4` → reads `x[4]`
- Cycle 3: AGU counter = 2 → emitted address = `bitrev(2) = 2` → reads `x[2]`
- ...

**Step 4 — Cost saving:**

Without AGU bit-reverse mode, a separate shuffle pass would require:
- Either $N \cdot \log_2(N) = 8 \cdot 3 = 24$ memory accesses to a lookup table
- Or $(N/2) \cdot \log_2(N) = 12$ in-place swaps

With AGU bit-reverse mode: **0 extra cycles** — the addressing is computed in parallel with each butterfly MAC.

**Step 5 — Computational cost of FFT itself:**

$$T_{\text{FFT}} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot \log_2(N) \text{ complex butterflies} = \frac{8}{2} \cdot 3 = 12 \text{ butterflies}$$

Each butterfly has 1 complex MAC (4 real MACs) → 48 real MACs total. With AGU supplying all addresses in parallel, each butterfly takes 1 cycle → 12 cycles for the FFT (ignoring twiddle factor loads).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on AGU/Code Efficiency questions:**

1. **Forgetting to say "in parallel"** — describing the AGU as a sequential unit loses 1–2 marks. Always emphasize that the AGU's address computation overlaps with the MAC/ALU cycle.

2. **Confusing "post-increment" with "pre-increment"** — many students write `R = R+1; EA = R` when the correct post-increment is `EA = R; R = R+1`. The order matters for boundary cases.

3. **Modulo addressing: not mentioning power-of-2 optimization** — the hardware implementation uses `& (N-1)`, not `% N`. Stating only the mathematical formula loses the hardware-insight mark.

4. **Bit-reversal: confusing "input bit-reversed" with "output bit-reversed"** — both are valid FFT conventions; the answer must specify *which* one is being implemented (Decimation-in-Time vs Decimation-in-Frequency).

5. **Loop unrolling: forgetting the code-size tradeoff** — answers that say "unroll completely for maximum speed" without noting the I-cache/flash cost lose 1 mark.

6. **Missing the MAC↔AGU connection** — a question asking "why is the FIR inner loop fast on a DSP?" requires mentioning **both** the single-cycle MAC *and* the AGU's parallel address calculation. Mentioning only one earns partial credit.

7. **Using `%` for modulo on non-power-of-2 N** — the AGU's hardware modulo only works for power-of-2 buffer sizes. For other sizes, software emulation is needed. This subtlety is a favorite KTU trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [x] **AGU = Address Generation Unit** — dedicated hardware for memory address computation, runs in parallel with MAC/ALU.
- [x] **Three Pillars of Code Efficiency** — speed, size, power. All must be optimized simultaneously.
- [x] **MAC is the heart of DSP** — single-cycle `a*b + acc`, requires two parallel operand fetches.
- [x] **AGU enables single-cycle memory access** for non-trivial patterns (circular, bit-reverse).
- [x] **Circular buffer** = sliding window in fixed memory, using `mod N` arithmetic; with power-of-2 N, becomes a single `&` operation.
- [x] **Bit-reversed addressing** is essential for FFT I/O, saving $N \cdot \log_2(N)$ shuffle cycles.
- [x] **Modified Harvard architecture** with separate P, X, Y memories enables dual-fetch in one cycle.
- [x] **Loop unrolling** trades code size for speed; bounded by $1 + L/B$ speedup maximum.
- [x] **Fixed-point > floating-point** on most low-end DSPs (4–10× speedup).
- [x] **Two AGUs** in modern DSPs (TMS320C6x, SHARC) allow dual-data fetch per cycle.
- [x] **Arithmetic Intensity (AI)** = ops per byte; higher AI = more compute-bound, less memory-bound.
- [x] **Cycles per FIR tap with MAC+AGU = 1**; without AGU = 2–3; without MAC = 5–6.
- [x] **KTU mark-earning phrases**: "in parallel with the MAC", "modulo addressing via bit-mask", "zero-overhead loop", "bit-reversal in hardware".
- [x] **Real-world applications** to mention: audio codecs, 5G baseband, MRI/ultrasound, motor control, radar.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If you remember just **one formula** for this module, remember:
> $$\boxed{\text{Cycles per FIR tap} = 1 \; (\text{MAC} + \text{AGU})}$$
> This single fact anchors most of the KTU Module 3 questions on DSP code efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
