topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RMS Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a *static-priority*, *preemptive* scheduling algorithm used in real-time and embedded systems to schedule a set of periodic tasks on a single processor. It was formally introduced by Liu and Layland (1973) in their seminal paper *"Scheduling Algorithms for Multiprogramming in a Hard Real-Time Environment"*.

Under RMS, **each periodic task is assigned a fixed priority that is inversely proportional to its period** — the task with the **shortest period (highest execution frequency)** receives the **highest priority**, and priorities never change dynamically at runtime. The scheduler is invoked whenever a task completes or a new task instance (job) is released, and the highest-priority ready task is always selected for execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition (verbatim syllabus terminology):**
> *"Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a fixed-priority preemptive scheduling algorithm in which task priorities are assigned based on task periods — shorter period implies higher priority. The algorithm is optimal among all fixed-priority algorithms for independent, preemptable, periodic tasks with deadlines equal to periods."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Hospital Emergency Triage Room

Imagine the emergency room of a busy government hospital. Patients (tasks) keep arriving at different intervals:

- A **heart-attack patient** may arrive **every few minutes** (short period, life-critical).
- A **fever case** may arrive **every few hours** (longer period, less critical).
- A **routine dressing change** may come **once a day** (long period, non-critical).

The triage nurse applies the **Rate Monotonic principle** naturally:
1. She **ranks patients by how frequently they need help**, not by when they arrived.
2. The most frequent, most critical case is **always seen first** if a doctor is free.
3. If a low-priority patient is being treated and a heart-attack case arrives, the low-priority patient is **preempted** (treatment paused).
4. Once the critical case is handled, the preempted patient **resumes** exactly where they left off.

This is **exactly** how RMS schedules CPU tasks on an embedded processor.

| Hospital Analogy | RMS Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Patient arrival rate | Task period $T_i$ |
| Heart-attack patient (arrives often) | Short period → **highest priority** |
| Fever case (arrives less often) | Longer period → **lower priority** |
| Preempting a routine checkup | **Preemptive dispatch** by higher-priority task |
| Treatment duration | Task execution time $C_i$ (or $e_i$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of RMS (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> 1. **Static priorities** — assigned *a priori* and never changed.
> 2. **Preemptive** — higher-priority task can interrupt a running lower-priority task.
> 3. **Optimal** among all static-priority algorithms (for the assumptions stated).
> 4. **Independent tasks** — no shared resources, no precedence constraints.
> 5. **Deadline = Period** — $D_i = T_i$ for every task $\tau_i$.
> 6. **Fixed known execution times** — $C_i$ is constant and known.

## 1.3 Underlying Assumptions (Liu–Layland Model)

RMS guarantees schedulability only under the following idealized conditions:

1. All tasks are **periodic** with constant inter-arrival times.
2. Tasks are **independent** (no shared data, no mutual exclusion, no communication).
3. The **deadline** of each task equals its **period** ($D_i = T_i$).
4. The **worst-case execution time (WCET)** $C_i$ is known and constant.
5. Tasks are **preemptable** at any instant with zero context-switch overhead.
6. The system has a **single processor** and no other load.
7. Tasks have **no phasing offset** (or equivalently, all tasks are released simultaneously at $t=0$ — the *critical instant*).

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical instant** for any task under RMS is the time at which all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with it. The response time at the critical instant is the **worst-case response time** for that task.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How RMS Works Step-by-Step

RMS scheduling operates in the following structured sequence at runtime:

1. **Design-time priority assignment:** For every task $\tau_i$ in the system, compute its period $T_i$. Sort tasks in **ascending order of period** and assign priorities $P_1 > P_2 > \dots > P_n$, where $\tau_1$ is the task with the smallest period.

2. **Hyperperiod construction:** Compute the **least common multiple (LCM)** of all task periods, called the *hyperperiod* $H$:
$$H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$$
The schedule repeats identically every $H$ time units, so schedulability analysis only needs to be performed over the interval $[0, H]$.

3. **Critical instant analysis:** To check whether a task will *ever* miss its deadline, it suffices to analyze the **worst-case** scenario in which the task is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks (critical instant).

4. **Runtime dispatch:** Whenever the CPU becomes idle, the scheduler selects the **ready** task with the **highest fixed priority**. If a new job of a higher-priority task is released during the execution of a lower-priority job, the lower-priority job is **immediately preempted**.

5. **Deadline verification:** A job of task $\tau_i$ must complete its $C_i$ units of execution within $T_i$ time units from its release. If all tasks meet this constraint, the system is **schedulable** under RMS.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every formula, symbol, and unit you must know for RMS-related KTU questions. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--|:--:|:--|:--|
| 1 | Task index set | $i$ | $i \in \lbrace 1, 2, \dots, n \rbrace$ | Number of tasks $n \geq 1$ |
| 2 | Period of task $\tau_i$ | $T_i$ | Given (design input) | Time units (ms, µs) |
| 3 | Worst-case execution time | $C_i$ | Given (design input) | Time units |
| 4 | Utilization of task $\tau_i$ | $u_i$ | $u_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq u_i \leq 1$ |
| 5 | **Total processor utilization** | $U$ | $U = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq U \leq 1$ |
| 6 | **Liu–Layland upper bound** | $U_{\text{lub}}(n)$ | $U_{\text{lub}}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | As $n \to \infty$, $U_{\text{lub}} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |
| 7 | Sufficient schedulability test | — | If $U \leq U_{\text{lub}}(n)$, then system is **schedulable** | Sufficient, **not necessary** |
| 8 | Necessary condition | — | If $U > 1$, the system is **unschedulable** | Necessary, not sufficient |
| 9 | Hyperperiod | $H$ | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ | Time units |
| 10 | Maximum response time | $R_i^{\max}$ | Found by iterative fixed-point of $W_i(t)$ | Must satisfy $R_i^{\max} \leq D_i$ |
| 11 | Worst-case interference on $\tau_i$ | $W_i(t)$ | $W_i(t) = C_i + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $hp(i)$ = set of higher-priority tasks |
| 12 | Response-time recurrence | $R_i$ | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterate until convergence or $R_i > D_i$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU board-exam pitfall:** The bound $U_{\text{lub}}(n)$ is a **sufficient** condition, **not necessary**. A task set with utilization *higher* than the bound may still be schedulable. Always pair this with the **exact Response-Time Analysis (RTA)** for a precise verdict.

## 2.3 Utilization-Bound Table (Memorize This!)

| Number of Tasks $n$ | $U_{\text{lub}}(n) = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Approx. Decimal |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | $1 \cdot (2 - 1)$ | **1.000** |
| 2 | $2 \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1)$ | **0.828** |
| 3 | $3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$ | **0.780** |
| 4 | $4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1)$ | **0.757** |
| 5 | $5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1)$ | **0.743** |
| 6 | $6 \cdot (2^{1/6} - 1)$ | **0.735** |
| 7 | $7 \cdot (2^{1/7} - 1)$ | **0.729** |
| 8 | $8 \cdot (2^{1/8} - 1)$ | **0.724** |
| 9 | $9 \cdot (2^{1/9} - 1)$ | **0.721** |
| 10 | $10 \cdot (2^{1/10} - 1)$ | **0.718** |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | **0.693** |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Embedded Engineering

RMS is the **de-facto scheduling policy** in many certified real-time operating systems and industry standards:

- **Avionics:** ARINC 653 / Integrated Modular Avionics — fixed-priority partitions.
- **Automotive:** OSEK/VDX and AUTOSAR OS — tasks scheduled using fixed priorities that mirror RMS principles.
- **Spacecraft:** NASA's VxWorks and RTEMS-based flight software.
- **Industrial control:** PLC scan cycles and DSP/FPGA interrupt priorities.
- **Consumer electronics:** RTOS kernels in set-top boxes, printers, and IoT gateways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RMS dominates in practice:** Even though Earliest Deadline First (EDF) is *theoretically* optimal (achieving 100% utilization), RMS is preferred in commercial systems because:
> 1. **Predictable cache behavior** — fixed priorities mean the same task is always picked, reducing cache pollution.
> 2. **Lower scheduling overhead** — no need to recompute deadlines at every tick.
> 3. **Easier certification** (DO-178C, ISO 26262) — deterministic behavior aids formal verification.
> 4. **Simpler kernel implementation** — a fixed priority queue suffices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Liu–Layland Utilization Bound

The bound $U_{\text{lub}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ was proved by Liu and Layland using the concept of the **critical instant**. The reasoning proceeds as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the worst-case scenario.**
For a given task $\tau_k$, the worst-case completion time occurs at the *critical instant* when $\tau_k$ is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks. Let $hp(k)$ denote the index set of higher-priority tasks.

**Step 2 — Bound the interference from higher-priority tasks.**
The number of jobs of a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ that can preempt $\tau_k$ within any interval of length $t$ is:
$$N_j(t) = \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

The total interference from all higher-priority tasks in time $t$ is at most:
$$I(t) = \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case response time inequality.**
For $\tau_k$ to be schedulable, the worst-case response time $R_k$ (a fixed point of the recurrence) must satisfy:
$$R_k \leq C_k + \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 4 — Necessary condition for schedulability.**
The system is schedulable only if total utilization $U \leq 1$. The Liu–Layland bound refines this: for the *worst-possible* (highest-density) task set that still meets deadlines, the maximum achievable utilization with $n$ fixed-priority tasks is exactly:
$$U_{\max}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

This can be derived by parameterizing the task periods. Let $T_i = 2^{1-i/n} \cdot T_{\max}$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$, and $C_i = T_i - T_{i+1}$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= T_i - T_{i+1} \\
&= 2^{1-i/n} T_{\max} - 2^{1-(i+1)/n} T_{\max} \\
&= T_{\max} \cdot 2^{1-i/n} \left(1 - 2^{-1/n}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The utilization contribution of task $\tau_i$ is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_i &= \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{T_{\max} \cdot 2^{1-i/n}\left(1 - 2^{-1/n}\right)}{T_{\max} \cdot 2^{1-i/n}} = 1 - 2^{-1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing over all $n$ tasks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{lub}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - 2^{-1/n}\right) = n \left(1 - 2^{-1/n}\right) = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) \cdot 2^{-1/n} \cdot 2^{1/n} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The simplified final expression (note: $1 - 2^{-1/n} = 2^{-1/n}(2^{1/n} - 1)$ which when summed gives the closed form):
$$U_{\text{lub}}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Limit as $n \to \infty$.**
Using the identity $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{lub}}(\infty) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\
&= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n} \\
&= \left. \frac{d}{dx} 2^{x} \right\vert_{x=0} \\
&= 2^{0} \ln 2 \\
&= \ln 2 \\
&\approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

So no matter how many tasks exist, RMS can guarantee schedulability of independent periodic tasks up to a **maximum utilization of $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Sufficient Test with $n=3$

**Problem:** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task $\tau_i$ | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\tau_1$ | 5 | 1 |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_3$ | 20 | 4 |

Determine whether the task set is schedulable under RMS.

**Step 1 — Priority assignment (ascending period = highest priority first).**
$\tau_1$ has the smallest period, so it gets the highest priority, followed by $\tau_2$, then $\tau_3$.

**Step 2 — Compute per-task utilization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200 \\
u_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.200 \\
u_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total utilization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.200 = 0.600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{lub}}(3) &= 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \\
&= 3 \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) \\
&= 3 \times 0.2599 \\
&\approx 0.780
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**
Since $U = 0.600 \leq U_{\text{lub}}(3) \approx 0.780$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS** by the sufficient test. ✅

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Utilization Exceeds Bound, but RTA still saves it

**Problem:** Two tasks with $(T_1, C_1) = (2, 1)$ and $(T_2, C_2) = (5, 2)$.

**Step 1 — Priority assignment.**
$\tau_1$ (period 2) has higher priority than $\tau_2$ (period 5).

**Step 2 — Total utilization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{5} = 0.500 + 0.400 = 0.900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with bound.**
$U_{\text{lub}}(2) = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.828$. Since $0.900 > 0.828$, the **Liu–Layland sufficient test is inconclusive**.

**Step 4 — Apply exact Response-Time Analysis for $\tau_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{2} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(1)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{2} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 2 = 4 \\
R_2^{(3)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(2)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{2} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 2 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The recurrence has converged at $R_2 = 4$ ms. Since $R_2 = 4 \leq D_2 = T_2 = 5$, **the task set IS schedulable**, even though utilization exceeds the Liu–Layland bound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key teaching point:** The Liu–Layland bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. Always use RTA for the exact verdict in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RMS vs EDF vs DMS

| Criterion | Rate Monotonic (RMS) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Deadline Monotonic (DMS) |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Priority type | Static (period-based) | Dynamic (deadline-based) | Static (deadline-based) |
| Optimality | Optimal among fixed-priority schemes | Optimal overall (theoretical 100%) | Optimal when $D_i \leq T_i$ |
| Maximum guaranteed utilization | $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$ | $100\%$ | $\leq \ln 2$ (less than RMS in general) |
| Runtime overhead | Low (priority queue) | High (sort deadlines each tick) | Low (priority queue) |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Moderate | Simple |
| Cache performance | Excellent (predictable) | Poor (frequent switches) | Excellent (predictable) |
| Used in | AUTOSAR, ARINC 653, VxWorks | Research / soft real-time | Hard RT with $D_i < T_i$ |
| Supports $D_i < T_i$? | No (assumes $D_i = T_i$) | Yes | Yes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RMS Scheduler

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the static and dynamic phases of an RMS-based real-time kernel. The system is decomposed into **offline design components** (priority assignment, schedulability test) and **online runtime components** (release, dispatch, preemption, completion).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Task Set Definition] --> B0[Measure Periods T_i and WCET C_i]
    B0 --> C0[Sort Tasks by Period Ascending]
    C0 --> D0[Assign Static Priorities P1 gt P2 gt ... gt Pn]
    D0 --> E0[Schedulability Test using Liu Layland Bound]
    E0 --> F0{Schedulable?}
    F0 -- No --> G0[Redesign Tasks Reduce C_i or Increase T_i]
    G0 --> B0
    F0 -- Yes --> H0[Build Priority Queue in RTOS Kernel]
    H0 --> I0[System Start t equals 0]
    I0 --> J0[Release All Tasks at Critical Instant]
    J0 --> K0[Scheduler Selects Highest Priority Ready Task]
    K0 --> L0[Dispatch Task to CPU]
    L0 --> M0{New Job of Higher Priority Released?}
    M0 -- Yes --> N0[Preempt Running Task Save Context]
    N0 --> K0
    M0 -- No --> O0[Task Continues Execution]
    O0 --> P0{Task Completed?}
    P0 -- No --> O0
    P0 -- Yes --> Q0[Update Ready Queue]
    Q0 --> R0{All Deadlines Met in Hyperperiod?}
    R0 -- No --> S0[Deadline Miss Logged System Failure]
    R0 -- Yes --> T0[Schedule Repeats for Next Hyperperiod]
    T0 --> I0
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram of a Task under RMS

The following diagram shows how an individual task $\tau_i$ transitions through its lifecycle states under a preemptive RMS scheduler.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Suspended
    Suspended --> Ready : Time t equals k times T_i New job released
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler dispatches highest priority task
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by higher priority job
    Running --> Completed : Execution finished at C_i units
    Completed --> Suspended : Wait for next period
    Completed --> [*] : Final job done
    Ready --> [*] : Deadline missed system failure
```

## 4.3 Timeline Gantt Chart of Example 1 (Conceptual)

A textual Gantt representation of the schedule for Example 1 over the first hyperperiod $H = \text{lcm}(5, 10, 20) = 20$ ms.

```mermaid
gantt
    title RMS Gantt Chart for Example 1 (Hyperperiod 20 ms)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section Tau1 P1
    Job1_1 :a1, 0, 1
    Job1_2 :a2, 5, 1
    Job1_3 :a3, 10, 1
    Job1_4 :a4, 15, 1
    section Tau2 P2
    Job2_1 :b1, 1, 2
    Job2_2 :b2, 11, 2
    section Tau3 P3
    Job3_1 :c1, 3, 4
    Job3_2 :c2, 13, 4
```

**Reading the chart:** At $t = 0$, all three tasks are released. $\tau_1$ runs first (0–1), then $\tau_2$ (1–3), then $\tau_3$ (3–7). At $t=5$, a new $\tau_1$ job preempts, and so on. Every task completes before its deadline. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid's Gantt syntax uses abstract time units. The diagram above is a **conceptual trace**; in KTU exams, you should draw a similar timeline on paper with clear time markings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]
**State the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm. Mention the four key assumptions made by the Liu–Layland model.**

**Model Answer:**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) is a **static-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm in which each periodic task is assigned a fixed priority inversely proportional to its period — the task with the **shortest period gets the highest priority**. The Liu–Layland model assumes:

1. All tasks are **periodic** and independent.
2. The **deadline equals the period** ($D_i = T_i$).
3. The **worst-case execution time $C_i$** is known and constant.
4. Tasks are **preemptable** at any time with negligible context-switch cost, on a single processor. *(3 marks)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Liu–Layland utilization bound for $n$ tasks? What is its limiting value as $n \to \infty$?**

**Model Answer:**

The Liu–Layland utilization bound is given by:
$$U_{\text{lub}}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

As $n \to \infty$, this expression approaches:
$$U_{\text{lub}}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$$

This means that RMS can **guarantee** schedulability of any independent periodic task set only if its total utilization does not exceed approximately **69.3%** in the worst case. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) Module questions of 14 marks provide an **internal choice** — students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Both options below are designed to be of equivalent difficulty and follow the KTU 2024 Scheme blueprint (typically two 7-mark sub-parts each).

### Question A (14 Marks) — Schedulability Numerical + RMS Properties

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]
**Consider three real-time tasks with the following parameters:**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\tau_1$ | 4 | 1 |
| $\tau_2$ | 6 | 1 |
| $\tau_3$ | 12 | 4 |

**Using the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm:**
1. Assign the priorities to the tasks.
2. Compute the total processor utilization and compare it with the Liu–Layland bound.
3. Determine whether the task set is schedulable. Justify your answer.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Priority Assignment (1 mark):**
Since priority is inversely proportional to period, the ascending-period order gives:
$\tau_1$ (T = 4) → highest priority,
$\tau_2$ (T = 6) → second priority,
$\tau_3$ (T = 12) → lowest priority.

**(2) Utilization computation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 \\
u_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167 \\
u_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{4}{12} \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= u_1 + u_2 + u_3 = 0.250 + 0.167 + 0.333 = 0.750
\end{aligned}
$$

Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$:
$$U_{\text{lub}}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.780$$

**(3) Schedulability decision (3 marks):**
Since $U = 0.750 \leq U_{\text{lub}}(3) \approx 0.780$, the sufficient test **PASSES**, and the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. ✅

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Explain the concept of "critical instant" in RMS. Why is the worst-case response time of a task observed at the critical instant?**

**Model Answer (key points):**

A **critical instant** for a task $\tau_k$ is the moment of time at which $\tau_k$ is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks (and these higher-priority tasks are also released simultaneously with each other). This produces the **maximum amount of preemption interference** that $\tau_k$ can ever experience from higher-priority jobs.

**Why it yields the worst-case response time:** *(7 marks broken down below)*

1. If higher-priority tasks are released **before** the release of $\tau_k$, then by the time $\tau_k$ starts, some of those jobs may have already completed, reducing the queue length. *(1 mark)*

2. If higher-priority tasks are released **after** the release of $\tau_k$, then $\tau_k$ can finish *before* being preempted. *(1 mark)*

3. Only when **all** higher-priority tasks are released at the **same instant** as $\tau_k$ do we get the **maximum number of pending higher-priority jobs** that can pile up and preempt $\tau_k$. *(2 marks)*

4. Furthermore, when these higher-priority tasks are also released **simultaneously with each other** at the critical instant, they are also **mutually out of phase by zero** in the *same* direction relative to $\tau_k$, maximizing cumulative interference. *(1 mark)*

5. Hence the response time of $\tau_k$ at the critical instant is the **upper bound** on its response time for all $t \geq 0$. If $\tau_k$ meets its deadline at the critical instant, it meets it at *every* release. *(1 mark)*

6. **Mathematically**, the response-time recurrence captures this:
$$R_k = C_k + \sum_{j \in hp(k)} \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
The $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ terms count the maximum number of pre-empting jobs in a window of length $R_k$, which is maximized at the critical instant. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — RTA Numerical + EDF Comparison

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Consider two real-time tasks: $\tau_1 = (T_1 = 4, C_1 = 2)$ and $\tau_2 = (T_2 = 10, C_2 = 4)$.**
1. Determine the priorities under RMS.
2. Compute total utilization and check Liu–Layland sufficient condition.
3. If the bound is inconclusive, perform **exact Response-Time Analysis** for $\tau_2$.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Priorities (1 mark):** $\tau_1$ (T = 4) is highest priority; $\tau_2$ (T = 10) is lowest priority.

**(2) Utilization & bound (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{4} + \frac{4}{10} = 0.500 + 0.400 = 0.900 \\
U_{\text{lub}}(2) &= 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 2 \times 0.4142 \approx 0.828
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.900 > 0.828$, the **sufficient test is inconclusive**.

**(3) Exact Response-Time Analysis for $\tau_2$ (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 4 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil \times 2 = 4 + 1 \times 2 = 6 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(1)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \times 2 = 4 + 2 \times 2 = 8 \\
R_2^{(3)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(2)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{4} \right\rceil \times 2 = 4 + 2 \times 2 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

The recurrence has converged: $R_2 = 8$ ms. Since $R_2 = 8 \leq D_2 = T_2 = 10$, **the task set is schedulable**. ✅

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Compare Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) with Earliest Deadline First (EDF). State clearly which one is theoretically optimal and why RMS is preferred in commercial RTOS design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RMS | EDF |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Priority type | Fixed (based on period) | Dynamic (based on nearest deadline) |
| Optimality | Optimal among static-priority | **Theoretically optimal overall** (achieves $U \leq 1$) |
| Utilization guarantee | $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ in the worst case | Up to $1.0$ (i.e., $100\%$) |
| Overhead | Low (priority queue) | High (sorting deadlines at every event) |
| Implementation | Simple | More complex |

**Key reasoning points: (7 marks)**

1. **Theoretical optimality:** EDF is the **provably optimal** uniprocessor scheduling algorithm for independent, preemptable periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$. It can schedule *any* task set with $U \leq 1$. *(2 marks)*

2. **Why RMS is preferred commercially:** *(3 marks)*
   - RMS priorities are **assigned offline once**, requiring only a static priority queue at runtime.
   - EDF requires a **sorted deadline queue** updated at every task release, completion, and tick — increasing CPU overhead and memory traffic.
   - RMS exhibits **better cache locality**: the same task is repeatedly scheduled, so its instructions and data stay in cache. EDF's constant task switching causes **cache thrashing**.
   - RMS is **easier to certify** under standards like DO-178C, ISO 26262, IEC 61508 because the worst-case behavior is highly predictable.
   - RMS has **lower interrupt latency** — no need to recompute the next task's deadline during an interrupt service routine.

3. **When to choose what:** Use RMS when the system is hard real-time, safety-critical, has a small number of well-characterized tasks, and $U \leq 0.7$ is achievable. Use EDF for soft real-time or high-utilization servers where dynamic decisions are acceptable. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE MARKS in RMS questions (READ CAREFULLY):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "schedulable" with "utilization below bound":** The Liu–Layland bound is **sufficient, not necessary**. A task set with $U > U_{\text{lub}}(n)$ may still be schedulable. Marks are lost if the student concludes "unschedulable" without trying RTA.
>
> 2. **Wrong priority assignment:** Students sometimes assign priority in the *descending* order of period (i.e., longest period = highest priority). This is the **opposite** of RMS. The shortest period MUST get the highest priority.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$** in the response-time recurrence. Without it, the analysis under-counts preemption jobs and gives an *optimistic* (unsafe) result.
>
> 4. **Convergence trap:** Students stop the RTA iteration after the *first* step or forget to check that the value has stopped increasing. The recurrence converges only when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$.
>
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing ms with µs. Always state and use a **single consistent time unit** throughout the calculation.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the necessary condition $U \leq 1$:** If $U > 1$, the system is *definitely* unschedulable under ANY algorithm, RMS or EDF. State this as a quick sanity check first.
>
> 7. **Confusing RMS with EDF in comparison answers:** Several students write "RMS is optimal" — correct only in the limited sense of being optimal *among fixed-priority* algorithms. EDF is the truly optimal uniprocessor scheduler.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision summary — go through this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- [x] **RMS = Rate Monotonic Scheduling** — static-priority, preemptive, period-based priority assignment.
- [x] **Priority rule:** Shortest period → highest priority. Memorize this as the *defining* property.
- [x] **Assumptions:** periodic, independent tasks; $D_i = T_i$; known WCET; preemptable; single processor.
- [x] **Critical instant:** simultaneous release of $\tau_k$ with all higher-priority tasks → worst-case response.
- [x] **Liu–Layland bound (sufficient test):**
  $$U_{\text{lub}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$
- [x] **Key numerical values to memorize:**
  $U_{\text{lub}}(1) = 1.000$, $U_{\text{lub}}(2) \approx 0.828$, $U_{\text{lub}}(3) \approx 0.780$, $U_{\text{lub}}(\infty) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- [x] **Optimality:** RMS is optimal *among fixed-priority* algorithms, not overall. EDF is the overall optimum.
- [x] **Sufficient vs. necessary:** $U \leq U_{\text{lub}}$ → schedulable. $U > U_{\text{lub}}$ → inconclusive (use RTA). $U > 1$ → definitely unschedulable.
- [x] **Response-Time Analysis (RTA) recurrence:**
  $$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
  Iterate until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$; then check $R_i \leq D_i$.
- [x] **Hyperperiod:** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$. Schedule repeats every $H$ units.
- [x] **RMS vs EDF:** RMS = low overhead + cache-friendly + certifiable; EDF = higher utilization ($U \leq 1$) + higher runtime cost.
- [x] **Practical use:** AUTOSAR OS, ARINC 653, VxWorks, RTEMS, FreeRTOS (configurable fixed priorities).
- [x] **Common exam traps:** wrong priority direction, missing $\lceil \cdot \rceil$, premature RTA termination, unit mismatch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EDF Algorithm and Resource Constraint Issue, Priority Inversion and Priority Inheritance Protocol, Priority Ceiling Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT751 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EDF Algorithm and Resource Constraint Issue, Priority Inversion and Priority Inheritance Protocol, Priority Ceiling Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Earliest Deadline First (EDF) Algorithm

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Earliest Deadline First (EDF) is a **dynamic-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm** for real-time systems in which, at every scheduling decision instant, the task whose absolute deadline is closest (earliest) in time is selected for execution on the processor. EDF is provably optimal for uniprocessor scheduling of **independent** periodic and aperiodic tasks when the processor utilization $U \le 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** EDF is the canonical example of a *dynamic-priority* algorithm. The fixed-priority counterpart is **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**. Both are examinable contrasts.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Queue:**
Imagine a busy airport security checkpoint with a single X-ray machine. A steady stream of passengers arrives, each holding a boarding pass. The boarding pass shows a *departure time*. The security officer practices EDF: whichever passenger's flight leaves *soonest* gets pushed to the front of the line. A passenger whose flight is in 10 minutes will be served before one whose flight is in 2 hours, regardless of who arrived first. The *arrival order* is irrelevant — only the *deadline* matters. If the officer keeps doing this and the total scanning time of all remaining passengers is less than the time until the next flight's deadline, everyone catches their flight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Term — Absolute Deadline ($d_i$):** $d_i = a_i + D_i$, where $a_i$ is arrival time and $D_i$ is relative deadline. The "earliest" of these is what EDF selects.

---

### 1.2 Resource Constraint Issue in Real-Time Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A *resource constraint* arises when two or more tasks require **mutually exclusive access** to a shared, non-preemptible resource (e.g., a serial bus, a sensor, a memory-mapped I/O port). Because the resource is **non-shareable**, only one task can hold it at any instant. This forces the scheduler to consider not just computation time but also **resource-holding intervals** when making preemptive decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus terms this as the **"Resource Constraint Issue"** — the scheduler's static plan can be invalidated by an unbounded resource-holding delay, causing deadlines to be missed even when the *computed* utilization is well below 1.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Single-Stall Restroom on a Construction Site:**
Three crews work on a building. Crew A is finishing the electrical wiring, Crew B is doing the plastering, and Crew C is installing windows. They must all share the *single portable toilet* (the resource). A project manager using EDF might say, "Whoever's deadline is closest goes first into the toilet." But what if Crew A is already inside (holding the resource) when Crew B's deadline becomes urgent? The manager cannot *yank* Crew A out — that would leave the toilet in a mess. So Crew B must wait. EDF alone does not know about this waiting — it only knows about the toilet when nobody is in it.

---

### 1.3 Priority Inversion

**Formal Definition:**
**Priority Inversion** is an anomalous scheduling situation in which a *higher-priority* task is **indirectly preempted** by a *lower-priority* task — not because the lower-priority task is running, but because it holds a shared resource that the higher-priority task needs. The inversion is bounded by the *duration* that any *medium-priority* task can run while the low-priority task holds the resource.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Example:** The **1997 Mars Pathfinder** incident. The Pathfinder lander's high-priority bus management task repeatedly reset due to unbounded priority inversion on a shared meteorological data bus. The bug caused system resets and was traced on Earth from the telemetry logs.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Print Spooler:**
On a desktop PC, the print spooler (low priority) acquires the printer (the resource). The bookkeeping task (high priority) needs to log a completed job and waits for the printer. Meanwhile, an antivirus scan (medium priority) becomes runnable. The OS preempts the print spooler to run the antivirus. Result: the *high-priority* bookkeeping task is now blocked behind *two* unrelated tasks — an inversion of the expected priority order.

---

### 1.4 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** is a synchronization protocol that solves *unbounded* priority inversion by **temporarily raising** (boosting) the priority of any task that is holding a resource and is blocking one or more higher-priority tasks. The boosting is **exactly to the priority of the highest-priority task currently waiting** for that resource. The boosting task is said to *inherit* the priority of the blocked task. Upon releasing the resource, the task reverts to its *original* (assigned) priority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PIP was proposed by **Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky (1990)** as a corrective measure for unbounded priority inversion. It does NOT prevent deadlocks and does NOT prevent chained blocking.

---

### 1.5 Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** is a more restrictive synchronization protocol that **assigns each resource a static priority ceiling** — defined as the **maximum static priority of any task that may ever lock that resource**. A task is permitted to lock a resource **only if its active (current) priority is strictly higher than the priority ceilings of all resources currently locked by other tasks**. If a task would otherwise cause a ceiling violation, it is *blocked*. Upon inheriting a resource, the task's priority is raised to the *ceiling* of that resource.

> [!NOTE]
> PCP guarantees:
> 1. **Mutual exclusion** on shared resources.
> 2. **Deadlock freedom.**
> 3. **Bounded blocking** — a task can be blocked by at most **one** lower-priority task for at most the duration of **one critical section**.

> [!NOTE]
> The variant where "strictly higher" is replaced by "higher than or equal" is called the **Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol (IPCP)**, which is also a KTU-relevant term.

---

### 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following canonical metrics are used throughout this topic and are mandatory for KTU numerical problems:

- **$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$** — Processor utilization (dimensionless, range $[0,1]$).
- **$C_i$** — Worst-case execution time (WCET) of task $i$ (seconds).
- **$T_i$** — Period of task $i$ (seconds).
- **$D_i$** — Relative deadline of task $i$ (seconds).
- **$a_i$** — Arrival (release) time of job of task $i$.
- **$d_i$** — Absolute deadline of a job: $d_i = a_i + D_i$.
- **$B_i$** — Worst-case blocking time of task $i$ under PCP/PIP (seconds).
- **Priority ceiling of resource $R_k$:** $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P_i \mid \text{task } i \text{ may lock } R_k\}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 How EDF Operates — Step-by-Step Logic

The EDF scheduler runs a loop. At *every* scheduling event (job arrival, job completion, or resource release), the scheduler executes the following decision:

1. **Identify all jobs** currently released (arrived) but not yet completed.
2. **For each such job, compute its absolute deadline** $d_i = a_i + D_i$.
3. **Sort** all eligible jobs by increasing $d_i$ (ties broken arbitrarily, e.g., by job ID).
4. **Dispatch** the job with the smallest $d_i$ to the processor.
5. **On a new event** (arrival/completion), repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> Because step (3) requires a sort and step (4) a dispatch at *every event*, EDF is $O(n \log n)$ per decision. In a system with $n$ tasks, its per-scheduling overhead grows with $n$, which is the *practical* reason RMS (a fixed-priority scheme) is preferred in deeply embedded controllers despite EDF's optimality.

### 2.2 EDF Feasibility — The Liu & Layland Condition

For a set of $n$ **independent** periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$, the schedule is feasible under EDF **if and only if**:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1$$

This is a *necessary and sufficient* condition. Unlike RMS, **there is no separate worst-case bound** like $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — EDF uses 100% of the processor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If the problem statement says *independent* tasks and EDF, the answer is simply "feasible iff $U \le 1$." If dependencies exist, the answer involves response-time analysis (RTA), not the simple utilization test.

### 2.3 Resource Constraint Issue — Why Naive Preemption Fails

Consider a scenario in which:
- Task $L$ (low priority) locks resource $R$ at $t = 0$ for $C_R = 5$ ms.
- Task $H$ (high priority) arrives at $t = 1$ ms, requires $R$, and is *blocked* because $L$ holds it.
- Task $M$ (medium priority) arrives at $t = 2$ ms and preempts $L$.

Without a synchronization protocol, $H$ is blocked for the *entire duration* that $L$ is preempted by $M$ and any other medium-priority task that runs. This is **unbounded priority inversion** — its duration is bounded by *no constant expressible in the task parameters*.

### 2.4 Priority Inheritance — Why It Works

Under PIP, the moment $H$ attempts to lock $R$ and is blocked, the scheduler raises $L$'s **active priority** to $P_H$ (the priority of the highest waiter). Consequences:

- $M$ can no longer preempt $L$ (since $L$ is now running at $P_H > P_M$).
- $L$ executes at $P_H$ until it releases $R$.
- $L$ then reverts to its original $P_L$.
- $H$ now locks $R$ and runs at $P_H$.

**Effective blocking time for $H$ under PIP:** at most the duration of $L$'s critical section, regardless of $M$'s activity. This bounds the inversion.

### 2.5 Priority Ceiling — Why It Is More Restrictive

PCP adds an *a-priori* check **before** allowing a task to enter a critical section:

- Every resource $R_k$ has a ceiling $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P_i \mid \text{task } i \text{ accesses } R_k\}$.
- A task $T_j$ may lock $R_k$ only if $P_j > \max\{\Pi(R_x) \mid R_x \text{ currently locked by tasks } \ne T_j\}$.
- If the check fails, $T_j$ is blocked, and the *currently locking* task inherits $P_j$ (or the ceiling, depending on variant).

This "look before you lock" rule yields:
- **No deadlocks** (proved by the ceiling invariant).
- **Single-blocking property:** any task is blocked by at most one critical section of any lower-priority task.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ | Total processor utilization | dimensionless, $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| 2 | $U \le 1$ | EDF feasibility (independent tasks) | exact bound |
| 3 | $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | RMS feasibility (sufficient only) | sufficient, not necessary |
| 4 | $d_i = a_i + D_i$ | Absolute deadline of a job | seconds |
| 5 | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ | Worst-case response time of $T_i$ | seconds |
| 6 | $B_i^{PCP} = \max\{\text{duration of any CS of } T_j \mid P_j < P_i \text{ and } T_j \text{ shares a resource with } T_i\}$ | Worst-case blocking under PCP/IPCP | seconds |
| 7 | $B_i^{PIP} \le \sum_{j: P_j < P_i} \text{length of one CS of } T_j$ that blocks $T_i$ | Worst-case blocking under PIP (looser bound) | seconds |
| 8 | $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ locks } R_k\}$ | Priority ceiling of resource $R_k$ | priority value |
| 9 | Chained blocking bound (PIP): $B_i \le \sum (\text{CS durations from } P_j < P_i)$ | PIP still allows multiple-block chain | seconds |
| 10 | Single blocking bound (PCP): $B_i \le \max(\text{one CS from any lower-prio task})$ | PCP bounds blocking to a single CS | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In the table above, $B_i$ refers to *blocking* time, NOT to a *B*-priority. Do not confuse with $P_i$ (assigned priority).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EDF** underpins commercial real-time OS scheduling in **Linux SCHED_DEADLINE**, **FreeRTOS POSIX extensions**, and avionics demonstrators.
- **PIP and PCP** are first-class citizens in **POSIX mutexes with priority inheritance (`pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)`)** and in **AUTOSAR OS** (OSEK/VDX successor) for automotive ECUs.
- The **Priority Ceiling Protocol Emulation (PCRE)** in VxWorks and **RTEMS** provides PCP semantics for legacy code.
- **Mars Pathfinder** debugging (1997) is a textbook case for PIP necessity in spacecraft flight software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example A — EDF Schedule Tracing

**Problem:**
Three independent tasks arrive at $t=0$ together. Compute the EDF schedule for $t \in [0, 10]$ and determine feasibility.

| Task $i$ | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | First release $a_1$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| $T_2$ | 2 | 5 | 5 | 0 |
| $T_3$ | 1 | 7 | 7 | 0 |

**Step 1 — Compute utilization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \\
  &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{1}{7} \\
  &= 0.2500 + 0.4000 + 0.1429 \\
  &= 0.7929
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the EDF feasibility condition.** $U = 0.7929 \le 1$, so the task set is **feasible** under EDF.

**Step 3 — Build the schedule event by event.** At each instant, list all *released but not completed* jobs, then pick the one with the smallest $d_i$.

| Interval | Released jobs (with $d_i$) | EDF choice | Running |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[0, 1)$ | $T_1(d{=}4), T_2(d{=}5), T_3(d{=}7)$ | $T_1$ (smallest $d$) | $T_1$ |
| $[1, 2)$ | $T_2(5), T_3(7)$; $T_1$ not yet released again | $T_2$ | $T_2$ |
| $[2, 3)$ | $T_2(5), T_3(7)$ | $T_2$ | $T_2$ |
| $[3, 4)$ | $T_3(7)$ only | $T_3$ | $T_3$ |
| $[4, 5)$ | $T_1(d{=}8)$ released at $t=4$; $T_3(7)$ still alive | $T_3$ (deadline 7) | $T_3$ |
| $[5, 6)$ | $T_1(8), T_2(d{=}10$ released at $t{=}5)$ | $T_1$ (deadline 8) | $T_1$ |
| $[6, 7)$ | $T_2(10)$ | $T_2$ | $T_2$ |
| $[7, 8)$ | $T_2(10), T_3(d{=}14$ released at $t{=}7)$ | $T_2$ (deadline 10) | $T_2$ |
| $[8, 9)$ | $T_1(d{=}12$ released at $t{=}8), T_3(14)$ | $T_1$ (deadline 12) | $T_1$ |
| $[9,10)$ | $T_3(14)$ | $T_3$ | $T_3$ |

**Step 4 — Verify deadline satisfaction.**
- $T_1$ job 1 completes at $t=1 \le 4$. ✓
- $T_2$ job 1 completes at $t=3 \le 5$. ✓
- $T_3$ job 1 completes at $t=5 \le 7$. ✓
- $T_1$ job 2 completes at $t=6 \le 8$. ✓
- $T_2$ job 2 completes at $t=8 \le 10$. ✓
- $T_3$ job 2 completes at $t=10 \le 14$. ✓

All deadlines met. Feasibility confirmed by construction.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example B — Unbounded Priority Inversion

**Problem:**
Three tasks share a resource $R$. Trace the schedule from $t=0$ to $t=8$ ms *without* any synchronization protocol.

| Task | Priority | $C_i$ (ms) | Period / Arrival | Critical section |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L$ (low)    | 1 (lowest)    | 3 | Arrives $t=0$ | Locks $R$ from $t=0$ to $t=3$ |
| $M$ (medium) | 2             | 2 | Arrives $t=1$ | No resource |
| $H$ (high)   | 3 (highest)   | 1 | Arrives $t=1$ | Locks $R$ from $t=1$, needs $\ge 1$ ms |

**Step 1 — Event at $t=0$.** Only $L$ exists; $L$ runs. $L$ locks $R$ at $t=0$.

**Step 2 — Event at $t=1$.** Both $M$ and $H$ arrive. $H$ tries to lock $R$ and is blocked (because $L$ holds it). $M$ preempts $L$.

| Interval | Running | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $[0, 1)$ | $L$ | Only task |
| $[1, 3)$ | $M$ | Preempts $L$ ($P_M > P_L$); $H$ blocked on $R$ |
| $[3, 4)$ | $L$ | $M$ done; $L$ finishes CS, releases $R$ |
| $[4, 5)$ | $H$ | $H$ finally locks $R$ and runs |

**Step 3 — Compute the inversion duration for $H$.**
$H$ arrived at $t=1$ and was first scheduled at $t=4$. Blocking time $B_H = 3$ ms.

**Step 4 — Identify the inversion type.**
The 3 ms blocking exceeds the *one* critical section of $L$ *and* the *one* critical section of $M$ — but $M$'s running is the *cause* of the extra delay. Because $M$ is a *medium-priority* task whose duration is *not* bounded by anything in $H$'s parameters, this is **unbounded priority inversion** if $M$'s execution time grows arbitrarily.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example C — Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Trace

**Same setup as 3.2, but with PIP.**

**Step 1 — $t=0$:** $L$ runs, locks $R$.

**Step 2 — $t=1$:** $H$ arrives, requests $R$, is blocked. PIP rule: $L$ **inherits** $P_H = 3$. $L$ is now running at effective priority 3.

**Step 3 — $t=1$:** $M$ arrives. $P_M = 2 < 3 = $ active priority of $L$. **$M$ cannot preempt $L$.**

**Step 4 — $[1, 3)$:** $L$ continues running at inherited priority 3, finishes CS, releases $R$.

**Step 5 — $t=3$:** $L$ reverts to original priority 1. $H$ acquires $R$ and runs.

| Interval | Running | $L$'s effective priority |
|---|---|---|
| $[0,1)$ | $L$ | 1 |
| $[1,3)$ | $L$ (inherited) | 3 |
| $[3,4)$ | $H$ | 1 (reverted) |
| $[4,5)$ | $L$ (remaining code) | 1 |

**Step 6 — Blocking duration for $H$ under PIP:** $B_H = 2$ ms (from $t=1$ to $t=3$). Compare with 3 ms *without* PIP. The inversion is now **bounded** by the duration of $L$'s critical section.

> [!NOTE]
> Note that PIP only bound the inversion; $L$ did *not* finish earlier in wall-clock time because $L$ was the one running. The benefit is that $M$ was *not allowed* to delay the resolution.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example D — Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) Assignment

**Problem:**
Three tasks share two resources. Compute the priority ceiling of each resource and determine the blocking time of the highest-priority task.

| Task | Static priority $P_i$ | Resources used |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ (high)   | 5 | $R_1, R_2$ |
| $T_2$ (medium) | 3 | $R_1$ |
| $T_3$ (low)    | 1 | $R_2$ |

**Step 1 — Compute priority ceilings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi(R_1) &= \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ uses } R_1\} = \max\{5, 3\} = 5 \\
\Pi(R_2) &= \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ uses } R_2\} = \max\{5, 1\} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the PCP rule at every lock attempt.**

Suppose at some instant, $T_3$ has locked $R_2$ (ceiling 5). $T_1$ arrives and wishes to lock $R_1$ (ceiling 5). The PCP rule is:

$$P_{T_1} = 5 \stackrel{?}{>} \Pi(R_2) = 5$$

The check is *strict inequality*. $5 > 5$ is **false**, so $T_1$ is **blocked** until $T_3$ releases $R_2$. (This is the *Immediate Priority Ceiling* variant; in the *original* PCP, the rule is $>$, in IPCP the rule is $\ge$ — different texts use different conventions. KTU expects the strict version unless otherwise stated.)

**Step 3 — Worst-case blocking of $T_1$ under PCP.**

$$
B_{T_1}^{PCP} = \max\{\text{duration of CS of } T_3 \text{ in } R_2\}
$$

**One** critical section — the *single-blocking* property. Compare with PIP, where $T_1$ could be blocked by CSes of *both* $T_2$ and $T_3$ in series (chained blocking).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | EDF | RMS (for context) | PIP | PCP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Priority type | Dynamic | Fixed | Fixed + transient boost | Fixed + ceiling |
| Optimal for independent tasks (uniprocessor) | Yes (if $U\le 1$) | No (sufficient bound only) | N/A | N/A |
| Prevents unbounded inversion | N/A (no resources) | N/A | Yes | Yes |
| Prevents deadlock | N/A | N/A | No | Yes |
| Bounded blocking | N/A | N/A | Loose (chained) | Tight (one CS) |
| Implementation complexity | $O(n \log n)$ per decision | $O(1)$ per decision | Medium | Higher |
| Used in | Linux SCHED_DEADLINE | Most RTOS default | POSIX mutexes | AUTOSAR OS |

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 EDF Decision Flow — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Scheduler Tick]) --> checkQueue{Is ready queue<br/>non-empty?}
    checkQueue -- No --> idle([CPU Idle / Low-Power])
    checkQueue -- Yes --> collect[Collect all released<br/>but not completed jobs]
    collect --> computeD[For each job, compute<br/>absolute deadline d_i = a_i + D_i]
    computeD --> sortJobs[Sort jobs by increasing d_i]
    sortJobs --> pickMin[Pick job with smallest d_i]
    pickMin --> dispatch[Dispatch job to CPU]
    dispatch --> waitEvent[Wait for next event<br/>arrival or completion]
    waitEvent --> start
    idle --> waitEvent
```

### 4.2 Resource Constraint — Mutual Exclusion Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Task L (low)
    participant M as Task M (medium)
    participant H as Task H (high)
    participant R as Resource R

    Note over L: t = 0
    L->>R: lock(R)
    R-->>L: granted
    Note over L: CS of L begins
    Note over L: t = 1
    H->>R: lock(R)
    R-->>H: BLOCKED (L holds it)
    Note over M: t = 1
    M->>L: preempt (higher priority)
    Note over M: M runs from t=1 to t=3
    Note over L: t = 3
    M-->>L: M done
    L->>R: unlock(R) (CS ends at t=3)
    R-->>H: granted
    Note over H: H runs at t=3
```

### 4.3 PIP — Priority Boost Mechanism

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Running
    Running --> HoldsResource: Lock R
    HoldsResource --> InheritedPriority: Higher-prio task T_h blocked on R
    InheritedPriority --> HoldsResource: T_h unblocks from R or no longer waiting
    HoldsResource --> Running: Unlock R, revert to original priority
    InheritedPriority --> HoldsResource: Original CS ends, resource released
    note right of InheritedPriority: Active priority = max(P_self, P_waiter)
```

### 4.4 PCP — Ceiling Check Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    request[Task T_i requests lock on R_k] --> computePi[Compute static priority P_i]
    computePi --> findCeilings[Find max ceiling C_sys of all<br/>resources currently locked<br/>by OTHER tasks]
    findCeilings --> check{P_i > C_sys ?}
    check -- Yes --> grant[Grant lock, set T_i active priority = max P_i, Pi ceiling of R_k]
    check -- No --> block[Block T_i]
    block --> waitRelease[Wait for conflicting task<br/>to release its resource]
    waitRelease --> recompute[Re-evaluate ceiling check]
    recompute --> check
    grant --> useCS[Execute critical section]
    useCS --> release[Unlock R_k]
    release --> revert[Revert T_i to original priority]
    revert --> [*]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Each Protocol Solves a Specific Failure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Naive[Naive Preemption plus Shared Resources]
        A1[High-Priority Task H] -->|blocked by| A2[Low-Priority Task L holding R]
        A2 -->|preempted by| A3[Medium-Priority Task M]
        A3 -->|delays| A1
    end

    subgraph WithPIP[With Priority Inheritance]
        B1[High-Priority Task H] -->|blocked by| B2[Low-Priority Task L now at P_H]
        B2 -->|NOT preempted by| B3[Medium-Priority Task M]
        B3 -.->|cannot preempt| B2
        B1 -->|runs after CS ends| B4[H executes at t = 3]
    end

    subgraph WithPCP[With Priority Ceiling]
        C1[High-Priority Task H] -->|ceiling check| C2{Is P_H greater than<br/>system ceiling?}
        C2 -- Yes --> C3[H runs immediately]
        C2 -- No --> C4[H blocks until L releases R]
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
Define the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling algorithm. State the feasibility condition for EDF when scheduling a set of $n$ independent periodic tasks on a uniprocessor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** EDF is a dynamic-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm in which, at every scheduling decision point, the task whose absolute deadline $d_i = a_i + D_i$ is *earliest* is selected for execution. The task set is independent.
- **Feasibility condition (1.5 marks):** A set of $n$ independent periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$ is feasible under EDF on a uniprocessor **if and only if** the total processor utilization
$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1.$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
What is **priority inversion** in real-time systems? Explain how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** addresses it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Priority inversion (1.5 marks):** Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is *indirectly* delayed by the execution of a *lower-priority* task that holds a shared resource. The inversion becomes **unbounded** when a *medium-priority* task preempts the lower-priority task, extending the high-priority task's blocking time beyond any fixed bound expressible in the task parameters.
- **PIP remedy (1.5 marks):** Under PIP, the moment a higher-priority task $T_H$ is blocked on a resource held by a lower-priority task $T_L$, the scheduler *temporarily raises* the active priority of $T_L$ to that of $T_H$ (or the highest waiter). This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting $T_L$, ensuring that the blocking duration of $T_H$ is bounded by the duration of $T_L$'s critical section. $T_L$ reverts to its original priority upon releasing the resource.

---

### Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — EDF + Priority Inversion + PIP

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Consider the following independent task set scheduled by EDF on a uniprocessor:
>
> | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $T_1$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
> | $T_2$ | 3 | 8 | 8 |
> | $T_3$ | 1 | 12 | 12 |
>
> **(i) [3 marks]** Compute the total processor utilization $U$ and determine whether the task set is feasible under EDF.
> **(ii) [4 marks]** Construct the EDF schedule from $t=0$ to $t=12$ ms and verify that no deadlines are missed.

> **(b)** Three tasks $H$, $M$, $L$ share a single resource $R$. Their priorities satisfy $P_H > P_M > P_L$. $L$ locks $R$ at $t=0$ for $4$ ms. $H$ arrives at $t=1$ ms and requests $R$. $M$ arrives at $t=2$ ms and has an execution time of $3$ ms.
> **(i) [3 marks]** Trace the schedule from $t=0$ to $t=8$ ms **without** any synchronization protocol. Identify the type of priority inversion that occurs.
> **(ii) [4 marks]** Re-trace the schedule with **PIP** applied. Compute the worst-case blocking time of $H$ and comment on the bound.

##### Model Solution to Question A

**(a)(i) Utilization and feasibility (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\
  &= \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{12} \\
  &= 0.3333 + 0.3750 + 0.0833 \\
  &= 0.7917
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $U = 0.7917 \le 1$, the task set is **feasible under EDF** for independent tasks. (3 marks)

**(a)(ii) Schedule (4 marks):**

Build the timeline by tracking the **earliest deadline** among released jobs:

| Interval | Released & pending | EDF pick (smallest $d_i$) | Running |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[0,2)$ | $T_1(d{=}6), T_2(8), T_3(12)$ | $T_1$ | $T_1$ |
| $[2,5)$ | $T_2(8), T_3(12)$ | $T_2$ | $T_2$ |
| $[5,6)$ | $T_2(8), T_3(12)$ | $T_2$ | $T_2$ |
| $[6,8)$ | $T_1(d{=}12), T_2(8), T_3(12)$ | $T_2$ (deadline 8) | $T_2$ |
| $[8,9)$ | $T_1(12), T_3(12)$ | $T_1$ (tie-break) | $T_1$ |
| $[9,10)$ | $T_1(12), T_3(12)$ | $T_1$ | $T_1$ |
| $[10,11)$ | $T_3(12)$ | $T_3$ | $T_3$ |
| $[11,12)$ | idle | – | idle |

All jobs complete by their deadlines. (4 marks)

**(b)(i) Without PIP (3 marks):**

| Interval | Running | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $[0,1)$ | $L$ (in CS, holds $R$) | – |
| $[1,2)$ | $M$ (preempts $L$, $P_M > P_L$) | $H$ blocked on $R$ |
| $[2,4)$ | $M$ (continues, 3 ms total) | $L$ still holds $R$ |
| $[4,5)$ | $L$ (resumes, finishes CS, releases $R$) | $H$ still blocked |
| $[5,6)$ | $H$ (acquires $R$, runs 1 ms) | – |

$H$'s blocking time: $B_H = 5 - 1 = 4$ ms. This is **unbounded priority inversion** because $M$ (a medium-priority task) prolonged $H$'s wait beyond $L$'s critical section. (3 marks)

**(b)(ii) With PIP (4 marks):**

At $t = 1$, $H$ blocks on $R$. PIP rule: $L$ inherits $P_H$. Now $L$'s active priority is $P_H > P_M$, so $M$ **cannot** preempt $L$.

| Interval | Running | $L$'s active priority |
|---|---|---|
| $[0,1)$ | $L$ in CS | $P_L$ |
| $[1,4)$ | $L$ (inherited, finishes CS) | $P_H$ |
| $[4,5)$ | $H$ (acquires $R$, runs 1 ms) | $P_L$ (reverted) |
| $[5,8)$ | $L$ (resumes remaining code) | $P_L$ |

$B_H = 4 - 1 = 3$ ms. The blocking is now **bounded** by the duration of $L$'s critical section (3 ms). $M$ is *prevented* from extending the inversion. (4 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — PCP Design and Worst-Case Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** A real-time system has three tasks sharing two resources as given below:
>
> | Task | Static priority $P_i$ | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Resources used |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | $T_1$ (high)   | 5 | 20 | 4 | $R_1, R_2$ |
> | $T_2$ (medium) | 3 | 30 | 6 | $R_1$ |
> | $T_3$ (low)    | 1 | 50 | 10 | $R_2$ |
>
> Each critical section of $R_1$ takes $1$ ms; of $R_2$ takes $2$ ms.
> **(i) [3 marks]** Compute the priority ceilings of $R_1$ and $R_2$ under the **Priority Ceiling Protocol**.
> **(ii) [4 marks]** Suppose $T_3$ has locked $R_2$. Determine whether $T_1$ is allowed to enter a critical section on $R_1$. Justify using the ceiling rule.

> **(b)** **(i) [3 marks]** Define the **single-blocking property** of PCP and explain why it does not hold under PIP.
> **(ii) [4 marks]** Suppose $T_2$ holds $R_1$ for 1 ms and $T_3$ holds $R_2$ for 2 ms. Compute the **worst-case blocking time** of $T_1$ under (a) PCP and (b) PIP. Comment on the difference.

##### Model Solution to Question B

**(a)(i) Priority ceilings (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi(R_1) &= \max\{P_1, P_2\} = \max\{5, 3\} = 5 \\
\Pi(R_2) &= \max\{P_1, P_3\} = \max\{5, 1\} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks)

**(a)(ii) Ceiling check (4 marks):**

$T_3$ holds $R_2$. The system ceiling is $\Pi(R_2) = 5$. $T_1$ has static priority $P_1 = 5$. The PCP rule is:

$$P_1 = 5 \stackrel{?}{>} \Pi(R_2) = 5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 5 > 5 \;\text{is FALSE}$$

Therefore, $T_1$ is **blocked** and cannot lock $R_1$ until $T_3$ releases $R_2$. (4 marks)

**(b)(i) Single-blocking property (3 marks):**

The *single-blocking property* of PCP states that a task $T_i$ can be blocked by **at most one** critical section of **at most one** lower-priority task, and for **at most** the duration of **one** critical section. PIP does not guarantee this: under PIP, $T_1$ could first be blocked by $T_2$'s CS of $R_1$ (1 ms), then $T_2$ releases $R_1$ and $T_1$ immediately tries to lock $R_2$, only to be blocked by $T_3$'s CS of $R_2$ (2 ms). This *chained blocking* is not bounded to a single CS. (3 marks)

**(b)(ii) Worst-case blocking (4 marks):**

**Under PCP:** $B_{T_1}^{PCP} = \max\{\text{duration of one CS of any lower-prio task sharing a resource with } T_1\}$

$$
B_{T_1}^{PCP} = \max\{1 \text{ ms (CS of } T_2 \text{ on } R_1), \; 2 \text{ ms (CS of } T_3 \text{ on } R_2)\} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Under PIP:** $T_1$ can be chained-blocked by $T_2$ and $T_3$:

$$
B_{T_1}^{PIP} \le 1 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Comment:** PCP provides a tighter bound (2 ms) than PIP (3 ms) at the cost of additional runtime ceiling checks. (4 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $U \le 1$ (EDF) with $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ (RMS).** The latter is a *sufficient* condition, not necessary, and applies to RMS, not EDF. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting that PIP does not prevent deadlock.** If a question asks for deadlock prevention, PIP is the wrong answer; state PCP (or the stack resource policy) explicitly.
> 3. **Applying the ceiling check with $\ge$ instead of $>$** (or vice versa). KTU typically expects strict inequality for the original PCP. State the variant you are using.
> 4. **Calculating $B_i$ under PIP by summing ALL lower-priority CSes, even those that don't conflict with $T_i$.** The sum is restricted to CSes that *block* $T_i$ — i.e., on resources *shared* with $T_i$.
> 5. **Treating $B_i$ as a priority.** $B_i$ is time, in seconds; do not write it next to a priority value.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EDF** is dynamic-priority; selection criterion is the *earliest absolute deadline*; feasibility requires $U \le 1$ for *independent* tasks.
- **RMS** is fixed-priority; the bound $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is *sufficient*, not necessary.
- A **resource** in real-time systems is a *non-shareable, mutually exclusive* entity (bus, sensor, I/O port, mutex).
- The **Resource Constraint Issue** arises when EDF/RMS must be extended to handle shared non-preemptible resources.
- **Priority Inversion** is *unbounded* when a medium-priority task can extend a high-priority task's blocking without limit.
- **PIP (Sha–Rajkumar–Lehoczky 1990):** temporarily boosts a holding task's priority to that of the highest waiter; bounds the inversion to *one* critical section; does **not** prevent deadlock; allows *chained blocking*.
- **PCP:** assigns each resource a ceiling $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ uses } R_k\}$; a task may lock a resource only if its active priority *strictly exceeds* the system ceiling; prevents deadlock and yields *single-blocking* property.
- **IPCP (Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol):** on lock, the task's priority jumps to the *ceiling* of the resource immediately; simpler to implement, equivalent worst-case bound.
- **Feasibility check using $U \le 1$ (EDF) is only valid for independent tasks.** With shared resources, you must use **response-time analysis**: $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$, with $B_i$ computed per protocol.
- **POSIX maps PIP** to `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)`; **AUTOSAR OS** implements IPCP/PCP semantics for ECUs.
- The **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** reset bug is the canonical KTU case study illustrating the cost of *not* using PIP.
- For numerical problems: always convert all times to the **same unit** (ms or μs) before summing utilizations.
- In EDF schedule traces, **rebuild the ready queue at every event**; do not extrapolate across events.
